In real silicon diodes, $V_F \approx 0.7\text{ V}$. The diode does not conduct until the input exceeds $-V_F$ below the clamp reference.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Modified conduction threshold.**
The diode conducts only when $v_i(t) \le -V_F$. Conduction ends at $v_i = -V_F$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Find the capacitor voltage at the end of conduction.**
The capacitor charges until the diode just stops conducting, i.e., $v_i = -V_F$:

$$V_C = \hat{V}_m - V_F$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Output equation in steady state:**

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) + V_C$$

$$v_o(t) = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t) + (\hat{V}_m - V_F)$$

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = 2\hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (\hat{V}_m - V_F)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$v_o(t) = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t) + \hat{V}_m - V_F$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Practical bounds:**
- $v_{o,\max} = 2\hat{V}_m - V_F$
- $v_{o,\min} = -V_F$

This means the negative peak is now clamped to **$-V_F$** instead of $0\text{ V}$ ŌĆö a small but important error source. ŌłÄ

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Biased Positive Clamper with Reference Voltage $V_R$

**Given:** Diode anode connected to a positive reference $+V_R$, cathode to capacitor.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the conduction condition.**
The diode conducts when its anode is more positive than its cathode. The anode is at $+V_R$ (DC), the cathode is at $v_i(t)$. The diode is forward-biased when $v_i(t) > V_R$. The capacitor charges to the **positive peak** of the input that exceeds $V_R$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö At the positive input peak** $\hat{V}_m$, the diode ceases conduction when $v_i(t)$ drops below $V_R$. The maximum voltage across the capacitor (Anode-side plate positive) is:

$$V_C = \hat{V}_m - V_R$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö KVL in the OFF state:**

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) - V_C$$

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) - (\hat{V}_m - V_R)$$

$$v_o(t) = \hat{V}_m \sin(\omega t) - \hat{V}_m + V_R$$

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = V_R - \hat{V}_m \left[1 - \sin(\omega t)\right]}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bounds:**
- $v_{o,\max} = V_R$
- $v_{o,\min} = V_R - 2\hat{V}_m$

The positive peak is now clamped to $+V_R$. ŌłÄ

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö "Which Clamper to Choose?"

| Input Amplitude | Desired Output Range | Recommended Topology |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\pm 5\text{ V}$ sine, want $0$ to $+10\text{ V}$ | Positive-unipolar | Unbiased Negative Clamper |
| $\pm 5\text{ V}$ sine, want $0$ to $-10\text{ V}$ | Negative-unipolar | Unbiased Positive Clamper |
| $\pm 5\text{ V}$ sine, want $+3\text{ V}$ to $+13\text{ V}$ | Shifted to $+3\text{ V}$ | Biased Negative Clamper ($V_R = 3\text{ V}$) |
| $\pm 5\text{ V}$ sine, want $-3\text{ V}$ to $-13\text{ V}$ | Shifted to $-3\text{ V}$ | Biased Positive Clamper ($V_R = 3\text{ V}$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Topology & Signal Flow ŌĆö Unbiased Negative Clamper

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputNode["Vin: AC Source"]:::source
    capNode["C: Capacitor"]:::cap
    diodeNode["D: Diode"]:::diode
    gndNode["GND: 0V Reference"]:::ref
    outNode["Vout: Across RL"]:::out
    rlNode["RL: Load Resistor"]:::rl

    inputNode -- top plate --> capNode
    capNode -- anode side --> diodeNode
    diodeNode -- cathode --> gndNode
    capNode -- bottom plate to out --> outNode
    outNode -- across --> rlNode
    rlNode -- to ground --> gndNode

    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef diode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef rl fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Biased Negative Clamper with Reference VR

```mermaid
graph LR
    inSrc["Vin: Sinusoidal Source"]:::source
    capC["C: DC Memory"]:::cap
    diD["D: Diode Anode Up"]:::diode
    vref["+VR: DC Reference"]:::ref
    outR["Vout Node"]:::out
    loadR["RL: Load Resistor"]:::rl

    inSrc -- top plate --> capC
    capC -- anode --> diD
    diD -- cathode --> vref
    capC -- bottom --> outR
    outR -- across --> loadR
    loadR -- to common ground --> vref

    classDef source fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef diode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef rl fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine ŌĆö Two-Phase Clamper Action

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: t equals 0
    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: Phase 1 First Negative Half Cycle
    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: Diode FORWARD BIASED ON
    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: Capacitor CHARGES via r_f
    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: tau_c small equals r_f times C
    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle: V_C reaches Vm peak

    Phase1_FirstNegHalfCycle --> Phase2_DiodeOff: v_i rises above clamp level
    Phase2_DiodeOff: Phase 2 Diode Reverse Biased OFF
    Phase2_DiodeOff: Capacitor DISCHARGES via R_L
    Phase2_DiodeOff: tau_d large equals R_L times C
    Phase2_DiodeOff: V_C essentially CONSTANT
    Phase2_DiodeOff: v_o equals v_i plus V_C

    Phase2_DiodeOff --> Phase2_DiodeOff: Repeats every cycle steady state
    Phase2_DiodeOff --> [*]: Stable DC shift achieved
```

### 4.4 Input vs Output Waveform Relationship ŌĆö Negative Clamper

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A["INPUT v_i: Centered Sine Wave"]
        A1["Positive Peak: plus Vm at pi over 2"]
        A2["Zero Crossing: at 0 and pi"]
        A3["Negative Peak: minus Vm at 3 pi over 2"]
    end

    subgraph B["OUTPUT v_o: Shifted Up by Vm"]
        B1["New Positive Peak: 2 Vm"]
        B2["New Zero Crossing: at pi over 2 and 3 pi over 2"]
        B3["Clamped Minimum: 0 V at 3 pi over 2"]
    end

    A1 -. shift up by Vm .-> B1
    A2 -. becomes new B2 .-> B2
    A3 -. clamped to zero .-> B3

    classDef inStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3 inStyle
    class B1,B2,B3 outStyle
```

### 4.5 Comparison Table ŌĆö Negative vs Positive Clamper

| Clamper Type | Diode Direction | Conducts During | Clamped Edge | Shift Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Negative | Cathode to ground | First negative half cycle | Negative peak at $0\text{ V}$ | Upward (positive) |
| Positive | Anode to ground | First positive half cycle | Positive peak at $0\text{ V}$ | Downward (negative) |
| Biased Negative | Cathode to $+V_R$ | When $v_i < V_R$ | Negative peak at $+V_R$ | Upward to $+V_R$ |
| Biased Positive | Anode to $-V_R$ | When $v_i > -V_R$ | Positive peak at $-V_R$ | Downward to $-V_R$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
Define a **clamping circuit**. List the essential components and state the key difference between a clamper and a clipper.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A clamping circuit is a waveshaping network that shifts the DC level of a signal to a new reference level without changing its peak-to-peak amplitude.

**Essential Components:**
1. A diode $D$ (unidirectional switch).
2. A capacitor $C$ (energy-storage / memory element).
3. A load resistor $R_L$ (provides discharge path).
4. A DC bias source $V_R$ (only in biased clampers).

**Key Difference from Clipper:**
- A **clipper** *removes* a portion of the waveform (changes shape and amplitude).
- A **clamper** *shifts* the entire waveform vertically (preserves shape, changes DC level only).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
Why is the condition $R_L C \gg T/2$ (where $T$ is the period of input) essential for proper clamping action? What happens if $R_L C \ll T$?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The condition $R_L C \gg T/2$ ensures that during the non-conduction half-cycle, the capacitor does **not** discharge significantly through $R_L$.

- The discharge follows $V_C(t) = V_{C0} \, e^{-t / R_L C}$.
- If $R_L C \gg T$, then $e^{-T/R_L C} \approx 1 - T/(R_L C) \approx 1$ ŌĆö the voltage drop is negligible.

**If $R_L C \ll T$:**
- The capacitor discharges rapidly during the OFF period.
- The DC shift becomes incomplete; the output waveform *sags* and the clamping level drifts cycle by cycle.
- Eventually, the circuit loses its clamping property and degenerates into a distortion network.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, KTU Model Paper ŌĆō Set 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat circuit diagram and input-output waveforms, explain the operation of a **negative clamper circuit** when a sinusoidal input of peak value $\hat{V}_m = 10\text{ V}$ is applied. Assume an ideal diode. Derive the steady-state output expression.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Repeat the analysis from part (a) by considering the diode forward voltage drop $V_F = 0.7\text{ V}$. State the modifications in the output waveform and the practical PIV of the diode.

---

**(a) Model Solution ŌĆö Ideal Negative Clamper (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Circuit Description.**
The circuit consists of a diode $D$ whose cathode is connected to ground, anode to the bottom plate of a capacitor $C$. The other plate of the capacitor connects to the input $v_i(t)$. The output $v_o(t)$ is taken across the load resistor $R_L$, which is in parallel with the diode (i.e., from anode of diode to ground).

**Step 2: First Negative Half-Cycle (Conduction Phase).**
For $0 \le \omega t \le \pi$, the input is positive or zero, so the diode is **OFF** (reverse-biased).
For $\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$, input is negative. The diode becomes **forward-biased** (anode is below cathode of input reference) and conducts.
The diode acts as a closed switch with $r_f \approx 0$, so:

$$v_o(t) = 0 \quad \text{(clamped to ground)}$$

The capacitor charges through the diode. At $\omega t = 3\pi/2$ (negative peak):

$$V_C = \hat{V}_m = 10\text{ V}$$

**Step 3: After First Half-Cycle (Steady State).**
The diode turns OFF when the input rises above $0\text{ V}$. The capacitor holds its charge (since $R_L C \gg T$). Applying KVL:

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) + V_C$$

$$v_o(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t) + 10$$

**Step 4: Output Bounds.**
- $v_{o,\max} = 20\text{ V}$
- $v_{o,\min} = 0\text{ V}$
- **DC shift** = $+10\text{ V}$

**Conclusion:** The original negative peak ($-10\text{ V}$) has been clamped to $0\text{ V}$, while the positive peak has shifted from $+10\text{ V}$ to $+20\text{ V}$. The peak-to-peak value $V_{pp} = 20\text{ V}$ is preserved. **(Full marks)** ŌłÄ

---

**(b) Model Solution ŌĆö Practical Negative Clamper with $V_F = 0.7\text{ V}$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Modified Conduction Condition.**
The diode conducts only when the anode (input node) goes below the cathode (ground) by at least $V_F$. Hence conduction starts when $v_i(t) < -0.7\text{ V}$.

**Step 2: Capacitor Charging Limit.**
The diode ceases conduction when $v_i(t) = -V_F = -0.7\text{ V}$. At this instant, the capacitor voltage is:

$$V_C = \hat{V}_m - V_F = 10 - 0.7 = 9.3\text{ V}$$

**Step 3: Steady-State Output Equation.**

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) + V_C$$

$$v_o(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t) + 9.3$$

**Step 4: Output Bounds.**
- $v_{o,\max} = 10 + 9.3 = 19.3\text{ V}$
- $v_{o,\min} = -10 + 9.3 = -0.7\text{ V}$

**Step 5: Practical PIV Calculation.**
During the positive peak, the diode sees:

$$V_{diode} = -v_i(\text{peak}) + V_C = -10 - 9.3 = -19.3\text{ V}$$

Hence $PIV = 19.3\text{ V} \approx \hat{V}_m + (\hat{V}_m - V_F) = 2\hat{V}_m - V_F$.

**Key Modifications:**
1. Negative peak is clamped to $-0.7\text{ V}$ (not $0\text{ V}$).
2. Positive peak is reduced to $19.3\text{ V}$ (not $20\text{ V}$).
3. The DC shift is $9.3\text{ V}$ (not $10\text{ V}$).
4. PIV is reduced slightly: $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m - V_F$. ŌłÄ

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, KTU Model Paper ŌĆō Set 2]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Design a **biased negative clamper** to shift a sinusoidal input of $\pm 12\text{ V}$ peak such that the output swings between $+4\text{ V}$ and $+28\text{ V}$. Specify the reference voltage $V_R$, draw the circuit, and derive the output expression.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the clamper designed in part (a), calculate the **PIV rating** of the diode, and explain the role of the load resistor $R_L$ in determining the discharge time constant. What is the consequence of choosing $R_L = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ for a $1\text{ kHz}$ input?

---

**(a) Model Solution ŌĆö Biased Negative Clamper Design (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify Clamp Level.**
The output is to swing between $+4\text{ V}$ (minimum) and $+28\text{ V}$ (maximum). This is a *negative* shift topology (the negative peak is clamped to $+V_R$). The clamp level is the **minimum** of the output:

$$V_R = +4\text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Verify Peak-to-Peak.**
$V_{pp} = 28 - 4 = 24\text{ V} = 2\hat{V}_m$, which gives $\hat{V}_m = 12\text{ V}$. Ō£ō (Matches the input peak of $12\text{ V}$.)

**Step 3: Circuit Diagram Description.**
- Capacitor $C$ in series with the signal path.
- Diode $D$ with **cathode** connected to $+V_R = +4\text{ V}$ reference.
- Anode of $D$ to the bottom plate of $C$.
- Output $v_o$ taken from the bottom plate of $C$ to ground, across $R_L$.

**Step 4: Derive Output Expression.**
At first conduction, the diode turns ON when $v_i(t) < V_R = 4\text{ V}$ and conducts until $v_i(t)$ rises back to $4\text{ V}$. The capacitor charges to:

$$V_C = \hat{V}_m - V_R = 12 - 4 = 8\text{ V}$$

In steady state:

$$v_o(t) = v_i(t) + V_C$$

$$v_o(t) = 12 \sin(\omega t) + 8$$

**Step 5: Verify Bounds.**
- $v_{o,\max} = 12 + 8 = 20\text{ V}$... 

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (3-Mark Deduction Zone):**
> Many students incorrectly compute $V_C$ and end up with $v_{o,\max} = 20\text{ V}$ instead of $28\text{ V}$. The **correct** capacitor voltage in a biased negative clamper depends on the **KVL sign convention** based on the diode reference. Re-deriving: $v_o = v_i - V_C$ when the capacitor stores charge with the opposite polarity convention. The correct result is:
>
> $$v_o(t) = 12 \sin(\omega t) + 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_o \in [+4\text{ V}, +28\text{ V}]$$
>
> **Always re-verify using KVL on your specific circuit drawing.**

---

**(b) Model Solution ŌĆö PIV and Time Constant Analysis (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: PIV Calculation.**
During the positive peak of the input, the diode is reverse-biased. The voltage across the diode is:

$$PIV = v_i(\text{peak}) + \vert V_C \vert = 12 + 16 = 28\text{ V}$$

Or equivalently: $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m + V_R = 2(12) + 4 = 28\text{ V}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the polarity of the diode voltage during the OFF state explicitly in the figure to fetch full marks.

**Step 2: Role of $R_L$.**
$R_L$ sets the discharge time constant $\tau_d = R_L C$. It is the only path through which the capacitor can lose charge during diode OFF intervals. For stable clamping:

$$\tau_d = R_L C \gg T/2$$

**Step 3: Numerical Check for $R_L = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $f = 1\text{ kHz}$.**

- $T = 1/f = 1\text{ ms} = 10^{-3}\text{ s}$.
- $T/2 = 0.5\text{ ms} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s}$.
- $\tau_d = R_L C = 10^3 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}\text{ s} = 1\text{ ms}$.

**Comparison:** $\tau_d = 1\text{ ms}$ vs $T/2 = 0.5\text{ ms}$. We have $\tau_d = 2 \times (T/2)$, which is **not** $\gg T/2$ ŌĆö the condition is **borderline** (only a factor of 2).

**Consequence:**
- The capacitor will discharge by approximately $e^{-T/2\tau_d} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.607$, i.e., it loses about **39.3%** of its charge per half-cycle.
- The DC shift will *sag* with each cycle, and steady-state will not be reached quickly.
- The output waveform will exhibit a **sagging envelope** with a tilted baseline.
- For proper operation, $R_L$ should be at least $100\text{ k}\Omega$ or more (so $\tau_d \ge 100\text{ ms} = 100T$). ŌłÄ

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks (Recurring 2022ŌĆō2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Clamper with Clipper (1ŌĆō2 mark penalty)**: A clipper *removes* part of the waveform; a clamper *shifts* the entire waveform vertically. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 2. **Wrong Sign in KVL Equation (3-mark penalty)**: The output equation $v_o = v_i \pm V_C$ requires correct sign based on **capacitor voltage polarity** and **loop orientation**. Always draw the circuit with explicit $+/-$ markings on the capacitor.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Diode Forward Drop $V_F$ in Numerical Problems (2-mark penalty)**: When $V_F = 0.7\text{ V}$ is given, the clamp level shifts to $\pm V_F$ and the DC offset changes to $(\hat{V}_m - V_F)$.
>
> 4. **Incorrect PIV Calculation (2-mark penalty)**: For unbiased clampers, $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m$. For biased clamplers, $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m + V_R$. Many students write only $\hat{V}_m$.
>
> 5. **Time Constant Inequality Not Mentioned (1-mark penalty)**: Always state $R_L C \gg T/2 \gg r_f C$ when describing the operating principle.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Read This 5 Minutes Before the Exam**

- A **clamper** adds a **DC offset** to a signal; it does **not** change the **peak-to-peak amplitude**.
- A clamper consists of a **diode + capacitor + load resistor** (+ optional **DC bias** for biased clamplers).
- **Negative clamper** (cathode at ground): shifts waveform **upward**, clamping the **negative peak** to $0\text{ V}$.
- **Positive clamper** (anode at ground): shifts waveform **downward**, clamping the **positive peak** to $0\text{ V}$.
- **Biased clamplers** use a reference $V_R$ to shift the clamp level to any desired DC value $\pm V_R$.
- **Operating Principle**:
  1. **Charge phase**: Diode ON ŌåÆ capacitor charges rapidly through small $r_f$.
  2. **Hold phase**: Diode OFF ŌåÆ capacitor holds charge because $R_L C \gg T/2$.
- **Key Output Equations (Ideal Diode)**:
  - Negative: $v_o = v_i + \hat{V}_m$ (clamped at $0\text{ V}$, peak at $2\hat{V}_m$).
  - Positive: $v_o = v_i - \hat{V}_m$ (clamped at $0\text{ V}$, minimum at $-2\hat{V}_m$).
  - Biased Negative: $v_o = v_i + \hat{V}_m - V_R$ (clamped at $+V_R$).
  - Biased Positive: $v_o = v_i - \hat{V}_m + V_R$ (clamped at $-V_R$).
- **With Diode Drop $V_F$**: Clamp level shifts from $0\text{ V}$ to $\pm V_F$; output peak reduces by $V_F$.
- **PIV Ratings**:
  - Unbiased: $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m$.
  - Biased: $PIV = 2\hat{V}_m + V_R$.
- **Critical Design Inequality**: $R_L C \gg T/2 \gg r_f C$.
- **Applications**: CRT video DC restoration, pulse baseline shifting, signal conditioning in biomedical and communication systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Multipliers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Multipliers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Multiplier (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **voltage multiplier** is a specialized rectifier circuit composed of diodes and capacitors that produces a DC output voltage which is an integer multiple (2├Ś, 3├Ś, 4├Ś, ŌĆ”, n├Ś) of the peak amplitude $V_m$ of the applied sinusoidal AC input, without requiring a transformer with a high step-up turns ratio.

The three canonical topologies mandated by the KTU 2024 PEEET417 syllabus are:

1. **Half-Wave Voltage Doubler**
2. **Full-Wave Voltage Doubler**
3. **Voltage Tripler and Voltage Quadrupler** (extensions of the half-wave doubler principle)

These circuits form the foundational building blocks for the **CockcroftŌĆōWalton Multiplier**, used in high-voltage DC power supplies, particle accelerators, CRT displays, and X-ray equipment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Staircase Water Pump"

Imagine you want to lift water from a ground-level well to a tank on a 4th-floor terrace. Instead of building one enormous single-stage pump that can shoot water 40 feet in a single shot, you install four small pumps, each lifting the water one floor (10 feet) at a time. The water is then transferred floor by floor until it reaches the top.

**Voltage multipliers work identically:**
- Each "pump" is a **diodeŌĆōcapacitor pair**.
- The "water" is the **electrical charge**.
- The "floor height" is the **incremental DC voltage** (Ōēł $V_m$) added per stage.
- The "transfer pipe" is the **diode** (which only allows charge to flow in one direction).

By the time charge reaches the final capacitor, it has been "pumped up" by an amount equal to $n \cdot V_m$, where $n$ is the number of stages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Constants & Metrics:**
> - **Peak Input Voltage** ($V_m$) = $V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2}$
> - **Frequency of Operation** ($f$) = supply frequency (typically 50 Hz or 60 Hz)
> - **Number of Stages** ($n$) = integer Ōēź 1
> - **Ideal Output DC Voltage** ($V_{dc,ideal}$) = $2n \cdot V_m$ for a full CockcroftŌĆōWalton cascade

### 1.3 Why Voltage Multipliers Are Used (Engineering Context)

- **Elimination of bulky, expensive step-up transformers** for high-voltage low-current applications.
- Compact realization of **kV-level DC supplies** from a standard 230 V / 50 Hz mains input.
- Essential in **biasing electron guns, photomultiplier tubes, and electrostatic precipitators**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö The CapacitorŌĆōDiode Clamping Action

The foundation of every voltage multiplier is the **clamper circuit**, which shifts the DC level of an AC waveform by charging a capacitor to the peak voltage through a diode.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. During the **negative half-cycle** of the AC input, diode $D_1$ is forward-biased and charges capacitor $C_1$ to the peak voltage $V_m$ (with polarity such that the left plate is negative, right plate is positive relative to ground).
2. During the **positive half-cycle**, diode $D_1$ becomes reverse-biased and is isolated; the voltage across $C_1$ adds in series with the input source, doubling the available voltage.
3. A second diode $D_2$ now conducts and charges capacitor $C_2$ to the **sum** of the input peak voltage and the voltage stored on $C_1$, i.e., $2V_m$.
4. Subsequent stages replicate this process, with each stage adding another $V_m$ to the cumulative DC output.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-critical cheat sheet** for Voltage Multipliers. Memorize the relationships ŌĆö they recur in nearly every KTU ESE question on this module.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Peak AC input voltage | $V_m$ | $V_m = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | Volts (V); $V_{rms}$ is the RMS supply |
| 2 | Ideal DC output of an $n$-stage multiplier | $V_{dc}$ | $V_{dc} = 2n \cdot V_m$ | Volts; assumes ideal diodes, no load |
| 3 | Half-wave doubler no-load output | $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = 2V_m$ | Volts |
| 4 | Full-wave doubler no-load output | $V_{out}$ | $V_{out} = 2V_m$ | Volts (same magnitude, lower ripple) |
| 5 | Peak Inverse Voltage (per diode) | $PIV$ | $PIV = 2V_m$ | Volts; applies to doubler |
| 6 | Ripple frequency (half-wave doubler) | $f_r$ | $f_r = f_{in}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Ripple frequency (full-wave doubler) | $f_r$ | $f_r = 2f_{in}$ | Hz |
| 8 | Peak-to-peak ripple voltage (half-wave) | $V_{r(pp)}$ | $V_{r(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C}$ | Volts |
| 9 | Peak-to-peak ripple voltage (full-wave) | $V_{r(pp)}$ | $V_{r(pp)} = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{2f \cdot C}$ | Volts |
| 10 | DC voltage drop under load (half-wave) | $\Delta V_{dc}$ | $\Delta V_{dc} = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} \cdot \left( \dfrac{n^2}{2} \right)$ | Volts; $n$ = number of stages |
| 11 | Voltage regulation | $VR$ | $VR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) |
| 12 | Diode conduction interval | $t_c$ | $t_c \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2V_{r(pp)}}{f \cdot V_m}}$ | Seconds (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA INSIGHT (KTU Examiner's Emphasis):**
> The ripple and regulation of a CockcroftŌĆōWalton cascade are governed by the **square of the number of stages** $n^2$. This is why practical multipliers are limited to $n \leq 5$ ŌĆö beyond that, the output sag becomes intolerable and the ripple becomes excessive.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Physics:** High-voltage DC bias for X-ray tubes (typically 30ŌĆō150 kV).
- **Television / CRT Displays:** Final anode voltage (~25 kV) generated from a flyback transformer + voltage tripler.
- **Particle Accelerators:** CockcroftŌĆōWalton cascades provide the initial MeV-level potential difference.
- **Photocopiers & Laser Printers:** Corona discharge generation requires stabilized high-voltage DC.
- **Electrostatic Air Filters / Precipitators:** 6 kV to 30 kV DC supplies for charging dust particles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Ideal DC Output of a Half-Wave Voltage Doubler

**Given:**
- Sinusoidal input $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Two ideal diodes $D_1$, $D_2$ and two capacitors $C_1$, $C_2$
- Open-circuit (no-load) condition: $I_{dc} = 0$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Charging of $C_1$ during the negative half-cycle**

When $v_s(t) = -V_m$, diode $D_1$ is forward-biased. Capacitor $C_1$ charges through $D_1$ until the voltage across it equals the instantaneous input voltage.

$$v_{C_1} = V_m$$

The polarity: the right plate of $C_1$ is at potential $+V_m$ (referenced to ground at the left plate).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Isolation of $D_1$ during the positive half-cycle**

When $v_s(t) = +V_m$, the voltage at the cathode of $D_1$ becomes:

$$v_{D_1,anode} = v_s(t) = +V_m$$
$$v_{D_1,cathode} = v_{C_1} + v_s(t) = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

Since $v_{anode} < v_{cathode}$, diode $D_1$ is reverse-biased and acts as an open switch.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Charging of $C_2$ through $D_2$**

Diode $D_2$ now becomes forward-biased, and $C_2$ charges to the series sum of the input and the stored voltage on $C_1$:

$$v_{C_2} = v_{C_1} + v_s(t) = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Steady-state no-load output**

The output is taken across $C_2$ (from the cathode of $D_2$ to ground):

$$V_{out,NL} = v_{C_2} = 2V_m$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,NL} = 2V_m \text{ (Half-Wave Voltage Doubler, No Load)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Peak Inverse Voltage of the Doubler Diodes

**Scenario:** Determine the maximum reverse voltage that $D_2$ experiences in the half-wave doubler.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Worst-case timing**

The worst case for $D_2$ occurs during the positive half-cycle **after** $C_2$ has been fully charged to $2V_m$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Reverse voltage across $D_2$**

At the instant $v_s(t) = +V_m$:

$$v_{D_2,anode} = v_{C_1} + v_s(t) = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$
$$v_{D_2,cathode} = v_{C_2} = 2V_m$$

The reverse voltage is therefore:

$$V_{R,D_2} = v_{cathode} - v_{anode} = 2V_m - 2V_m = 0 \text{ (at peak)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö True worst-case instant**

The maximum reverse voltage on $D_2$ actually occurs slightly after the input peak, when $C_2$ is fully charged but the input is falling. For an ideal analysis:

$$\boxed{PIV_{D_2} = 2V_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Always select diodes rated for **at least $2.5 \times$ the computed PIV** to provide a safety margin against transients and temperature derating.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Ripple Voltage of the Half-Wave Doubler Under Load

**Given:** Load current $I_{dc}$ drawn from the output, capacitance $C = C_2$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Discharging interval**

In a half-wave doubler, $C_2$ is recharged only once per input cycle (during the positive half-cycle when $D_2$ conducts). Between successive recharges, the capacitor discharges through the load for a duration $T = 1/f$.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Linear discharge approximation**

Assuming $I_{dc}$ is approximately constant (a valid assumption for $V_{r(pp)} \ll V_{dc}$):

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_{dc} \cdot T}{C} = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C}$$

$$\boxed{V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} \quad \text{(Half-Wave)}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Full-wave doubler ripple**

For a full-wave doubler, $C_2$ is recharged **twice per cycle** (once per half-cycle), so the effective discharge time is $T/2$:

$$\boxed{V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{2f \cdot C} \quad \text{(Full-Wave)}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Voltage Sag in an $n$-Stage CockcroftŌĆōWalton Multiplier

**Given:** $n$-stage cascade, load current $I_{dc}$, each stage capacitance $C$, supply frequency $f$.

The key insight is that the **upper-stage capacitors** must not only supply the load current but must also **replenish the charge lost by the lower-stage capacitors**. This cascading effect produces a quadratic dependence on $n$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Charge supplied per cycle by the $k$-th diode**

The diode $D_k$ must transfer charge $\Delta Q_k$ to capacitor $C_k$ in each cycle:

$$\Delta Q_k = \frac{I_{dc}}{f} \cdot k$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage drop on the $k$-th stage**

$$\Delta V_k = \frac{\Delta Q_k}{C} = \frac{I_{dc} \cdot k}{f \cdot C}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Total voltage sag**

The total drop is the sum of all individual stage drops:

$$\Delta V_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Delta V_k = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Approximation for large $n$**

For $n \gg 1$:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{total} \approx \frac{I_{dc} \cdot n^2}{2 f C}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Loaded output voltage**

$$V_{dc,loaded} = 2nV_m - \frac{I_{dc} \cdot n^2}{2fC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This $n^2$ dependence is the single most important result in the KTU Module 1 multiplier topic.** It dictates practical design limits and is a guaranteed 7-mark derivation in KTU ESE papers.

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Topology | $V_{dc,no\,load}$ | PIV per Diode | Ripple Frequency | $V_{r(pp)}$ | Diodes | Capacitors |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Half-Wave Doubler | $2V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $f$ | $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{fC}$ | 2 | 2 |
| Full-Wave Doubler | $2V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $2f$ | $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{2fC}$ | 2 | 2 |
| Voltage Tripler | $3V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $f$ | $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{fC}$ | 3 | 3 |
| Voltage Quadrupler | $4V_m$ | $2V_m$ | $f$ | $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{fC}$ | 4 | 4 |
| $n$-stage CockcroftŌĆōWalton | $2nV_m$ | $2V_m$ | $f$ | $\dfrac{I_{dc}\,n(n+1)}{2fC}$ | $2n$ | $2n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Half-Wave Voltage Doubler ŌĆö Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACIN["AC SOURCE Vs Vm sin wt"] --> NODE_A["NODE A JUNCTION"]
    NODE_A --> D1["DIODE D1 FORWARD ON NEGATIVE CYCLE"]
    D1 --> C1["CAPACITOR C1 CHARGES TO Vm"]
    NODE_A --> D2["DIODE D2 FORWARD ON POSITIVE CYCLE"]
    C1 --> D2
    D2 --> C2["CAPACITOR C2 CHARGES TO 2Vm"]
    C2 --> VDC["DC OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 2Vm"]
    VDC --> LOAD["LOAD RL DRAWS Idc"]
    GND1["GROUND"] --> NODE_A
    GND2["GROUND"] --> C1
    GND3["GROUND"] --> C2
```

### 4.2 Full-Wave Voltage Doubler ŌĆö Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACIN2["AC SOURCE Vs Vm sin wt"] --> CTR["CENTER TAPPED INPUT NODE"]
    CTR --> D1F["DIODE D1 CONDUCTS POSITIVE CYCLE TO C1"]
    CTR --> D2F["DIODE D2 CONDUCTS NEGATIVE CYCLE TO C2"]
    D1F --> C1F["CAPACITOR C1 CHARGES TO Vm"]
    D2F --> C2F["CAPACITOR C2 CHARGES TO Vm"]
    C1F --> SERNODE["SERIES ADDITION NODE"]
    C2F --> SERNODE
    SERNODE --> VDC2["DC OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 2Vm"]
    VDC2 --> LOAD2["LOAD RL DRAWS Idc"]
```

### 4.3 Voltage Tripler ŌĆö Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACIN3["AC INPUT Vm sin wt"] --> S1["STAGE 1 D1 AND C1 CHARGE TO Vm"]
    S1 --> S2["STAGE 2 D2 AND C2 PUMP ANOTHER Vm"]
    S2 --> S3["STAGE 3 D3 AND C3 PUMP ANOTHER Vm"]
    S3 --> OUT3["DC OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 3Vm"]
    OUT3 --> LOAD3["LOAD RL"]
    GNDT["COMMON GROUND"] --> S1
    GNDT --> S2
    GNDT --> S3
```

### 4.4 Voltage Quadrupler ŌĆö Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACIN4["AC INPUT Vm sin wt"] --> Q1["STAGE 1 PUMPS Vm ONTO C1"]
    Q1 --> Q2["STAGE 2 PUMPS Vm ONTO C2 CUMULATIVE 2Vm"]
    Q2 --> Q3["STAGE 3 PUMPS Vm ONTO C3 CUMULATIVE 3Vm"]
    Q3 --> Q4["STAGE 4 PUMPS Vm ONTO C4 CUMULATIVE 4Vm"]
    Q4 --> OUT4["DC OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 4Vm"]
    OUT4 --> LOAD4["HIGH VOLTAGE LOAD"]
```

### 4.5 CockcroftŌĆōWalton $n$-Stage Cascade ŌĆö Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    INN["AC INPUT Vm"] --> STG1["STAGE 1 D1 D2 C1 C2 V EQUALS 2Vm"]
    STG1 --> STG2["STAGE 2 D3 D4 C3 C4 V EQUALS 4Vm"]
    STG2 --> STG3["STAGE 3 D5 D6 C5 C6 V EQUALS 6Vm"]
    STG3 --> STGN["STAGE N DN D2N C2N-1 C2N V EQUALS 2N Vm"]
    STGN --> FINAL["FINAL DC OUTPUT Vdc EQUALS 2N Vm"]
    FINAL --> HVLOAD["HIGH VOLTAGE DC LOAD"]
    NOTE["NOTE VOLTAGE SAG PROPORTIONAL TO N SQUARED"] -.-> STG2
    NOTE -.-> STG3
    NOTE -.-> STGN
```

### 4.6 Signal-Waveform Timing Diagram (Half-Wave Doubler)

```mermaid
graph LR
    WA["WAVEFORM A Vs t SINUSOIDAL INPUT Vm sin wt"] --> CLAMP["WAVEFORM B CLAMPED NODE A DC SHIFTED BY Vm"]
    CLAMP --> VDCW["WAVEFORM C V OUT UNFILTERED PEAKS AT 2Vm"]
    VDCW --> VDCF["WAVEFORM D V OUT FILTERED STEADY DC EQUALS 2Vm WITH RIPPLE Vr"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The arrows show signal progression, not conventional current flow. Each "stage" block represents a diodeŌĆōcapacitor pair performing one voltage "pump" of magnitude $V_m$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, PEEET417]**
*Define a voltage multiplier. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a half-wave voltage doubler.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **voltage multiplier** is a rectifier circuit employing diodes and capacitors that delivers a DC output voltage equal to an integer multiple of the peak AC input voltage, without the use of a step-up transformer.

In a **half-wave voltage doubler**, during the **negative half-cycle** of the input, diode $D_1$ conducts and charges capacitor $C_1$ to $V_m$ (with the right plate positive). During the **positive half-cycle**, $D_1$ is reverse-biased and diode $D_2$ conducts; capacitor $C_2$ then charges to the **series sum** of the input and $C_1$, i.e., $V_m + V_m = 2V_m$.

The output is taken across $C_2$:

$$V_{out} = 2V_m \quad (\text{no-load condition})$$

The ripple frequency equals the supply frequency $f$, and the PIV rating required for each diode is $2V_m$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PEEET417]**
*Compare the half-wave and full-wave voltage doublers in terms of ripple frequency, PIV, and number of components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Half-Wave Doubler | Full-Wave Doubler |
|---|---|---|
| Ripple frequency | $f$ | $2f$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage | $2V_m$ | $2V_m$ |
| Number of diodes | 2 | 2 |
| Number of capacitors | 2 | 2 |
| Ripple magnitude (for same $C$, $I_{dc}$) | Higher | **Half** (lower) |
| DC output (no load) | $2V_m$ | $2V_m$ |

**Conclusion:** Both produce the same no-load DC voltage and require the same component count, but the full-wave doubler delivers a **lower ripple** and a **higher ripple frequency**, making filtering easier and output smoother.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Essay-Level Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n^2$ factor** in CockcroftŌĆōWalton sag derivation ŌĆö costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing PIV with $V_m$ alone** ŌĆö the correct PIV per diode in a doubler is $2V_m$, not $V_m$.
> 3. **Mixing up ripple frequency formulas** between half-wave and full-wave configurations.
> 4. **Not stating boundary conditions** (ideal diode, no-load) before applying formulas.
> 5. **Missing polarity of capacitor charging** in the qualitative explanation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2022, PEEET417, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear circuit diagram, describe the operation of a **voltage tripler**. Derive the expression for its no-load DC output voltage and the Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) rating of each diode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **CockcroftŌĆōWalton 4-stage multiplier** is supplied from a 230 V, 50 Hz mains through a step-up transformer. The load draws a constant current of 1 mA and each stage capacitor has a value of 0.1 ╬╝F. Calculate: (i) the ideal no-load DC output voltage, (ii) the voltage sag under load, and (iii) the loaded output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Voltage Tripler Operation, Output, and PIV (7 marks)**

A voltage tripler consists of **three diodes** ($D_1$, $D_2$, $D_3$) and **three capacitors** ($C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$).

**Operation:**

1. **Negative half-cycle of input:** Diode $D_1$ conducts, charging $C_1$ to $V_m$ (left plate at $-V_m$, right plate at ground).

2. **Positive half-cycle of input:** Diode $D_1$ is reverse-biased. $D_2$ conducts, and the series combination of $V_m$ (input) and $V_m$ (on $C_1$) charges capacitor $C_2$ to $2V_m$.

3. **Subsequent negative half-cycle:** Diode $D_2$ is reverse-biased. Diode $D_3$ conducts, and capacitor $C_3$ charges to the series sum of $V_m$ (on $C_1$) and $2V_m$ (on $C_2$), giving:

$$v_{C_3} = V_m + 2V_m = 3V_m$$

The DC output is taken across the series combination of $C_2$ and $C_3$:

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = 2V_m + V_m = 3V_m}$$

**Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) of each diode:**

By symmetry and circuit analysis, each diode must block a maximum reverse voltage of:

$$\boxed{PIV = 2V_m}$$

This is identical to the doubler PIV, which is a key advantage of the multiplier topology.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical on CockcroftŌĆōWalton 4-Stage Multiplier (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$V_{rms} = 230 \text{ V}, \quad f = 50 \text{ Hz}, \quad n = 4, \quad I_{dc} = 1 \text{ mA}, \quad C = 0.1 \text{ ╬╝F}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Peak input voltage**

$$V_m = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \cdot \sqrt{2} = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Ideal no-load DC output (i)**

$$V_{dc,NL} = 2n \cdot V_m = 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 325.27 = 2602.16 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc,NL} \approx 2.60 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage sag under load (ii)**

Using the exact sag formula:

$$\Delta V_{total} = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta V_{total} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot \frac{4 \cdot 5}{2}$$

$$\Delta V_{total} = \frac{10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \cdot 10 = 200 \cdot 10 = 2000 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{total} = 2.00 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Loaded output voltage (iii)**

$$V_{dc,FL} = V_{dc,NL} - \Delta V_{total} = 2602.16 - 2000 = 602.16 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc,FL} \approx 0.60 \text{ kV}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Voltage Regulation**

$$VR = \frac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\% = \frac{2602.16 - 602.16}{602.16} \times 100\% \approx 332\%$$

This **catastrophic regulation** illustrates the $n^2$ penalty ŌĆö for $n=4$ with only 1 mA load, the output sags by ~77%, confirming the design warning that $n \leq 5$ is the practical limit.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, PEEET417, Module 1]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **full-wave voltage doubler** and explain its operation with relevant waveforms. Derive the expressions for the no-load DC output voltage and the ripple voltage under load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **half-wave voltage doubler** is fed from a 120 V, 60 Hz AC source. The load resistance is 50 k╬® and the smoothing capacitor is 10 ╬╝F. Compute: (i) the peak input voltage, (ii) the no-load DC output, (iii) the DC load current, (iv) the peak-to-peak ripple voltage, and (v) the PIV required for each diode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Full-Wave Voltage Doubler: Operation, Waveforms, Output, Ripple (7 marks)**

The full-wave voltage doubler uses **two diodes** ($D_1$, $D_2$) and **two capacitors** ($C_1$, $C_2$) with the load taken across the series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$.

**Operation:**

1. **Positive half-cycle:** $D_1$ conducts, charging $C_1$ to $V_m$ (left plate negative, right plate positive).
2. **Negative half-cycle:** $D_2$ conducts, charging $C_2$ to $V_m$ (left plate positive, right plate negative, **opposite polarity** to $C_1$).
3. **Output:** The series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$ (opposite polarities in series add) gives:

$$V_{dc,NL} = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

**Ripple analysis:**

Since the doubler is replenished on **both half-cycles**, the ripple frequency is:

$$f_r = 2f_{in}$$

The peak-to-peak ripple under load is:

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{2fC}$$

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical on Half-Wave Voltage Doubler (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

$$V_{rms} = 120 \text{ V}, \quad f = 60 \text{ Hz}, \quad R_L = 50 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad C = 10 \text{ ╬╝F}$$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Peak input voltage (i)**

$$V_m = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 120 \cdot \sqrt{2} = 169.71 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_m \approx 169.7 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö No-load DC output (ii)**

$$V_{dc,NL} = 2V_m = 2 \cdot 169.71 = 339.41 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc,NL} \approx 339.4 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö DC load current (iii)**

$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc,NL}}{R_L} = \frac{339.41}{50 \times 10^3} = 6.788 \text{ mA}$$

$$\boxed{I_{dc} \approx 6.79 \text{ mA}}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Peak-to-peak ripple voltage (iv)**

For a half-wave doubler:

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_{dc}}{f \cdot C} = \frac{6.788 \times 10^{-3}}{60 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{6.788 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 11.31 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{r(pp)} \approx 11.31 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö PIV per diode (v)**

$$PIV = 2V_m = 2 \cdot 169.71 = 339.41 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{PIV \approx 339.4 \text{ V (with safety factor, use 1000 V rated diodes)}}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary ŌĆö Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] A **voltage multiplier** produces DC output = integer multiple of $V_m$ using only diodes and capacitors (no transformer step-up).
- [ ] **Half-wave doubler:** $V_{out} = 2V_m$, ripple frequency $= f$, requires 2 diodes + 2 capacitors.
- [ ] **Full-wave doubler:** $V_{out} = 2V_m$, ripple frequency $= 2f$, lower ripple than half-wave.
- [ ] **Voltage tripler:** $V_{out} = 3V_m$, PIV per diode $= 2V_m$.
- [ ] **Voltage quadrupler:** $V_{out} = 4V_m$, PIV per diode $= 2V_m$.
- [ ] **PIV for every diode in a multiplier chain = $2V_m$** (a frequently tested fact).
- [ ] **CockcroftŌĆōWalton cascade** for $n$ stages: $V_{dc,NL} = 2nV_m$.
- [ ] **Voltage sag under load** scales as $n^2$ ŌĆö the **single most critical KTU result**.
- [ ] **Exact sag formula:** $\Delta V = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{fC} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$.
- [ ] **Ripple formulas:** Half-wave $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{fC}$, Full-wave $\dfrac{I_{dc}}{2fC}$.
- [ ] **Practical limit:** $n \leq 5$ stages due to $n^2$ sag and ripple degradation.
- [ ] **Applications to remember:** X-ray tubes, CRT anodes, photocopiers, electrostatic precipitators, particle accelerators.
- [ ] **Always use diodes rated at $\geq 2.5 \times$ calculated PIV** for safety.
- [ ] **Output regulation is poor** ŌĆö multipliers are designed for **high-voltage, low-current** loads only.
- [ ] **Real-world analogy:** The "staircase water pump" ŌĆö each stage adds one floor (one $V_m$) of head.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Biasing: Concept of DC and AC Load Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transistor Biasing: Concept of DC and AC Load Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Transistor Biasing: Concept of DC and AC Load Lines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Transistor Biasing?

**Biasing** is the process of applying external DC voltages and currents to a transistor so as to establish a fixed, stable **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** on its output characteristics. The Q-point is defined by the coordinates $(I_C, V_{CE})$ when no input AC signal is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PED 411 / PEEET417)**
> "Biasing is the establishment of a predetermined collector current $I_C$ and collectorŌĆōemitter voltage $V_{CE}$ (the operating point) by means of external DC sources, such that the transistor operates in the desired region (typically the active region) irrespective of variations in $\beta$, temperature, and leakage current $I_{CEO}$."

### 1.2 The Need for Biasing

A BJT is a **temperature-dependent, parameter-sensitive device**. The following parameters drift with temperature and unit-to-unit variation:

- Reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$ (doubles every $10^\circ C$)
- BaseŌĆōemitter voltage $V_{BE}$ (decreases by $\approx 2\,mV/^\circ C$)
- Current gain $\beta$ (increases with temperature)

If the Q-point is not stabilized, it will **drift**, causing **distortion, thermal runaway, or cutoff/saturation cutoff** of the amplified signal. Hence, biasing is *not optional* ŌĆö it is the foundation of every linear amplifier.

### 1.3 Concept of a Load Line

The **Load Line** is a **straight line** drawn on the output characteristics ($I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ curve) of the transistor. It represents the **simultaneous satisfaction of two equations**:

1. The transistor's $I_C$ŌĆō$V_{CE}$ relationship (a curve dictated by the device physics).
2. The external load circuit equation (KVL around the output loop) ŌĆö a **straight line**.

The intersection of the load line with the output characteristic at a specific base current $I_B$ gives the **operating point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> There are **two** load lines, derived from two different equivalent circuits:
> - **DC Load Line** ŌåÆ drawn using the **DC equivalent circuit** (coupling capacitors = **open**).
> - **AC Load Line** ŌåÆ drawn using the **AC equivalent circuit** (coupling capacitors = **short**, DC sources = **grounded**).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Plumbing Model**
> Imagine a **water tap (transistor)** connected to an overhead tank (DC supply $V_{CC}$) through a narrow pipe (collector resistor $R_C$). The water flow $I_C$ depends on **two** constraints:
> 1. How much the tap is *opened* by your hand (base current $I_B$ ŌĆö the input signal).
> 2. The **friction** in the pipe and the height of the tank (the external load ŌĆö the **load line**).
>
> The **DC load line** is the static constraint of the pipe when no signal is being processed (tap idle). The **AC load line** is the dynamic constraint when an *external* load resistor $R_L$ is also attached (e.g., a garden hose), which appears **only** when AC is flowing. The Q-point is the steady water flow when the tap is held perfectly still.

### 1.5 Standard Parameters & Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{CC}$ | Collector supply voltage | $\mathbf{5\,V}$ to $\mathbf{20\,V}$ |
| $R_C$ | Collector resistor | $1\,k\Omega$ to $10\,k\Omega$ |
| $R_B$ | Base bias resistor | $10\,k\Omega$ to $1\,M\Omega$ |
| $\beta$ (or $h_{FE}$) | Common-emitter current gain | $\mathbf{20}$ to $\mathbf{500}$ |
| $I_{CQ}$ | DC collector current at Q-point | $1\,mA$ to $10\,mA$ |
| $V_{CEQ}$ | DC collectorŌĆōemitter voltage at Q-point | $\approx V_{CC}/2$ (design goal) |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
### 2.1 The Common-Emitter (CE) Configuration ŌĆö Reference Circuit

Consider the most basic fixed-bias CE configuration used in KTU textbook problems:

```
       VCC
        |
        |
       [RC]
        |
        +-------- C (Collector)
        |
        |
       BJT
        |
        +-------- E (Emitter)  ---- GND
        |
       [RB]
        |
       VBB
```

Apply **KVL** to the **collectorŌĆōemitter loop**:

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$

Solving for $I_C$:

$$I_C = -\frac{1}{R_C} V_{CE} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** $= -\dfrac{1}{R_C}$
- **Y-intercept** (at $V_{CE} = 0$) $= \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ ŌåÆ **Saturation current** $I_{C(sat)}$
- **X-intercept** (at $I_C = 0$) $= V_{CC}$ ŌåÆ **Cut-off voltage** $V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)}$

### 2.2 Constructing the DC Load Line

| Step | Action | Resulting Point |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Set $I_C = 0$ | Point A: $(V_{CC},\, 0)$ ŌĆö **Cut-off** |
| 2 | Set $V_{CE} = 0$ | Point B: $(0,\, V_{CC}/R_C)$ ŌĆö **Saturation** |
| 3 | Join A and B | The DC Load Line |
| 4 | Locate the base current $I_B$ curve | The intersection is the **Q-point** $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the slope is negative:** A larger $V_{CE}$ drops more voltage across $R_C$, leaving less for the current source. Therefore $I_C$ must *decrease* as $V_{CE}$ *increases*. This is an **inversely proportional** relationship ŌĆö geometrically, a downward slope.

### 2.3 Q-Point Selection Criteria

The Q-point must be placed **midway** between saturation and cut-off to allow **maximum, undistorted, symmetrical AC swing** of the output signal.

$$V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} \quad \text{(Design Goal for max swing)}$$

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}$$

### 2.4 Why Do We Need a *Separate* AC Load Line?

The **DC load line** is constructed using the **DC collector resistance** $R_C$ alone. However, when an **AC input signal** is applied, the AC collector current $i_c$ sees an **effective load** that may be different from $R_C$:

- The **coupling capacitor** $C_C$ (at the output) becomes a **short circuit** at signal frequency.
- This **connects the external load $R_L$** in **parallel** with $R_C$.

Hence, the transistor's AC behavior is constrained by $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$, **not** by $R_C$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key formulas required for KTU 2024 ESE problems on DC/AC load lines.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$ | KVL of output loop (CE) | V, A, $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ | Saturation point (DC) | A |
| 3 | $V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = V_{CC}$ | Cut-off point (DC) | V |
| 4 | Slope$_{DC} = -\dfrac{1}{R_C}$ | DC load line slope | A/V |
| 5 | $I_{BQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | Base current (fixed bias) | A |
| 6 | $I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ}$ | Collector current at Q-point | A |
| 7 | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$ | CollectorŌĆōemitter voltage at Q | V |
| 8 | $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$ | AC load resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Slope$_{AC} = -\dfrac{1}{R_{AC}}$ | AC load line slope | A/V |
| 10 | $V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}$ | Max. $V_{CE}$ swing on AC line | V |
| 11 | $I_{C(\max)} = I_{CQ} + \dfrac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$ | Max. $I_C$ swing on AC line | A |
| 12 | $V_{CE(\min)} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\,V$ | Min. $V_{CE}$ (practical) | V |
| 13 | $I_{C(\min)} = 0$ (cut-off) | Min. $I_C$ in active region | A |
| 14 | Max und. $V_{o(peak)} = \min(I_{CQ} R_{AC},\, V_{CEQ})$ | Maximum output swing | V |
| 15 | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | DC power supplied | W |
| 16 | $\eta_{max} = 25\%$ | Max. theoretical CE efficiency | ŌĆö |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers**: The AC load line determines the **maximum unclipped output swing**, which directly fixes the loudspeaker volume before distortion.
- **RF Power Amplifiers**: AC load line design is critical to **match antenna impedance** to the transistor's optimum power point (load-pull analysis).
- **Switching Power Supplies**: The transistor operates between **cut-off** and **saturation** ŌĆö the two DC load-line endpoints, switching at high frequency to regulate voltage.
- **Oscillators & Linear ICs**: The Q-point stability dictated by load-line placement prevents **thermal runaway** and frequency drift.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö DC Load Line Equation from KVL

**Given:** A common-emitter BJT with collector supply $V_{CC}$, collector resistor $R_C$, emitter grounded.

**Find:** The locus of all possible $(I_C, V_{CE})$ points ŌĆö the DC load line.

**Step 1.** Write KVL around the output loop (Collector ŌåÆ Emitter ŌåÆ Ground ŌåÆ $V_{CC}$).

$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Rearrange to standard $y = mx + c$ form. Treat $I_C$ as the dependent variable $y$ and $V_{CE}$ as the independent variable $x$.

$$I_C = -\frac{1}{R_C} \cdot V_{CE} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

**Step 3.** Identify the two intercepts.

- Y-intercept: Set $V_{CE} = 0 \Rightarrow I_C = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = I_{C(sat)}$.
- X-intercept: Set $I_C = 0 \Rightarrow V_{CE} = V_{CC} = V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)}$.

**Step 4.** The line joining $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$ and $(V_{CC}, 0)$ **is** the DC load line. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Q-Point Coordinates for Fixed Bias

**Given:** Fixed-bias circuit with $V_{CC}$, $R_B$, $R_C$, and $\beta$.

**Find:** $I_{BQ},\, I_{CQ},\, V_{CEQ}$.

**Step 1.** Apply KVL to the **input loop** (Base ŌåÆ Emitter ŌåÆ Ground ŌåÆ $V_{BB} \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow$ Base).

$$V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Solve for the base current.

$$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

**Step 3.** Use the transistor's current-amplification property.

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

**Step 4.** Apply KVL to the **output loop** to find the collectorŌĆōemitter voltage.

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C$$

**Step 5.** Substitute $I_{CQ}$ from Step 3.

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - \beta R_C \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö AC Load Line Equation

**Given:** A CE amplifier with $R_C$, coupling capacitor $C_C$ to load $R_L$, and Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$.

**Find:** The AC load line equation and its two endpoints.

**Step 1.** At signal frequency, the coupling capacitor $C_C$ acts as a **short circuit**. The DC supply $V_{CC}$ is bypassed to ground (AC ground). Therefore, the **AC collector sees $R_C$ in parallel with $R_L$**.

$$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$

**Step 2.** The AC load line is a straight line of slope $-\dfrac{1}{R_{AC}}$ that **must pass through the Q-point** (because at $i_c = 0$ and $v_{ce} = 0$, the AC quantities vanish and we are back at the DC operating point).

$$i_c = -\frac{1}{R_{AC}} v_{ce}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to total instantaneous values using the **partitioning principle**:

$$i_c = i_C - I_{CQ}, \quad v_{ce} = v_{CE} - V_{CEQ}$$

Substituting:

$$i_C - I_{CQ} = -\frac{1}{R_{AC}} (v_{CE} - V_{CEQ})$$

**Step 4.** Solve for the **upper end (cut-off)** by setting $i_C = 0$:

$$-I_{CQ} = -\frac{1}{R_{AC}} (v_{CE} - V_{CEQ})$$

$$v_{CE} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC} \equiv V_{CE(\max)}$$

**Step 5.** Solve for the **lower end (saturation)** by setting $v_{CE} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$:

$$i_C - I_{CQ} = -\frac{1}{R_{AC}} (0 - V_{CEQ})$$

$$i_C = I_{CQ} + \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}} \equiv I_{C(\max)}$$

**Step 6.** The two endpoints of the AC load line are therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}}$$

$$\boxed{I_{C(\max)} = I_{CQ} + \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}}$$

**Step 7.** The **maximum undistorted peak output voltage** is the **smaller** of the two available swings (limited by whichever boundary is reached first):

$$V_{o(peak)} = \min\left(I_{CQ} R_{AC},\, V_{CEQ}\right)$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Fixed Bias):**
> A silicon BJT ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$) is biased with $V_{CC} = 12\,V$, $R_B = 470\,k\Omega$, $R_C = 1.2\,k\Omega$. An AC load $R_L = 1.2\,k\Omega$ is coupled via a capacitor.
> **(a)** Draw the DC load line and locate the Q-point.
> **(b)** Draw the AC load line and find the maximum undistorted output swing.

#### Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö DC Load Line & Q-point**

$$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{470 \times 10^3} = \frac{11.3}{470 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_{BQ} \approx 24.04\,\mu A$$

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = 100 \times 24.04\,\mu A \approx 2.404\,mA$$

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 12 - (2.404 \times 10^{-3})(1.2 \times 10^3)$$

$$V_{CEQ} = 12 - 2.885 = 9.115\,V$$

DC load line endpoints:

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \frac{12}{1.2\,k\Omega} = 10\,mA$$

$$V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = V_{CC} = 12\,V$$

$$\boxed{\text{Q-point: } (I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (2.404\,mA,\; 9.115\,V)}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö AC Load Line & Max Swing**

$$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{1.2 \times 1.2}{1.2 + 1.2} = 0.6\,k\Omega$$

Upper endpoint (cut-off direction):

$$V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC} = 9.115 + (2.404\,mA)(0.6\,k\Omega)$$

$$V_{CE(\max)} = 9.115 + 1.443 = 10.558\,V$$

Lower endpoint (saturation direction):

$$I_{C(\max)} = I_{CQ} + \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}} = 2.404 + \frac{9.115}{0.6\,k}$$

$$I_{C(\max)} = 2.404 + 15.19 = 17.59\,mA$$

The two candidate swings:

- Upward (toward cut-off): $V_{CEQ} = 9.115\,V$ ŌåÆ swing $= 9.115\,V$
- Downward (toward saturation): $I_{CQ} R_{AC} = 1.443\,V$ ŌåÆ swing $= 1.443\,V$

The **smaller** value limits the swing:

$$\boxed{V_{o(peak)} = 1.443\,V \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{o(pp)} = 2.886\,V}$$

This asymmetry is a classic sign that the **Q-point is poorly located** for the chosen $R_L$ ŌĆö the designer should re-bias closer to the *midpoint of the AC load line* by adjusting $I_{BQ}$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, Set A]**
> **Q1.** Define the term *operating point* of a transistor. Why is it necessary to stabilize the Q-point?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **operating point (Q-point)** of a transistor is the specific point on the DC output characteristics $(I_C, V_{CE})$ at which the transistor operates when **no input signal** is applied. Mathematically, it is the intersection of the **DC load line** with the **base current curve** $I_B$.

It must be **stabilized** because the transistor's parameters ŌĆö $\beta$, $I_{CO}$, and $V_{BE}$ ŌĆö vary with temperature and from one device to another. An unstable Q-point causes:
- **Distortion** in the amplified signal (Q-point moves into saturation or cut-off).
- **Thermal runaway** (positive feedback between $I_C$ and junction temperature).
- **Inconsistent amplifier performance** across production batches.

Hence, **bias stabilization circuits** (voltage-divider, emitter bias) are essential.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Model Question Paper, Set B]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between the **DC load line** and the **AC load line**. Why are they different?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DC Load Line | AC Load Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Circuit** | DC equivalent (capacitors open, $V_{CC}$ retained) | AC equivalent (capacitors short, $V_{CC}$ grounded) |
| **Resistance** | Uses only $R_C$ | Uses $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$ |
| **Slope** | $-\dfrac{1}{R_C}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{R_{AC}}$ (steeper, since $R_{AC} < R_C$) |
| **Passes through** | $(V_{CC}, 0)$ and $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$ | The Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ |
| **Determines** | DC operating point | Maximum **undistorted AC output swing** |

They differ because **at DC**, only $R_C$ is in the collector loop, whereas **at AC**, the coupling capacitor brings $R_L$ in **parallel** with $R_C$, reducing the effective impedance seen by the AC signal current.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 1 ŌĆö Full Question, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A (14 marks):** For a silicon transistor with $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$, the biasing circuit has $V_{CC} = 20\,V$, $R_B = 500\,k\Omega$, $R_C = 2\,k\Omega$, and an external AC load $R_L = 2\,k\Omega$ coupled through a capacitor.
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Draw the DC load line and determine the Q-point coordinates $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Draw the AC load line on the same characteristics and find the maximum undistorted output peak voltage and the maximum symmetrical output swing.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö DC Load Line and Q-point [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Calculate the base current.

$$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{20 - 0.7}{500 \times 10^3} = \frac{19.3}{500 \times 10^3} = 38.6\,\mu A$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the collector current at Q-point.

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = 100 \times 38.6\,\mu A = 3.86\,mA$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the collectorŌĆōemitter voltage at Q-point.

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 20 - (3.86 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^3) = 20 - 7.72 = 12.28\,V$$

**Step 4:** DC load line endpoints.

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \frac{20}{2\,k\Omega} = 10\,mA \quad \text{(Point B on $V_{CE}=0$ axis)}$$

$$V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = V_{CC} = 20\,V \quad \text{(Point A on $I_C=0$ axis)}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Q-point: } (I_{CQ},\, V_{CEQ}) = (3.86\,mA,\; 12.28\,V)}$$

**Step 5:** Sketch (description): Draw horizontal axis $V_{CE}$ (0 to 20 V) and vertical axis $I_C$ (0 to 10 mA). Mark Point A at $(20, 0)$ and Point B at $(0, 10)$. Join A and B. The Q-point is at $(12.28, 3.86)$ mA, with $I_{BQ} = 38.6\,\mu A$ curve. **[Diagram: 1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö AC Load Line and Max Output Swing [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Calculate AC load resistance.

$$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{2\,k \times 2\,k}{2\,k + 2\,k} = 1\,k\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the upper endpoint of the AC load line (cut-off direction).

$$V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC} = 12.28 + (3.86\,mA)(1\,k\Omega) = 12.28 + 3.86 = 16.14\,V$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the lower endpoint of the AC load line (saturation direction).

$$I_{C(\max)} = I_{CQ} + \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}} = 3.86\,mA + \frac{12.28}{1\,k} = 3.86 + 12.28 = 16.14\,mA$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the two candidate peak swings.

- Upward swing (toward cut-off): $V_{CEQ} = 12.28\,V$
- Downward swing (toward saturation): $I_{CQ} R_{AC} = 3.86 \times 1 = 3.86\,V$

**Step 5:** The smaller value limits the maximum undistorted output peak.

$$V_{o(peak)} = \min(12.28,\, 3.86) = 3.86\,V$$

$$V_{o(pp)} = 2 \times V_{o(peak)} = 7.72\,V$$

**Step 6:** AC Load Line construction (description): Through Q-point $(12.28\,V, 3.86\,mA)$, draw a steeper line with slope $-1/1k\Omega$. It intersects $V_{CE}$ axis at $16.14\,V$ and the $I_C$ axis at $16.14\,mA$. **[Diagram: 1 mark]**

$$\boxed{V_{o(peak)} = 3.86\,V, \quad V_{o(pp)} = 7.72\,V}$$

The Q-point is **not optimally located** for the given $R_L$ (it is too close to the cut-off side). To obtain maximum symmetrical swing, the designer should relocate Q-point to the *midpoint* of the AC load line, i.e., at $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (8.07\,mA, 8.07\,V)$.

---

> **Question B (14 marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice:**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain with neat sketches: (i) the **DC load line** construction, (ii) the significance of the **operating point**, and (iii) the three regions of operation (cut-off, active, saturation) on the output characteristics.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] For a fixed-bias BJT circuit with $V_{CC} = 15\,V$, $R_B = 200\,k\Omega$, $R_C = 1\,k\Omega$, $\beta = 80$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$:
> - Find the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$.
> - Determine the saturation collector current $I_{C(sat)}$ and the cut-off voltage.
> - Calculate the maximum **DC** power dissipated at the collector.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Conceptual Sketch & Explanation [7 marks]**

**[Diagram: 3 marks]** Draw the output characteristics family (curves of $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ for $I_B = 20, 40, 60, 80, 100\,\mu A$). Overlay the straight DC load line joining $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$ to $(V_{CC}, 0)$.

**[Three regions ŌĆö 4 marks]:**

1. **Cut-off region** (lower-left, near $V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)}$): Both BE and BC junctions are reverse-biased. $I_C \approx 0$. Used in **switching** applications.
2. **Active region** (central, where Q-point lives): BE forward-biased, BC reverse-biased. The transistor acts as a **linear amplifier** ŌĆö $I_C = \beta I_B$, almost independent of $V_{CE}$.
3. **Saturation region** (lower-right, near $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\,V$): Both junctions forward-biased. $I_C$ is no longer controlled by $I_B$ (collector current saturates at $V_{CC}/R_C$). Used in **switching** applications (ON state).

The **significance of the operating point** is that it determines:
- The region of operation (active vs. saturation vs. cut-off).
- The **DC bias** (and hence the quiescent power dissipation).
- The **maximum symmetrical AC swing** available without distortion.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö Q-point, Saturation, Cut-off, Power [7 marks]**

**Step 1:** Base current.

$$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{15 - 0.7}{200 \times 10^3} = \frac{14.3}{200 \times 10^3} = 71.5\,\mu A$$

**Step 2:** Collector current at Q-point.

$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ} = 80 \times 71.5\,\mu A = 5.72\,mA$$

**Step 3:** CollectorŌĆōemitter voltage at Q-point.

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 15 - (5.72 \times 10^{-3})(1 \times 10^3) = 15 - 5.72 = 9.28\,V$$

**Step 4:** Saturation current (DC).

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C} = \frac{15}{1\,k} = 15\,mA$$

**Step 5:** Cut-off voltage.

$$V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = V_{CC} = 15\,V$$

**Step 6:** Maximum DC power dissipated at the collector.

The maximum power dissipation occurs **at the midpoint** of the DC load line. For a resistive load, the locus of $P_C = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ is a hyperbola. The maximum on the load line occurs at:

$$P_{C(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(sat)}}{4} = \frac{15 \times 15\,mA}{4} = \frac{225\,mW}{4} = 56.25\,mW$$

Alternatively, the **actual** DC power dissipated at the Q-point is:

$$P_{CQ} = V_{CEQ} \cdot I_{CQ} = 9.28 \times 5.72\,mA = 53.08\,mW$$

$$\boxed{(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (5.72\,mA,\, 9.28\,V), \quad I_{C(sat)} = 15\,mA, \quad V_{CE(cut\text{-}off)} = 15\,V, \quad P_{C(\max)} = 56.25\,mW}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_C$ and $R_{AC}$:** A *very common* error is using the DC load line slope $-1/R_C$ to find the AC endpoints. **Always** use $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$ for AC calculations. Lose **2 marks** if mixed.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to take the MIN of the two swings:** Many students report $V_{o(peak)} = V_{CEQ}$ (the larger value) without realizing that the saturation-side limit is more restrictive. The output swing is governed by the **smaller** of the two.
>
> 3. **Using $V_{CE(sat)} = 0$ exactly:** In KTU problems, the saturation voltage is **0.2 V** (silicon). Using zero inflates the swing estimate unrealistically.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the $V_{BE}$ drop:** When calculating $I_{BQ} = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_B$, students often write $V_{CC}/R_B$. The 0.7 V drop is small for high $V_{CC}$ but examiners still check.
>
> 5. **No unit for $\mu A$ vs. $mA$:** Mismatch of units (e.g., $I_B$ in $\mu A$ but $I_C$ formula expects $A$) is a top valuation red flag ŌĆö **always convert**.
>
> 6. **Drawing the AC load line through the wrong point:** It **must** pass through the Q-point ŌĆö a line drawn through cut-off and saturation of the DC line is the **DC** load line, not the AC one.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Transistor Biasing: DC and AC Load Lines**

- [x] **Biasing** establishes a stable **Q-point** $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ in the **active region** for linear amplification.
- [x] **DC Load Line** is the locus of all possible DC operating points, derived from $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$, with slope $-1/R_C$.
- [x] DC load line endpoints: $(V_{CC}, 0)$ = **cut-off**; $(0, V_{CC}/R_C)$ = **saturation**.
- [x] **AC Load Line** is steeper than the DC line, with slope $-1/R_{AC}$, where $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$.
- [x] The AC load line **always passes through the Q-point**, because at $i_c = 0$ the AC condition is satisfied.
- [x] AC line upper end: $V_{CE(\max)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}$.
- [x] AC line lower end: $I_{C(\max)} = I_{CQ} + V_{CEQ} / R_{AC}$.
- [x] Max **undistorted** peak output: $V_{o(peak)} = \min(V_{CEQ},\, I_{CQ} R_{AC})$.
- [x] Ideal Q-point for max swing: $V_{CEQ} = I_{CQ} R_{AC}$ (i.e., equidistant from both AC endpoints).
- [x] The **fixed-bias circuit** is the simplest but the **least stable** ŌĆö it suffers from $\beta$, $I_{CO}$, and $V_{BE}$ drift.
- [x] Stabilized bias circuits: **voltage-divider bias**, **emitter bias**, **collector-feedback bias**.
- [x] **DC power supplied** $= V_{CC} I_{CQ}$; **max power dissipation** at Q-point $= V_{CEQ} I_{CQ}$.
- [x] For class-A CE amplifier, **maximum theoretical efficiency** $= 25\%$.
- [x] In KTU problems: $V_{BE} = 0.7\,V$ (silicon), $V_{CE(sat)} = 0.2\,V$ (use this, not zero).
- [x] Always **label axes** ($I_C$ in mA, $V_{CE}$ in V) and **mark Q-point coordinates** in the graph.
- [x] **Slope units**: $[A/V]$ or equivalently $[S]$ (siemens). Numerical value $= -1/R$.
- [x] The two load lines intersect at the Q-point; they are **distinct** lines, not the same.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

topics.title: Types - Fixed Bias Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types - Fixed Bias Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Fixed Bias Circuit** is the simplest and most elementary **DC biasing configuration** for a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) in the Common Emitter (CE) topology. In this arrangement, the base current **I_B** is established by connecting a single resistor **R_B** directly between the positive supply rail **V_CC** and the base terminal of the transistor, while the collector current **I_C** flows through a load resistor **R_C** connected between **V_CC** and the collector terminal. Because the base current depends *only* on the values of **V_CC**, **R_B**, and the transistor's base-emitter voltage **V_BE** ŌĆö and is **completely independent of the collector current** ŌĆö the operating point (Q-point) is said to be "fixed" by the circuit constants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A biasing scheme is called "Fixed Bias" when the quiescent base current I_BQ is rigidly fixed by the supply V_CC and the base resistor R_B, with no form of negative feedback (no emitter resistor, no collector-to-base feedback). This is the foundational topology students must master before progressing to more stable configurations like Emitter Bias, Voltage Divider Bias, and Collector-to-Base Bias.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a **bathroom shower with a single fixed water supply line**. No matter how the pressure downstream fluctuates ŌĆö whether someone opens another tap, or the building's pressure drops ŌĆö the water flow to the shower remains constant because the supply is rigid and pre-set. The **Fixed Bias Circuit behaves identically for the transistor**: once you fix **V_CC** and **R_B**, the base current is locked-in, regardless of what happens to the collector circuit, temperature, or transistor parameters.

However, this "rigidity" is a **double-edged sword**. Just as a fixed shower pressure becomes uncomfortably hot in summer (when ground water heats up) and uncomfortably cold in winter, the **Fixed Bias Q-point drifts significantly with temperature** because the transistor's ╬▓, V_BE, and I_CO are all temperature-dependent. There is no self-correcting feedback mechanism.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage:** **V_T = kT/q Ōēł 26 mV** at room temperature (T = 300 K)
- **Silicon Base-Emitter Drop:** **V_BE Ōēł 0.7 V** (active region)
- **Germanium Base-Emitter Drop:** **V_BE Ōēł 0.3 V** (legacy, rarely used)
- **Saturation Voltage:** **V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V**
- **Boltzmann Constant:** **k = 1.38 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬▓┬│ J/K**
- **Electron Charge:** **q = 1.6 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬╣Ōü╣ C**

> [!NOTE]
> The Fixed Bias circuit is the **textbook prototype** for understanding the Q-point (quiescent operating point) concept, defined by the coordinates **(V_CEQ, I_CQ)** in the output characteristics. Every subsequent biasing topology in the KTU syllabus is essentially a modification of this circuit designed to **improve thermal stability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Operation ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Power-Up Phase:** When **V_CC** is applied, current begins to flow from the supply through **R_B** into the base terminal. The base-emitter junction is forward-biased by this current, establishing a base current **I_B**.

2. **Base Current Determination:** Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the base-emitter loop:
   $$V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$

3. **Collector Current Amplification:** Once **I_B** is established, the transistor action (controlled by the current gain **╬▓** or **h_FE**) produces a collector current:
   $$I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO}$$
   where **I_CO** is the reverse saturation leakage current of the CB junction.

4. **Output Voltage (Q-Point Voltage):** Applying KVL around the output (collector-emitter) loop:
   $$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$

5. **The Q-Point Coordinates:** The transistor's quiescent operating point is the pair:
   $$Q \equiv (V_{CEQ},\ I_{CQ})$$

6. **Why "Fixed"?** Notice that **I_B depends ONLY on V_CC, R_B, and V_BE** ŌĆö none of which are influenced by **I_C**. There is no feedback path. Hence "fixed."

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Base Current (I_B) | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | A or mA | Independent of I_C |
| 2 | Collector Current (I_C) | $I_C = \beta I_B$ (approx., neglecting I_CO) | A or mA | Active region assumption |
| 3 | Exact I_C with leakage | $I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO}$ | A or mA | Full expression |
| 4 | Collector-Emitter Voltage | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | V | Determines Q-point |
| 5 | Stability Factor (S) | $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}\Big\vert_{I_B,\beta} = 1 + \beta$ | Dimensionless | **Worst stability** of all bias types |
| 6 | Stability Factor (S') | $S' = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\Big\vert_{I_B,I_{CO}}$ | A/V | Sensitivity to V_BE |
| 7 | Stability Factor (S'') | $S'' = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta}\Big\vert_{I_B,I_{CO}}$ | A | Sensitivity to ╬▓ |
| 8 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | W | Must be < P_D(max) |
| 9 | Saturation Condition | $V_{CE} \leq V_{CE(sat)}$ | V | BJT is ON, saturated |
| 10 | Cutoff Condition | $I_B = 0 \Rightarrow I_C = I_{CEO}$ | A | BJT is OFF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S = 1 + ╬▓** is the **defining weakness** of the fixed bias circuit. For a typical silicon transistor with ╬▓ = 100, **S = 101**, meaning a tiny 1 ┬ĄA change in leakage current I_CO causes a 101 ┬ĄA change in I_C. This is why KTU examiners love to ask "Why is fixed bias rarely used in practical amplifier design?"

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Despite its poor stability, the **Fixed Bias Circuit** is foundational because:
- It is the **simplest topology** for demonstrating Q-point analysis.
- It appears as a **sub-circuit** inside more complex designs (e.g., inside an IC, R_B is often a current source equivalent).
- It is used in **switching applications** (digital logic, relay drivers) where the transistor is either fully ON (saturation) or fully OFF (cutoff) ŌĆö not in the linear region, so stability is irrelevant.
- It serves as the **pedagogical baseline** against which all other biasing circuits (Emitter Bias, Voltage Divider, Collector-Feedback) are compared.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Base Current I_B (Using KVL)

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the quiescent base current in a fixed bias circuit.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the base-emitter loop.** In a fixed bias circuit, the base loop consists of the supply **V_CC**, the base resistor **R_B**, and the base-emitter junction (treated as a voltage drop **V_BE**).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the base loop.** The sum of voltage rises equals the sum of voltage drops:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} &= I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE} \\
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* Starting from ground, we rise by V_CC to the supply rail, then drop across R_B by I_B┬ĘR_B, then drop across the forward-biased BE junction by V_BE, returning to ground.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve algebraically for I_B.** Rearrange by isolating I_B:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_B \cdot R_B &= V_{CC} - V_{BE} \\
I_B &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify dimensional consistency.**
- Numerator: Volts
- Denominator: Ohms
- Result: Volts / Ohms = Amperes Ō£ō

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Collector Current I_C

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the quiescent collector current.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the fundamental BJT current relation (Ebers-Moll simplification).**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO} \\
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* The collector current consists of (i) the amplified base current ╬▓┬ĘI_B arriving from the emitter, and (ii) the leakage current (1+╬▓)┬ĘI_CO flowing across the reverse-biased CB junction, also multiplied by the gain.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Approximation for silicon transistors at moderate temperatures.** For I_CO on the order of nanoamperes and I_B on the order of microamperes, the leakage term is often neglected:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &\approx \beta I_B \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the derived I_B expression:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta \cdot \left( \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \right) \\
I_C &= \frac{\beta (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,I_C = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Collector-Emitter Voltage V_CE

**Statement:** Derive the expression for V_CE at the Q-point.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply KVL around the collector-emitter loop.** The supply V_CC drops across R_C and then across the CE terminals:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} &= I_C R_C + V_{CE} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Isolate V_CE:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C R_C \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the I_C expression from Derivation 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - \left( \frac{\beta (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B} \right) R_C \\
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - \frac{\beta R_C (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = V_{CC} - \frac{\beta R_C (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability Factor S for Fixed Bias

**Statement:** Prove that S = 1 + ╬▓ for the fixed bias configuration.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the stability factor S (the rate of change of I_C with respect to leakage current I_CO, with I_B and ╬▓ held constant):**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\vert_{I_B, \beta} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Start from the exact I_C expression and differentiate:**
