$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO} \\
\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} &= \frac{\partial}{\partial I_{CO}} \left[ \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO} \right] \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Recognize that I_B is a CONSTANT in fixed bias (it does not depend on I_C).** Therefore, ╬▓┬ĘI_B is constant with respect to I_CO, and its derivative is zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} &= 0 + (1 + \beta)(1) \\
S &= 1 + \beta \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Use the general S-formula to confirm.** The general formula for S in any BJT bias circuit is:

$$\begin{aligned}
S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \left(\dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}\right)} \\
\end{aligned}$$

For fixed bias, **I_B is a constant**, so dI_B/dI_C = 0. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta (0)} = 1 + \beta \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,S = 1 + \beta\,} \quad \text{(Highest instability among all standard bias topologies)}$$

---

### 3.5 KTU Numerical Example (Model Question Pattern)

**Given:**
- V_CC = 12 V
- R_B = 500 k╬®
- R_C = 1 k╬®
- ╬▓ = 100
- V_BE = 0.7 V (silicon)

**Find:** I_B, I_C, V_CE, and state the region of operation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate I_B using the derived formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{500 \times 10^3} \\
I_B &= \frac{11.3}{500 \times 10^3} = 22.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} \\
I_B &= 22.6 \text{ ┬ĄA} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate I_C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_C &= \beta I_B = 100 \times 22.6 \times 10^{-6} \\
I_C &= 2.26 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} = 2.26 \text{ mA} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate V_CE:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (2.26 \times 10^{-3})(1 \times 10^3) \\
V_{CE} &= 12 - 2.26 = 9.74 \text{ V} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify active region operation:**
- V_CE = 9.74 V > V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V Ō£ō
- I_B > 0 Ō£ō
- **Conclusion:** BJT is in the **Active (Linear) Region** Ō£ō

**Step 5 ŌĆö Calculate the stability factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= 1 + \beta = 1 + 100 = 101 \\
\end{aligned}$$

This means the circuit is **highly unstable** ŌĆö a 1 ┬ĄA increase in leakage current causes a 101 ┬ĄA increase in collector current, which will shift the Q-point drastically with temperature.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Fixed Bias Circuit

Since a true physical schematic requires graphical circuit symbols, the following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional flow architecture** of the fixed bias circuit ŌĆö showing how voltage and current signals propagate from supply to transistor terminals.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUP["V_CC Supply Rail (Positive DC)"] --> RBnode["R_B Base Resistor (Fixed Value)"]
    RBnode --> Bnode["BJT Base Terminal (B)"]
    Bnode --> BEJ["Base-Emitter Junction (V_BE = 0.7V)"]
    BEJ --> ENODE["BJT Emitter Terminal (E) Grounded"]
    SUP --> RCnode["R_C Collector Resistor"]
    RCnode --> CNODE["BJT Collector Terminal (C)"]
    Bnode --> CTRL["Transistor Action: I_C = beta x I_B"]
    CTRL --> CNODE
    CNODE --> VCEout["V_CE Output across C-E terminals"]
    ENODE --> VCEout
    IBQ["I_B = V_CC - V_BE over R_B"] -.-> RBnode
    ICQ["I_C = beta x I_B (Active Region)"] -.-> CNODE

    classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef resistor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef bjt fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000

    class SUP supply
    class RBnode,RCnode resistor
    class Bnode,BEJ,ENODE,CNODE,CTRL bjt
    class VCEout,IBQ,ICQ output
```

### 4.2 Q-Point Determination Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Begin: Given V_CC, R_B, R_C, beta, V_BE"] --> CALCIB["Step 1: Compute I_B = V_CC minus V_BE over R_B"]
    CALCIB --> CALCIC["Step 2: Compute I_C = beta x I_B"]
    CALCIC --> CALCVCE["Step 3: Compute V_CE = V_CC minus I_C x R_C"]
    CALCVCE --> REGION{"Step 4: Check Region of Operation"}
    REGION -- "V_CE greater than V_CE_sat" --> ACTIVE["ACTIVE REGION (Amplifier Mode)"]
    REGION -- "V_CE less than V_CE_sat" --> SAT["SATURATION REGION (Switch ON)"]
    REGION -- "I_B = 0" --> CUT["CUTOFF REGION (Switch OFF)"]
    ACTIVE --> STAB["Step 5: Compute Stability Factor S = 1 + beta"]
    SAT --> NOTE1["Note: S irrelevant in switching"]
    CUT --> NOTE1
    STAB --> END1["Q-Point = V_CE , I_C ; Design Complete"]
    NOTE1 --> END1

    classDef startend fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000

    class START,END1 startend
    class CALCIB,CALCIC,CALCVCE,STAB calc
    class REGION decision
    class ACTIVE,SAT,CUT,NOTE1 result
```

### 4.3 Key Architectural Observations

- **Single Power Rail:** The fixed bias circuit requires only **one DC supply** (V_CC), unlike dual-supply biasing schemes.
- **No Feedback Path:** Critically, there is **no connection** between the collector and the base, and **no emitter resistor**. This is what makes S = 1 + ╬▓.
- **Direct Coupling:** The base is **directly coupled** to V_CC through R_B, which means any ripple on the supply rail propagates directly to the base.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
Define the term "Fixed Bias" as applied to a BJT. Why is the stability factor S = 1 + ╬▓ for this configuration?

**Model Answer:**
A **Fixed Bias Circuit** is a BJT biasing arrangement in which the base current I_B is rigidly set by a single resistor R_B connected between the supply V_CC and the base, with no feedback path. The base current is independent of collector current, and hence the operating point is "fixed."

The **stability factor** is defined as **S = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéI_CO**. Since in fixed bias, I_B is constant (ŌłéI_B/ŌłéI_C = 0), substituting into the general formula:

$$S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta(\partial I_B/\partial I_C)} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - 0} = 1 + \beta$$

This represents the **worst thermal stability** of any standard bias topology.

---

**Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
A BJT in fixed bias has V_CC = 10 V, R_B = 200 k╬®, R_C = 2 k╬®, ╬▓ = 50, V_BE = 0.7 V. Calculate I_B and V_CE.

**Model Answer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{10 - 0.7}{200 \times 10^3} = \frac{9.3}{200 \times 10^3} \\
I_B &= 46.5 \text{ ┬ĄA} \\
I_C &= \beta I_B = 50 \times 46.5 \text{ ┬ĄA} = 2.325 \text{ mA} \\
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 10 - (2.325 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^3) \\
V_{CE} &= 10 - 4.65 = 5.35 \text{ V} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Q-Point: (5.35 V, 2.325 mA), Active Region.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] For a BJT with fixed bias configuration, derive the expressions for the quiescent base current I_B, collector current I_C, and collector-emitter voltage V_CE. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A silicon transistor (╬▓ = 100, V_BE = 0.7 V) is used in a fixed bias circuit with V_CC = 15 V, R_B = 400 k╬®, and R_C = 2 k╬®. Calculate I_B, I_C, V_CE, and the stability factor S. Comment on the thermal stability of this circuit.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation:**

Applying KVL to the base-emitter loop:

$$V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE} \Rightarrow I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

Applying the BJT current relation in the active region (neglecting I_CO):

$$I_C = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}$$

Applying KVL to the collector-emitter loop:

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = V_{CC} - \frac{\beta R_C (V_{CC} - V_{BE})}{R_B}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) The BJT operates in the active region, (ii) Leakage current I_CO is negligible, (iii) V_BE = 0.7 V (silicon, constant), (iv) ╬▓ is constant and independent of I_C.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_B &= \frac{15 - 0.7}{400 \times 10^3} = \frac{14.3}{400 \times 10^3} = 35.75 \text{ ┬ĄA} \\
I_C &= 100 \times 35.75 \text{ ┬ĄA} = 3.575 \text{ mA} \\
V_{CE} &= 15 - (3.575 \text{ mA})(2 \text{ k}\Omega) = 15 - 7.15 = 7.85 \text{ V} \\
S &= 1 + \beta = 1 + 100 = 101 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on Stability:** With **S = 101**, the circuit is **highly thermally unstable**. A small change in I_CO (e.g., due to temperature rise) produces a 101-fold magnified change in I_C, shifting the Q-point significantly. **This makes the fixed bias circuit unsuitable for analog amplifier applications** in practice; it is reserved primarily for switching applications where the BJT is either in cutoff or saturation.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat functional diagram, the operation of a fixed bias BJT circuit. State its major drawback.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the stability factor S for a fixed bias circuit. If ╬▓ = 80, by what factor does a 5 ┬ĄA increase in I_CO affect the collector current?

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Operation and Diagram:**

In a fixed bias BJT circuit, the supply V_CC is connected to the collector through resistor R_C, and to the base through resistor R_B. The emitter is grounded. When V_CC is applied, current flows through R_B into the base, forward-biasing the base-emitter junction. This base current I_B is determined by V_CC, R_B, and V_BE, and is independent of the collector current. The transistor action then produces a collector current I_C = ╬▓┬ĘI_B, which flows through R_C developing a voltage drop I_C┬ĘR_C. The collector-emitter voltage is V_CE = V_CC - I_C┬ĘR_C, establishing the Q-point (V_CEQ, I_CQ).

**Major Drawback:** The stability factor S = 1 + ╬▓ is very high, making the circuit **highly susceptible to thermal runaway**. Any increase in temperature increases I_CO, which increases I_C, which further raises the temperature ŌĆö leading to a destructive positive feedback loop.

**(b) Stability Factor Derivation:**

The general stability factor formula is:

$$S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \left(\dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}\right)}$$

In fixed bias, I_B is constant ŌåÆ ŌłéI_B/ŌłéI_C = 0:

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - 0} = 1 + \beta \\
S &= 1 + 80 = 81 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Effect on I_C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_C &= S \cdot \Delta I_{CO} = 81 \times 5 \text{ ┬ĄA} \\
\Delta I_C &= 405 \text{ ┬ĄA} = 0.405 \text{ mA} \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** A 5 ┬ĄA rise in I_CO causes a **405 ┬ĄA increase** in I_C ŌĆö a magnification factor of **81├Ś**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting V_BE:** Many students compute I_B = V_CC/R_B instead of (V_CC - V_BE)/R_B. For silicon, this gives a ~6% error ŌĆö enough to lose a full mark in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing I_CO with I_CEO:** I_CEO = (1+╬▓)┬ĘI_CO is the *leakage current with base open*, NOT the collector current in active mode. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Not stating region of operation:** Every numerical MUST conclude with "BJT is in Active/Saturation/Cutoff region." Skipping this loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up S, S', and S'':** S = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéI_CO; S' = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéV_BE; S'' = ŌłéI_C/Ōłé╬▓. Many students write the wrong denominator.
> 5. **Sign of V_BE:** In the forward-biased BE junction, V_BE = +0.7 V (P-side higher than N-side). Reversing this sign is a common error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ŌÜĪ **Fixed Bias = "No Feedback."** The defining feature is the absence of any path from collector to base or emitter to ground (through a resistor).
- ŌÜĪ **Master Formula (I_B):** $I_B = (V_{CC} - V_{BE}) / R_B$ ŌĆö commit this to memory; it appears in **every** KTU problem.
- ŌÜĪ **Master Formula (I_C):** $I_C = \beta I_B$ (active region, neglecting I_CO).
- ŌÜĪ **Master Formula (V_CE):** $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ ŌĆö this locates the Q-point horizontally on the load line.
- ŌÜĪ **Stability Factor:** $S = 1 + \beta$ ŌĆö the **highest (worst)** among all standard bias topologies. Always state this when asked about the drawback.
- ŌÜĪ **Thermal Runaway:** Fixed bias is vulnerable because increased T ŌåÆ increased I_CO ŌåÆ increased I_C ŌåÆ increased power dissipation ŌåÆ further T rise (positive feedback).
- ŌÜĪ **Practical Use:** Restricted to **switching applications** (relay drivers, LED drivers, digital logic interfaces), NOT linear amplification.
- ŌÜĪ **Load Line:** The DC load line on the I_CŌĆōV_CE graph spans from (V_CC, 0) to (0, V_CC/R_C). The Q-point lies on this line.
- ŌÜĪ **Region of Operation Test:** V_CE > V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V AND I_B > 0 ŌåÆ Active. Otherwise: Saturation (V_CE Ōēż 0.2 V) or Cutoff (I_B = 0).
- ŌÜĪ **Temperature Dependents:** ╬▓ Ōåæ with T, V_BE Ōåō with T (Ōēł -2 mV/┬░C), I_CO Ōåæ exponentially with T ŌĆö all three destabilize the Q-point.
- ŌÜĪ **Examiners' Favorite Question:** "Compare fixed bias and emitter bias in terms of stability factor" ŌĆö know that emitter bias gives S = (1+╬▓)/(1+╬▓┬Ę╬▒) which is much smaller.
- ŌÜĪ **Single-Supply Topology:** Unlike op-amp circuits, fixed bias uses only V_CC (no negative supply), making it suitable for single-rail battery-powered designs.
- ŌÜĪ **Quick Sanity Check:** If computed V_CE < 0.2 V, the BJT is saturated ŌĆö reduce ╬▓┬ĘR_C/R_B ratio or increase R_B to push the Q-point back into the active region.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Self-Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Self-Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Self-Bias** is a single-supply biasing configuration used primarily for **Field Effect Transistors (JFETs and depletion-mode MOSFETs)** in which the DC gate-to-source bias voltage $V_{GS}$ is generated *internally* by the drain current $I_D$ of the device itself flowing through a source resistor $R_S$. No separate gate-bias battery or negative supply is required ŌĆö the circuit is said to be "self-biasing" because the device creates its own operating-point bias from the single positive drain supply $V_{DD}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box ŌĆö Self-Bias**
> A biasing scheme in which the gate is held at **0 V (DC ground)** and the source is raised to a positive potential above ground by the voltage drop across $R_S$. The result is a **negative $V_{GS}$** for an n-channel JFET/depletion MOSFET, which is the required polarity to operate the device in its pinch-off (saturation) region.

## 1.2 The Governing Relationship

The fundamental equation of self-bias is obtained directly from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied between the gate and the source:

$$V_{GS} \;=\; V_G \;-\; V_S \;=\; 0 \;-\; I_D R_S \;=\; -\,I_D R_S$$

This single relationship, when combined with **Shockley's square-law equation** (the device's transfer characteristic), completely determines the DC operating point (Q-point) of the transistor.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Ball-and-Bowl" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a heavy bowling ball resting in a shallow bowl on a tilted table. The ball naturally rolls *downhill* until it settles at a specific equilibrium position determined by gravity, the bowl's curvature, and the tilt angle. In self-bias, $R_S$ acts as the **tilt of the table** ŌĆö the larger $R_S$, the more "tilt," and the further the operating point shifts away from $I_{DSS}$ (the "ball-at-the-top" position when $V_{GS}=0$). The ball *finds its own resting place* without anyone pushing it ŌĆö exactly like the transistor "finding" its own $I_D$ and $V_{GS}$ from the interaction of the load line and the transfer curve.

A simpler everyday analogy: a **thermostat-controlled heater**. The heater produces heat (drain current), the thermostat (self-bias feedback) senses the temperature and *automatically* adjusts the fuel input so that a stable equilibrium temperature is maintained ŌĆö no human operator needed. Likewise, $R_S$ "senses" $I_D$ and "adjusts" $V_{GS}$ to establish a stable Q-point.

## 1.4 Why Self-Bias Matters in Real Engineering

| Engineering Need | How Self-Bias Solves It |
|---|---|
| Portability (battery operation) | Uses only **one** supply rail ($V_{DD}$), not two |
| Thermal stability | Negative feedback through $R_S$ stabilizes $I_D$ against temperature drift |
| Cost & PCB area | Eliminates gate-bias divider or negative-rail regulator |
| Reproducibility across device lots | Q-point is set by $R_S$, not by the (highly variable) $I_{DSS}$) |

The standard metrics used throughout this module are:
- $I_{DSS}$: drain current at $V_{GS}=0$, typical range **0.5 mA ŌĆō 50 mA**
- $V_P$ (or $V_{GS(\text{off})}$): pinch-off voltage, typically **ŌłÆ1 V to ŌłÆ10 V**
- $R_S$: source resistor, typically **100 ╬® ŌĆō 10 k╬®**
- $R_G$: gate-return resistor, typically **Ōēź 1 M╬®** (essentially opens DC, grounds AC)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

The self-bias circuit for an **n-channel JFET** (or depletion-mode MOSFET) is built from just four components and one supply:

1. **Drain supply $V_{DD}$** is applied to the drain through a drain resistor $R_D$.
2. **Source resistor $R_S$** is connected between the source terminal and ground. Drain current $I_D$ flows through it, raising the source above ground by $V_S = I_D R_S$.
3. **Gate-return resistor $R_G$** connects the gate to ground. Because the JFET gate is a reverse-biased PN junction, its DC current is essentially zero (gate leakage is in nA or pA), so $V_G = 0$ to an excellent approximation.
4. **DC KVL around the gateŌĆōsource loop** gives $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D R_S$. Because $I_D > 0$ and $R_S > 0$, $V_{GS}$ is automatically **negative** ŌĆö exactly the polarity required to keep an n-channel JFET in the pinch-off (active/saturation) region.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "self"?** The supply $V_{DD}$ does not directly set $V_{GS}$. Instead, the *device's own output current* $I_D$ is routed through $R_S$ and converted into the control voltage $V_{GS}$. The bias is therefore an **emergent property** of the closed loop (device $\to$ $R_S$ $\to$ $V_{GS}$ $\to$ $I_D$ $\to$ device).

## 2.2 The Two Governing Equations (KTU Core Equations)

The Q-point $(I_{DQ}, V_{GSQ})$ is the simultaneous solution of two equations:

**Equation A ŌĆö Shockley's square-law (device transfer curve):**

$$I_D \;=\; I_{DSS}\,\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}, \qquad V_P < V_{GS} < 0$$

**Equation B ŌĆö Self-bias line (circuit constraint):**

$$V_{GS} \;=\; -\,I_D R_S$$

Substituting Equation B into Equation A produces a single quadratic in $I_D$:

$$I_D \;=\; I_{DSS}\,\left(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$$

The two roots of this quadratic give the possible operating points. The **physically valid root** is the smaller one (the JFET operates on the lower, stable branch of its transfer curve).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All variables, all units, all conditions ŌĆö printed once, used everywhere in Module 1.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | Boundary / Sign |
|---|---|---|---|
| $I_{DSS}$ | Drain current with gate shorted to source | mA | Positive, **device parameter** |
| $V_P$ | Pinch-off voltage (=$V_{GS(\text{off})}$) | V | **Negative** for n-channel |
| $V_{GS}$ | Gate-to-source voltage | V | Negative, $V_P < V_{GS} \le 0$ |
| $I_D$ | Drain current (DC) | mA | $0 < I_D \le I_{DSS}$ |
| $R_S$ | Source self-bias resistor | k$\Omega$ | Positive |
| $R_D$ | Drain load resistor | k$\Omega$ | Positive |
| $R_G$ | Gate-return resistor | M$\Omega$ | $\ge 1\ \mathrm{M}\Omega$ typical |
| $V_{DD}$ | Single positive supply | V | Positive |
| $V_{DS}$ | Drain-to-source voltage | V | Must exceed $V_{DS(\text{sat})}$ |
| $V_{DS(\text{sat})}$ | Minimum $V_{DS}$ to remain in pinch-off | V | $V_{GS} - V_P$ |
| $g_{m0}$ | Transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$ | mS | $2 I_{DSS} / \vert V_P \vert$ |
| $g_m$ | Transconductance at Q-point | mS | $g_{m0}\,(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$ |

### High-Yield Closed-Form Solution for $I_{DQ}$

A standard KTU shortcut result, derived by substituting $x = I_D R_S / \vert V_P \vert$ and solving the resulting quadratic, gives:

$$I_{DQ} \;=\; \frac{1}{R_S^{\,2}}\,\Bigl[\,-\vert V_P \vert \;-\; \frac{V_P}{I_{DSS} R_S} \;+\; \sqrt{\bigl(\tfrac{V_P}{I_{DSS} R_S} + \vert V_P \vert\bigr)^2 \;-\; V_P^{\,2}}\,\Bigr]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU board exams, you are **not** required to memorize this closed form. You must be able to (a) set up the quadratic, (b) justify the choice of root, and (c) substitute numbers. The formula sheet above is the only allowed reference material.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio preamplifier front-ends** (JFET phono stages): self-bias gives ultra-high input impedance ($R_G \ge 1\ \mathrm{M}\Omega$) without a divider that would load the source.
- **RF mixer and oscillator stages**: stable Q-point reduces frequency drift with temperature.
- **Instrumentation charge amplifiers**: the high input impedance of a self-biased JFET preserves the DC accuracy of piezoelectric and pH sensors.
- **Switching-mode power controllers**: depletion-mode MOSFETs in self-bias remain OFF at $V_{GS}=0$, enabling *fail-safe* start-up behaviour.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Circuit Law to Quadratic in $I_D$

We begin from the two governing equations and execute every algebraic step.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply KVL on the gate-source mesh.**

The gate node is connected to ground through $R_G$. Because the JFET gate draws negligible DC current ($I_G \approx 0$), there is no DC voltage drop across $R_G$, hence:

$$V_G \;=\; 0\ \text{V}$$

The source node sits above ground by the drop across $R_S$:

$$V_S \;=\; I_D R_S$$

Therefore:

$$V_{GS} \;=\; V_G - V_S \;=\; 0 - I_D R_S \;=\; -I_D R_S$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the bias constraint into Shockley's equation.**

Shockley's equation (transfer curve) is:

$$I_D \;=\; I_{DSS}\,\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\Bigr)^{\!2}$$

Insert $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$:

$$I_D \;=\; I_{DSS}\,\Bigl(1 - \frac{-I_D R_S}{V_P}\Bigr)^{\!2} \;=\; I_{DSS}\,\Bigl(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P}\Bigr)^{\!2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Take the square root on both sides (note: choose the positive root because $I_D \ge 0$):**

$$\sqrt{I_D} \;=\; \sqrt{I_{DSS}}\,\Bigl(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{V_P}\Bigr)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Rearrange to isolate the linear and quadratic terms.**

Let $a = R_S / V_P$. Then:

$$\sqrt{I_D} \;=\; \sqrt{I_{DSS}} \;+\; a\sqrt{I_{DSS}}\,\sqrt{I_D}$$

Bring all $\sqrt{I_D}$ terms to the left:

$$\sqrt{I_D}\,(1 - a\sqrt{I_{DSS}}\,) \;=\; \sqrt{I_{DSS}}$$

Solve for $\sqrt{I_D}$:

$$\sqrt{I_D} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{I_{DSS}}}{1 - a\sqrt{I_{DSS}}\,} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{I_{DSS}}}{1 - \frac{R_S\sqrt{I_{DSS}}}{V_P}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Square both sides to obtain $I_{DQ}$:**

$$I_{DQ} \;=\; \frac{I_{DSS}}{\Bigl(1 - \dfrac{R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}}}{V_P}\Bigr)^{\!2}}$$

This is the closed-form KTU-board result for an n-channel JFET in self-bias. The denominator is positive, the numerator is positive, so $I_{DQ}$ is positive as required.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check.** As $R_S \to 0$, the denominator $\to 1$ and $I_{DQ} \to I_{DSS}$. As $R_S \to \infty$, the denominator $\to \infty$ and $I_{DQ} \to 0$. Both limits match physical intuition: no source resistor means the JFET is fully ON at $I_{DSS}$; an infinite source resistor chokes off the current to zero.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

**Problem.** An n-channel JFET has $I_{DSS} = 8\ \mathrm{mA}$ and $V_P = -4\ \mathrm{V}$. It is self-biased with $R_S = 1\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$. Determine $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, and the transconductance $g_m$ at the Q-point.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the auxiliary term $R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}} / V_P$.**

$$R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}} \;=\; (1\ \mathrm{k}\Omega)\,\sqrt{8\ \mathrm{mA}} \;=\; (1000)\,\sqrt{0.008} \;=\; (1000)(0.08944) \;=\; 89.44$$

$$\frac{R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}}}{V_P} \;=\; \frac{89.44}{-4} \;=\; -22.36$$

The denominator:

$$1 - (-22.36) \;=\; 23.36$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $I_{DQ}$:**

$$I_{DQ} \;=\; \frac{8\ \mathrm{mA}}{(23.36)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{8\ \mathrm{mA}}{545.7} \;=\; 0.01466\ \mathrm{mA} \;=\; 14.66\ \mu\mathrm{A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $V_{GSQ}$:**

$$V_{GSQ} \;=\; -I_{DQ} R_S \;=\; -(0.01466\ \mathrm{mA})(1\ \mathrm{k}\Omega) \;=\; -0.01466\ \mathrm{V} \;=\; -14.66\ \mathrm{mV}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $g_m$ at the Q-point.**

The transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$ is:

$$g_{m0} \;=\; \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 8\ \mathrm{mA}}{4\ \mathrm{V}} \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}$$

The transconductance at the Q-point:

$$g_m \;=\; g_{m0}\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\Bigr) \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}\Bigl(1 - \frac{-0.01466}{-4}\Bigr) \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}\,(1 - 0.003665)$$

$$g_m \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS} \times 0.9963 \;=\; 3.985\ \mathrm{mS} \;\approx\; 3.99\ \mathrm{mS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation.** The Q-point is very close to $I_{DSS}$ because the source resistor is too small to bias the JFET off significantly. To push the Q-point towards the midpoint ($I_D = I_{DSS}/4$) the designer must choose $R_S \approx \vert V_P \vert / \sqrt{I_{DSS}}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Bias Schemes

| Feature | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage-Divider Bias | Current-Source Bias |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Supplies required | 2 ($V_{DD}$ and $V_{GG}$) | **1** | 1 | 1 |
| Thermal stability (stability factor $S$) | Poor ($S \gg 1$) | Good ($S \approx 1.5$) | Very good ($S \approx 0.5$) | Excellent ($S \to 0$) |
| Q-point set by | $V_{GG}$ and $R_S$ | $R_S$ alone | $R_1, R_2, R_S$ | Reference current $I_{REF}$ |
| Input impedance at gate | $R_G$ | $R_G$ (very high) | $R_1 \Vert R_2$ (lower) | $R_G$ |
| Component count | 3 | **2** | 4 | 4+ |
| KTU exam weightage | Low | **High** | Medium | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Self-Bias

The following Mermaid diagram captures the closed-loop feedback nature of the self-bias configuration. The signal flow shows how the *output* current of the device is routed back to *control* the input voltage of the same device ŌĆö a textbook negative-feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VDD Positive Supply Rail"] --> B["Drain Resistor RD"]
    B --> C["Drain Node D"]
    C --> D["JFET Channel n-channel"]
    D --> E["Source Node S"]
    E --> F["Source Resistor RS"]
    F --> G["Ground 0V Reference"]
    H["Gate Resistor RG high value"] --> I["Gate Node G"]
    I --> D
    H --> G
    E --> J["Negative Feedback Path VS equals ID times RS"]
    J --> K["VGS equals VG minus VS equals minus ID RS"]
    K --> I
    C --> L["Output Node VDS taken across device"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic-Level Functional Map

The diagram below emphasizes the *role* of each component rather than its exact pictorial symbol, which is a KTU-friendly abstraction.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPLY ["DC Power Subsystem"]
        s1["V_DD Single Rail Source"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT ["Output Network"]
        o1["R_D converts I_D to V_D"]
        o2["Drain terminal D delivers amplified signal"]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE ["Active Device"]
        d1["JFET or Depletion MOSFET"]
        d2["Transfer Curve I_D vs V_GS"]
    end

    subgraph BIAS ["Self-Bias Feedback Network"]
        b1["R_S converts I_D to V_S"]
        b2["R_G sets V_G equal to 0 V"]
        b3["V_GS equals V_G minus V_S"]
    end

    s1 --> o1 --> d1
    d1 --> b1 --> b3
    b2 --> b3
    b3 --> d2
    d2 --> d1
    d1 --> o2
```

## 4.3 The Q-Point Construction (Graphical Method)

The DC operating point is the *intersection* of the device transfer curve and the self-bias load line. The self-bias line passes through the origin $(I_D=0,\ V_{GS}=0)$ and through the point $(I_D = I_{DSS},\ V_{GS} = -I_{DSS} R_S)$ on the $V_{GS}$ axis extension ŌĆö the two intercepts are:

- $V_{GS}$-intercept: $V_{GS} = -I_{DSS} R_S$
- $I_D$-intercept: $I_D = -V_P / R_S$ (extrapolated, beyond the physical curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Transfer curve Shockley parabola"] --> C["Intersection point Q-point"]
    B["Self-bias line V_GS equals minus I_D R_S"] --> C
    C --> D["Read coordinates I_DQ and V_GSQ"]
    C --> E["Compute V_DS equals V_DD minus I_DQ R_D plus R_S"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Set B, Q.1.a]**
> *"What is meant by self-bias in a JFET amplifier? Write the equation relating $V_{GS}$ and $I_D$ in such a bias arrangement."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Self-bias is a single-supply biasing technique for JFETs (or depletion-mode MOSFETs) in which the DC gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$ is derived from the device's own drain current $I_D$ flowing through a source resistor $R_S$, with the gate connected to ground through a high-value resistor $R_G$. No separate gate-bias supply is required, and the circuit operates from a single positive rail $V_{DD}$.

**Equation (1 mark):**

$$V_{GS} \;=\; -\,I_D\,R_S$$

This negative value of $V_{GS}$ is precisely what is required to keep the n-channel JFET in its pinch-off (active) region.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Set A, Q.1.b]**
> *"Why is a self-bias circuit preferred over a fixed-bias circuit for JFET amplifiers?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The self-bias configuration is preferred for three reasons:
1. **Single-supply operation:** It needs only $V_{DD}$, eliminating the need for a separate negative gate-bias supply.
2. **Improved thermal stability:** The negative feedback through $R_S$ reduces the sensitivity of the Q-point to temperature-induced changes in $I_{DSS}$ and $V_P$.
3. **Independence from device parameter spread:** The Q-point is set primarily by the external $R_S$, not by the highly variable parameter $I_{DSS}$, making the design reproducible across different JFET samples from the same lot.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Two Independent Alternatives)

### Question A ŌĆö 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Q.3.a, Module 1, 14 marks]**
> *For the n-channel JFET self-bias circuit shown, $I_{DSS} = 10\ \mathrm{mA}$, $V_P = -5\ \mathrm{V}$, $R_S = 1.2\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 2.2\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$, and $V_{DD} = 15\ \mathrm{V}$.*

#### (a) Determine the Q-point $(I_{DQ},\ V_{GSQ})$ and verify the JFET is operating in the pinch-off (saturation) region. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Formulate the self-bias constraint:**

$$V_{GS} \;=\; -I_D R_S \;=\; -(1.2\ \mathrm{k}\Omega)\,I_D$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Shockley's equation:**

$$I_D \;=\; I_{DSS}\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\Bigr)^{2} \;=\; 10\ \mathrm{mA}\,\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{-5}\Bigr)^{2} \;=\; 10\ \mathrm{mA}\,\Bigl(1 + \frac{V_{GS}}{5}\Bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $V_{GS} = -1.2\,I_D$ (with $I_D$ in mA):**

$$I_D \;=\; 10\ \mathrm{mA}\,\Bigl(1 + \frac{-1.2\,I_D}{5}\Bigr)^{2} \;=\; 10\ \mathrm{mA}\,(1 - 0.24\,I_D)^{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Expand the square:**

$$I_D \;=\; 10\,(1 - 0.48\,I_D + 0.0576\,I_D^{\,2}) \;=\; 10 - 4.8\,I_D + 0.576\,I_D^{\,2}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Rearrange into standard quadratic form $a\,I_D^{\,2} + b\,I_D + c = 0$:**

$$0.576\,I_D^{\,2} \;-\; 4.8\,I_D \;-\; I_D \;+\; 10 \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 0.576\,I_D^{\,2} - 5.8\,I_D + 10 \;=\; 0$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the quadratic formula:**

$$I_D \;=\; \frac{5.8 \pm \sqrt{(5.8)^{2} - 4(0.576)(10)}}{2(0.576)} \;=\; \frac{5.8 \pm \sqrt{33.64 - 23.04}}{1.152}$$

$$I_D \;=\; \frac{5.8 \pm \sqrt{10.60}}{1.152} \;=\; \frac{5.8 \pm 3.256}{1.152}$$

**Two roots:** $I_D = 7.85\ \mathrm{mA}$ **or** $I_D = 2.21\ \mathrm{mA}$.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Choose the physically valid root.**

The first root, $I_D = 7.85\ \mathrm{mA}$, would give $V_{GS} = -1.2 \times 7.85 = -9.42\ \mathrm{V}$, which is **more negative than $V_P = -5\ \mathrm{V}$** ŌĆö physically impossible (the JFET would be cut off). Therefore the valid root is:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} \;=\; 2.21\ \mathrm{mA}, \qquad V_{GSQ} \;=\; -1.2 \times 2.21 \;=\; -2.65\ \mathrm{V}}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Verify pinch-off (saturation) operation.**

The minimum drain-to-source voltage for saturation is:

$$V_{DS(\mathrm{sat})} \;=\; V_{GS} - V_P \;=\; -2.65 - (-5) \;=\; 2.35\ \mathrm{V}$$

The actual drain-to-source voltage is:

$$V_{DS} \;=\; V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) \;=\; 15 - 2.21\ \mathrm{mA} \times (2.2 + 1.2)\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$$

$$V_{DS} \;=\; 15 - 2.21 \times 3.4 \;=\; 15 - 7.51 \;=\; 7.49\ \mathrm{V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 7.49\ \mathrm{V} > V_{DS(\mathrm{sat})} = 2.35\ \mathrm{V}$, the JFET is **confirmed to be in the pinch-off (saturation) region**. Ō£ö

#### (b) Compute the transconductance $g_m$ at the Q-point and the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ of a common-source amplifier built on this self-bias stage, assuming a load resistor $R_L = 4.7\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$ is AC-coupled to the drain. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $g_{m0}$ (transconductance at $V_{GS}=0$):**

$$g_{m0} \;=\; \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 10\ \mathrm{mA}}{5\ \mathrm{V}} \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $g_m$ at the Q-point:**

$$g_m \;=\; g_{m0}\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\Bigr) \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}\Bigl(1 - \frac{-2.65}{-5}\Bigr) \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}\,(1 - 0.53) \;=\; 4 \times 0.47$$

$$\boxed{g_m \;=\; 1.88\ \mathrm{mS}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö AC equivalent circuit.**

At signal frequencies, $R_S$ is bypassed by a large capacitor $C_S$ (assumed ideal short), so the source is at AC ground. The total AC load at the drain is $R_D \Vert R_L$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the small-signal voltage gain:**

$$A_v \;=\; -g_m\,(R_D \Vert R_L) \;=\; -(1.88\ \mathrm{mS})\,(2.2\ \mathrm{k}\Omega \Vert 4.7\ \mathrm{k}\Omega)$$

$$R_D \Vert R_L \;=\; \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7}\ \mathrm{k}\Omega \;=\; \frac{10.34}{6.9}\ \mathrm{k}\Omega \;=\; 1.498\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v \;=\; -(1.88\ \mathrm{mS})\,(1.498\ \mathrm{k}\Omega) \;=\; -2.82$$

$$\boxed{A_v \;\approx\; -2.82\ \mathrm{V/V}}$$

The negative sign indicates the **180┬░ phase inversion** characteristic of a common-source amplifier.

---

### Question B ŌĆö 14 Marks *(Alternative Choice)*
> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Q.3.b, Module 1, 14 marks]**
> *An n-channel JFET in self-bias has the following data: $I_{DSS} = 16\ \mathrm{mA}$, $V_P = -4\ \mathrm{V}$, $V_{DD} = 20\ \mathrm{V}$, $R_D = 1.5\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$, and the source resistor is bypassed at AC.*

#### (a) Choose the value of $R_S$ so that the Q-point is set at $I_{DQ} = I_{DSS}/4 = 4\ \mathrm{mA}$. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $V_{GSQ}$ from Shockley's equation at the desired $I_{DQ}$:**

$$I_{DQ} \;=\; I_{DSS}\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\Bigr)^{2}$$

$$4 \;=\; 16\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{-4}\Bigr)^{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{1}{4} \;=\; \Bigl(1 + \frac{V_{GSQ}}{4}\Bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Take the square root of both sides:**

$$\frac{1}{2} \;=\; 1 + \frac{V_{GSQ}}{4} \quad (\text{negative root rejected: } -\tfrac{1}{2} \text{ would give } V_{GSQ} = -6\ \mathrm{V} < V_P)$$

$$\frac{V_{GSQ}}{4} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; V_{GSQ} \;=\; -2\ \mathrm{V}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Use the self-bias constraint to find $R_S$:**

$$R_S \;=\; \frac{-V_{GSQ}}{I_{DQ}} \;=\; \frac{-(-2\ \mathrm{V})}{4\ \mathrm{mA}} \;=\; \frac{2}{0.004}\ \Omega \;=\; 500\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_S \;=\; 500\ \Omega}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify pinch-off operation.**

$V_{DS(\mathrm{sat})} = V_{GSQ} - V_P = -2 - (-4) = 2\ \mathrm{V}$.

$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S) = 20 - 4 \times (1.5 + 0.5) = 20 - 8 = 12\ \mathrm{V}$.

Since $12\ \mathrm{V} > 2\ \mathrm{V}$, the JFET is comfortably in saturation. Ō£ö

#### (b) If the bypass capacitor on $R_S$ fails open-circuit, calculate the new small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ and compare it to the value with the bypass in place. Use $R_L = 10\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Transconductance at the Q-point:**

$$g_{m0} \;=\; \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 16\ \mathrm{mA}}{4\ \mathrm{V}} \;=\; 8\ \mathrm{mS}$$

$$g_m \;=\; g_{m0}\Bigl(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\Bigr) \;=\; 8\Bigl(1 - \frac{-2}{-4}\Bigr) \;=\; 8 \times 0.5 \;=\; 4\ \mathrm{mS}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Gain with the bypass capacitor working (Case I ŌĆö source at AC ground):**

$$A_{v,\text{bypassed}} \;=\; -g_m\,(R_D \Vert R_L) \;=\; -(4\ \mathrm{mS})\,(1.5\ \mathrm{k}\Omega \Vert 10\ \mathrm{k}\Omega)$$

$$R_D \Vert R_L \;=\; \frac{1.5 \times 10}{11.5} \;=\; 1.304\ \mathrm{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v,\text{bypassed}} \;=\; -(4)(1.304) \;=\; -5.22\ \mathrm{V/V}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Gain with the bypass capacitor failed open (Case II ŌĆö unbypassed source degeneration):**

The standard JFET small-signal result with an unbypassed $R_S$ is:

$$A_{v,\text{unbypassed}} \;=\; -\frac{g_m\,(R_D \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

$$g_m R_S \;=\; (4\ \mathrm{mS})(500\ \Omega) \;=\; 2$$

$$A_{v,\text{unbypassed}} \;=\; -\frac{(4)(1.304)}{1 + 2} \;=\; -\frac{5.22}{3} \;=\; -1.74\ \mathrm{V/V}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Comparison and physical insight.**

| Condition | $\vert A_v \vert$ | Loss in dB |
|---|---|---|
| Bypassed (normal) | 5.22 | 0 dB (reference) |
| Unbypassed (fault) | 1.74 | $20\log_{10}(5.22/1.74) = 9.55\ \mathrm{dB}$ loss |

The unbypassed source resistor introduces **degenerative feedback** that reduces the gain by approximately **9.6 dB** but, in exchange, dramatically improves linearity, increases input dynamic range, and widens the bandwidth ŌĆö a deliberate design trade-off in many high-fidelity audio stages.

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{unbypassed}} \;=\; -1.74\ \mathrm{V/V}, \quad \text{a } 9.55\ \mathrm{dB} \text{ loss from the bypassed case}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to reject the spurious quadratic root.** The quadratic in $I_D$ always has two roots; the larger one corresponds to $V_{GS} < V_P$, which is physically forbidden. Awarding full marks requires **explicitly stating** which root is taken and **why** the other is rejected.
> 2. **Sign errors on $V_P$.** For n-channel JFETs, $V_P$ is **negative**. Plugging $|V_P|$ instead of $V_P$ into the Shockley equation flips the sign of the bias line and produces a wrong Q-point. Always write the sign explicitly.
> 3. **Failing to verify the pinch-off condition.** Even if $I_{DQ}$ and $V_{GSQ}$ are correctly computed, you must check $V_{DS} > V_{DS(\mathrm{sat})}$ to receive the final "operation-region" mark. Skipping this verification costs up to 2 of the 14 marks.
> 4. **Using $R_S$ un-bypassed in the AC analysis by mistake.** When the problem states "$R_S$ is bypassed by $C_S$," the small-signal source is at AC ground and $R_S$ does **not** appear in the gain expression. Conversely, when $C_S$ is *failed-open*, the $1 + g_m R_S$ denominator **must** appear.
> 5. **Unit confusion between mA and A.** When substituting $I_{DSS}$ and $I_D$ in the same equation, convert both to the same prefix before applying the quadratic formula. Mixing mA with V and k$\Omega$ is the single most common arithmetic error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Self-Bias (Module 1)**

- **Definition in one line:** A single-supply FET bias scheme where $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ is generated by the device's own drain current.
- **Core equation set:** Shockley $I_D = I_{DSS}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)^2$ **AND** bias line $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ ŌĆö must be solved *simultaneously*.
- **Sign convention (n-channel):** $I_{DSS} > 0$, $V_P < 0$, $V_{GS} < 0$, $I_D > 0$.
- **Two intercepts of the self-bias line:** $V_{GS} = -I_{DSS} R_S$ (on the $V_{GS}$ axis) and $I_D = -V_P/R_S$ (extrapolated on the $I_D$ axis).
- **Closed-form Q-point current:** $I_{DQ} = I_{DSS} / (1 - R_S \sqrt{I_{DSS}}/V_P)^2$.
- **Saturation check:** $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ}(R_D + R_S)$ **must exceed** $V_{DS(\mathrm{sat})} = V_{GSQ} - V_P$.
- **Transconductance formulas:** $g_{m0} = 2 I_{DSS}/\vert V_P \vert$, and $g_m = g_{m0}(1 - V_{GSQ}/V_P)$.
- **Bypassed vs unbypassed $R_S$:** Bypassed ŌåÆ max gain $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert R_L)$. Unbypassed ŌåÆ reduced gain $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert R_L)/(1 + g_m R_S)$.
- **Stability advantage:** $R_S$ provides DC negative feedback that reduces the Q-point's sensitivity to temperature and to device-to-device variation in $I_{DSS}$.
- **Component count:** Only two resistors ($R_S$, $R_D$) plus the gate-return $R_G$ are required ŌĆö the simplest of all FET biasing schemes.
- **Typical exam trap:** Always solve the **quadratic** in $I_D$, reject the larger root, and verify pinch-off. Marks are distributed across all three steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Divider Bias
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Divider Bias

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage Divider Bias (VDB)** is a BJT biasing configuration in which two resistors **RŌéü** and **RŌéé** are connected in series across the DC supply **V_CC**, forming a potential divider that sets a fixed base bias voltage **V_B** at the junction. An emitter resistor **R_E** provides DC negative feedback, making the quiescent operating point (Q-point) of the transistor **largely independent of the current gain ╬▓**, temperature variations, and device-to-device parameter spread.

This biasing scheme is also called **Universal Bias** or **Self-Bias with Voltage Divider** and is the **most widely used biasing method** in linear amplifier design because it satisfies the four cardinal requirements of a good bias circuit:

1. Establish a stable **Q-point (I_CQ, V_CEQ)**.
2. Make **I_C independent of ╬▓**.
3. Provide good **thermal stability** (low Stability Factor **S**).
4. Allow maximum **AC output swing** without distortion.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy ŌĆö The Thermostatic Shower:**
> Imagine a shower where two valves (RŌéü and RŌéé) work together to fix the *base temperature* of water, while a third valve at the outlet (**R_E**) automatically reduces flow when the water gets too hot (temperature rise in BJT). The combination of "preset" (voltage divider) plus "automatic correction" (R_E feedback) gives a steady, comfortable output regardless of how the heater fluctuates. The transistor's Q-point behaves the same way: **VŌéü and VŌéé preset the bias, and R_E corrects any drift due to ╬▓ or temperature.**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **V_BE (silicon, active region):** **0.7 V**
- **V_BE (germanium, active region):** **0.3 V**
- **Thermal voltage V_T:** **Ōēł 26 mV** at 300 K
- **Rule of thumb for ╬▓-independence:** **╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé**
- **Typical Stability Factor range:** **S Ōēł 1.01 to 10** (lower is better)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology & Operation

The Voltage Divider Bias circuit has the following components:

- **RŌéü:** Upper resistor of the divider, connected between **V_CC** and the **Base**.
- **RŌéé:** Lower resistor of the divider, connected between the **Base** and **Ground**.
- **R_C:** Collector resistor, connected between **V_CC** and the **Collector** (sets the gain and Q-point).
- **R_E:** Emitter resistor, connected between the **Emitter** and **Ground** (provides thermal stability).
- **C_E:** Emitter bypass capacitor (across R_E) ŌĆö improves AC gain by shorting R_E at signal frequencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The presence of **R_E without C_E** introduces **DC negative feedback**. Any tendency of I_C to rise (due to temperature or ╬▓ increase) causes V_E to rise, which reduces V_BE (= V_B ŌłÆ V_E), thereby opposing the original rise in I_C. This self-correcting action is the heart of voltage divider bias stability.

### 2.2 Two Methods of Analysis

There are two standard approaches to solve a VDB circuit:

| Method | When to Use | Key Assumption |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Exact Method (Thevenin's)** | Always valid | None ŌĆö uses V_TH and R_TH |
| **Approximate Method** | When **╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé** | Base current I_B is negligible; I_C Ōēł I_E |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board problems, the **exact method** is mandatory unless the question explicitly states the condition for the approximate method. Always show the Thevenin conversion first.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly applicable to KTU 2024 Scheme ESE problems** and have been verified against standard solid-state device references.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Thevenin Voltage at Base:} \quad V_{TH} = V_{CC}\cdot\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(2) Thevenin Resistance at Base:} \quad R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(3) Base-Emitter KVL Loop:} \quad V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(4) Base Current (Exact):} \quad I_B = \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(5) Collector Current (Exact):} \quad I_{CQ} = \beta \cdot I_B = \frac{\beta(V_{TH}-V_{BE})}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(6) Emitter Current:} \quad I_E = (\beta+1)I_B \approx I_C \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(7) Approximate Base Current:} \quad I_B \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1} \quad \text{(when I_B << I_2)} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(8) Approximate Collector Current:} \quad I_{CQ} \approx \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_E} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(9) Collector-Emitter Voltage:} \quad V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(10) Approximate V_CE:} \quad V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC} - I_C(R_C+R_E) \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(11) Stability Factor (General):} \quad S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(12) VDB Stability Factor:} \quad S = \frac{(1+\beta)\bigl(1+\frac{R_E}{R_1}\bigr)}{1+\beta+\frac{R_E}{R_2}} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(13) Design Rule for ╬▓-independence:} \quad \beta R_E \ge 10 R_2 \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(14) Output Voltage Swing Limit:} \quad V_{CEQ} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{2} \quad \text{(for max swing)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In the formula table, the symbol `Ōłź` denotes **parallel combination**, and the symbol `┬Ę` denotes **multiplication**. Do not confuse RŌéüŌłźRŌéé with the OR logic operator.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Voltage Divider Bias is the **workhorse bias topology** in:

- **Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier** input stages in audio equipment.
- **Operational amplifier input stages** (e.g., LM741, ╬╝A741, TL081).
- **RF amplifier front-ends** in communication receivers.
- **Transistor switching circuits** in power electronics and digital logic interfacing.
- **Differential amplifier tails** and **current-mirror reference stages** in analog ICs.

The reason for its universal adoption is its **excellent Q-point stability**, which ensures predictable circuit behavior across manufacturing tolerances and operating temperature ranges (typically **ŌłÆ55 ┬░C to +125 ┬░C** for military-grade silicon BJTs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stability Factor S for VDB

> [!NOTE]
> The Stability Factor **S = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéI_CO** quantifies how sensitively the collector current I_C responds to a change in the reverse saturation current I_CO (which doubles for every **10 ┬░C** rise in junction temperature).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the collector current equation in terms of base current:**

$$
I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop (from V_TH to ground):**

$$
V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute I_E = I_C + I_B (KCL at the transistor):**

$$
V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (I_C + I_B) R_E
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for I_B in terms of I_C:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_C R_E}{R_{TH} + R_E}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiate the I_C expression partially with respect to I_CO while treating V_BE as approximately constant:**

$$
\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \beta \cdot \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} + (\beta+1)
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Differentiate the I_B equation with respect to I_C and invert (chain rule):**

$$
\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_{CO}} = -\frac{R_E}{R_{TH}+R_E} \cdot \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Substitute back and solve for S = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéI_CO:**

$$
S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH}+R_E}}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Substitute R_TH = RŌéü RŌéé / (RŌéü + RŌéé) to obtain the VDB-specific form:**

$$
S = \frac{(1+\beta)\left(1 + \dfrac{R_E}{R_1}\right)}{1+\beta + \dfrac{R_E}{R_2}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a **board-exam favorite derivation**. Memorize the algebraic form: numerator has (1+╬▓)(1+R_E/RŌéü), denominator has (1+╬▓+R_E/RŌéé).

### 3.2 Derivation of Q-Point (Exact Method)

> [!NOTE]
> Given: **V_CC = 12 V, RŌéü = 10 k╬®, RŌéé = 5 k╬®, R_C = 2 k╬®, R_E = 1 k╬®, ╬▓ = 100, V_BE = 0.7 V**. Compute the Q-point (I_CQ, V_CEQ).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Thevenin Voltage V_TH:**

$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC}\cdot\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 12\cdot\frac{5\text{ k}\Omega}{10\text{ k}\Omega+5\text{ k}\Omega} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 4\text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the Thevenin Resistance R_TH:**

$$
R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{10\text{ k}\Omega \times 5\text{ k}\Omega}{10\text{ k}\Omega+5\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{50}{15}\text{ k}\Omega \approx 3.33\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Base Current I_B:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E} = \frac{4 - 0.7}{3.33\text{ k}\Omega + (101)(1\text{ k}\Omega)} = \frac{3.3}{104.33\text{ k}\Omega}
$$

$$
I_B \approx 31.63\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Collector Current I_CQ:**

$$
I_{CQ} = \beta \cdot I_B = 100 \times 31.63\ \mu\text{A} = 3.163\text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Emitter Current I_E:**

$$
I_E = (\beta+1) I_B = 101 \times 31.63\ \mu\text{A} \approx 3.195\text{ mA}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the Collector-Emitter Voltage V_CEQ:**

$$
V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 12 - (3.163\text{ mA})(2\text{ k}\Omega) - (3.195\text{ mA})(1\text{ k}\Omega)
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} = 12 - 6.326 - 3.195 = 2.479\text{ V}
$$

### 3.3 Verification with Approximate Method

Check the rule: **╬▓┬ĘR_E = 100 ├Ś 1 k╬® = 100 k╬®** and **10┬ĘRŌéé = 10 ├Ś 5 k╬® = 50 k╬®**.
Since **100 k╬® Ōēź 50 k╬®**, the approximate method is valid.

$$
I_{CQ} \approx \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{4-0.7}{1\text{ k}\Omega} = 3.3\text{ mA}
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC} - I_C(R_C+R_E) = 12 - 3.3\text{ mA} \times 3\text{ k}\Omega = 12 - 9.9 = 2.1\text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The approximate method gives slightly higher I_CQ (3.3 mA vs 3.163 mA) and slightly lower V_CEQ (2.1 V vs 2.479 V). The error is about **4.3%**, which is acceptable for engineering hand-calculations.

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix (Comparative Study of Biasing Schemes)

| Biasing Method | I_C Dependence on ╬▓ | Stability Factor S | Thermal Stability | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Bias (Base Bias)** | Highly dependent (I_C = ╬▓┬ĘV_CC/R_B) | S = (1+╬▓) | **Very Poor** | Low |
| **Collector-to-Base Bias** | Moderate (feedback reduces variation) | S = (1+╬▓)/(1+╬▓┬ĘR_C/(R_C+R_B)) | Fair | Low |
| **Emitter Bias (R_E only)** | Low (independent of ╬▓ if R_B small) | S = (1+╬▓)/(1+╬▓) = 1 | **Excellent** | Medium |
| **Voltage Divider Bias** | **Lowest (╬▓-independent when ╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé)** | S = (1+╬▓)(1+R_E/RŌéü)/(1+╬▓+R_E/RŌéé) | **Excellent** | **High (Favourite)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional flow** of analysis for a Voltage Divider Bias circuit, since Mermaid cannot directly render a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VCC Supply Source"] --> B["Resistor R1: Upper Divider"]
    B --> C["Junction Node: Base of BJT"]
    C --> D["Resistor R2: Lower Divider"]
    D --> E["Ground Reference Node"]
    C --> F["BJT Active Region: VBE = 0.7V"]
    F --> G["Emitter Resistor RE: DC Feedback"]
    G --> E
    A --> H["Resistor RC: Collector Load"]
    H --> I["Output Node: VCE at Collector"]
    F --> I
    J["Thevenin Equivalent: VTH and RTH"] --> C
    K["Beta and VBE: Device Parameters"] --> F
```

### 4.2 Thevenin Equivalent Reduction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Original VDB Circuit: R1, R2, RE, RC, VCC"] --> B["Step 1: Isolate R1 and R2 Divider"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute VTH = VCC times R2 over R1 plus R2"]
    B --> D["Step 3: Compute RTH = R1 parallel R2"]
    C --> E["Step 4: Draw Simplified Base Loop with VTH, RTH, VBE, RE"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Step 5: Apply KVL to Obtain IB"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Compute IC equals beta times IB"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Apply Output KVL to Obtain VCEQ"]
    H --> I["Final Q-Point: ICQ and VCEQ"]
```

### 4.3 Operating Region Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Computed VCEQ Value"] --> B{"VCEQ greater than 0V and less than VCC?"}
    B -->|Yes| C{"VCEQ greater than VCEsat approximately 0.2V?"}
    B -->|No| D["Invalid: Circuit Error"]
    C -->|Yes| E["ACTIVE REGION: Linear Amplification OK"]
    C -->|No| F["SATURATION REGION: Switch ON state"]
    A --> G{"ICQ greater than 0 and less than ICCmax?"}
    G -->|No| H["Invalid: Transistor Cutoff or Damaged"]
    G -->|Yes| I["Bias Valid: Q-Point Established"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> For maximum **AC output swing without clipping**, the ideal Q-point lies at the **midpoint of the DC load line**, i.e., **V_CEQ Ōēł V_CC/2**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**
*What is meant by Voltage Divider Bias? Why is it called the most widely used biasing scheme for a BJT in amplifier circuits?*

**Model Answer:**

Voltage Divider Bias (VDB) is a biasing configuration in which a pair of resistors RŌéü and RŌéé form a potential divider across V_CC to set a fixed base voltage V_B, while an emitter resistor R_E provides DC negative feedback. It is the most widely used biasing scheme because it makes the Q-point (I_CQ, V_CEQ) **independent of the transistor current gain ╬▓** when the design condition **╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé** is satisfied. This ensures excellent thermal stability (low Stability Factor S), immunity to device-to-device ╬▓ variation, and predictable performance across temperature changes. Additionally, the Q-point can be easily designed at the center of the DC load line (**V_CEQ Ōēł V_CC/2**) to allow maximum AC output swing without distortion.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**
*Define the Stability Factor S for a BJT biasing circuit. Write the expression for S in Voltage Divider Bias and explain how it depends on R_E.*

**Model Answer:**

The Stability Factor **S = ŌłéI_C/ŌłéI_CO** is defined as the rate of change of collector current I_C with respect to the reverse saturation current I_CO, with all other parameters held constant. It quantifies the thermal stability of the Q-point. For Voltage Divider Bias, the expression is:

$$
S = \frac{(1+\beta)\left(1+\dfrac{R_E}{R_1}\right)}{1+\beta+\dfrac{R_E}{R_2}}
$$

The stability factor **decreases as R_E increases** (because the numerator term R_E/RŌéü grows slower than the denominator term R_E/RŌéé in practical designs), which means a **larger R_E gives better thermal stability**. However, R_E cannot be made arbitrarily large because it consumes DC voltage drop (V_E = I_E┬ĘR_E) and reduces V_CEQ, limiting the AC output swing. A practical compromise is to choose R_E such that V_E is approximately **10% to 20% of V_CC**.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]**

**(a)** For the Voltage Divider Bias circuit shown with **V_CC = 15 V, RŌéü = 8 k╬®, RŌéé = 4 k╬®, R_C = 1.5 k╬®, R_E = 1 k╬®, ╬▓ = 120, V_BE = 0.7 V**, determine the Q-point (I_CQ, V_CEQ) using the **exact Thevenin method**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the Stability Factor S for the same circuit and comment on the thermal stability. Verify whether the **approximate method** is applicable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Thevenin Voltage V_TH:**

$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC}\cdot\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 15 \cdot \frac{4\text{ k}\Omega}{8\text{ k}\Omega + 4\text{ k}\Omega} = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 5\text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Thevenin Resistance R_TH:**

$$
R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{8 \times 4}{8+4}\text{ k}\Omega = \frac{32}{12}\text{ k}\Omega = 2.667\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Base Current I_B:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E} = \frac{5-0.7}{2.667\text{ k}\Omega + (121)(1\text{ k}\Omega)} = \frac{4.3}{123.667\text{ k}\Omega}
$$

$$
I_B \approx 34.77\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Collector Current I_CQ:**

$$
I_{CQ} = \beta \cdot I_B = 120 \times 34.77\ \mu\text{A} = 4.172\text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Emitter Current I_E:**

$$
I_E = (\beta+1) I_B = 121 \times 34.77\ \mu\text{A} \approx 4.207\text{ mA}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Collector-Emitter Voltage V_CEQ:**

$$
V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E = 15 - (4.172)(1.5) - (4.207)(1) = 15 - 6.258 - 4.207
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} \approx 4.535\text{ V}
$$

**Result:** The Q-point is **(I_CQ, V_CEQ) = (4.172 mA, 4.535 V)**, which lies in the **active region** (V_CEQ > V_CEsat Ōēł 0.2 V).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Stability Factor Formula:**

$$
S = \frac{(1+\beta)\left(1+\dfrac{R_E}{R_1}\right)}{1+\beta+\dfrac{R_E}{R_2}}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the numerical values:**

$$
S = \frac{(1+120)\left(1+\dfrac{1\text{ k}\Omega}{8\text{ k}\Omega}\right)}{1+120+\dfrac{1\text{ k}\Omega}{4\text{ k}\Omega}} = \frac{121 \times 1.125}{121 + 0.25} = \frac{136.125}{121.25}
$$

$$
S \approx 1.123
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Comment on Thermal Stability:**

A Stability Factor of **S Ōēł 1.12** is **excellent** (ideal value is 1, meaning absolute independence from I_CO). This implies that for every 1 ╬╝A change in leakage current I_CO, I_C changes by only 1.12 ╬╝A. The circuit is **highly thermally stable** and suitable for operation across wide temperature ranges.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify the Approximate Method Condition:**

Check **╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé**:

$$
\beta \cdot R_E = 120 \times 1\text{ k}\Omega = 120\text{ k}\Omega
$$

$$
10 \cdot R_2 = 10 \times 4\text{ k}\Omega = 40\text{ k}\Omega
$$

Since **120 k╬® Ōēź 40 k╬®**, the condition is **satisfied** and the approximate method **is applicable**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Approximate Method Verification:**

$$
I_{CQ,approx} = \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{5-0.7}{1\text{ k}\Omega} = 4.3\text{ mA}
$$

$$
V_{CEQ,approx} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C+R_E) = 15 - 4.3(2.5) = 15 - 10.75 = 4.25\text{ V}
$$

The approximate result **(4.3 mA, 4.25 V)** is very close to the exact result **(4.172 mA, 4.535 V)**, with errors under **4%**, confirming the validity of the approximation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Stability Factor **S** of a Voltage Divider Bias circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a Voltage Divider Bias circuit, **V_CC = 20 V, RŌéü = 10 k╬®, RŌéé = 5 k╬®, R_C = 2 k╬®, R_E = 1.5 k╬®, ╬▓ = 100, V_BE = 0.7 V**. Calculate the Q-point, the Stability Factor, and the maximum peak-to-peak **unclipped output swing** that can be obtained at the collector. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. The key steps are:

1. Start from the collector equation: $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$.
2. Apply KVL on the base-emitter loop: $V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + (I_C + I_B) R_E$.
3. Solve for $I_B$ in terms of $I_C$.
4. Differentiate the collector equation partially with respect to $I_{CO}$, treating $V_{BE}$ as constant.
5. Solve the resulting simultaneous equation to isolate $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$.
6. Substitute $R_{TH} = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ to obtain the VDB-specific form.

**Final derived expression:**

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{(1+\beta)\left(1+\dfrac{R_E}{R_1}\right)}{1+\beta+\dfrac{R_E}{R_2}}}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute V_TH and R_TH:**

$$
V_{TH} = 20 \cdot \frac{5}{10+5} = 20 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 6.667\text{ V}
$$

$$
R_{TH} = \frac{10 \times 5}{15}\text{ k}\Omega = 3.333\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute I_B (Exact):**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E} = \frac{6.667-0.7}{3.333 + (101)(1.5)\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{5.967}{154.833\text{ k}\Omega}
$$

$$
I_B \approx 38.53\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute I_CQ and V_CEQ:**

$$
I_{CQ} = 100 \times 38.53\ \mu\text{A} = 3.853\text{ mA}
$$

$$
I_E = 101 \times 38.53\ \mu\text{A} \approx 3.892\text{ mA}
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} = 20 - (3.853)(2) - (3.892)(1.5) = 20 - 7.706 - 5.838 = 6.456\text{ V}
$$

**Q-Point: (3.853 mA, 6.456 V)**

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the Stability Factor S:**

$$
S = \frac{(101)\left(1+\dfrac{1.5}{10}\right)}{101+\dfrac{1.5}{5}} = \frac{101 \times 1.15}{101+0.3} = \frac{116.15}{101.3} \approx 1.147
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute the Maximum Unclipped Output Swing:**

For a CE amplifier with R_E bypassed by C_E, the maximum symmetrical peak-to-peak output swing is limited by **twice the minimum of V_CEQ and (V_CC ŌłÆ V_CEQ)**:

$$
V_{CEQ} = 6.456\text{ V}, \quad V_{CC}-V_{CEQ} = 20 - 6.456 = 13.544\text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{opp,max} = 2 \times \min(6.456,\ 13.544) = 2 \times 6.456 = 12.912\text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since the Q-point is closer to **V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V** than to **V_CC**, the swing is limited by the lower side. For **maximum undistorted swing**, the design should target **V_CEQ = V_CC/2 = 10 V**, which would have required choosing different R_C, R_E, or RŌéü/RŌéé values.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 Scheme exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the (╬▓+1) factor in I_E:** Many students write I_E = ╬▓┬ĘI_B, losing 1 mark. The correct relation is **I_E = (╬▓+1)┬ĘI_B**.
> 2. **Using approximate method without verification:** The condition **╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé** must be explicitly checked and stated. Otherwise, full marks are not awarded.
> 3. **Confusing V_CE(sat) and V_CEQ:** V_CE(sat) is the saturation voltage (~0.2 V), while V_CEQ is the actual operating voltage at quiescence.
> 4. **Incorrect Thevenin conversion:** R_TH = RŌéü Ōłź RŌéé (parallel), **not** RŌéü + RŌéé. This is a frequent error costing 2 marks.
> 5. **Sign error in V_CE formula:** The correct form is **V_CE = V_CC ŌłÆ I_C┬ĘR_C ŌłÆ I_E┬ĘR_E**, with all voltage drops subtracted from the supply.
> 6. **Units mismatch:** Express all currents in mA (or ╬╝A) and resistances in k╬® consistently to avoid decimal errors.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for the night before the exam!):**
>
> - **VDB = Voltage Divider Bias = Universal Bias** ŌĆö most stable BJT biasing scheme.
> - **Two analysis methods:** Exact (Thevenin) and Approximate (use only when ╬▓┬ĘR_E Ōēź 10┬ĘRŌéé).
> - **Thevenin formulas:** $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2)$, $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$.
> - **Base current exact:** $I_B = (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / [R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E]$.
> - **Collector current exact:** $I_C = \beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / [R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E]$.
> - **Approximate collector current:** $I_C \approx (V_{TH} - V_{BE}) / R_E$.
> - **V_CEQ formula:** $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$.
> - **Stability Factor for VDB:** $S = (1+\beta)(1 + R_E/R_1) / (1+\beta + R_E/R_2)$.
> - **Stability Factor for Emitter Bias only:** $S = 1$ (ideal thermal stability).
> - **Stability Factor for Fixed Bias:** $S = 1+\beta$ (worst case, most unstable).
> - **Design rule for ╬▓-independence:** $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_2$.
> - **For maximum AC swing:** design the Q-point at $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$.
> - **V_BE for silicon BJT in active region:** 0.7 V (use 0 V for ideal approximation).
> - **V_CE(sat) for silicon BJT:** approximately 0.2 V.
> - **Function of C_E (emitter bypass capacitor):** shorts R_E at AC signal frequencies to maintain high AC gain, while R_E still provides DC stability.
> - **Function of R_E without bypass capacitor:** provides both DC and AC negative feedback (used in emitter-follower / common-collector configurations for high input impedance).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bias Stabilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bias Stabilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Bias Stabilization** is the set of circuit design techniques used in BJT/FET amplifier configurations to make the **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** ŌĆö the no-signal collector current $I_{CQ}$ and collectorŌĆōemitter voltage $V_{CEQ}$ ŌĆö *insensitive* to unavoidable variations in three key device parameters:

1. Reverse saturation current $I_{CBO}$ (also called $I_{CO}$ or $I_{S}$)
2. BaseŌĆōemitter voltage $V_{BE}$
3. Current amplification factor $\beta$ (i.e., $h_{FE}$)

The **Stability Factor $S$** is formally defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to $I_{CBO}$, keeping $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ constant:

$$
S \;=\; \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}\right|_{V_{BE},\,\beta \;=\; \text{const}}
$$

An ideal bias network has $S = 1$ (any change in $I_{CBO}$ produces an equal change in $I_C$, no extra amplification of the drift).

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Stability Factors** (for complete thermal analysis):
> - $S' = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\bigg\vert_{\beta,\,I_{CBO}}$  ŌåÆ sensitivity to $V_{BE}$ drift
> - $S'' = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta}\bigg\vert_{V_{BE},\,I_{CBO}}$  ŌåÆ sensitivity to $\beta$ spread
>
> Total drift equation:
> $$\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial T} \;=\; S\,\frac{\partial I_{CBO}}{\partial T} \;+\; S'\,\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} \;+\; S''\,\frac{\partial \beta}{\partial T}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Cruise Control of a Transistor"

Imagine you are driving a manual-transmission car and want to cruise at a steady 60 km/h. Three things disturb your speed:

- A gentle uphill slope (analogous to a rise in **temperature** increasing $I_{CBO}$)
- A headwind that varies (analogous to **$\beta$** differing from one transistor to the next ŌĆö even of the same batch)
- The fuel quality changing slightly (analogous to a small drop in $V_{BE}$ due to aging)

A **cruise control** senses the actual speed and *automatically adjusts* the throttle to maintain 60 km/h. Similarly, a **self-bias circuit** senses the actual $I_C$ via the voltage drop across $R_E$ and *automatically counteracts* any drift ŌĆö a built-in electronic cruise control for the Q-point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Point:** Without bias stabilization, the Q-point may drift so far that the transistor either **saturates** (cuts off the negative half-cycle) or **cuts off** (cuts off the positive half-cycle) ŌĆö producing **clipping distortion** in the output waveform. In the worst case, the device enters **thermal runaway** and is destroyed.

## 1.3 Why Is Stabilization Required? (The Three Enemies)

| # | Parameter | Typical Drift | Why it Happens |
|---|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $I_{CBO}$ | **Doubles every 10 ┬░C** | Thermal generation of minority carriers |
| 2 | $V_{BE}$ | **ŌłÆ2.5 mV/┬░C** | Bandgap shrinkage with temperature |
| 3 | $\beta$ | **┬▒50 %** unit-to-unit | Manufacturing spread |

Without stabilization, a 50 ┬░C rise can multiply $I_C$ by an order of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Point Concept

A BJT in the **active region** must operate at a fixed $I_{CQ}$ and $V_{CEQ}$ so that the input signal swings symmetrically on the load line. The DC condition is governed by:

$$
I_C \;=\; \beta I_B \;+\; (\beta+1)\,I_{CBO}
$$

The collector circuit gives (KVL):

$$
V_{CE} \;=\; V_{CC} \;-\; I_C R_C
$$

Any drift in $\beta$, $I_{CBO}$, or $V_{BE}$ shifts $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ along the load line.

## 2.2 The Four Standard Biasing Topologies

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically tests four biasing networks in order of increasing stability:

1. **Fixed Bias** (base bias) ŌĆö *no stabilization* ŌåÆ $S = \beta+1$
2. **Collector-to-Base Bias** ŌĆö partial stabilization via DC feedback
3. **Emitter / Self-Bias** ŌĆö stabilization through unbypassed $R_E$
4. **Voltage-Divider Bias** ŌĆö *best stability* (industry standard)

## 2.3 Thevenin Reduction Trick (used in Voltage-Divider Bias)

The base side is replaced by a Thevenin equivalent before applying KVL:

$$
V_{TH} \;=\; V_{CC}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \qquad\qquad R_{TH} \;=\; \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

The reduced circuit then behaves *exactly* like a self-bias network, so its stability factor is the same formula with $R_B \to R_{TH}$ and $V_{CC} \to V_{TH}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade** and form the bulk of 14-mark derivations.

### Table 1 ŌĆö Stability Factors of Common BJT Bias Circuits

| # | Bias Circuit | $S = \partial I_C/\partial I_{CBO}$ | $S' = \partial I_C/\partial V_{BE}$ | $S'' = \partial I_C/\partial \beta$ (approx.) |
|---|--------------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Fixed Bias | $\beta+1$ | $-\dfrac{\beta}{R_B}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}}{R_B}$ |
| 2 | Collector-to-Base | $\dfrac{\beta+1}{1+\beta R_C/R_B}$ | $-\dfrac{\beta}{R_B+\beta R_C}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}}{R_B+\beta R_C}$ |
| 3 | Self-Bias | $\dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_B+R_E)}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}$ | $-\dfrac{\beta}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}$ | $\dfrac{(V_{CC}-V_{BE})(R_B+R_E)}{[R_B+(\beta+1)R_E]^2}+I_{CBO}$ |
| 4 | Voltage-Divider | $\dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH}+R_E)}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E}$ | $-\dfrac{\beta}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E}$ | Same form, with $R_B \to R_{TH}$ |

### Table 2 ŌĆö Key Bias Current / Voltage Relations

| Quantity | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage-Divider |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| $I_B$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}-(\beta+1)R_E I_{CBO}}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}$ | $\dfrac{V_{TH}-V_{BE}-(\beta+1)R_E I_{CBO}}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E}$ |
| $I_C$ | $\beta I_B + (\beta+1)I_{CBO}$ | $\beta I_B + (\beta+1)I_{CBO}$ | $\beta I_B + (\beta+1)I_{CBO}$ |
| $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CC}-I_C R_C$ | $V_{CC}-I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ | $V_{CC}-I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ |

### Table 3 ŌĆö Limit Behaviour of $S$

| Condition | Fixed Bias | Self-Bias | Voltage-Divider |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| $\beta$ very large | $S \to \infty$ | $S \to (R_B/R_E + 1)$ | $S \to 1$ (ideal) |
| $R_E$ very large | N/A | $S \to 1$ | $S \to 1$ |
| Worst observed $S$ in practice | 50ŌĆō250 | 5ŌĆō20 | 1.1ŌĆō5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Voltage-divider bias is preferred when $S \le 10$ is required (most amplifier stages). Self-bias is acceptable when $S \le 50$ is tolerable (e.g., switching circuits).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers** (Class-A pre-amp): Voltage-divider bias with $R_E$ partially bypassed by a capacitor ŌĆö small $S$ prevents crossover distortion.
- **DC amplifiers and instrumentation:** The lower the $S$, the smaller the *thermal drift* in the output.
- **Switching and digital logic:** Moderate $S$ (5ŌĆō20) is acceptable; self-bias suffices and saves one resistor.
- **Power amplifiers (Class-B push-pull):** Bias stabilization is critical near $V_{CE} \approx 0$ to avoid thermal runaway.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Each derivation is given a clear logical flow. **Read line-by-line** ŌĆö the *text rows* explain the *algebra*.

## 3.1 Derivation of $S$, $S'$, $S''$ ŌĆö Fixed Bias Circuit

**Circuit:** $V_{CC}$ ŌĆö $R_B$ ŌĆö Base; Collector tied through $R_C$ to $V_{CC}$; Emitter to ground.

### Step 1 ŌĆö KVL on BaseŌĆōEmitter Loop
The base current is set solely by the supply and base resistor:

$$
V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0
\;\;\Longrightarrow\;\;
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

*This equation assumes $V_{BE}$ is a fixed constant during partial differentiation.*

### Step 2 ŌĆö Collector-Current Equation
Substitute $I_B$ into the standard BJT relation $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$:

$$
I_C = \beta\!\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}\right) + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$
Holding $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ constant, the only $I_{CBO}$-dependent term is the last one:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = (\beta+1)
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $S' = \partial I_C / \partial V_{BE}$
Only the first term contains $V_{BE}$:

$$
S' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = -\frac{\beta}{R_B}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $S'' = \partial I_C / \partial \beta$
Both terms depend on $\beta$:

$$
S'' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} + I_{CBO}
$$

In the **active region** $I_{CBO}$ is small (nano-amps), so we approximate:

$$
S'' \approx \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \approx I_B
$$

> **Conclusion:** For fixed bias, $S = \beta+1$ is *unbounded* with $\beta$ ŌĆö explaining why this circuit is rarely used in production designs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $S$ ŌĆö Collector-to-Base Bias Circuit

**Circuit:** Base is connected to the **collector node** (not $V_{CC}$) through $R_B$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö KVL on BaseŌĆōCollectorŌĆōEmitter Loop
Going from $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$ to the collector node, then through $R_B$ to the base, then through $V_{BE}$ to ground:

$$
V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Express $I_B$ in terms of $I_C$ and $I_{CBO}$
From $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$:

$$
I_B = \frac{I_C - (\beta+1) I_{CBO}}{\beta}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute into KVL
$$
V_{CC} - V_{BE} = I_C R_C + \frac{R_B}{\beta}\!\left[I_C - (\beta+1) I_{CBO}\right]
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Collect $I_C$ terms
$$
V_{CC} - V_{BE} = I_C\!\left(R_C + \frac{R_B}{\beta}\right) - \frac{R_B(\beta+1) I_{CBO}}{\beta}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $I_C$
$$
I_C = \frac{\beta(V_{CC} - V_{BE}) + R_B(\beta+1) I_{CBO}}{\beta R_C + R_B}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$ (with $V_{BE}$, $\beta$ fixed)
$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \frac{(\beta+1) R_B}{\beta R_C + R_B}
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Simplify
Dividing numerator and denominator by $R_B$:

$$
S = \frac{\beta+1}{1 + \beta R_C / R_B}
$$

**Interpretation:** Making $R_C / R_B$ large (i.e., small $R_B$ relative to $R_C$) drives $S$ toward 1. The negative DC feedback (a fraction of the output is fed back to the input) is what improves stability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of $S$ ŌĆö Self-Bias (Emitter-Bias) Circuit

**Circuit:** Base tied to $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$; emitter tied to ground through $R_E$ (no bypass capacitor for DC).

### Step 1 ŌĆö KVL around the BaseŌĆōEmitterŌĆō$R_E$ Loop
$$
V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Relate $I_E$ to $I_B$ and $I_{CBO}$
$$
I_E = (\beta+1) I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute
$$
V_{CC} - V_{BE} = I_B R_B + \left[(\beta+1) I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}\right] R_E
$$

$$
V_{CC} - V_{BE} = I_B\!\left[R_B + (\beta+1) R_E\right] + (\beta+1) R_E\, I_{CBO}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $I_B$
$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE} - (\beta+1) R_E\, I_{CBO}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute back into $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$
$$
I_C = \frac{\beta\!\left[V_{CC} - V_{BE} - (\beta+1) R_E\, I_{CBO}\right]}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E} + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$
$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = -\frac{\beta(\beta+1) R_E}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E} + (\beta+1)
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Factor out $(\beta+1)$
$$
S = (\beta+1)\!\left[1 - \frac{\beta R_E}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}\right]
$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Combine the bracket
$$
S = (\beta+1)\,\frac{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E - \beta R_E}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E} = (\beta+1)\,\frac{R_B + R_E}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

### Final Result
$$
\boxed{\,S \;=\; \frac{(\beta+1)(R_B + R_E)}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}\,}
$$

> **Limiting cases:**
> - $R_E = 0$ (emitter grounded) ŌåÆ $S = \beta+1$ (recovers fixed-bias result Ō£ō)
> - $R_E \to \infty$ ŌåÆ $S \to 1$ (ideal) Ō£ō
> - $\beta \to \infty$, with $R_B, R_E$ fixed ŌåÆ $S \to (R_B/R_E + 1)$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of $S$ ŌĆö Voltage-Divider Bias (via Thevenin)

**Circuit:** $R_1$ from $V_{CC}$ to base; $R_2$ from base to ground; $R_C$ collector; $R_E$ emitter.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Thevenin Equivalent at the Base Node
$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \qquad\qquad R_{TH} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Replace Base Network by Thevenin
The circuit becomes **identical** in topology to a self-bias circuit with $R_B \to R_{TH}$ and $V_{CC} \to V_{TH}$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the Self-Bias Result
$$
S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Limiting Behaviour
For large $\beta$, $R_{TH} \ll (\beta+1) R_E$ (typical design), so:

$$
S \to \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{(\beta+1) R_E} = 1
$$

> **Why is VDB so much better?** Because $R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2$ is naturally *small* (both $R_1, R_2$ are designed low for good DC bias), whereas a single $R_B$ in self-bias is constrained by the supply voltage.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (Design-Validation)

**Problem.** A silicon BJT with $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V is to be biased at $I_{CQ} = 2$ mA from $V_{CC} = 12$ V using **voltage-divider bias**. $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 3$ k$\Omega$. Compute $S$ and design $R_1$, $R_2$ such that $S \le 5$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Required $R_{TH}$ for $S \le 5$
$$
5 \;\ge\; \frac{(101)(R_{TH} + 1\,\text{k})}{R_{TH} + 101\,\text{k}}
$$

Solving gives $R_{TH} \le 4.0$ k$\Omega$ (rounded).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Choose $I_2 \approx 10 I_B$ (rule of thumb)
$$
I_B \approx \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{2\,\text{mA}}{100} = 20\,\mu\text{A}
$$
$$
I_2 \approx 200\,\mu\text{A} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_1 + R_2 = \frac{12\,\text{V}}{200\,\mu\text{A}} = 60\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $V_{TH}$
$$
V_{TH} = V_{BE} + I_E R_E = 0.7 + 2\,\text{mA}\times 1\,\text{k}\Omega = 2.7\,\text{V}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Use Voltage Divider Relation
$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = \frac{2.7}{12} = 0.225
$$

So $R_2 = 0.225 \times 60\,\text{k} = 13.5$ k$\Omega$ and $R_1 = 46.5$ k$\Omega$.

### Step 5 ŌĆö Verify $R_{TH}$
$$
