R_{TH} = \frac{46.5 \times 13.5}{46.5 + 13.5} \approx 10.47\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

So $S = \frac{101 \times (10.47+1)}{10.47 + 101} \approx 9.5$. To meet $S\le 5$ we need a lower $I_2/I_B$ ratio (e.g., $I_2 = 50 I_B$):

- $I_2 = 1$ mA ŌåÆ $R_1+R_2 = 12$ k$\Omega$ ŌåÆ $R_1 = 9.3$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 2.7$ k$\Omega$ ŌåÆ $R_{TH} = 2.1$ k$\Omega$ ŌåÆ $S \approx 3.2$ Ō£ō

This illustrates the *iteration* KTU examiners love to test.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    factors --> betaVar["beta variation"]

    factors --> vbeVar["V_BE shift"]
    betaVar --> chooseQ{Q2 Acceptable S value?}
    tempVar --> chooseQ
    vbeVar --> chooseQ
    chooseQ -->|S less than 250 ok| fixB["Fixed Bias  S equals beta plus 1"]
    chooseQ -->|S less than 50| cB["Collector to Base Bias"]
    chooseQ -->|S less than 20| sB["Self Bias  uses R_E feedback"]
    chooseQ -->|S less than 5 best| vdb["Voltage Divider Bias  ideal S near 1"]
    fixB --> recap["All feed back to a single goal  stable Q point"]
    cB --> recap
    sB --> recap
    vdb --> recap
```

## 4.2 Voltage-Divider Bias ŌĆö Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vccNode["VCC supply rail"] --> r1Node["R1  upper divider"]
    vccNode --> r2Node["R2  lower divider to GND"]
    r1Node --> joinNode["Base node  VTH RTH appear here"]
    r2Node --> joinNode
    joinNode --> thevNode["Thevenin block  VTH VCC R2 over R1 plus R2"]
    thevNode --> bjtNode["BJT base terminal"]
    bjtNode --> emitNode["Emitter terminal"]
    emitNode --> reNode["R_E  DC negative feedback"]
    reNode --> gndNode["Ground or negative rail"]

    rcNode --> vccNode
    collNode --> outNode["Vout  amplified AC signal"]
```

## 4.3 The Three Feedback Loops in One Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    param["Disturbance: beta up, I_CBO up, V_BE shift"] --> shift["I_C tries to increase"]
    shift --> senseR["Voltage drop across R_E rises  V_E increases"]
    senseR --> vbeReduce["V_BE effective  V_B minus V_E  shrinks"]
    vbeReduce --> ibDrop["I_B drops  because V_BE fell"]
    ibDrop --> icDrop["I_C drops back toward design value"]
    icDrop --> stableNode["Q-point restored  stable operating condition"]
    stableNode -.feedback closed.-> shift

    style param fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style stableNode fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#339966
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this in an exam:** Trace the arrows in words ŌĆö "a rise in $I_C$ creates a larger $V_E$ which subtracts from $V_B$ to reduce $V_{BE}$, which reduces $I_B$, which reduces $I_C$ back" ŌĆö this is **negative feedback** in DC form.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Define the stability factor $S$ in a transistor biasing circuit. Why is it important to keep $S$ as close to unity as possible?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stability factor $S$ is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CBO}$, while keeping $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ constant:

$$
S = \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}\right|_{V_{BE},\,\beta = \text{const}}
$$

$S$ quantifies the **sensitivity of the Q-point to temperature and unit-to-unit $\beta$ variations**. If $S$ is large, even a small increase in $I_{CBO}$ (which doubles every 10 ┬░C) is amplified and produces a large change in $I_C$, shifting the Q-point toward saturation or cutoff. If $S = 1$, the Q-point is *insensitive* ŌĆö a 1 ┬ĄA rise in $I_{CBO}$ produces only a 1 ┬ĄA rise in $I_C$ ŌĆö guaranteeing linear, distortion-free amplification and preventing thermal runaway. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**List any three BJT biasing circuits and state the stability-factor expression for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Bias Circuit | Stability Factor $S$ |
|---|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Fixed Bias (Base Bias) | $S = \beta+1$ |
| 2 | Collector-to-Base Bias | $S = \dfrac{\beta+1}{1+\beta R_C/R_B}$ |
| 3 | Self-Bias (Emitter Bias) | $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_B+R_E)}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}$ |
| 4 | Voltage-Divider Bias | $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH}+R_E)}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E}$ (same form as self-bias, with $R_B \to R_{TH}$) |

Among these, **voltage-divider bias** offers the lowest $S$ and is the industry standard. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Convention:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks divided as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

### Ō£ģ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** For the **fixed-bias** BJT circuit, derive expressions for the three stability factors $S$, $S'$, and $S''$. State the result of $S$ and comment on its stability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **self-bias (emitter-bias)** BJT circuit, derive the expression for $S$ step-by-step. What are the conditions to make $S$ approach unity? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Given:** BJT in CE configuration, $V_{CC}$ connected to collector through $R_C$, base connected to $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$, emitter grounded.

**Step 1 ŌĆö KVL on input loop:**
$$
V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Collector current:**
$$
I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO} = \beta \frac{V_{CC}-V_{BE}}{R_B} + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $S$:**
$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \beta+1
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute $S'$:**
$$
S' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = -\frac{\beta}{R_B}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute $S''$ (neglecting the small $I_{CBO}$ term):**
$$
S'' \approx \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Since $S = \beta+1$ grows without bound as $\beta$ increases, the fixed-bias circuit is **highly unstable**. A temperature rise that increases $I_{CBO}$ is amplified $\beta+1$ times in $I_C$, leading to Q-point shift and possible thermal runaway. Hence, fixed bias is rarely used in production amplifiers.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Given:** BJT with $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$ to base, $R_E$ from emitter to ground, $R_C$ from $V_{CC}$ to collector.

**Step 1 ŌĆö KVL on baseŌĆōemitter loop:**
$$
V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$:**
$$
V_{CC} - V_{BE} = I_B[R_B + (\beta+1) R_E] + (\beta+1) R_E\, I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $I_B$:**
$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE} - (\beta+1) R_E I_{CBO}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute into $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$:**
$$
I_C = \frac{\beta(V_{CC}-V_{BE})}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E} + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}\!\left[1 - \frac{\beta R_E}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}\right]
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Differentiate w.r.t. $I_{CBO}$:**
$$
S = (\beta+1)\,\frac{R_B + R_E}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Conditions for $S \to 1$:** (i) $R_E \to \infty$, (ii) $R_B \ll R_E$ in the large-$\beta$ limit, (iii) $\beta$ large with $R_{TH}/R_E$ small.

> **Conclusion:** $S$ is *bounded* and can be driven close to 1 by choosing $R_E$ sufficiently large (typically $R_E \ge 10 \cdot R_C$ is impractical, so a compromise value is used).

---

### Ō£ģ QUESTION B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, derive the stability factor $S$ of the **voltage-divider bias** circuit using Thevenin's theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **thermal runaway**? Derive the condition for thermal stability in a BJT and explain how self-bias prevents it. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Draw the circuit:** $R_1$ from $V_{CC}$ to base, $R_2$ from base to ground, $R_C$ from $V_{CC}$ to collector, $R_E$ from emitter to ground.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Thevenize the base network:**
$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}, \qquad R_{TH} = R_1 \,\Vert\, R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The reduced circuit is topologically identical to a self-bias circuit** with $R_B \to R_{TH}$ and $V_{CC} \to V_{TH}$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the self-bias result directly:**
$$
S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Limiting behaviour:** For typical designs $R_{TH} \ll (\beta+1) R_E$, giving:

$$
S \to \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{(\beta+1) R_E} = 1
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Numerical check:** With $\beta = 100$, $R_{TH} = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$:

$$
S = \frac{101 \times 3}{2 + 101} \approx 2.94
$$

(compared to $S = 101$ for fixed bias with the same $\beta$ ŌĆö a **~34├Ś improvement**).

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Definition (2 marks):** *Thermal runaway* is a self-reinforcing destructive cycle in a BJT where an increase in junction temperature $T_J$ increases $I_{CBO}$ (and hence $I_C$), which increases power dissipation $P_D = V_{CE} I_C$, which further increases $T_J$ ŌĆö eventually destroying the device.

**Derivation of the stability condition (4 marks):**

Heat balance: $T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA}$, where $\theta_{JA}$ is the junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (┬░C/W).

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{\partial T_J}{\partial T_J} = 1 = \theta_{JA}\,\frac{\partial P_D}{\partial T_J}
$$

For stability we need:

$$
\theta_{JA}\,\frac{\partial P_D}{\partial T_J} \;<\; 1
$$

or equivalently

$$
\frac{\partial P_D}{\partial T_J} \;<\; \frac{1}{\theta_{JA}}
$$

Now, $P_D = V_{CE} I_C$:

$$
\frac{\partial P_D}{\partial T_J} = V_{CE}\,\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial T_J} + I_C\,\frac{\partial V_{CE}}{\partial T_J}
$$

Ignoring the small $V_{CE}$ term and using $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$:

$$
\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial T_J} \approx S\,\frac{\partial I_{CBO}}{\partial T_J} \approx 0.07\,S\,I_{CBO}
$$

(using the rule of thumb that $I_{CBO}$ doubles per 10 ┬░C, i.e., $\partial I_{CBO}/\partial T \approx 0.07 I_{CBO}$).

**Stability condition** becomes:
$$
0.07\,S\,I_{CBO}\,V_{CE}\,\theta_{JA} \;<\; 1
$$

**How self-bias prevents runaway (1 mark):** The unbypassed $R_E$ provides *DC negative feedback*. As $I_C$ rises with temperature, $V_E = I_E R_E$ rises, which *reduces* $V_{BE}$ and hence $I_B$, counteracting the rise. This is mathematically expressed by $S$ being small ŌĆö typically $S \le 10$ ŌĆö which makes the runaway inequality easily satisfied.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in bias-stabilization questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(\beta+1)$ factor** in the $I_{CBO}$ term of $I_C$. The correct expression is $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$, *not* $\beta I_B + I_{CBO}$. Marks are routinely docked 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Differentiating the wrong variable.** $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$ means $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ must be **held constant** during differentiation. Students often incorrectly take the total derivative.
> 3. **Mixing up $R_B$ and $R_{TH}$.** Voltage-divider bias has $R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2$, not $R_1 + R_2$ or just $R_2$.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** KTU examiners *require* units (V, mA, k$\Omega$) in every numerical answer line.
> 5. **Not stating the design assumption** $I_2 \gg I_B$ (rule of thumb) in voltage-divider design problems.
> 6. **Skipping the limiting-case discussion.** Always state "as $\beta \to \infty$, $S \to 1$" or "as $R_E \to \infty$, $S \to 1$" ŌĆö this single line is worth 1 mark and many students miss it.
> 7. **In thermal-runaway problems**, writing $\partial P_C / \partial T_J$ instead of $\partial P_D / \partial T_J$ (the dissipated, not the consumed, power ŌĆö the inequality is about the *rate of change of dissipation*).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ­¤ōī One-Paragraph Summary

> Bias stabilization makes the Q-point of a BJT insensitive to **temperature**, **$\beta$ spread**, and **$V_{BE}$ drift**. The figure of merit is the **stability factor** $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$, with $S = 1$ being the ideal (any drift in $I_{CBO}$ produces an equal drift in $I_C$, no amplification). Four standard circuits ŌĆö fixed, collector-to-base, self-bias, and voltage-divider ŌĆö span $S$ values from $\beta+1$ down to nearly 1. Self-bias and voltage-divider bias use **DC negative feedback** through an unbypassed $R_E$. Thevenin reduction lets you derive the VDB result from the self-bias result with a simple $R_B \to R_{TH}$ substitution. Thermal runaway is the catastrophic failure mode that bias stabilization is designed to prevent.

### ­¤Ä» Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$ ŌĆö *memorize the $(\beta+1)$!*
- [ ] $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO} = \beta+1$ for **fixed bias** (worst)
- [ ] $S = \dfrac{\beta+1}{1+\beta R_C/R_B}$ for **collector-to-base bias**
- [ ] $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_B+R_E)}{R_B+(\beta+1)R_E}$ for **self-bias**
- [ ] $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH}+R_E)}{R_{TH}+(\beta+1)R_E}$ for **voltage-divider** with $R_{TH} = R_1 \Vert R_2$
- [ ] $V_{TH} = V_{CC}\,R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ ŌĆö voltage-divider Thevenin
- [ ] $I_{CBO}$ doubles every 10 ┬░C; $V_{BE}$ decreases by ~2.5 mV/┬░C
- [ ] For $S \le 5$: use voltage-divider with $R_{TH} \le 0.1 R_E$ (large $\beta$)
- [ ] $I_{CBO}$ filter: $I_{CBO}(\text{Ge}) \approx 10\times$ $I_{CBO}(\text{Si})$
- [ ] Thermal stability condition: $\theta_{JA} \,\partial P_D/\partial T_J < 1$
- [ ] $S$ ranges: fixed (50ŌĆō250), CŌĆōB (5ŌĆō30), self (2ŌĆō15), VDB (1ŌĆō5)
- [ ] **Voltage-divider bias is the universal industry choice** for linear amplifiers
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Circuits: Astable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching Circuits: Astable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Astable Multivibrator** is a free-running, regenerative electronic oscillator circuit that possesses **no stable state**. It continuously alternates between two **quasi-stable (temporary) states** without any external triggering pulse, producing a **rectangular (square) wave** output. The term "astable" literally means "a-stable" (not stable), indicating that the circuit is inherently unstable and perpetually switches between two transistors conducting states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The astable multivibrator is categorized under *Switching Circuits* in Module 1 of **PEEET417 - Solid State Devices**. It forms the foundational building block for understanding relaxation oscillators, clock generators, and pulse generation circuits in digital and analog systems.

## 1.2 Circuit Topology (At a Glance)

The classical astable multivibrator employs:
- **Two cross-coupled BJT transistors** (QŌéü and QŌéé) in symmetrical or asymmetrical configuration.
- **Two coupling capacitors** (CŌéü and CŌéé) connecting the collector of one transistor to the base of the other.
- **Two base resistors** (R_B1 and R_B2) connecting bases to the supply voltage V_CC (providing bias).
- **Two collector resistors** (R_C1 and R_C2) limiting collector currents.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The Seesaw / Tilt-Bucket)

Imagine two buckets connected at their bases by a pivot (a seesaw). When you pour water into Bucket A, it becomes heavy and tips down. The pivot motion causes Bucket B to flip up, which immediately drains its water out (through a bottom hole). Once empty, the now-lighter Bucket B returns to the down position, causing Bucket A to flip and empty. This perpetual tipping-draining cycle is exactly what happens in an astable multivibrator:

- The "water level" represents the **base charge** of each transistor.
- The "tipping" represents the **transistor switching ON/OFF**.
- The "draining" represents the **capacitor discharge** through the base resistor.
- The cycle **never stops** on its own ŌĆö hence *astable*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "quasi-stable"?** Each state (QŌéü ON, QŌéé OFF) or (QŌéü OFF, QŌéé ON) is held only temporarily ŌĆö the duration is determined by the RC time constant. The circuit spontaneously leaves this state and enters the other, never settling permanently in either.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage (V_T)** = kT/q Ōēł **25.85 mV at 300 K (room temperature)**
- **Base-Emitter ON Voltage (V_BE(sat))** Ōēł **0.7 V** for silicon BJTs
- **Collector-Emitter Saturation Voltage (V_CE(sat))** Ōēł **0.2 V**
- **Output Amplitude** Ōēł **V_CC ŌłÆ V_CE(sat) Ōēł V_CC**
- **Typical Frequency Range:** **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio to RF pulse generation)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Phases ŌĆö The Heart of the Astable

The astable multivibrator operates in **two quasi-stable phases** that alternate continuously. Let us define the two phases explicitly:

### **Phase 1: QŌéü ON (Saturated), QŌéé OFF (Cut-off)**

- QŌéü saturated ŌåÆ V_CE1 = V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V ŌåÆ **Collector of QŌéü is LOW**.
- QŌéé cut-off ŌåÆ No collector current ŌåÆ V_C2 Ōēł V_CC ŌåÆ **Collector of QŌéé is HIGH**.
- CŌéé (connected between CŌéé node and BŌéü) charges through R_C2 toward V_CC.
- CŌéü (connected between CŌéü node and BŌéé) discharges through R_B1. As CŌéü discharges, the voltage at the base of QŌéé rises exponentially from ŌłÆV_CC toward +V_CC.
- When V_B2 crosses 0.7 V, QŌéé turns ON, triggering the regenerative switch.

### **Phase 2: QŌéé ON (Saturated), QŌéü OFF (Cut-off)**

This is the exact mirror image of Phase 1.
- QŌéé saturated ŌåÆ V_C2 is LOW.
- QŌéü cut-off ŌåÆ V_C1 is HIGH.
- CŌéü charges through R_C1.
- CŌéé discharges through R_B2, eventually turning QŌéü back ON.

## 2.2 Why Does the Switching Happen? (The Regenerative Loop)

The cross-coupling creates a **positive feedback loop**. If QŌéü's collector voltage rises by ╬öV, this rise is coupled via CŌéü to QŌéé's base, which increases I_C2, pulling QŌéé's collector down. This fall couples via CŌéé to QŌéü's base, which increases I_C1, pulling QŌéü's collector even higher. This **snowballs in microseconds** ŌĆö the speed of the transition is limited only by transistor transit times, not by RC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regenerative action is the reason the transitions are so sharp** (giving rectangular waveform), while the RC time constants control *how long* the circuit remains in each state.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|:-----------|:-------------|:------------|:----------|
| TŌéü | Time duration when QŌéü is OFF (QŌéé ON) | $T_1 = R_{B1} C_1 \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, R_{B1} C_1$ | seconds |
| TŌéé | Time duration when QŌéé is OFF (QŌéü ON) | $T_2 = R_{B2} C_2 \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, R_{B2} C_2$ | seconds |
| T | Total Time Period | $T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\,(R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2)$ | seconds |
| f | Frequency of Oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2}$ | Hz |
| D | Duty Cycle (% time QŌéü is OFF) | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{R_{B1} C_1}{R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2} \times 100\%$ | % |
| V_out | Peak Output Voltage | $V_{out} \approx V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx V_{CC}$ | Volts |
| V_B(min) | Base Voltage of OFF transistor (at switchover) | $V_{B(\text{min})} = -V_{CC} + 2 V_{BE(\text{sat})} \approx -V_{CC} + 1.4\,V$ | Volts |
| Žä | Time constant of each phase | $\tau = R_B \, C$ | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a SYMMETRIC astable multivibrator** (where R_B1 = R_B2 = R_B and CŌéü = CŌéé = C), the formulas simplify to:
> - $T = 2 \times 0.693 \, R_B C = 1.386 \, R_B C$
> - $f = \dfrac{1}{1.386 \, R_B C} = \dfrac{0.722}{R_B C}$
> - **Duty Cycle = 50%** (perfect square wave)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The astable multivibrator is the workhorse of:

- **Square-wave and pulse generators** in laboratory instruments (function generators).
- **Clock generation** in early digital logic systems and microcontroller sub-circuits.
- **LED flashers and indicator circuits** (e.g., bicycle turn signals, decorative lighting).
- **Tone generators** in simple electronic sirens and alarms.
- **Time-base generators** in CROs (Cathode Ray Oscilloscopes).
- **Voltage-to-Frequency converters** when modified (VCO behavior).
- **Missing pulse detectors** and **oscillator alarm circuits**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Trade-off:** Lower C values ŌåÆ higher frequency but susceptible to noise. Lower R values ŌåÆ higher frequency but increased power consumption. Typical design uses R_B in **k╬® to M╬®** and C in **pF to ╬╝F**.

## 2.5 Assumptions Made in the Standard Analysis

The classical 0.693 factor is derived under these assumptions:
1. V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0 (output swings rail-to-rail at collector).
2. V_BE(sat) Ōēł 0 (base voltage during ON is approximately ground).
3. The discharging capacitor voltage crosses the threshold at the moment of switching.
4. Transistor switching times are negligible compared to RC time constants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Time Period

We will derive the duration of Phase 1 (QŌéé ON, QŌéü OFF) ŌĆö labeled **TŌéü**. The derivation of TŌéé follows by symmetry.

### **Initial Conditions at the Start of Phase 1**

At the instant QŌéé just turned ON and QŌéü just turned OFF (i.e., the regenerative transition just completed):
- CŌéé is fully charged with the polarity: **plate connected to QŌéü's collector is at +V_CC** (since QŌéü is now OFF, its collector jumps to V_CC) and **plate connected to QŌéé's base is at V_BE(sat) Ōēł 0.7 V** (since QŌéé is ON).
- CŌéü is fully charged with the polarity: **plate connected to QŌéé's collector is at V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0 V** (QŌéé is ON) and **plate connected to QŌéü's base is at +V_CC ŌłÆ V_BE Ōēł V_CC** (was being charged previously).
- **Crucial Step:** When QŌéé's collector suddenly drops from V_CC to 0 V, the **voltage across CŌéü cannot change instantaneously**. Therefore, the base of QŌéü, which was at V_CC, suddenly drops to **V_B1(0+) = V_CC ŌłÆ V_CC = 0 V... wait, corrected**: The collector of QŌéé was at V_CC, now drops to 0. The other plate of CŌéü (at QŌéü's base) was at V_CC. The capacitor voltage was V_C1 = V_CC ŌłÆ 0 = V_CC. After the sudden drop, the base of QŌéü becomes:

$$
V_{B1}(0^+) = V_{C2}(\text{new}) - V_{C1}(\text{initial}) = 0 - V_{CC} = -V_{CC}
$$

So QŌéü's base is driven hard negative to **ŌłÆV_CC**, ensuring QŌéü stays OFF.

### **The Discharge Equation**

During Phase 1, QŌéü is OFF, QŌéé is ON. CŌéü discharges through R_B1 toward V_CC. The base voltage of QŌéü follows the standard capacitor-charging-via-R formula, but since it is discharging from ŌłÆV_CC toward +V_CC, the equation is:

$$
V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-V_{CC})) \cdot e^{-t / R_{B1} C_1}
$$

$$
V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t / R_{B1} C_1}
$$

We can verify: at t = 0, V_B1 = V_CC ŌłÆ 2V_CC = ŌłÆV_CC Ō£ō. As t ŌåÆ Ōł×, V_B1 ŌåÆ V_CC Ō£ō.

### **Finding the Switchover Time TŌéü**

QŌéü turns ON when V_B1(t) reaches the turn-on threshold V_BE(sat) Ōēł 0.7 V. For simplicity, the standard analysis approximates this threshold as 0 V (or absorbs V_BE(sat) into the constant ŌĆö exact KTU board answers use **0 V** for the crossover threshold):

$$
0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_1 / R_{B1} C_1}
$$

$$
2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_1 / R_{B1} C_1} = V_{CC}
$$

$$
e^{-T_1 / R_{B1} C_1} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
-\frac{T_1}{R_{B1} C_1} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)
$$

$$
T_1 = R_{B1} C_1 \ln(2)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_1 = 0.693 \, R_{B1} C_1}
$$

### **Deriving TŌéé by Symmetry**

By exchanging the subscripts (1 Ōåö 2) and applying the identical analysis to Phase 2:

$$
\boxed{T_2 = 0.693 \, R_{B2} C_2}
$$

### **Total Time Period and Frequency**

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\, R_{B1} C_1 + 0.693\, R_{B2} C_2
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 0.693\, (R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2)}
$$

$$
\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2}}
$$

### **The 0.693 and 1.44 Numerical Constants ŌĆö Where Do They Come From?**

- $\ln(2) = 0.693147...$ ŌĆö appears because the capacitor voltage must drop to **half** its initial swing (from ŌłÆV_CC toward +V_CC, crossing 0 at the halfway point).
- $1/0.693 = 1.4427...$ ŌĆö appears in the frequency formula as the reciprocal of the ln(2) factor when both RC terms are summed.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An astable multivibrator has R_B1 = R_B2 = 20 k╬®, CŌéü = CŌéé = 0.01 ╬╝F, V_CC = 12 V. Find the frequency, time period, and duty cycle.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the configuration** ŌĆö Since R_B1 = R_B2 and CŌéü = CŌéé, this is a **symmetric** astable. The duty cycle is therefore 50%.

**Step 2: Calculate Time Period**

$$
T = 1.386 \, R_B \, C = 1.386 \times (20 \times 10^3) \times (0.01 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
T = 1.386 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 1.386 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 2.772 \times 10^{-4} \, s = 277.2 \, \mu s}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Frequency**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2.772 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f \approx 3607 \, Hz \approx 3.61 \, kHz}
$$

**Step 4: Verify Duty Cycle**

$$
D = \frac{T_1}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.693 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}{2.772 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
D = \frac{1.386 \times 10^{-4}}{2.772 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

**Step 5: Output Amplitude**

$$
V_{out} \approx V_{CC} = 12 \, V \, (\text{peak-to-peak})
$$

## 3.3 Output Waveform Characteristics

The output waveform at the collector of QŌéü is a **rectangular wave** with:
- **HIGH level** Ōēł V_CC (when QŌéü is OFF).
- **LOW level** Ōēł V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V (when QŌéü is ON).
- **Rise time** determined by R_C1 and the parasitic capacitances of QŌéü.
- **Fall time** determined by the saturation storage time of QŌéü.
- **The leading edge is sharp** due to regenerative action; the trailing edge has a small overshoot due to the coupling capacitor's discharge.

## 3.4 Analysis of the Non-Symmetric (Asymmetric) Astable

When R_B1 CŌéü ŌēĀ R_B2 CŌéé, the output is **not** a 50% duty cycle square wave but a **rectangular pulse train** with unequal HIGH and LOW times.

**Example:** If R_B1 = 10 k╬®, CŌéü = 0.1 ╬╝F, R_B2 = 50 k╬®, CŌéé = 0.01 ╬╝F:

$$
T_1 = 0.693 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.693 \, ms
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.693 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 0.3465 \, ms
$$

$$
T = 1.0395 \, ms, \quad f = 962 \, Hz, \quad D = 66.7\%
$$

This asymmetric behavior is exploited in **pulse-position modulation** and **variable duty-cycle chopper** circuits.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Astable Multivibrator

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the astable multivibrator ŌĆö showing the cross-coupling, RC time-constant blocks, and switching feedback paths that produce the oscillation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC["DC Supply VCC"] --> RC1["Collector Resistor RC1"]
    VCC --> RC2["Collector Resistor RC2"]
    VCC --> RB1["Base Resistor RB1"]
    VCC --> RB2["Base Resistor RB2"]

    RC1 --> Q1C["Collector Node of Q1"]
    RC2 --> Q2C["Collector Node of Q2"]
    RB1 --> Q1B["Base Node of Q1"]
    RB2 --> Q2B["Base Node of Q2"]

    Q1C --> Q1["BJT Transistor Q1"]
    Q1B --> Q1
    Q1 --> GND1["Ground via Q1 Emitter"]

    Q2C --> Q2["BJT Transistor Q2"]
    Q2B --> Q2
    Q2 --> GND2["Ground via Q2 Emitter"]

    Q1C -- "Coupling Capacitor C1" --> Q2B
    Q2C -- "Coupling Capacitor C2" --> Q1B

    Q1C --> OUT1["Output Vout1 at Collector of Q1"]
    Q2C --> OUT2["Output Vout2 at Collector of Q2"]

    PHASE1["Phase 1 Timer Block: T1 = 0.693 RB1 C1"] -.controls.-> Q1
    PHASE2["Phase 2 Timer Block: T2 = 0.693 RB2 C2"] -.controls.-> Q2

    REGEN["Regenerative Feedback Loop Cross Coupling"] -.enforces.-> Q1
    REGEN -.enforces.-> Q2
```

## 4.2 State Transition Flow (Timing Diagram as a Flowchart)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["State A: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF"] -->|After time T2| T1["Regenerative Switch"]
    T1 --> S2["State B: Q1 OFF, Q2 ON"]
    S2 -->|After time T1| T2["Regenerative Switch"]
    T2 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Idealized Waveform Diagram (Conceptual ŌĆö Represented as a Block Flow)

Since a true waveform plot cannot be drawn in Mermaid, the following **functional block flow** maps the voltage levels at each key node as the circuit transitions:

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Phase1["PHASE 1: Duration T1 = 0.693 RB1 C1"]
        P1A["VC1: HIGH approx VCC"] --> P1B["VB2: Ramps up from -VCC toward VCC exponentially"]
        P1B --> P1C["VC2: LOW approx 0.2 V"]
        P1C --> P1D["VB1: VBE sat approx 0.7 V"]
    end

    subgraph Switch1["REGENERATIVE TRANSITION 1"]
        SW1A["VB2 crosses 0.7 V threshold"] --> SW1B["Q2 turns ON sharply"]
        SW1B --> SW1C["VC2 collapses to 0.2 V"]
        SW1C --> SW1D["C1 coupling drives VB1 hard negative to -VCC"]
        SW1D --> SW1E["Q1 turns OFF sharply, VC1 jumps to VCC"]
    end

    subgraph Phase2["PHASE 2: Duration T2 = 0.693 RB2 C2"]
        P2A["VC2: LOW approx 0.2 V"] --> P2B["VB1: Ramps up from -VCC toward VCC exponentially"]
        P2B --> P2C["VC1: HIGH approx VCC"]
        P2C --> P2D["VB2: VBE sat approx 0.7 V"]
    end

    subgraph Switch2["REGENERATIVE TRANSITION 2"]
        SW2A["VB1 crosses 0.7 V threshold"] --> SW2B["Q1 turns ON sharply"]
        SW2B --> SW2C["VC1 collapses to 0.2 V"]
        SW2C --> SW2D["C2 coupling drives VB2 hard negative to -VCC"]
        SW2D --> SW2E["Q2 turns OFF sharply, VC2 jumps to VCC"]
    end

    Phase1 --> Switch1
    Switch1 --> Phase2
    Phase2 --> Switch2
    Switch2 --> Phase1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The inner state of each transistor (ON/OFF) is shown as the voltage at the *base node* ŌĆö when base is at V_BE(sat) Ōēł 0.7 V, the transistor is ON. The exponential ramp on the base of the OFF transistor is the **RC charging curve** that determines the dwell time in each phase.

## 4.4 Component-Level Schematic Reference (Text Representation)

For your lab reference, the complete collector-coupled BJT astable is wired as:

- **Q1 (NPN, e.g., BC107):** Emitter ŌåÆ GND; Collector ŌåÆ [R_C1] ŌåÆ V_CC; Base ŌåÆ [R_B1] ŌåÆ V_CC and ŌåÆ [C1] ŌåÉ Collector of Q2.
- **Q2 (NPN, e.g., BC107):** Emitter ŌåÆ GND; Collector ŌåÆ [R_C2] ŌåÆ V_CC; Base ŌåÆ [R_B2] ŌåÆ V_CC and ŌåÆ [C2] ŌåÉ Collector of Q1.
- **Power supply:** Typically +5 V, +9 V, or +12 V DC, single rail.
- **Outputs:** Available at the collector of Q1 (V_C1) and collector of Q2 (V_C2) ŌĆö these are **complementary** (180┬░ out of phase).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an astable multivibrator. Why is it called "astable"?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An astable multivibrator is a regenerative electronic circuit employing two cross-coupled BJT transistors that produces a continuous rectangular waveform at its output. It is called "astable" because it has **no stable state** ŌĆö it continuously alternates between two quasi-stable states, QŌéü ON/QŌéé OFF and QŌéü OFF/QŌéé ON, without any external trigger. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. State the expressions for the time period and frequency of oscillation of a symmetric astable multivibrator.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a symmetric astable multivibrator (R_B1 = R_B2 = R_B, CŌéü = CŌéé = C):
- Time period: $T = 1.386\, R_B C$ (1.5 marks)
- Frequency: $f = \dfrac{1}{1.386\, R_B C} = \dfrac{0.722}{R_B C}$ (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a collector-coupled astable multivibrator using BJT and explain its operation with the help of waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (Textual Description for 2 marks):**
The astable multivibrator consists of two NPN transistors QŌéü and QŌéé with their emitters grounded. The collector of QŌéü is connected to V_CC through R_C1 and the collector of QŌéé is connected to V_CC through R_C2. The base of QŌéü is connected to V_CC through R_B1 and to the collector of QŌéé through coupling capacitor CŌéé. The base of QŌéé is connected to V_CC through R_B2 and to the collector of QŌéü through coupling capacitor CŌéü. The output is taken from the collector of either transistor.

**Operation (5 marks):**

*Phase 1: Assume QŌéü is ON and QŌéé is OFF.*
- QŌéü saturated ŌåÆ V_C1 Ōēł V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0.2 V (LOW output).
- QŌéé cut-off ŌåÆ V_C2 Ōēł V_CC (HIGH output).
- CŌéé charges through R_C2 toward V_CC. CŌéü (which was charged to V_CC with the polarity that keeps QŌéé's base positive) now discharges through R_B1.
- The voltage at the base of QŌéé rises exponentially toward V_CC according to V_B2(t) = V_CC ŌłÆ 2V_CC┬Ęe^(ŌłÆt/R_B1C1).
- When V_B2 reaches 0.7 V, QŌéé turns ON, ending Phase 1.

*Regenerative Transition:*
- QŌéé turning ON pulls V_C2 down sharply. This negative step is coupled through CŌéü to QŌéü's base, driving V_B1 to ŌłÆV_CC. QŌéü turns OFF.
- V_C1 rises sharply to V_CC. This positive step is coupled through CŌéé to QŌéé's base, ensuring QŌéé stays ON.

*Phase 2: QŌéé ON, QŌéü OFF.*
- This is the mirror image of Phase 1, with QŌéü and QŌéé swapped.
- CŌéü charges through R_C1; CŌéé discharges through R_B2.
- After time TŌéü = 0.693 R_B1 C1, the cycle repeats.

*Waveforms:* V_C1, V_C2, V_B1, V_B2 are rectangular waves with V_C1 and V_C2 being 180┬░ out of phase, V_B1 and V_B2 showing exponential ramps during the OFF phase and a sharp negative spike at the start of the OFF phase.

---

**(b)** In an astable multivibrator, R_C1 = R_C2 = 1 k╬®, R_B1 = R_B2 = 100 k╬®, CŌéü = CŌéé = 0.01 ╬╝F, and V_CC = 10 V. Calculate the time period, frequency, and duty cycle of the output waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recognize symmetric configuration**
Since R_B1 = R_B2 = R_B = 100 k╬® and CŌéü = CŌéé = C = 0.01 ╬╝F, the circuit is **symmetric**, so duty cycle = 50%. (1 mark)

**Step 2: Calculate the time period**

$$
T = 1.386 \, R_B \, C = 1.386 \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (0.01 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
T = 1.386 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-8} = 1.386 \times 10^{-3} \, s
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 1.386 \, ms} \quad (2 \, \text{marks})
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the frequency**

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.386 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 721.5 \, Hz
$$

$$
\boxed{f \approx 721.5 \, Hz \approx 0.722 \, kHz} \quad (2 \, \text{marks})
$$

**Step 4: State the duty cycle**

$$
\boxed{D = 50\%} \quad (1 \, \text{mark})
$$

**Step 5: Output amplitude**

$$
V_{out, pp} \approx V_{CC} = 10 \, V \quad (1 \, \text{mark})
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the time period of oscillation of a non-symmetric astable multivibrator. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. V_CE(sat) Ōēł 0 V (transistor saturated acts as a short circuit from collector to emitter).
2. V_BE(sat) Ōēł 0 V (transistor ON keeps base at ground potential).
3. Transistor switching times are negligible compared to RC time constants.
4. Coupling capacitors CŌéü and CŌéé have no leakage and are lossless.
5. The base voltage of the OFF transistor crosses the turn-on threshold (0 V) at the moment of state transition.

**Derivation of TŌéü (Phase 1 ŌĆö QŌéé ON, QŌéü OFF) (3 marks):**

At the start of Phase 1, the collector of QŌéé drops from V_CC to 0 V. Since the voltage across CŌéü cannot change instantaneously, the base of QŌéü is driven from 0 V to ŌłÆV_CC. QŌéü remains OFF.

CŌéü now discharges through R_B1 toward V_CC. The base voltage of QŌéü evolves as:

$$
V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2 V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t / R_{B1} C_1}
$$

QŌéü turns ON when V_B1(t) = 0 (the turn-on threshold):

$$
0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-T_1 / R_{B1} C_1}
$$

Solving:

$$
e^{-T_1 / R_{B1} C_1} = \frac{1}{2} \implies \frac{T_1}{R_{B1} C_1} = \ln(2)
$$

$$
\boxed{T_1 = R_{B1} C_1 \ln(2) = 0.693 \, R_{B1} C_1}
$$

**Derivation of TŌéé by Symmetry (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{T_2 = 0.693 \, R_{B2} C_2}
$$

**Total Time Period (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\, (R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2)}
$$

---

**(b)** An astable multivibrator has R_B1 = 20 k╬®, CŌéü = 0.1 ╬╝F, R_B2 = 80 k╬®, CŌéé = 0.05 ╬╝F, and V_CC = 12 V. Find the time period, frequency, and percentage of time the output at collector of QŌéü remains HIGH. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate TŌéü (QŌéü OFF duration ŌĆö Output at collector of QŌéü is HIGH)** (2 marks)

$$
T_1 = 0.693 \times R_{B1} \times C_1 = 0.693 \times 20 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
T_1 = 0.693 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.386 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 1.386 \, ms
$$

**Step 2: Calculate TŌéé (QŌéü ON duration ŌĆö Output at collector of QŌéü is LOW)** (2 marks)

$$
T_2 = 0.693 \times R_{B2} \times C_2 = 0.693 \times 80 \times 10^3 \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.693 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} = 2.772 \times 10^{-3} \, s = 2.772 \, ms
$$

**Step 3: Total time period** (1 mark)

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 = 1.386 + 2.772 = 4.158 \, ms
$$

**Step 4: Frequency** (1 mark)

$$
f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{4.158 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 240.5 \, Hz
$$

**Step 5: Percentage of time V_C1 remains HIGH** (1 mark)

$$
\% \, \text{HIGH} = \frac{T_1}{T} \times 100 = \frac{1.386}{4.158} \times 100 \approx 33.33\%
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TŌéü with QŌéü's ON time** ŌĆö TŌéü is the duration when **QŌéü is OFF** (QŌéé is ON). Examiners explicitly check if students identify which transistor is OFF during which interval.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** ŌĆö Always convert k╬® to ╬® and ╬╝F to F before multiplying. Many students lose a mark by writing R┬ĘC in mixed units.
> 3. **Missing the symmetry assumption** ŌĆö In symmetric designs, you MUST explicitly state "since R_B1 = R_B2 and CŌéü = CŌéé, the duty cycle is 50%." Examiners award marks for *stating* the symmetry, not just using it.
> 4. **Wrong polarity of the initial base voltage** ŌĆö The OFF transistor's base does NOT start at 0 V; it starts at **ŌłÆV_CC** due to the sudden coupling step. Students often get this wrong in derivations.
> 5. **Confusing V_CE(sat) with 0 V** ŌĆö In exact derivations, V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V and V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V should appear. Using 0 V is a simplifying approximation; in KTU answers, it is acceptable to use 0 V but should be declared as an assumption.
> 6. **Not drawing waveforms** ŌĆö Waveforms are worth 2-3 marks. Always draw V_C1, V_C2, V_B1, V_B2 with the exponential ramps clearly visible on the bases.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Astable = "no stable state"** ŌĆö the circuit perpetually switches between two quasi-stable states.
- **Two cross-coupled BJTs** with collector-to-base capacitive coupling and base-to-V_CC resistive bias form the canonical topology.
- **Regenerative (positive) feedback** ensures sharp, fast switching transitions (the rectangular wave's edges).
- **RC time constants** determine *dwell time* in each quasi-stable state.
- **Key formula (symmetric):** $T = 1.386\, R_B C$, $f = 0.722 / (R_B C)$, **Duty cycle = 50%**.
- **Key formula (asymmetric):** $T = 0.693\, (R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2)$, $f = 1.44 / (R_{B1} C_1 + R_{B2} C_2)$.
- **Outputs at the two collectors are 180┬░ out of phase** (complementary).
- **Initial base voltage of the OFF transistor = ŌłÆV_CC** (not 0 V) ŌĆö this is a key derivation point.
- **The factor 0.693 = ln(2)** comes from the capacitor voltage needing to cross the halfway point of its exponential discharge.
- **Applications:** Square-wave generators, clock oscillators, LED flashers, alarm tone generators, VCOs, time-base circuits.
- **Design tip:** Use V_CC = 5 V or 12 V for standard BJT astables; ensure R_B C >> transistor storage time to prevent race conditions.
- **Output amplitude** Ōēł V_CC (peak-to-peak).
- **Major KTU pitfall:** Confusing which time period corresponds to which transistor's OFF duration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bistable and Monostable Multivibrators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bistable and Monostable Multivibrators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Multivibrator?

A **Multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic switching circuit that uses two amplifying devices (BJTs, JFETs, or op-amps) cross-coupled by resistive or RC networks. It is fundamentally a **regenerative positive feedback** system whose output can exist in one of several discrete stable states, depending on the topology used.

The term "multi-vibrator" was coined by **Abraham and Bloch (1919)** because the square-wave output contains a fundamental frequency along with a rich spectrum of harmonics, hence "many vibrations."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 Focus):**
> A multivibrator is a sequential regenerative switching circuit having **two states**, classified by the *number of stable states* it possesses: **Bistable (2 stable), Monostable (1 stable), and Astable (0 stable)**. The Bistable and Monostable variants are the focus of this module.

## 1.2 Classification of Multivibrators

| Type | Stable States | Also Called | Typical Use |
|------|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Bistable** | 2 stable states | Flip-Flop | Memory cell, latch, register |
| **Monostable** | 1 stable + 1 quasi-stable | One-shot / Mono | Pulse stretching, debouncing |
| **Astable** | 0 stable (free-running) | Square-wave generator | Clock oscillators, tone generation |

> [!NOTE]
> The Bistable and Monostable multivibrators are **triggered circuits** ŌĆö they require an external trigger pulse to change states, unlike the Astable which is self-oscillating.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Two-Switch Light Bulb

Think of a **light bulb in a staircase** that can be turned ON or OFF from either the top or bottom of the stairs.

- A **Bistable Multivibrator** is exactly this: press the top switch ŌåÆ light ON, press the bottom switch ŌåÆ light OFF, and the light *stays* in whichever state you last left it. **Two stable states** ŌĆö it remembers your last action forever (until you toggle again).
- A **Monostable Multivibrator** is like a **doorbell button**: normally the bell is silent (one stable state), but when you press it (apply a trigger), the bell rings for a *fixed duration* (quasi-stable state) and then automatically turns OFF, returning to silence. **One stable state** plus one *temporary* state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The regenerative feedback (each transistor's collector feeds the *other* transistor's base) is the *engine* that snaps the circuit into a stable state almost instantaneously ŌĆö the switching transition is so fast that it is essentially limited only by the transistor's switching speed, typically **10ŌĆō50 ns** for small-signal BJTs.

## 1.4 Why Bistable/Monostable Matter in Modern Electronics

These circuits are the *ancestors* of every digital memory element:
- The basic **SR Flip-Flop** in CMOS logic *is* a bistable multivibrator.
- The **monostable** is the basis of pulse-stretching, missing-pulse detection, and digital debounce circuits.
- The **Schmitt trigger** (an emitter-coupled bistable) provides noise-immune waveform shaping ŌĆö used in every microcontroller's input pin.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU convention assumes **NPN silicon BJTs** with $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7\ \text{V}$ and $V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0.2\ \text{V}$ unless otherwise stated. The supply voltage is typically a single **$V_{CC} = +10\ \text{V}$ or +12 V** for board problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bistable Multivibrator ŌĆö Collector-Coupled Type

The **collector-coupled bistable multivibrator** (also called a basic flip-flop) uses two cross-coupled CE-configured BJTs sharing a common emitter resistor (or with emitter directly grounded).

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology & Working Principle

Two transistors Q1 and Q2 are connected so that the **collector of Q1 drives the base of Q2** via resistor $R_1$, and the **collector of Q2 drives the base of Q1** via resistor $R_1'$. Bias resistors $R_2$ and $R_2'$ connect the bases to $V_{BB}$ (typically $-V_{BB}$) to ensure one transistor remains OFF.

**Operation Cycle (Two Stable States):**

1. **Assume Q1 is ON, Q2 is OFF (State 1):**
   - Q1 is in saturation: $V_{C1} = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$.
   - This low voltage, fed through $R_1$ to the base of Q2, holds Q2 in cutoff.
   - Q2 OFF ŌćÆ $V_{C2} \approx V_{CC}$ (high).
   - This high $V_{C2}$ is fed back through $R_1'$ to Q1's base, keeping Q1 in saturation.
   - **The circuit locks into this state indefinitely** ŌĆö this is the *first stable state*.

2. **Apply a negative trigger to Q1's base (or positive to Q2's base):**
   - Q1 starts to come out of saturation ŌåÆ $V_{C1}$ rises.
   - This rise couples to Q2's base ŌåÆ Q2 starts conducting.
   - As Q2 conducts, $V_{C2}$ falls ŌåÆ couples back to Q1's base, driving it harder into cutoff.
   - **Regenerative action** causes an avalanche-like snap to the *second stable state* (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON).
   - This state is also permanent until the next trigger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition is **regenerative** because the loop gain $A_1 \cdot A_2 \ge 1$ (in fact, much greater). Once initiated by the trigger, the feedback sustains and accelerates the switching, producing a near-vertical output transition. The time taken for this transition is the **propagation delay** $t_{pd}$, typically **10ŌĆō50 ns**.

### 2.1.2 Condition for Saturation (Design Constraint)

For Q1 to be in saturation when Q2 is OFF, the base current $I_{B1}$ must satisfy:

$$
I_{B1} \geq \frac{I_{C1(\text{sat})}}{h_{FE(\text{min})}}
$$

where

$$
I_{C1(\text{sat})} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}
$$

and the base current is supplied via $R_1'$ from $V_{CC}$ (since Q2 is OFF, no current is drawn from Q1's base through $R_1$):

$$
I_{B1} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE1}}{R_1' + R_1'}
$$

Wait ŌĆö let me redo this with the proper bias arrangement. The standard KTU diagram has:
- $R_C, R_C'$ at the collectors
- $R_1, R_1'$ cross-coupling resistors
- $R_2, R_2'$ base bias to $-V_{BB}$ (reverse bias to keep the OFF transistor firmly off)

The base current of the ON transistor Q1 is:

$$
I_{B1} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_1'} - \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})} + V_{BB}}{R_2'}
$$

The saturation condition:

$$
I_{B1} \geq \frac{I_{C1(\text{sat})}}{\beta_{\min}} = \frac{1}{\beta_{\min}} \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C}
$$

### 2.1.3 Output Voltage Levels

$$
V_{OH} = V_{CC} \quad (\text{when corresponding transistor is OFF})
$$

$$
V_{OL} = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V} \quad (\text{when corresponding transistor is ON})
$$

$$
V_{O(\text{pp})} = V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx V_{CC}
$$

---

## 2.2 Bistable Multivibrator ŌĆö Schmitt Trigger (Emitter-Coupled)

The **Schmitt trigger** is an *emitter-coupled* bistable multivibrator. It has a single input and produces two distinct output levels, with **hysteresis** between the switching thresholds.

### 2.2.1 Switching Thresholds (UTP and LTP)

For a Schmitt trigger with input $v_i$ applied at Q1's base, Q2's base tied to a reference $V_{ref}$:

**Upper Threshold Point (UTP)** ŌĆö input rising, Q1 just turns ON, Q2 turns OFF:

$$
V_{UTP} = V_{ref} + V_{BE(\text{on})} - I_E \cdot R_E
$$

For the common case with Q2's base grounded ($V_{ref} = 0$) and symmetric supply:

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_C} \cdot V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}
$$

Wait ŌĆö let me use the *textbook-standard* form for emitter-coupled bistable with $V_{ref} = 0$:

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_F} \cdot (V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})})
$$

where $R_F$ is the feedback resistor from Q2's collector to Q1's base.

**Lower Threshold Point (LTP)** ŌĆö input falling, Q1 turns OFF, Q2 turns ON:

$$
V_{LTP} = -V_{UTP} \quad (\text{for symmetric design with } V_{ref} = 0)
$$

### 2.2.2 Hysteresis Voltage (KTU High-Yield Quantity)

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}
$$

For symmetric design:

$$
V_H = 2 V_{UTP} = \frac{2 R_1}{R_F} (V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hysteresis is the *defining feature* of the Schmitt trigger. It makes the circuit **immune to noise** of amplitude less than $V_H/2$ around either threshold ŌĆö this is why the Schmitt trigger is the standard input stage for noisy digital lines, including the RESET and INTERRUPT pins of microcontrollers.

---

## 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator ŌĆö Collector-Coupled Type

The **monostable multivibrator** (one-shot) has **one stable state** and **one quasi-stable state**.

### 2.3.1 Stable State (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON)

- In the resting state, Q1 is held OFF by a negative bias $V_{BB}$ through $R_B$.
- Q2 is held ON by base current supplied through $R_C$ (since Q1 is OFF, $V_{C1}$ is high).

### 2.3.2 Triggering Action

- A **negative trigger at Q1's base** (or positive at Q2's base) initiates the cycle.
- Q1 turns ON, $V_{C1}$ drops from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$.
- This negative step is coupled through capacitor $C$ to Q2's base, driving Q2 deep into cutoff.
- $V_{C2}$ rises to $V_{CC}$ ŌĆö this is the **output pulse leading edge**.

### 2.3.3 Quasi-Stable State

While in the quasi-stable state:
- Q1 is ON, Q2 is OFF.
- Capacitor $C$ charges (or discharges) through $R_B$ toward $V_{CC}$, but the moment the voltage at Q2's base crosses zero (going from a large negative value back up), Q2 turns back ON and the circuit snaps back to the stable state.

### 2.3.4 Pulse Width Derivation

The voltage at Q2's base during the quasi-stable state follows an exponential charging toward $V_{CC}$, but starting from $V_{BE2(\text{on})}$ (Ōēł 0.7 V) it has just been pulled down to $-V_{CC} + V_{BE(\text{on})}$... Let me derive it cleanly.

**Initial condition (just after trigger, $t = 0^+$):**

The capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously. Just *before* the trigger, $V_{C1} = V_{CC}$ and $V_{B2} = V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$. So the voltage across $C$ is:

$$
V_C(0^-) = V_{C1} - V_{B2} = V_{CC} - 0.7
$$

After the trigger, $V_{C1}$ snaps to $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$, so:

$$
V_{B2}(0^+) = V_{C1}(0^+) - V_C(0^-) = 0.2 - (V_{CC} - 0.7) = -(V_{CC} - 0.5)
$$

Approximately:

$$
V_{B2}(0^+) \approx -(V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})})
$$

**Final condition ($t \to \infty$):**

The capacitor charges through $R_B$ from $V_{BB}$ (negative supply) toward $V_{BB}$ (because the only DC path is through $R_B$ to $V_{BB}$)... 

Actually, for the standard KTU monostable, the base of the OFF transistor is tied to $-V_{BB}$ through $R_B$, so the capacitor charges toward $V_{BB}$ (a negative value) ŌĆö but in many KTU textbook configurations, it's tied to $V_{CC}$ through $R_B$ so the target is $+V_{CC}$.

The standard KTU form (most common in board questions) is:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_{B2}(0^+)) \, e^{-t/RC}
$$

The transistor Q2 turns back ON when $V_{B2}(t)$ reaches $V_{BE(\text{on})}$:

$$
V_{BE(\text{on})} = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - V_{B2}(0^+)) \, e^{-T/RC}
$$

Solving for the pulse width $T$:

$$
T = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_{B2}(0^+)}{V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}\right)
$$

With $V_{B2}(0^+) \approx -(V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})})$:

$$
T = RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{2V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}{V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}\right)
$$

For $V_{CC} \gg V_{BE(\text{on})}$:

$$
\boxed{T \approx RC \cdot \ln(2) \approx 0.693 \, RC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pulse width of a monostable is **independent of the trigger amplitude or width** (provided the trigger exceeds the threshold to start the regenerative action). This **predictable, well-defined** timing makes the monostable ideal for fixed-width pulse generation and digital timing.

### 2.3.5 Recovery Time

After the circuit returns to the stable state, capacitor $C$ must recharge through $R_C$ to its original voltage. The recovery time is:

$$
t_r \approx (3 \text{ to } 5) R_C C
$$

The circuit is **non-responsive** during this recovery ŌĆö it will ignore triggers. The minimum time between triggers is $T + t_r$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | Output High (Bistable) | $V_{OH} = V_{CC}$ | +10 V or +12 V |
| 2 | Output Low (Bistable) | $V_{OL} = V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | **0.2 V** |
| 3 | Peak-to-Peak Output | $V_{O(\text{pp})} = V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | Ōēł $V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | Saturation Condition | $I_B \geq I_{C(\text{sat})} / \beta_{\min}$ | $\beta_{\min} = 50$ (assumed) |
| 5 | UTP (Schmitt, symmetric) | $V_{UTP} = (R_1 / R_F)(V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})})$ | Volts |
| 6 | LTP (Schmitt, symmetric) | $V_{LTP} = -V_{UTP}$ | Volts |
| 7 | Hysteresis Voltage | $V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP}$ | Volts |
| 8 | Monostable Pulse Width | $T = 0.693 \, RC$ (approx) | Seconds |
| 9 | Recovery Time | $t_r \approx 4 R_C C$ | Seconds |
| 10 | Regenerative Loop Gain | $A_{\text{loop}} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \geq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Propagation Delay | $t_{pd}$ | **10ŌĆō50 ns** (BJT) |
| 12 | Base-Emitter ON voltage | $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ | **0.7 V** |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use **$\ln 2 = 0.693$** which is the KTU-board-accepted approximation. Using $\ln 2 = 0.69$ is also acceptable. Do **not** use $\log_{10}$ ŌĆö KTU always uses natural log in timing derivations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Bistable (Flip-Flop):** Static RAM (SRAM) bit cell, debounce circuit for mechanical switches, divider-by-2 in counters, Schmitt-trigger input buffer.
2. **Monostable:** Missing-pulse detector in medical instruments (heart-rate monitors), digital delay line, retriggerable timer in 555-monostable mode, time-of-flight measurement in LIDAR.
3. **Schmitt Trigger:** Noise-immune logic-level translation (e.g., 24 V industrial signals to 3.3 V MCU), square-wave shaping from sine input, RC relaxation oscillator (when paired with a single RC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU board problems, **state the assumptions first** ($V_{BE(\text{on})}=0.7$ V, $V_{CE(\text{sat})}=0.2$ V, $\beta$ large enough for saturation, $I_{CBO}$ negligible). Examiners award at least 1 mark out of 7 for a clean assumption declaration.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Amplitude and Saturation Condition of a Collector-Coupled Bistable Multivibrator

**Problem Setup:** Consider a collector-coupled bistable multivibrator with $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_C = R_C' = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_1 = R_1' = 15\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_2' = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{BB} = -6\ \text{V}$, $\beta_{\min} = 50$, $V_{BE(\text{on})}=0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_{CE(\text{sat})}=0.2\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1: Compute the output levels.**

When Q1 is ON (saturated) and Q2 is OFF:
- Output at Q1's collector: $V_{OL} = V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0.2\ \text{V}$
- Output at Q2's collector: $V_{OH} = V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$ (no current through $R_C'$ since Q2 is OFF and the load is the base of Q1 which draws a tiny current).

$$
V_{O(\text{pp})} = V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 12 - 0.2 = 11.8\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the saturation collector current $I_{C1(\text{sat})}$.**

When Q1 is saturated, the current through $R_C$ is:

$$
I_{C1(\text{sat})} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_C} = \frac{12 - 0.2}{1.2\ \text{k}\Omega} = \frac{11.8}{1200} \approx 9.83\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the minimum base current required for saturation.**

$$
I_{B1(\text{min})} = \frac{I_{C1(\text{sat})}}{\beta_{\min}} = \frac{9.83\ \text{mA}}{50} \approx 0.197\ \text{mA} = 197\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the actual base current supplied to Q1.**

The base of Q1 is connected to two paths:
- From $V_{CC}$ through $R_1'$ (since Q2's collector is at $V_{CC}$ when Q2 is OFF) ŌĆö this **supplies** current to Q1's base.
- From $-V_{BB}$ through $R_2'$ ŌĆö this **withdraws** current from Q1's base (because $V_{BB}$ is negative).

Using superposition (KCL at Q1's base, assuming $V_{B1} = V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V):

$$
I_{B1} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_1'} - \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})} + V_{BB}}{R_2'}
$$

Note: $V_{BB} = -6\ \text{V}$, so $V_{BE(\text{on})} + V_{BB} = 0.7 + (-6) = -5.3\ \text{V}$. The negative sign means the term becomes *positive* (current flows *from* the base node toward $-V_{BB}$ when this is negative, i.e., into the negative supply, which means it actually pulls current **out** of the base). Let me re-derive with explicit signs.

Let $V_B = 0.7\ \text{V}$ (Q1 ON). Then:
- Current from $V_{CC}$ through $R_1'$ into the base: $I_1 = (V_{CC} - V_B) / R_1' = (12 - 0.7)/15\ \text{k}\Omega = 11.3/15\ \text{k}\Omega \approx 0.753\ \text{mA}$.
- Current from base to $-V_{BB}$ through $R_2'$: $I_2 = (V_B - V_{BB})/R_2' = (0.7 - (-6))/100\ \text{k}\Omega = 6.7/100\ \text{k}\Omega = 0.067\ \text{mA}$.

This second current is *leaving* the base node and entering the $-V_{BB}$ supply, so:

$$
I_{B1} = I_1 - I_2 = 0.753 - 0.067 = 0.686\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 5: Check the saturation condition.**

$$
I_{B1} = 686\ \mu\text{A} \quad \text{vs} \quad I_{B1(\text{min})} = 197\ \mu\text{A}
$$

Since $I_{B1} > I_{B1(\text{min})}$, the saturation condition is satisfied. In fact, the **overdrive factor** is:

$$
\text{OF} = \frac{I_{B1}}{I_{B1(\text{min})}} = \frac{686}{197} \approx 3.48
$$

This means the transistor is *firmly* in saturation ŌĆö important for a stable bistable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU-board level):** A common mistake is to forget the **$-V_{BB}$** bias current. Always check both the supply *into* the base (from $V_{CC}$ via $R_1'$) and the *leak* out of the base (toward $-V_{BB}$ via $R_2'$). The difference is the *net* base drive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pulse Width of a Monostable Multivibrator

**Problem Setup:** Monostable MV with $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$, $V_{CC} = 10\ \text{V}$, $V_{BE(\text{on})}=0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_{CE(\text{sat})}=0.2\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1: Identify initial and final voltages at the OFF transistor's base.**

Just before the trigger ($t = 0^-$):
- Q1 is OFF, Q2 is ON, so $V_{C1}(0^-) = V_{CC} = 10\ \text{V}$ and $V_{B2}(0^-) = V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7\ \text{V}$.
- Voltage across capacitor: $V_C(0^-) = V_{C1}(0^-) - V_{B2}(0^-) = 10 - 0.7 = 9.3\ \text{V}$.

Just after the trigger ($t = 0^+$):
- Q1 turns ON, Q2 turns OFF. $V_{C1}(0^+) = V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0.2\ \text{V}$.
- Capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously: $V_C(0^+) = 9.3\ \text{V}$.
- Therefore: $V_{B2}(0^+) = V_{C1}(0^+) - V_C(0^+) = 0.2 - 9.3 = -9.1\ \text{V}$.

This matches the approximation $-(V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{on})}) = -(10 - 0.7) = -9.3\ \text{V}$ ŌĆö the small difference of 0.2 V comes from $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ vs $V_{BE(\text{on})}$; both are KTU-acceptable.

**Step 2: Identify the target voltage for Q2's base.**

The base of Q2 charges toward $V_{CC}$ (positive supply, via $R_B$). The transistor Q2 turns back ON when its base reaches $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7\ \text{V}$.

**Step 3: Write the charging equation.**

For a capacitor charging from $V_{B2}(0^+)$ toward $V_{CC}$ through $RC$:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} + \bigl(V_{B2}(0^+) - V_{CC}\bigr) \, e^{-t/RC}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{B2}(t) = 10 + (-9.1 - 10) \, e^{-t/RC} = 10 - 19.1 \, e^{-t/RC}
$$

**Step 4: Set $V_{B2}(T) = V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7\ \text{V}$ and solve for $T$.**

$$
0.7 = 10 - 19.1 \, e^{-T/RC}
$$

$$
19.1 \, e^{-T/RC} = 9.3
$$

$$
e^{-T/RC} = \frac{9.3}{19.1} \approx 0.4869
$$

$$
-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln(0.4869) \approx -0.7197
$$

$$
T = 0.7197 \, RC
$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

$$
RC = 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 10^3 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 0.7197 \times 1\ \text{ms} \approx 0.72\ \text{ms}}
$$

**Step 6: Cross-check with the approximate formula $T = 0.693 RC$.**

$$
T_{\text{approx}} = 0.693 \times 1\ \text{ms} = 0.693\ \text{ms}
$$

The error is about 4%, arising from the approximation $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx V_{BE(\text{on})}$. For KTU board purposes, the answer $T \approx 0.693 RC$ is the **standard accepted form**, but the full derivation (above) scores full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis Voltage

**Problem Setup:** Schmitt trigger with $R_C = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 500\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 100\ \Omega$, $R_F = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{CC} = \pm 12\ \text{V}$ (symmetric supply), $V_{BE(\text{on})}=0.7\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1: Find the UTP.**

When input $v_i$ rises to the UTP, Q1 just turns ON and Q2 turns OFF. The base of Q1 is at $v_i = V_{UTP}$, and the base of Q2 is at **ground** (reference, $V_{ref} = 0$).

For Q2 to be just at the edge of conduction, its emitter (shared) is at:

$$
V_E = V_{ref} - V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0 - 0.7 = -0.7\ \text{V}
$$

Wait ŌĆö for the standard emitter-coupled Schmitt, the reference is at Q2's base, and we have a **single positive supply** $V_{CC}$ with emitter resistor $R_E$ to ground. The current through $R_E$ is the sum of both transistor emitter currents. When Q2 is OFF, all the current flows through Q1.

Let me use the standard textbook form (Millman & Halkias, adopted by KTU):

For the emitter-coupled bistable with **Q2's base grounded as reference**, the UTP is determined by the condition that Q2's base-emitter is just at threshold. The current through $R_E$ when Q2 is OFF is supplied entirely by Q1. This current is determined by the voltage drop across $R_E$, which in turn depends on the input voltage at Q1's base.

**Method 1 (Direct ŌĆö using loop equation):**

At the UTP, $V_{B1} = V_{UTP}$, $V_{B2} = 0$, and $V_{BE2} = V_{BE(\text{on})}$ (just at threshold). The emitter voltage is $V_E = V_{B2} - V_{BE2} = 0 - 0.7 = -0.7\ \text{V}$.

Hmm ŌĆö this gives a negative emitter voltage, which requires a negative supply or a level-shifted reference. The standard approach uses Q2's base biased at a small positive $V_{ref}$.

**Method 2 (Using the cleaner textbook form):**

For an emitter-coupled bistable with $V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$ (or ground with bias network), the **standard KTU formula** is:

$$
V_{UTP} = V_{ref} + \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_{C2}} \cdot (V_{CC} - V_{ref}) - V_{BE(\text{on})} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_{C2}}\right)
$$

For the simplified case with $V_{ref}$ chosen such that $R_E$ current is symmetric, the KTU-board answer is usually given as:

$$
V_{UTP} = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_C} V_{OH} - \frac{R_C}{R_1 + R_C} V_{BE(\text{on})}
$$

For $R_1 \ll R_C$ (a common design choice), this simplifies to:

$$
V_{UTP} \approx \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_C} V_{OH}
$$

**Step 2: Find the LTP.**

By symmetry (Q1 and Q2 swapped when input falls), and for the symmetric case with $V_{ref}$ at midpoint:

$$
V_{LTP} = -V_{UTP}
$$

**Step 3: Compute hysteresis.**

$$
V_H = V_{UTP} - V_{LTP} = 2 V_{UTP}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above derivation shows the *method*. In a typical KTU question, the student is given the **component values and asked to compute $V_{UTP}$, $V_{LTP}$, and $V_H$**. The exact formula to use depends on the circuit topology shown in the figure, which is why **exam questions always include a circuit diagram** for Schmitt-trigger problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Quick-Reference Comparison

| Feature | Bistable (Collector-Coupled) | Bistable (Schmitt) | Monostable |
|---------|------------------------------|---------------------|------------|
| Stable states | 2 | 2 (with hysteresis) | 1 |
| Trigger | Required to switch | Input crosses threshold | Required to start pulse |
| Output levels | $V_{CC}$, $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | $V_{OH}$, $V_{OL}$ | $V_{CC}$ (pulse), 0 (rest) |
| Timing element | None (memory) | None (instantaneous) | **RC** (defines pulse width) |
| Recovery time | None | None | $4 R_C C$ (typical) |
| Hysteresis | None (sharp switching) | **Yes** ($V_H = 2V_{UTP}$) | N/A |
| Loop gain at rest | $\gg 1$ (regenerative) | $\geq 1$ | $\gg 1$ in Q-stable |
| Key design constraint | $I_B \geq I_{C(\text{sat})}/\beta$ | $V_H$ must exceed noise | $T = 0.693 RC$ |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bistable Multivibrator ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram captures the **two stable states and the trigger-driven transitions** of a collector-coupled bistable multivibrator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On] --> B[Initial Transient]
    B --> C[Symmetry Breaks]
    C --> D[State 1: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF]
    C --> E[State 2: Q1 OFF, Q2 ON]
    D --> F[Trigger at Q1 Base NEG]
    E --> G[Trigger at Q2 Base NEG]
    F --> H[Regenerative Switching Loop]
    G --> H
    H --> E
    H --> D
    D -.Holds Indefinitely.-> D
    E -.Holds Indefinitely.-> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two "Holds Indefinitely" self-loops on the stable states represent the **memory property** of the bistable ŌĆö this is what makes it useful as a 1-bit storage element.

---

## 4.2 Monostable Multivibrator ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rest State Q1 OFF, Q2 ON] --> B[External Trigger Pulse]
    B --> C[Regenerative Switching]
    C --> D[Quasi Stable State: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF]
    D --> E[Capacitor C charges through RB]
    E --> F{VB2 reaches VBE threshold}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Regenerative Switch Back]
    G --> A
    A -.Output at VCE sat level.-> A
    D -.Output pulse width T approx 0.69 RC.-> D
```

---

## 4.3 Schmitt Trigger ŌĆö Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Rising] --> B{vi reaches VUTP}
    B -->|No| A
    B -->|Yes| C[Q1 ON, Q2 OFF]
    C --> D[Output jumps to VOL]
    D --> E[Input Falling]
    E --> F{vi reaches VLTP}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Q1 OFF, Q2 ON]
    G --> H[Output jumps to VOH]
    H --> A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Schmitt trigger's **two distinct thresholds** (UTP and LTP) ŌĆö separated by the hysteresis voltage $V_H$ ŌĆö are the reason it appears as the input stage of nearly every digital IC, including op-amps, microcontrollers, and GPIO pins.

---

## 4.4 Cross-Coupling Topology ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Q1 Collector] -->|R1| B[Q2 Base]
    B --> Q2[Q2]
    Q2 --> C[Q2 Collector]
    C -->|R1 prime| D[Q1 Base]
    D --> Q1[Q1]
    Q1 --> A
    Q1 --> E[Emitter Resistor RE or GND]
    Q2 --> E
```

This closed loop illustrates the **regenerative feedback** that gives the multivibrator its characteristic snap-action. Note the **cross-coupling** ŌĆö Q1's output drives Q2's input, and Q2's output drives Q1's input, creating a *positive feedback* loop with loop gain $\geq 1$.

---

## 4.5 State Transition Timing Diagram ŌĆö Monostable Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trigger Pulse at t=0] --> B[t0: Q1 turns ON, Q2 turns OFF]
    B --> C[Output rises from VOL to VCC]
    C --> D[Capacitor C charges exponentially]
    D --> E[t=T: VB2 reaches VBE threshold]
    E --> F[Q2 turns ON, Q1 turns OFF]
    F --> G[Output falls back to VOL]
    G --> H[Capacitor C recovers through RC]
    H --> I[Circuit ready for next trigger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **quasi-stable state** has a finite duration $T \approx 0.693 RC$ ŌĆö this is the *engineered* pulse width. After $T$, the circuit automatically returns to the stable state.
