<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
**Define voltage gain of an amplifier. What is meant by the $-3\,\text{dB}$ frequency in the context of frequency response?**

**Model Answer:**

Voltage gain $A_v$ is the ratio of output signal voltage to input signal voltage of an amplifier: $A_v = v_o / v_{in}$. In dB: $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert v_o / v_{in} \vert$.

The $-3\,\text{dB}$ frequency is the cutoff frequency at which the magnitude of the voltage gain drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its mid-band (maximum) value. It marks the boundary of the useful bandwidth ŌĆö at this point, the output power falls to half (since $P \propto V^2$).

There are two such frequencies: $f_L$ (lower) and $f_H$ (upper). The bandwidth is $BW = f_H - f_L$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
**State Miller's theorem. Why is the Miller effect important in determining the high-frequency response of a CE amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**

**Miller's Theorem:** If an impedance $Z$ is connected between input node 1 and output node 2 of a network with voltage gain $A_v = V_2/V_1$, then $Z$ can be replaced by:

- An impedance $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ between node 1 and ground.
- An impedance $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - 1/A_v}$ between node 2 and ground.

**Importance:** In a CE amplifier, the feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ (baseŌĆōcollector) appears across input and output. Since $A_v$ is large and negative, $C_\mu$ is **multiplied by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$** at the input side. This large Miller capacitance dominates the high-frequency response, lowering $f_H$ drastically and limiting the bandwidth. Without considering Miller's effect, the predicted $f_H$ would be unrealistically high.

---

## ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Answer **any ONE** from the two choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]`

**For a common-emitter BJT amplifier with $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\,\text{V}$, $V_{CC} = 12\,\text{V}$, bias resistors $R_1 = 33\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, and the transistor has $C_\pi = 10\,\text{pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\,\text{pF}$, $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$:**

**(a) Determine the mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,\text{mid}}$.** (7 marks)

**(b) Calculate the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ using Miller's theorem.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Mid-Band Gain**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate DC bias and small-signal parameters:**

Thevenin voltage at base:
$$
V_{BB} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{33+10} = 12 \cdot \frac{10}{43} = 2.79\,\text{V}
$$

Thevenin resistance:
$$
R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{33 \cdot 10}{33+10} = \frac{330}{43} = 7.67\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

Assuming $I_E \approx I_C$:
$$
I_C = \frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{2.79 - 0.7}{R_E}
$$

Assuming a standard $R_E$ (let us take $R_E = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ from typical bias design):
$$
I_C = \frac{2.09}{1\,\text{k}\Omega} = 2.09\,\text{mA}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $g_m$ and $r_\pi$:**

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2.09 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 80.4\,\text{mA/V} = 0.0804\,\text{S}
$$

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0804} = 1244\,\Omega \approx 1.24\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate $R_C'$:**

$$
R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \cdot 10}{4.7+10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Mid-band voltage gain:**

$$
A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_C' = -0.0804 \cdot 3197 = -257
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(257) = 48.2\,\text{dB}
$$

**Part (b): Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$:**

$$
C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = 2\,\text{pF} \cdot (1 - (-257)) = 2 \cdot 258 = 516\,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total input capacitance:**

$$
C_{\text{in,tot}} = C_\pi + C_{M1} = 10 + 516 = 526\,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equivalent input resistance (mid-band, high frequency = $C_C$ shorts):**

$$
R_{\text{in,eq}} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1\,\text{k} \parallel 7.67\,\text{k} \parallel 1.24\,\text{k}
$$

Intermediate: $1 \parallel 7.67 = \frac{7.67}{8.67} = 884.6\,\Omega$

$$
R_{\text{in,eq}} = 884.6 \parallel 1240 = \frac{884.6 \cdot 1240}{884.6 + 1240} = \frac{1096904}{2124.6} = 516.3\,\Omega
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate $f_H$:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{in,eq}} C_{\text{in,tot}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 516.3 \cdot 526 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2.716 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{1.706 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.86 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Hz} \approx 586\,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H = 257 \cdot 586\,\text{kHz} \approx 150.6\,\text{MHz}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]`

**A common-source MOSFET amplifier has $g_m = 2\,\text{mA/V}$, $R_D = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_{gd} = 1\,\text{pF}$, and source resistance $R_S = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$.**

**(a) Derive the expression for the mid-band voltage gain and calculate its value.** (7 marks)

**(b) Apply Miller's theorem to find the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ and the gain-bandwidth product.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Mid-Band Voltage Gain**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Drain equivalent resistance:**

$$
R_D' = R_D \parallel R_L = \frac{10 \cdot 10}{10+10} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Mid-band voltage gain (CS amplifier):**

Standard small-signal analysis of CS amplifier with current source $g_m v_{gs}$ and drain load $R_D'$:

$$
A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_D' = -2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{3} = -10
$$

$$
A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20\,\text{dB}
$$

The negative sign indicates a $180┬░$ phase inversion between input and output ŌĆö a characteristic of CS and CE amplifiers.

**Part (b): Upper Cutoff Frequency and GBW**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Miller's theorem to $C_{gd}$:**

The voltage gain from gate to drain is $A_v = -g_m R_D' = -10$.

$$
C_{M1} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = 1\,\text{pF} \cdot (1 - (-10)) = 11\,\text{pF}
$$

$$
C_{M2} = C_{gd}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) = 1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{10}\right) = 1.1\,\text{pF} \approx 1\,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total input capacitance:**

$$
C_{\text{in,tot}} = C_{gs} + C_{M1} = 5 + 11 = 16\,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equivalent input resistance:**

For a MOSFET, the gate draws no DC current, so $R_G \to \infty$. Therefore $R_{\text{in,eq}} \approx R_S = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Upper cutoff frequency:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{in,eq}} \cdot C_{\text{in,tot}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 5 \times 10^{3} \cdot 16 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 80 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1}{5.027 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.99\,\text{MHz}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H = 10 \cdot 1.99\,\text{MHz} = 19.9\,\text{MHz}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Miller's Theorem:** Students often write $C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + A_v)$, but since $A_v$ is **negative** for CE/CS amplifiers, the correct form is $C_\mu(1 - A_v)$, which becomes $C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. **Lose 2 marks** for sign mistakes.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_\pi$ or $C_{gs}$:** The total input capacitance is $C_\pi + C_{M1}$ (or $C_{gs} + C_{M1}$), **not** just $C_{M1}$. Omitting this gives an erroneously high $f_H$.
> 3. **Wrong equivalent resistance:** Use $R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$ (BJT) or $R_S \parallel R_G$ (MOSFET, with $R_G$ large). Using $R_C$ or $R_D$ at the input is a fatal error.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Capacitances in **pF** must be converted to **F** (multiply by $10^{-12}$). Frequency answers in kHz or MHz must be explicitly stated.
> 5. **Forgetting the mid-band derivation:** Question (a) often requires the **mid-band gain first** to be used in (b) for Miller multiplication. Skipping (a) invalidates (b).
> 6. **Decimals vs Fractions:** Board examiners prefer symbolic form for derivation, then numerical substitution at the end.

---

## ­¤¦Ā Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ **Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** is the ratio $v_o / v_{in}$, expressed linearly or in dB ($20\log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$).
- Ō£ģ **Frequency Response** of an amplifier is **band-pass**: low, mid, and high regions.
- Ō£ģ **Mid-band:** Coupling/bypass capacitors are shorts; junction capacitors are opens. Gain is constant and maximum.
- Ō£ģ **Low-frequency cutoff ($f_L$):** Determined by coupling ($C_C$) and bypass ($C_E$, $C_S$) capacitors. Each capacitor contributes a pole: $f_{Li} = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{eq},i} C_i)$.
- Ō£ģ **High-frequency cutoff ($f_H$):** Determined by junction capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$) and **Miller's Theorem**.
- Ō£ģ **Miller's Theorem (MUST MEMORISE):** $C_{M1} = C(1 - A_v)$; $C_{M2} = C(1 - 1/A_v)$. For inverting amplifiers, $C_{M1} = C(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ ŌĆö this is huge and dominates $f_H$.
- Ō£ģ **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (for $f_H \gg f_L$).
- Ō£ģ **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H$ is a **constant for a given transistor** and serves as a **figure-of-merit**.
- Ō£ģ **Trade-off:** Higher mid-band gain (larger $R_C$/$R_D$) ŌåÆ larger Miller multiplication ŌåÆ lower $f_H$ ŌåÆ **lower $BW$**, but $GBW$ stays roughly constant.
- Ō£ģ **Cutoff identification:** $-3\,\text{dB}$ corresponds to $\vert A_v \vert$ dropping to $0.707$ of mid-band value.
- Ō£ģ **Phase:** CE/CS amplifiers invert the signal ($180┬░$ phase shift); CC/CD (emitter/source follower) do not.
- Ō£ģ **Bode plot slope:** $20\,\text{dB/decade}$ per pole in the roll-off regions; $-3\,\text{dB}$ at each cutoff frequency.
- Ō£ģ **Application domains:** Audio (low $f_L$ critical), Video/RF (high $f_H$ critical), Op-amps (constant $GBW$).
- Ō£ģ **Quick recall formulas (MUST memorise for KTU):**
$$
A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CE}) = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)
$$
$$
A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CS}) = -g_m(R_D \parallel R_L)
$$
$$
C_{\text{in,tot}} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C') \quad \text{(CE)}
$$
$$
f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{in,eq}} \cdot C_{\text{in,tot}}}
$$
$$
GBW = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Analysis of CE Configuration - Small Signal Hybrid-PI Model for Mid and Low Frequency (Gain, Input and Output Impedance)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆö Hybrid-ŽĆ Model (Giacoletto Model)**
> The **Hybrid-ŽĆ small-signal model** is a two-port, linear, parameter-based equivalent circuit of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) that is valid for **low and mid-frequency** small-signal analysis. It is constructed by linearizing the BJT's non-linear EbersŌĆōMoll equations around a quiescent (Q-point) operating point, yielding four intrinsic small-signal parameters: the **transconductance $g_m$**, the **base-emitter dynamic input resistance $r_\pi$**, the **output resistance $r_o$** (Early resistance), and the **feedback resistance $r_\mu$**. For mid and low frequencies, the parasitic capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) are treated as **open circuits**, simplifying the model to a purely resistive network.

The Common Emitter (CE) configuration using this model is the most widely used amplifier topology in analog integrated circuits because it provides simultaneous **voltage gain**, **current gain**, and **moderate power gain**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Smart Water Valve"**
> Imagine a **smart water valve** connected between a high-pressure main supply (VCC) and the drain pipe (ground). The amount of water flowing from the main to the drain is **collector current $I_C$**. The valve is controlled by a tiny mechanical lever ŌĆö this lever represents the **base current $I_B$**. A very small movement of the lever (small base current) controls a massive water flow (large collector current) because of the mechanical leverage ratio $\beta$. 
> 
> - The **stiffness of the lever mechanism** is the resistance $r_\pi$ ŌĆö it determines how much lever-movement (voltage) is needed to produce a given water-flow change.
> - The **amplification factor** of the lever is the transconductance $g_m$ ŌĆö converting lever-input (voltage) to water-output (current).
> - The **slight leakage of water backward** through the valve seals is the Early resistance $r_o$.
> 
> When you "wiggle" the lever slightly (small AC signal), the water-flow changes proportionally ŌĆö and we can analyze this with linear (small-signal) equations.

## 1.3 Key Small-Signal Parameters at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> The four intrinsic small-signal parameters of the hybrid-ŽĆ model are **evaluated entirely from the DC Q-point** of the transistor. This is a powerful feature: once the bias is set, the AC behavior is fully determined.

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | Voltage-to-current gain | Siemens (A/V) or mA/V |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi$ | Input resistance looking into base | Ohms (╬®) |
| Output resistance (Early) | $r_o$ | Collector-emitter output resistance | Ohms (╬®) |
| Feedback resistance | $r_\mu$ | Base-collector feedback | Ohms (╬®) |

> **Thermal Voltage (constant)**: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hybrid-ŽĆ Equivalent Circuit (Mid/Low Frequency)

In the mid-band and low-frequency range, the depletion-region and diffusion capacitances of the BJT ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, $C_{cs}$) are treated as **open circuits** because their reactances $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ are negligibly large. The simplified resistive hybrid-ŽĆ model is shown conceptually below (full schematic in Section 4).

The model consists of:
- A **resistance $r_\pi$** between base (B) and emitter (E).
- A **dependent current source $g_m \cdot v_{be}$** from collector (C) to emitter (E), controlled by the base-emitter voltage.
- An **output resistance $r_o$** from collector (C) to emitter (E).
- A **feedback resistance $r_\mu$** between base (B) and collector (C).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Hybrid-ŽĆ"?** The model's shape resembles the Greek letter **ŽĆ (pi)**, with the input port on the left (base) and the output port on the right (collector), sharing the common emitter terminal. It is called "hybrid" because its parameters use a **mix (hybrid)** of open-circuit and short-circuit impedance/admittance concepts.

## 2.2 Derivation of the Four Parameters from the Q-Point

### (a) Transconductance $g_m$
The collector current of a BJT in the active region is governed by the EbersŌĆōMoll relation:
$$I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$
Differentiating with respect to $V_{BE}$ (with $V_{CE}$ held constant, since at low frequency we ignore the Early effect's small-signal contribution through $r_o$ first):
$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\right|_{Q} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

### (b) Base-Emitter Dynamic Resistance $r_\pi$
Using the fundamental BJT relation $I_C = \beta I_B$, we have $I_B = I_C / \beta$. The base-emitter voltage change is:
$$r_\pi = \left.\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial I_B}\right|_{Q} = \frac{V_T}{I_B} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$$

### (c) Output Resistance $r_o$ (Early Resistance)
The Early effect causes $I_C$ to rise slightly with $V_{CE}$ for a fixed $V_{BE}$. The slope of the $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ curve in the active region gives:
$$r_o = \left.\frac{\partial V_{CE}}{\partial I_C}\right|_{Q} = \frac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C} \approx \frac{V_A}{I_C}$$
where $V_A$ is the **Early voltage** (typically **50 V to 200 V** for silicon BJTs).

### (d) Feedback Resistance $r_\mu$
This represents the small-signal feedback through the reverse-biased base-collector junction:
$$r_\mu = \beta \cdot r_o$$
Since $r_\mu$ is typically in the range of **mega-ohms to giga-ohms**, it is often **neglected** in mid/low-frequency analysis unless very high precision is needed.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mid/Low-Frequency CE Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are valid for the Common-Emitter (CE) configuration with the BJT in the active region, at frequencies where coupling and bypass capacitors are shorts and junction capacitances are open circuits.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (Exact) | Simplified Form | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | $g_m \approx 40 \cdot I_C (\text{mA})$ mA/V | $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 2 | Input resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | $r_\pi = \beta V_T / I_C$ | Purely resistive at low/mid freq |
| 3 | Output resistance | $r_o = (V_A + V_{CE})/I_C$ | $r_o \approx V_A / I_C$ | Due to Early effect |
| 4 | Feedback resistance | $r_\mu = \beta \cdot r_o$ | Often neglected | $r_\mu \gg r_\pi$ |
| 5 | Input impedance (base) | $Z_b = r_\pi$ | ŌĆö | No emitter resistor |
| 6 | Input impedance (with $R_E$) | $Z_b = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$ | $\approx r_\pi + \beta R_E$ | $R_E$ unbypassed |
| 7 | Total input impedance | $Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert Z_b$ | ŌĆö | With bias network |
| 8 | Output impedance | $Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o$ | $\approx R_C$ if $r_o \gg R_C$ | Looking into collector |
| 9 | Voltage gain (no $R_E$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)$ | $A_v = -\beta (R_C \vert\vert R_L) / r_\pi$ | CE, mid-band |
| 10 | Voltage gain (with $R_E$) | $A_v = -g_m R_C' / (1 + g_m R_E)$ | $\approx -R_C' / R_E$ | $R_E$ unbypassed, $g_m R_E \gg 1$ |
| 11 | Current gain | $A_i = \beta$ | $A_i = g_m r_\pi$ | Short-circuit current gain |
| 12 | Power gain | $A_p = \vert A_v \vert \cdot \vert A_i \vert$ | $\approx \beta^2 R_C / r_\pi$ | Approximate |

> **Note on notation:** $R_C' = R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o$ is the **total AC load resistance** at the collector. The double bar $\vert\vert$ denotes **parallel combination** (not absolute value).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-amp input stages** (e.g., 741 op-amp): The differential input pair uses CE amplifiers with emitter degeneration (unbypassed $R_E$) to set a stable, predictable transconductance $g_m$ that defines the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product.
- **Audio pre-amplifiers**: CE amplifiers with bypassed $R_E$ provide high gain in the audio band (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz), well within the mid-frequency region.
- **Bias-stable IC design**: The hybrid-ŽĆ model is the foundation of SPICE BJT models (GummelŌĆōPoon), used universally in VLSI design.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Voltage Gain $A_v$ for CE without Emitter Resistor

We analyze the **CE amplifier with collector resistor $R_C$, no emitter resistor, and load $R_L$** at the collector output.

### Step 1: Replace BJT with Hybrid-ŽĆ Small-Signal Model
The coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are treated as **short circuits** at mid-frequency. The DC supply VCC becomes AC ground. The simplified small-signal circuit has:
- Input $v_i$ applied between base and emitter.
- $r_\pi$ between base and emitter.
- Dependent source $g_m v_{be}$ from collector to emitter.
- $r_o$ from collector to emitter.
- $R_C$ from collector to ground (since VCC is AC ground).
- $R_L$ from collector to ground (AC load).

### Step 2: Write the Input Loop Equation
Since the emitter is grounded (in CE), the base-emitter voltage equals the input voltage:
$$v_{be} = v_i$$

### Step 3: Compute the Collector Current
The dependent current source delivers a current:
$$i_c = g_m v_{be} = g_m v_i$$
This current flows **out of the collector node** through the external load.

### Step 4: Compute the Output Voltage
The output voltage is the voltage developed across the parallel combination of $R_C$, $R_L$, and $r_o$. The current $g_m v_i$ flows through this parallel network, creating a voltage drop **opposite in polarity** to $v_i$ (phase inversion ŌĆö a hallmark of CE amplifiers):
$$v_o = -g_m v_i \cdot (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o)$$

### Step 5: Form the Voltage Gain Ratio
$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Sign Interpretation:** The minus sign indicates a **180┬░ phase reversal** between input and output ŌĆö a fundamental property of the CE configuration, making it inherently an **inverting amplifier**.

### Step 6: Approximation When $r_o$ is Neglected
In most discrete designs, $r_o$ is much larger than $R_C \vert\vert R_L$, so:
$$A_v \approx -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L) = -\frac{\beta (R_C \vert\vert R_L)}{r_\pi}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Voltage Gain $A_v$ for CE with Unbypassed Emitter Resistor $R_E$

Now we add $R_E$ in the emitter leg, **without** a bypass capacitor. This introduces **emitter degeneration**, which trades gain for stability and linearity.

### Step 1: Input Loop ŌĆö Apply KVL
Going around the base-emitter loop:
$$v_i = v_{be} + i_e R_E$$
where $i_e$ is the small-signal emitter current.

### Step 2: Relate Emitter Current to Base Current
$$i_e = (\beta + 1) i_b \approx \beta i_b \quad \text{(for large } \beta\text{)}$$

### Step 3: Express $v_{be}$ in Terms of $i_b$
$$v_{be} = i_b r_\pi$$

### Step 4: Solve for $i_b$ in Terms of $v_i$
Substituting:
$$v_i = i_b r_\pi + (\beta + 1) i_b R_E = i_b [r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E]$$
$$i_b = \frac{v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$

### Step 5: Compute Collector Current
$$i_c = \beta i_b = \frac{\beta v_i}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$

### Step 6: Compute Output Voltage
$$v_o = -i_c \cdot (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o)$$

### Step 7: Form the Voltage Gain
$$\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -\frac{\beta (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o)}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8: Rewrite Using $g_m$
Since $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$:
$$A_v = -\frac{\beta (R_C \vert\vert R_L)}{(\beta/g_m) + (\beta + 1) R_E}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\beta$:
$$A_v = -\frac{(R_C \vert\vert R_L)}{(1/g_m) + (1 + 1/\beta) R_E} \approx -\frac{g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** When $g_m R_E \gg 1$, the gain **becomes independent of $g_m$ and $\beta$**, and simplifies to:
> $$A_v \approx -\frac{R_C \vert\vert R_L}{R_E}$$
> This is the principle of **"gain stabilization by emitter degeneration"** ŌĆö the gain depends only on the **ratio of two external passive resistors**, which are far more stable than the BJT's temperature-dependent $\beta$ and $g_m$.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Input Impedance $Z_{in}$

### Case A: No $R_E$ (or $R_E$ fully bypassed)
Looking into the base, ignoring $r_\mu$ feedback:
$$Z_b = \frac{v_i}{i_b} = r_\pi$$
With the bias network $R_1$ and $R_2$:
$$Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert r_\pi$$

### Case B: With Unbypassed $R_E$
$$Z_b = \frac{v_i}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E \approx r_\pi + \beta R_E$$
With the bias network:
$$Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert [r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E]$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Output Impedance $Z_{out}$

To find $Z_{out}$, we **deactivate the independent input source** ($v_i = 0$, so $i_b = 0$ and $g_m v_{be} = 0$). The dependent current source becomes an open circuit. Looking back into the collector:
$$Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o$$

Since $r_o$ is typically much larger than $R_C$ (e.g., $r_o = 100$ k╬® vs. $R_C = 2$ k╬®):
$$Z_{out} \approx R_C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** The output impedance of a CE amplifier is **relatively high** (in the k╬® range) compared to a Common-Collector (emitter follower) stage, where $Z_{out} \approx R_E / \beta$ is very low. This makes CE a **poor voltage buffer** but an excellent **voltage gain stage**.

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A silicon BJT (╬▓ = 100, $V_A = 100$ V) is biased at $I_C = 2$ mA, $V_{CE} = 5$ V. The CE amplifier has $R_C = 4.7$ k╬®, $R_E = 1$ k╬® (unbypassed), $R_1 = 33$ k╬®, $R_2 = 12$ k╬®, and load $R_L = 10$ k╬®. Calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$, $A_v$, $Z_{in}$, and $Z_{out}$.

### Step 1: Transconductance
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769 \text{ A/V} \approx 76.9 \text{ mA/V}$$

### Step 2: Base-Emitter Resistance
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0769} = 1300 \, \Omega = 1.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$
**Verification using $V_T$:** $r_\pi = \beta V_T / I_C = (100)(26\text{mV})/(2\text{mA}) = 1.3$ k╬® Ō£ō

### Step 3: Output Resistance
$$r_o = \frac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C} = \frac{100 + 5}{2 \text{ mA}} = \frac{105 \text{ V}}{2 \text{ mA}} = 52.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 4: AC Load at Collector
$$R_C' = R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o = 4.7\text{k} \vert\vert 10\text{k} \vert\vert 52.5\text{k}$$
$$\frac{1}{R_C'} = \frac{1}{4.7} + \frac{1}{10} + \frac{1}{52.5} = 0.2128 + 0.1 + 0.0190 = 0.3318 \text{ (k}\Omega)^{-1}$$
$$R_C' = 3.014 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 5: Voltage Gain (with $R_E$ unbypassed)
$$A_v = -\frac{g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E} = -\frac{(0.0769)(3014)}{1 + (0.0769)(1000)} = -\frac{231.8}{1 + 76.9} = -\frac{231.8}{77.9} = -2.976 \approx -2.98$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check using simplified formula** (since $g_m R_E = 76.9 \gg 1$): $A_v \approx -R_C'/R_E = -3014/1000 = -3.01$ ŌĆö close to the exact value of $-2.98$. Ō£ō

### Step 6: Input Impedance at Base
$$Z_b = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E = 1300 + (101)(1000) = 1300 + 101000 = 102.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 7: Total Input Impedance
$$Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert Z_b = 33\text{k} \vert\vert 12\text{k} \vert\vert 102.3\text{k}$$
$$\frac{1}{R_{12}} = \frac{1}{33} + \frac{1}{12} = 0.0303 + 0.0833 = 0.1136 \text{ (k}\Omega)^{-1}$$
$$R_{12} = 8.80 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$Z_{in} = 8.80 \text{ k}\Omega \vert\vert 102.3 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{8.80 \times 102.3}{8.80 + 102.3} = \frac{900.2}{111.1} = 8.10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Step 8: Output Impedance
$$Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega \vert\vert 52.5 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{4.7 \times 52.5}{4.7 + 52.5} = \frac{246.75}{57.2} = 4.31 \text{ k}\Omega$$

---

## 3.6 Final Analytical Results Table

| Quantity | Computed Value | Magnitude Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $g_m$ | 76.9 mA/V | High transconductance for 2 mA bias |
| $r_\pi$ | 1.3 k╬® | Modest input resistance |
| $r_o$ | 52.5 k╬® | Large, but not infinite |
| $A_v$ | ŌłÆ2.98 | Low gain (due to unbypassed $R_E$) |
| $Z_{in}$ | 8.10 k╬® | Set mostly by bias network |
| $Z_{out}$ | 4.31 k╬® | Set mostly by $R_C$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion from this example:** Adding unbypassed $R_E = 1$ k╬® reduced the gain from a theoretical $-g_m R_C' \approx -231$ (without $R_E$) to only $-2.98$ ŌĆö a **reduction by a factor of ~78**. This is the cost of gain stability. To recover the lost gain, we would place a **bypass capacitor $C_E$** in parallel with $R_E$, shorting it out at signal frequencies.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Hybrid-ŽĆ Small-Signal Model (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **hybrid-ŽĆ model topology** with all four intrinsic parameters, including their **functional roles** in the CE signal path. This is the canonical low/mid-frequency equivalent circuit.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeB[Base B]:::inNode
    nodeE[Emitter E]:::gndNode
    nodeC[Collector C]:::outNode

    nodeB --- nodeRpi(("r_pi"))
    nodeRpi --- nodeE

    nodeB --- nodeRmu(("r_mu"))
    nodeRmu --- nodeC

    nodeC --- nodeRo(("r_o"))
    nodeRo --- nodeE

    nodeC --- nodeGm[("g_m x v_be")]:::depSrc
    nodeGm --- nodeE

    classDef inNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef gndNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef depSrc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **gold node (B)** is the input port ŌĆö where $v_{be}$ is sensed.
- The **green node (E)** is the common terminal ŌĆö grounded in CE configuration.
- The **blue node (C)** is the output port ŌĆö where $i_c$ flows out.
- The **pink diamond** represents the **voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$** ŌĆö the amplification engine.
- $r_\pi$ (base-emitter dynamic resistance) is the **input divider**.
- $r_\mu$ (feedback resistance) is the **parasitic feedback** path.
- $r_o$ (Early resistance) is the **output current-source non-ideality**.

---

## 4.2 Complete CE Amplifier Small-Signal Analysis Flowchart

The following flowchart describes the **step-by-step procedure** for analyzing a CE amplifier using the hybrid-ŽĆ model. This is the exact method KTU examiners expect you to follow in long-answer questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start: Given CE circuit with Q-point and component values]:::stepNode
    step1[Step 1: Verify BJT in active region: V_CE greater than V_CE_sat]:::stepNode
    step2[Step 2: Compute Q-point: I_C and V_CE from bias analysis]:::stepNode
    step3[Step 3: Replace BJT with hybrid-pi model]:::stepNode
    step4[Step 4: Deactivate DC sources: VCC becomes ground, set v_i as input]:::stepNode
    step5[Step 5: Compute g_m = I_C / V_T]:::calcNode
    step6[Step 6: Compute r_pi = beta / g_m]:::calcNode
    step7[Step 7: Compute r_o = V_A / I_C]:::calcNode
    step8[Step 8: Form input loop: v_i = i_b x Z_b]:::calcNode
    step9[Step 9: Solve for i_b in terms of v_i]:::calcNode
    step10[Step 10: Compute i_c = beta x i_b]:::calcNode
    step11[Step 11: Compute v_o = - i_c x R_C prime]:::calcNode
    step12[Step 12: A_v = v_o / v_i]:::calcNode
    step13[Step 13: Z_in = R_1 parallel R_2 parallel Z_b]:::calcNode
    step14[Step 14: Z_out = R_C parallel r_o]:::calcNode
    endA[End: Report A_v, Z_in, Z_out with units]:::resultNode

    startA --> step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11 --> step12 --> step13 --> step14 --> endA

    classDef stepNode fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calcNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resultNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Frequency-Region Classification of the BJT Model

A block diagram showing **where the hybrid-ŽĆ model is valid** versus where more complex models (high-frequency, transit-time) are required.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeLF[Low Frequency: f less than f_L]:::regNode
    nodeMF[Mid Frequency: f_L to f_beta]:::regNode
    nodeHF[High Frequency: f greater than f_beta]:::regNode

    nodeLF --- nodeM1["Hybrid-pi resistive model nCoupling caps short nJunction caps open"]:::modelNode
    nodeMF --- nodeM2["Same hybrid-pi model nFlat gain region nA_v maximum and constant"]:::modelNode
    nodeHF --- nodeM3["Hybrid-pi with C_pi and C_mu nMiller theorem applied nGain rolls off at -20 dB/decade"]:::modelNode

    classDef regNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef modelNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **mid-frequency band** is the region where the gain is **maximum, flat, and independent of frequency**. The lower cutoff $f_L$ is set by coupling and bypass capacitors; the upper cutoff $f_\beta$ (or $f_T$) is set by the internal capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$. The hybrid-ŽĆ model **without** the capacitors is the **mid-band model**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A consists of short-answer questions requiring concise, definition-based responses.

---

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Draw the small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ model of a BJT and define the parameters $g_m$ and $r_\pi$. Derive the relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

The hybrid-ŽĆ model represents the BJT as a linear two-port network valid for low/mid-frequency small-signal analysis. It consists of:
- Input resistance $r_\pi$ between base and emitter.
- Voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$ from collector to emitter.
- Output resistance $r_o$ between collector and emitter.
- Feedback resistance $r_\mu$ between base and collector.

**Definitions:**
- **Transconductance $g_m$:** The rate of change of collector current with respect to base-emitter voltage, with collector-emitter voltage held constant. $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
- **Base-emitter dynamic resistance $r_\pi$:** The small-signal input resistance looking into the base terminal with the emitter grounded. $r_\pi = V_T / I_B = \beta V_T / I_C$.

**Relationship:** Since $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $g_m = I_C / V_T$, we have $I_C = g_m V_T$. Substituting:
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{\beta V_T}{g_m V_T} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$$
$$\boxed{r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta}$$

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: For a CE amplifier using the hybrid-ŽĆ model, write the expression for the voltage gain with and without an unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$. Explain the trade-off.**

**Model Answer:**

**Without $R_E$ (or $R_E$ fully bypassed):**
$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o) \approx -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)$$

**With unbypassed $R_E$:**
$$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx -\frac{R_C \vert\vert R_L}{R_E} \quad \text{(when } g_m R_E \gg 1\text{)}$$

**Trade-off:** The unbypassed $R_E$ **reduces the voltage gain** but provides:
- **Gain stability** against variations in $\beta$, $g_m$, and temperature.
- **Increased input impedance** (linear in $R_E$).
- **Improved linearity** (reduced distortion) by introducing local negative feedback.
- **Wider bandwidth** as a beneficial side effect.

The bypass capacitor $C_E$ in parallel with $R_E$ restores the high AC gain at signal frequencies while preserving DC bias stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question offers **internal choice** between two options. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternative selections ŌĆö students answer either one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**A BJT in CE configuration has the following parameters: $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 1.5$ mA, $V_A = 100$ V, $R_C = 5$ k╬®, $R_E = 1.2$ k╬® (unbypassed), $R_1 = 40$ k╬®, $R_2 = 15$ k╬®, $R_L = 8$ k╬®. Take $V_T = 26$ mV.**

#### **Part (a) (7 Marks): Draw the small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent circuit and calculate the values of $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_o$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Draw the hybrid-ŽĆ model** with $r_\pi$ from B to E, dependent source $g_m v_{be}$ from C to E, $r_o$ from C to E, and $r_\mu$ from B to C (often omitted at low/mid frequency). Bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ at input; $R_C$, $R_L$ at output; $R_E$ in emitter.

**Step 2: Calculate $g_m$:**
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.5 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.0577 \text{ A/V} = 57.7 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $r_\pi$:**
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.0577} = 2079.7 \, \Omega \approx 2.08 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Calculate $r_o$:**
$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} = \frac{100 \text{ V}}{1.5 \text{ mA}} = 66.67 \text{ k}\Omega$$

#### **Part (b) (7 Marks): Calculate the voltage gain $A_v$, input impedance $Z_{in}$, and output impedance $Z_{out}$ of the amplifier.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total AC load at collector:**
$$\frac{1}{R_C'} = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{66.67} = 0.2 + 0.125 + 0.015 = 0.340 \text{ (k}\Omega)^{-1}$$
$$R_C' = 2.941 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Voltage Gain (unbypassed $R_E$):**
$$A_v = -\frac{g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E} = -\frac{0.0577 \times 2941}{1 + 0.0577 \times 1200} = -\frac{169.7}{1 + 69.24} = -\frac{169.7}{70.24} = -2.416$$

**Step 3: Input Impedance at Base:**
$$Z_b = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E = 2.08 \text{ k}\Omega + (121)(1.2 \text{ k}\Omega) = 2.08 + 145.2 = 147.28 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Total Input Impedance:**
$$R_1 \vert\vert R_2 = \frac{40 \times 15}{40 + 15} = \frac{600}{55} = 10.91 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$Z_{in} = 10.91 \text{ k}\Omega \vert\vert 147.28 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{10.91 \times 147.28}{10.91 + 147.28} = \frac{1606.8}{158.19} = 10.16 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Output Impedance:**
$$Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o = 5 \text{ k}\Omega \vert\vert 66.67 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{5 \times 66.67}{5 + 66.67} = \frac{333.35}{71.67} = 4.65 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Final Results:**
$$\boxed{A_v = -2.42, \quad Z_{in} = 10.16 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad Z_{out} = 4.65 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) (7 Marks): With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expressions for the voltage gain, input impedance, and output impedance of a CE amplifier using the hybrid-ŽĆ model, assuming the emitter resistor is fully bypassed by a capacitor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Draw the CE small-signal circuit** with BJT replaced by hybrid-ŽĆ model: $r_\pi$ between B and E, dependent source $g_m v_{be}$ from C to E, $r_o$ from C to E. Bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a divider at the base. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ to ground at signal frequency.

**Step 2: Voltage Gain Derivation:**
With $R_E$ shorted by $C_E$, the emitter is at AC ground:
$$v_{be} = v_i$$
$$i_c = g_m v_{be} = g_m v_i$$
$$v_o = -i_c \cdot (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o)$$
$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L \vert\vert r_o) \approx -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)$$

**Step 3: Input Impedance Derivation:**
Looking into the base:
$$Z_b = \frac{v_i}{i_b} = r_\pi$$
With bias network:
$$Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert r_\pi$$

**Step 4: Output Impedance Derivation:**
Set $v_i = 0$ ŌåÆ $i_b = 0$ ŌåÆ $g_m v_{be} = 0$ (dependent source becomes open). Looking into collector:
$$Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o \approx R_C$$

**Summary of Results (bypassed $R_E$):**
$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L), \quad Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert r_\pi, \quad Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o}$$

---

**(b) (7 Marks): A CE amplifier has $R_C = 2.2$ k╬®, $R_L = 4.7$ k╬®, $\beta = 150$, $I_C = 2$ mA, $V_T = 25$ mV, and a fully bypassed emitter resistor. Compute the mid-band voltage gain, and comment on what happens if the bypass capacitor is removed.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $g_m$:**
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{25 \text{ mV}} = 0.08 \text{ A/V} = 80 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2: Compute AC load at collector (assuming $r_o \gg R_C, R_L$):**
$$R_C' = R_C \vert\vert R_L = \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} = 1.499 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Mid-band Voltage Gain (bypassed $R_E$):**
$$A_v = -g_m R_C' = -(0.08)(1499) = -119.9 \approx -120$$

**Step 4: Compute $r_\pi$:**
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{150}{0.08} = 1875 \, \Omega = 1.875 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Effect of Removing the Bypass Capacitor:**
Assume $R_E = 1$ k╬® (typical) and unbypassed. Then:
$$A_v' = -\frac{g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E} = -\frac{119.9}{1 + (0.08)(1000)} = -\frac{119.9}{81} = -1.48$$

**Comments:**
- The gain drops from **ŌłÆ120 to ŌłÆ1.48** ŌĆö a reduction by a factor of ~81.
- However, the gain becomes **approximately $-R_C'/R_E = -1.5$**, which is **independent of $\beta$ and temperature**.
- The **input impedance increases** from $r_\pi = 1.875$ k╬® to $r_\pi + \beta R_E = 1.875 + 150 = 151.9$ k╬®, making the amplifier a better **voltage amplifier** (less loading of the source).
- The **bandwidth increases** because of reduced Miller effect, and **linearity improves** due to negative feedback.
- The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is therefore placed to **restore the high AC gain** while $R_E$ provides **DC bias stability**.

**Final Results:**
$$\boxed{A_v(\text{bypassed}) = -120, \quad A_v(\text{unbypassed}) \approx -1.48}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_v$** ŌĆö The CE amplifier is an **inverting** amplifier. A positive $A_v$ indicates a sign error. (Cost: 1 mark)
> 
> 2. **Using $V_T = 26$ mV when the problem specifies a different value** ŌĆö Always check the problem statement for the given $V_T$. At 300 K, use 25.85 mV or 26 mV as instructed. (Cost: 1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to compute $r_o$** ŌĆö Even if neglected in the final gain, examiners often allocate **1 mark** for explicitly stating $r_o = V_A / I_C$ and showing that $r_o \gg R_C$.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up $R_C$ and $R_C'$ (AC load)** ŌĆö The DC load $R_C$ and AC load $R_C \vert\vert R_L$ are **different** when a coupling capacitor connects to $R_L$. Mid-band gain uses the **AC load**. (Cost: 2 marks)
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the bias network in $Z_{in}$** ŌĆö Students often report $Z_{in} = r_\pi$ and forget $R_1 \vert\vert R_2$. (Cost: 1 mark)
> 
> 6. **Writing $g_m \cdot r_o = 1$** ŌĆö This is **incorrect**. The correct relation is $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta$. The product $g_m \cdot r_o$ has no standard name; $\mu = g_m r_o$ is sometimes used for the **amplification factor** but is not commonly required in CE analysis.
> 
> 7. **Using $\beta + 1$ vs. $\beta$ inconsistently** ŌĆö For $i_e = i_c + i_b = \beta i_b + i_b = (\beta+1) i_b$. The factor $(\beta+1)$ is correct; some textbooks approximate it as $\beta$ for $\beta \gg 1$. Be consistent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density revision checklist for the small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ CE analysis topic:**

- Ō£ģ The **hybrid-ŽĆ model** is the **mid-frequency** small-signal BJT model ŌĆö **resistive only**, no capacitors.
- Ō£ģ Four parameters: $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$, $r_\mu$. At low/mid frequency, $r_\mu$ is often neglected because $r_\mu \gg r_\pi$.
- Ō£ģ **$g_m = I_C / V_T$** ŌĆö depends **only** on the Q-point collector current and temperature.
- Ō£ģ **$r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / I_C$** ŌĆö decreases as $I_C$ increases.
- Ō£ģ **$r_o = V_A / I_C$** ŌĆö Early resistance; sets the output impedance ceiling.
- Ō£ģ **Voltage gain (no $R_E$):** $A_v = -g_m (R_C \vert\vert R_L)$ ŌĆö **negative** (inverting).
- Ō£ģ **Voltage gain (with $R_E$):** $A_v \approx -R_C / R_E$ for $g_m R_E \gg 1$ ŌĆö gain **stabilized** by resistor ratio.
- Ō£ģ **Input impedance (no $R_E$):** $Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert r_\pi$.
- Ō£ģ **Input impedance (with $R_E$):** $Z_{in} = R_1 \vert\vert R_2 \vert\vert [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]$ ŌĆö **boosted** by $\beta R_E$.
- Ō£ģ **Output impedance:** $Z_{out} = R_C \vert\vert r_o \approx R_C$ ŌĆö **independent** of $R_E$.
- Ō£ģ **Current gain:** $A_i = \beta$ (in CE) ŌĆö the highest among BJT configurations.
- Ō£ģ **Power gain:** $A_p = \vert A_v \vert \cdot \vert A_i \vert$ ŌĆö maximum in CE configuration.
- Ō£ģ The **bypass capacitor $C_E$** is selected such that its reactance at the lowest signal frequency is $\ll R_E$, i.e., $X_{C_E} = 1/(2\pi f_L C_E) \le R_E / 10$.
- Ō£ģ Always check the **DC Q-point** ($I_C$, $V_{CE}$) before doing AC analysis ŌĆö the small-signal parameters **depend entirely** on the Q-point.
- Ō£ģ The hybrid-ŽĆ model is **invalid at high frequencies** (above $f_\beta$) ŌĆö junction capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ must be added for high-frequency analysis.
- Ō£ģ **Phase:** CE gives **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output ŌĆö useful in oscillator and feedback design.
- Ō£ģ The **sign convention** in the dependent source: $i_c = g_m v_{be}$ flows **into the collector** (i.e., from C to E in the model), creating a voltage drop that makes $v_o$ **opposite in sign** to $v_i$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ­¤öĘ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **High-Frequency Equivalent Circuit of a BJT** is a small-signal linear model of the Bipolar Junction Transistor that accurately represents its dynamic behavior when the input signal frequency is high enough that the internal capacitive (charge-storage) effects of the device can no longer be neglected. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this is implemented through the **Hybrid-ŽĆ Model** (proposed by L. J. Giacoletto), augmented with the base-spreading resistance, the baseŌĆōemitter diffusion + depletion capacitance $C_{\pi}$, and the baseŌĆōcollector (Miller) capacitance $C_{\mu}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** At low frequencies, the BJT is modeled by the simplified h-parameter equivalent circuit (resistors only). As frequency rises, the **internal PN-junction capacitances** $C_{\pi}$ and $C_{\mu}$ become significant because their reactances ($1/j\omega C$) become comparable to the resistive impedances of the device. This shifts the modeling requirement from the *low-frequency* model to the *high-frequency* hybrid-ŽĆ model.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Bucket & Pipe" Model

Imagine a BJT as a **water-tap system**:

- The **base current** $I_B$ is the small force you apply to the handle.
- The **collector current** $I_C$ is the powerful jet of water coming out.
- The **amplification** ($\beta$) is the leverage ratio between the handle-force and the jet.

At **low frequencies (DC)**, the water simply flows; we only need to model the *resistive* pressure drops. But at **high frequencies**, the handle is being wiggled so fast that:

1. The water has **inertia** ŌĆö it cannot start and stop instantly (this is $C_{\pi}$, the *diffusion capacitance* of the base-emitter junction storing minority carriers).
2. The pipe walls **flex** ŌĆö a sudden change in collector voltage pushes back on the base side (this is $C_{\mu}$, the *junction depletion capacitance* of the reverse-biased base-collector diode).

So the high-frequency model is simply the low-frequency model **plus two tiny "inertia" capacitors** that dominate behavior when the signal wiggles too fast for the transistor to keep up.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{26\text{ mV}}$ at **300 K** (room temperature)
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\text{ J/K}}$
- **Electronic charge:** $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}}$
- **Magnitude of low-frequency common-emitter current gain:** $\beta_0 = h_{fe}$ (typically **50ŌĆō300** for a small-signal BJT)
- **Standard units:** $g_m$ in Siemens (A/V), $r_\pi$ in Ohms ($\Omega$), $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ in picofarads (pF) or femtofarads (fF), frequencies in Hz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams:** The hybrid-ŽĆ parameters are *directly* related to the operating point (Q-point) of the BJT. Thus, the high-frequency model is not fixed; **$g_m$ and $r_\pi$ change the moment the bias current $I_C$ changes.** This is the #1 reason why bias-design is so crucial in RF amplifier stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ­¤öĘ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a New Model at High Frequencies? ŌĆö The Logical Chain

The standard textbook progression of small-signal BJT modeling follows this reasoning chain:

1. **Step 1 ŌĆö DC Analysis:** The Q-point ($I_C$, $I_B$, $V_{CE}$) is fixed by the bias network using the Ebers-Moll / large-signal equations.
2. **Step 2 ŌĆö Low-Frequency AC Model:** The BJT is replaced by its **h-parameter (or simplified hybrid-ŽĆ) equivalent**, which contains only resistors: $r_\pi$, $\beta \cdot i_b$ current source, and $r_o$. This is valid while $\omega \to 0$.
3. **Step 3 ŌĆö The Frequency Limitation:** Real BJTs contain two charge-storage mechanisms:
   - **Diffusion capacitance $C_d$** across the forward-biased base-emitter junction (minority-carrier storage in the base).
   - **Depletion capacitance $C_j$** across the reverse-biased base-collector junction.
4. **Step 4 ŌĆö The Hybrid-ŽĆ High-Frequency Model:** These two capacitances are lumped as $C_\pi$ (Ōēł $C_d + C_{je}$) and $C_\mu$ (Ōēł $C_{jc}$), and inserted into the low-frequency model.
5. **Step 5 ŌĆö The Cutoff Frequencies:** The capacitors introduce poles in the transfer function, leading to a frequency-dependent current gain and a 3-dB cutoff frequency.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Element

| Element | Physical Origin | "Why" Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| $r_\pi$ | Emitter-base dynamic resistance | Models the resistive path between base and emitter for small AC signals; $r_\pi = V_T/I_B = \beta/g_m$. |
| $g_m v_{be}$ (current source) | Transconductance | The fundamental *active* property: a small change in $V_{BE}$ produces a change in $I_C$ given by $g_m = I_C/V_T$. |
| $r_x$ | Base spreading resistance | The ohmic resistance of the *bulk* semiconductor between the base terminal and the active base region. Always in series with the base lead. |
| $C_\pi$ | Diffusion + BE depletion cap | The "inertia" of minority carriers crossing the base plus the depletion-region charge variation at the emitter junction. |
| $C_\mu$ | BC depletion capacitance | The feedback capacitance from collector to base. Through **Miller's theorem**, it gets multiplied by voltage gain, dominating the high-frequency roll-off. |
| $r_o$ | Output resistance (Early effect) | Often *neglected* at high frequencies in introductory KTU problems for simplicity. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table.** Every KTU ESE and Module Internal question on this topic maps directly to one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance | A/V (S) |
| 2 | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \dfrac{V_T}{I_B}$ | BaseŌĆōemitter input resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | $r_x$ | Base-spreading resistance (given or neglected) | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | $C_\pi = C_{de} + C_{je}$ | BaseŌĆōemitter capacitance (diffusion + depletion) | F |
| 5 | $C_\mu = C_{\mu b} + C_{jc}$ | BaseŌĆōcollector capacitance | F |
| 6 | $A_i(s) = \dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}$ | Short-circuit CE current gain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | $\beta$-cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | Transition (unity-gain) frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $f_\alpha = \dfrac{\beta_0}{\beta_0 + 1} f_\alpha^{(\text{CB})} = (\beta_0+1) f_\beta$ | $\alpha$-cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)$ | Miller capacitance (CE amplifier) | F |
| 11 | $R_B = r_\pi \,\Vert\, (r_x + R_S)$ | Effective base resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 12 | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\, R_B \, C_{in}}$, $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_M$ | Upper 3-dB frequency (CE with $R_L$) | Hz |
| 13 | GBW $= A_{mid} \cdot f_H$ | Gain-bandwidth product | Hz |
| 14 | $A_{mid} = -g_m R_L \cdot \dfrac{r_\pi}{r_\pi + r_x + R_S}$ | Midband voltage gain of CE stage | dimensionless |

> **Notation rule used in KTU papers:** $\beta$ without subscript is the *low-frequency* current gain $\beta_0$. A frequency-dependent gain is written $\beta(jf)$ or $\beta(\omega)$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RF/Microwave Amplifier Design:** The transition frequency $f_T$ is the *primary figure of merit* for choosing a BJT in RF front-ends. Modern high-$f_T$ RF transistors have $f_T$ in the **GHz range** (e.g., 25 GHz, 60 GHz).
- **Broadband Amplifier Design:** The gain-bandwidth product (GBW) is *constant* for a given transistor and load. Designers trade voltage gain for bandwidth by choosing a smaller $R_L$.
- **Oscillator & Switching Circuit Design:** Knowing $f_T$ prevents accidental operation in the lossy region near cutoff; in switching applications, $C_\mu$ directly sets the storage and fall times.
- **SPICE Modeling:** The hybrid-ŽĆ model is the *direct basis* for the SPICE `.model` BJT card (parameters `BF`, `VAF`, `CJE`, `CJC`, `RB`, etc.).
- **Communication Receivers:** IF (intermediate-frequency) amplifiers at **455 kHz** and **10.7 MHz** must operate *far below* $f_T$ to maintain linear gain, which constrains BJT selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ­¤öĘ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** Every step in the derivation must be shown. Marks are awarded for *clear logical transitions*, not just the final equation. Follow the structure below exactly.

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between Transconductance $g_m$ and BaseŌĆōEmitter Resistance $r_\pi$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start from the Ebers-Moll collector current equation** (assume $V_{BE} \gg V_T$ so the ŌĆō1 is dropped):

$$
I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate $I_C$ with respect to $V_{BE}$ to obtain the small-signal transconductance:**

$$
g_m \equiv \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\right|_{Q} = \frac{I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}}{V_T} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define $r_\pi$ as the small-signal input resistance seen looking into the base (with collector shorted to AC ground):**

$$
r_\pi = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Note that $i_c = g_m v_{be}$ and $i_c = \beta_0 i_b$ in the low-frequency model. Equate these two expressions for $i_c$:**

$$
g_m v_{be} = \beta_0 i_b
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Solve for $v_{be}/i_b$:**

$$
\frac{v_{be}}{i_b} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Hence the closed-form relation:**

$$
\boxed{r_\pi = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m}}
$$

> **Sanity check:** A BJT with $I_C = 1\text{ mA}$ and $\beta_0 = 100$ gives $g_m = (1\text{ mA})/(26\text{ mV}) = 38.46\text{ mA/V}$ and $r_\pi = 100/0.03846 = 2.6\text{ k}\Omega$. Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Short-Circuit Common-Emitter Current Gain $A_i = i_c/i_b$

We short the output terminals so $v_{ce} = 0$. The small-signal circuit reduces to: $i_b$ enters $r_\pi$ in parallel with $C_\pi$, with the current source $g_m v_{be}$ in the collector branch, and $C_\mu$ between base and collector. Since the output is shorted, $C_\mu$ is effectively in parallel with $C_\pi$ at the input.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write KCL at the base node (input mesh):**

$$
i_b = \frac{v_{be}}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi v_{be} + j\omega C_\mu v_{be}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Factor out $v_{be}$:**

$$
i_b = v_{be}\left[\frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega(C_\pi + C_\mu)\right]
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Solve for $v_{be}$ in terms of $i_b$:**

$$
v_{be} = \frac{i_b}{\dfrac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega(C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Write the collector current. The current source gives $g_m v_{be}$, and the short-circuit also carries the current through $C_\mu$. The total short-circuit output current is:**

$$
i_c = g_m v_{be} - j\omega C_\mu v_{be} = v_{be}\,(g_m - j\omega C_\mu)
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute $v_{be}$ from Step 3. (For $f \ll f_T$, the term $j\omega C_\mu$ is negligible compared to $g_m$, since $g_m \gg \omega C_\mu$ in this regime. We therefore keep only the dominant $g_m v_{be}$ term.) This gives the standard KTU result:**

$$
A_i = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{g_m}{\dfrac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega(C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j\omega \, r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Identify the $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$ as the pole:**

$$
f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi \, r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

> **Final form (boxed for KTU answer script):**

$$
\boxed{A_i(j\omega) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}}
$$

> **Significance:** This is a *single-pole low-pass* response. The magnitude $|A_i| = \beta_0 / \sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}$ rolls off at **ŌĆō20 dB/decade** beyond $f_\beta$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Transition Frequency $f_T$ (Unity-Gain Bandwidth)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Set the magnitude of the short-circuit current gain to unity:**

$$
|A_i| = \frac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_\beta)^2}} = 1
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö At $f \gg f_\beta$, the "1" inside the square root can be dropped:**

$$
\frac{\beta_0}{f_T / f_\beta} \approx 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_T \approx \beta_0 \, f_\beta
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $f_\beta = 1/[2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ and $\beta_0 = g_m r_\pi$:**

$$
f_T = \beta_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m r_\pi}{2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}}
$$

> **Crucial KTU insight:** $f_T$ is *independent of $\beta_0$*. Doubling $\beta_0$ does not double $f_T$ ŌĆö it merely *reduces* $r_\pi$ in the same proportion, leaving $g_m$ (set by $I_C$) as the only knob. **To increase $f_T$, increase the quiescent collector current $I_C$.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Miller's Theorem Applied to $C_\mu$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Statement of Miller's Theorem:** An impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes where the voltage gain (from input node to output node) is $K = v_o/v_i$ can be replaced by two impedances to ground:

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{Z}{1 - K} \quad \text{(referred to input)}, \qquad Z_{out} = \frac{Z \cdot K}{K - 1} \quad \text{(referred to output)}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö In the CE stage, $Z = 1/(j\omega C_\mu)$ bridges base and collector. The voltage gain from base to collector is approximately $K = -g_m R_L$ at midband.**

**Step 3 ŌĆö Input-referred impedance of $C_\mu$:**

$$
Z_{in,\mu} = \frac{1/(j\omega C_\mu)}{1 - (-g_m R_L)} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö This is equivalent to a new capacitance $C_M$ to ground at the input node:**

$$
C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)
$$

$$
\boxed{C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)}
$$

> **Output-referred capacitance** $C_{M,out} \approx C_\mu (1 + 1/g_m R_L) \approx C_\mu$ (negligible change), so it is usually ignored.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$ of a CE Stage (with Source Resistance $R_S$ and Load $R_L$)

**Step 1 ŌĆö The total input capacitance is the parallel combination of $C_\pi$ and the Miller capacitance:**

$$
C_{in} = C_\pi + C_M = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö The total resistance seen by $C_{in}$ at the base node is the parallel combination of $r_\pi$ and the Th├®venin resistance looking back into the source (including $r_x$):**

$$
R_B = r_\pi \parallel (R_S + r_x)
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö The upper 3-dB frequency of this single-pole network is:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_B C_{in}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi [\,r_\pi \parallel (R_S + r_x)\,]\,[\,C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L)\,]}}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö The midband voltage gain is:**

$$
A_{mid} = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = -g_m R_L \cdot \frac{r_\pi}{r_\pi + r_x + R_S}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
\boxed{\text{GBW} = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H}
$$

> **Key qualitative result:** $f_H$ is set by the **slowest** of two poles, but in most practical CE designs, the Miller-multiplied $C_\mu$ dominates ŌĆö this is why $r_x$ and $C_\mu$ are the *most important* parasitic elements in high-frequency design.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** $I_C = 5\text{ mA}$, $\beta_0 = 150$, $C_\pi = 20\text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\text{ pF}$, $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $r_x = 100\,\Omega$, $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$. Find $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $f_\beta$, $f_T$, $C_M$, $f_H$, and GBW.

**Solution Table (one entry per logical step):**

| Step | Quantity | Formula | Substitution | Numerical Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_m$ | $I_C/V_T$ | $(5 \times 10^{-3})/(26 \times 10^{-3})$ | $0.1923\text{ A/V} = 192.3\text{ mS}$ |
| 2 | $r_\pi$ | $\beta_0/g_m$ | $150/0.1923$ | $780\,\Omega$ |
| 3 | $f_\beta$ | $1/[2\pi r_\pi(C_\pi+C_\mu)]$ | $1/[2\pi(780)(22\text{ pF})]$ | $9.27\text{ MHz}$ |
| 4 | $f_T$ | $g_m/[2\pi(C_\pi+C_\mu)]$ | $0.1923/[2\pi(22\text{ pF})]$ | $1.39\text{ GHz}$ |
| 5 | $g_m R_L$ | ŌĆö | $0.1923 \times 5000$ | $961.5$ |
| 6 | $C_M$ | $C_\mu(1+g_mR_L)$ | $2\text{ pF} \times 962.5$ | $1.925\text{ nF}$ |
| 7 | $C_{in}$ | $C_\pi + C_M$ | $20\text{ pF} + 1925\text{ pF}$ | $1.945\text{ nF}$ |
| 8 | $R_S + r_x$ | ŌĆö | $1000 + 100$ | $1100\,\Omega$ |
| 9 | $R_B$ | $r_\pi \parallel (R_S + r_x)$ | $780 \times 1100 / (780+1100)$ | $456.4\,\Omega$ |
| 10 | $f_H$ | $1/(2\pi R_B C_{in})$ | $1/[2\pi(456.4)(1.945\text{ nF})]$ | $179.1\text{ kHz}$ |
| 11 | $A_{mid}$ | $-g_m R_L \cdot r_\pi/(r_\pi+R_S+r_x)$ | $-961.5 \times 780/1880$ | $-399$ |
| 12 | GBW | $\vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H$ | $399 \times 179.1\text{ kHz}$ | $71.5\text{ MHz}$ |

> **Observation:** The Miller effect has blown $C_\mu$ from 2 pF to **1.925 nF** ŌĆö almost **1000├Ś increase**. This single phenomenon is the bottleneck of every CE amplifier.

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Complete Hybrid-ŽĆ High-Frequency Model

```mermaid
graph LR

    nodeBS -- r pi base emitter resistance --> nodeE
    nodeBS -- C pi diffusion plus depletion cap --> nodeE
    nodeBS -- C mu base collector feedback cap --> nodeC

    nodeC -- r o output resistance --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeOut
```

> **Reading guide:** Apply a small-signal input $v_{be}$ between nodes B' and E. The dependent current source $g_m v_{be}$ flows from C to E. The capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ shunt their respective nodes at high frequency. The output voltage $v_o$ is taken from C with respect to E.

## 4.5 Component-Location Map (Textual Schematic)

| Component | Physical Location in the BJT |
|---|---|
| $r_x$ | Between the external base lead and the active intrinsic base region (bulk semiconductor resistance) |
| $r_\pi$ | Across the forward-biased base-emitter junction |
| $g_m v_{be}$ | Active transconductance element (current source from C to E, controlled by $v_{be}$) |
| $C_\pi$ | Directly across the base-emitter junction (diffusion + depletion parts) |
| $C_\mu$ | Across the base-collector junction (depletion capacitance, the feedback element) |
| $r_o$ | Across the output (collectorŌĆōemitter) ŌĆö due to the Early effect |
