# ­¤öĘ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Define the transition frequency $f_T$ of a BJT. Derive its expression in terms of $g_m$, $C_\pi$, and $C_\mu$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **transition frequency $f_T$** is defined as the frequency at which the magnitude of the common-emitter short-circuit current gain $\vert A_i \vert$ becomes **unity** (i.e., 0 dB).
>
> From the short-circuit gain expression derived in Section 3.2:
>
> $$A_i(j\omega) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}$$
>
> For $f \gg f_\beta$, the magnitude reduces to $\vert A_i \vert \approx \beta_0 f_\beta / f$. Setting $\vert A_i \vert = 1$ gives $f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta$. Substituting $f_\beta = 1/[2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ and using $\beta_0 = g_m r_\pi$:
>
> $$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$
>
> $f_T$ is the **figure of merit** that limits the highest usable frequency of the BJT. (3 marks ŌĆö 1 for definition, 1 for derivation step, 1 for final boxed formula.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Adapted]**
**What is the significance of the Miller capacitance in a common-emitter amplifier? Write its expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Miller capacitance** $C_M$ is the equivalent input capacitance that results from the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ being "amplified" by the voltage gain of the CE stage, through **Miller's theorem**.
>
> The voltage gain from base to collector is approximately $K = -g_m R_L$ at midband. The feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ between base and collector appears, when referred to the input node, as a much larger capacitance to ground:
>
> $$C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)$$
>
> **Significance:** It **dominates** the input capacitance of the CE amplifier, drastically reducing the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. A 2 pF feedback capacitor can become 1 nF at the input, which is why CE stages have poor high-frequency response compared to CB or cascode stages. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions with KTU Internal Choice

### ­¤ōī Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 2, Model Paper]**
**For a BJT used in a common-emitter amplifier, the following data is given: $I_C = 2\text{ mA}$, $\beta_0 = 120$, $C_\pi = 15\text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 2\text{ pF}$, $r_x = 50\,\Omega$, $V_T = 26\text{ mV}$, $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, and $R_L = 4\text{ k}\Omega$.**

**(a)** Draw the complete high-frequency hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent circuit and label every element. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the midband voltage gain, the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$, and the gain-bandwidth product. State whether this stage is suitable for an audio amplifier (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Hybrid-ŽĆ High-Frequency Model (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $g_m$:**

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769\text{ A/V} = 76.9\text{ mS}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $r_\pi$:**

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.0769} = 1560\,\Omega \approx 1.56\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö List of elements (draw with these values):**

| Element | Value |
|---|---|
| $r_x$ | $50\,\Omega$ (between B and B') |
| $r_\pi$ | $1.56\text{ k}\Omega$ (B' to E) |
| $C_\pi$ | $15\text{ pF}$ (B' to E) |
| $C_\mu$ | $2\text{ pF}$ (B' to C) |
| $g_m v_{be}$ current source | $76.9\,v_{be}\text{ mA}$ (C to E) |
| $R_S$ | $600\,\Omega$ (in series with input) |
| $R_L$ | $4\text{ k}\Omega$ (at the output) |

**Description of circuit (text equivalent for answer script):** The model has the source $v_s$ feeding through $R_S$ and $r_x$ to the internal base node B'. From B' to E, $r_\pi$ and $C_\pi$ are in parallel. From B' to the collector node C, the capacitor $C_\mu$ is connected. A voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$ flows from C to E, and the load $R_L$ is connected from C to E.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Midband Gain, $f_H$, and GBW (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Midband voltage gain:**

$$
A_{mid} = -g_m R_L \cdot \frac{r_\pi}{r_\pi + r_x + R_S} = -(0.0769)(4000) \cdot \frac{1560}{1560 + 50 + 600}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Simplify the factor:**

$$
\frac{1560}{2210} = 0.7059
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $A_{mid}$:**

$$
A_{mid} = -307.6 \times 0.7059 = -217.1
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute Miller capacitance:**

$$
C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L) = 2\text{ pF} \times (1 + 307.6) = 2 \times 308.6\text{ pF} = 617.2\text{ pF}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Total input capacitance:**

$$
C_{in} = C_\pi + C_M = 15 + 617.2 = 632.2\text{ pF}
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Effective base resistance:**

$$
R_B = r_\pi \parallel (R_S + r_x) = \frac{1560 \times 650}{1560 + 650} = \frac{1{,}014{,}000}{2210} = 458.8\,\Omega
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Upper cutoff frequency:**

$$
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_B C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 458.8 \times 632.2 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.823 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 548.5\text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Gain-bandwidth product:**

$$
\text{GBW} = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = 217.1 \times 548.5\text{ kHz} \approx 119.1\text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 9 ŌĆö Audio-band suitability check:**

> The required audio bandwidth is 20 Hz to **20 kHz**. Our computed $f_H \approx 548.5\text{ kHz}$ is **far above** 20 kHz, so the stage easily covers the audio band. **Conclusion: The stage IS suitable for an audio amplifier.** (1 mark reserved for the conclusion.)

---

### ­¤ōī Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**(a)** Starting from the hybrid-ŽĆ model, derive the expression for the short-circuit common-emitter current gain $A_i = i_c/i_b$ and identify the $\beta$-cutoff frequency $f_\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BJT has $g_m = 50\text{ mS}$, $C_\pi = 10\text{ pF}$, and $C_\mu = 1.5\text{ pF}$. Compute $f_\beta$ and $f_T$ if $\beta_0 = 100$. If the load resistance is doubled, by what factor does the Miller-multiplied input capacitance change? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation of $A_i$ and $f_\beta$ (7 marks)

(Refer to Section 3.2 for the full derivation. The answer script must show all six steps.)

**Final boxed result:**

$$
A_i(j\omega) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}, \qquad f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}
$$

(Mark distribution: 1 mark for stating the model, 2 marks for KCL equations, 2 marks for algebraic simplification, 1 mark for the boxed $A_i$, 1 mark for the boxed $f_\beta$.)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $r_\pi$:**

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.05} = 2000\,\Omega = 2\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute $f_\beta$:**

$$
f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times (10 + 1.5) \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 11.5\text{ pF}} = 6.92\text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $f_T$:**

$$
f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta = 100 \times 6.92\text{ MHz} = 692\text{ MHz}
$$

(Alternatively, $f_T = g_m/[2\pi(C_\pi+C_\mu)] = 0.05/[2\pi \times 11.5\text{ pF}] = 692\text{ MHz}$. Ō£ō)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Original Miller capacitance (assume initial $R_L = R_{L1}$):**

$$
C_{M1} = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_{L1}) = 1.5\text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.05 \times R_{L1})
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Doubled load $R_{L2} = 2R_{L1}$:**

$$
C_{M2} = 1.5\text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.05 \times 2R_{L1}) = 1.5\text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.1 R_{L1})
$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Ratio:**

$$
\frac{C_{M2}}{C_{M1}} = \frac{1 + 0.1 R_{L1}}{1 + 0.05 R_{L1}}
$$

**Step 7 ŌĆö Numerical example (take $R_{L1} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ for illustration):**

$$
C_{M1} = 1.5\text{ pF} \times 51 = 76.5\text{ pF}, \quad C_{M2} = 1.5\text{ pF} \times 101 = 151.5\text{ pF}
$$

$$
\frac{C_{M2}}{C_{M1}} = \frac{151.5}{76.5} \approx 1.98
$$

> **Conclusion:** Doubling the load *approximately doubles* the Miller-multiplied input capacitance (when $g_m R_L \gg 1$, which is the usual case). The factor approaches 2 in the high-gain limit. (1 mark for the conclusion.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $C_\mu$ in $f_\beta$.** Many students write $f_\beta = 1/(2\pi r_\pi C_\pi)$ and miss the $C_\mu$ term. Always write $f_\beta = 1/[2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$. **(ŌĆō1 to ŌĆō2 marks)**
> 2. **Using $C_M$ alone in $f_H$ instead of $C_\pi + C_M$.** The total input capacitance is the *sum* of $C_\pi$ and the Miller capacitance. **(ŌĆō1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing $\beta_0$ with $h_{fe}$.** In KTU notation, $\beta_0$ is the *low-frequency* CE current gain ŌĆö they are equal in magnitude, but you must use the correct symbol for the question's context.
> 4. **Neglecting $r_x$ in part (a) but using it in part (b).** Be consistent. If $r_x$ is given, include it in both the equivalent circuit and the gain formula.
> 5. **Units in $f_T$ computation:** $g_m$ in A/V, $C_\pi + C_\mu$ in F. Always convert pF to F before dividing. **(ŌĆō1 mark if forgotten)**
> 6. **Not boxing the final answer.** KTU examiners award 0.5ŌĆō1 mark for clearly highlighted final expressions.
> 7. **Mixing up $f_\beta$ and $f_T$.** $f_\beta$ is the **ŌĆō3 dB cutoff** of $\beta$. $f_T$ is the **unity-gain** frequency. $f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta$, **not** $f_T = f_\beta/\beta_0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision before entering the KTU exam hall:**

- Ō£ģ **Hybrid-ŽĆ model** includes $r_\pi$, $g_m v_{be}$ current source, $r_x$, $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, and optionally $r_o$.
- Ō£ģ **$g_m = I_C/V_T$** ŌĆö increases linearly with collector current.
- Ō£ģ **$r_\pi = \beta_0/g_m = V_T/I_B$** ŌĆö decreases as $I_C$ increases.
- Ō£ģ **$C_\pi$** = diffusion + BE depletion capacitance. **$C_\mu$** = BC depletion capacitance (feedback).
- Ō£ģ **Short-circuit CE current gain:** $A_i = \beta_0 / [1 + j(f/f_\beta)]$.
- Ō£ģ **$\beta$-cutoff frequency:** $f_\beta = 1/[2\pi r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$.
- Ō£ģ **Transition frequency:** $f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta = g_m/[2\pi(C_\pi+C_\mu)]$ ŌĆö independent of $\beta_0$.
- Ō£ģ **Miller's theorem:** $C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)$ ŌĆö the dominant high-frequency limitation in CE stages.
- Ō£ģ **Upper 3-dB frequency (CE with source and load):** $f_H = 1/[2\pi R_B C_{in}]$, where $R_B = r_\pi \parallel (R_S + r_x)$ and $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_M$.
- Ō£ģ **Midband voltage gain:** $A_{mid} = -g_m R_L \cdot r_\pi/(r_\pi + r_x + R_S)$.
- Ō£ģ **GBW = $|A_{mid}| \cdot f_H$** is a *constant* for a given transistor and bias.
- Ō£ģ **To increase $f_T$**, increase $I_C$ (this raises $g_m$). **To increase $f_H$**, reduce $R_L$ or use a CB/cascode configuration.
- Ō£ģ **CE stages have poor high-frequency response** due to Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$. **CB stages do not suffer from Miller effect** because their voltage gain is near unity.
- Ō£ģ Always express capacitances in **farads (F)** when computing frequencies in Hz. Convert pF ŌåÆ F by multiplying by $10^{-12}$.

> **­¤¤ó Best of luck for your KTU ESE! Keep this checklist open during your last revision pass.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Miller Effect
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Miller Effect

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Miller Effect** is a fundamental phenomenon in electronic circuit theory that describes the multiplication of an effective input capacitance due to the presence of a feedback capacitance (or impedance) connected between the input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier. This effect is mathematically formalized through **Miller's Theorem** (proposed by John Milton Miller in 1920), which states that an impedance $Z_f$ bridging the input and output terminals of an inverting gain stage can be replaced by two equivalent shunt impedances ŌĆö one at the input and one at the output ŌĆö whose values are scaled by the voltage gain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Miller Effect is a high-weightage topic in Solid State Devices (PEEET417) and is essential for understanding the high-frequency limitations of **BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)** and **FET (Field Effect Transistor)** amplifiers, particularly the reduction in bandwidth due to the **Miller Capacitance** appearing between the drain/collector and the gate/base terminals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants/Standard Metrics Used in this Topic:**
> - Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - In silicon-based MOSFETs, typical oxide capacitance $C_{ox} \approx 10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ F/cm┬▓
> - Miller Multiplication Factor for typical CE/CS amplifiers: $1 + \vert A_v \vert \approx 50$ to $200$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö Intuitive Explanation

Imagine you are pulling a **rubber band** stretched between two points ŌĆö one attached to a fixed wall and the other to a moving lever. If the lever moves a small amount but pulls a **lot of string** from a spool attached to the wall, the amount of string that gets pulled from the spool appears much larger than the actual lever movement.

Similarly, in an **inverting amplifier**, a small change $\Delta V_{in}$ at the input node causes a much larger (and inverted) change $A_v \cdot \Delta V_{in}$ at the output node. The voltage **across** the feedback capacitor $C_f$ is therefore:

$$\Delta V_{across} = V_{in} - V_{out} = V_{in} - A_v V_{in} = V_{in}(1 - A_v)$$

Since $A_v$ is negative for an inverting amplifier, the voltage across $C_f$ is magnified by a factor of $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. The **charge** that must flow through the capacitor is also magnified, making the capacitor appear much larger at the input. This is the **Miller Effect** in its essence ŌĆö the apparent magnification of capacitance.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Think of the Miller Effect as a "lever" ŌĆö a small input signal yanks the output, and that movement pulls extra charge off the input capacitor. The input "sees" a capacitance far bigger than what is physically present.

---

## 1.3 Why is the Miller Effect Important?

The Miller Effect is critical because it:

1. **Reduces the bandwidth** of amplifiers by introducing a dominant low-frequency pole.
2. **Limits high-frequency performance** of common-emitter (CE) and common-source (CS) amplifiers.
3. **Influences the choice of transistor configuration** ŌĆö common-collector (CC) and common-drain (CD) stages avoid severe Miller multiplication, which is why they are used as **buffers**.
4. **Affects switching speed** in digital circuits and high-speed analog design.
5. **Motivates the use of bootstrapping** and **cascode topologies** to neutralize the Miller capacitance.

---

## 1.4 Prerequisite Concepts (Recall)

Before proceeding, ensure you understand the following:

- **Inverting amplifier** configuration and its voltage gain $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$ (negative value).
- **KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law)** and **KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law)** in the frequency domain.
- **Impedance** of a capacitor: $Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$.
- **Two-port network analysis** ŌĆö input port and output port.
- **BJT/FET small-signal models** including $C_{bc}$ (collector-base) and $C_{gd}$ (gate-drain) parasitic capacitances.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Miller's Theorem ŌĆö Formal Statement

**Miller's Theorem** states that for an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ and an impedance $Z$ connected between the input node and the output node, the impedance $Z$ can be replaced by two equivalent shunt impedances:

- $Z_1$ connected from the **input** node to **ground**.
- $Z_2$ connected from the **output** node to **ground**.

Where:

$$Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \quad \text{...(Miller Input Impedance)}$$

$$Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1} \quad \text{...(Miller Output Impedance)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For an inverting amplifier, $A_v$ is negative.** Therefore, $1 - A_v = 1 + \vert A_v \vert$, which is a large positive number, making $Z_1$ very small (i.e., the effective input impedance is much smaller than $Z$).

---

## 2.2 Derivation Logic ŌĆö Step-by-Step Reasoning

### Step 1: Original Circuit Setup

Consider an inverting amplifier with input voltage $V_1$, output voltage $V_2$, voltage gain $A_v = V_2 / V_1$, and an impedance $Z$ connecting the input to the output.

### Step 2: Current Flowing Through Z

The current flowing from input to output through the impedance $Z$ is:

$$I_Z = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z} = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z}$$

### Step 3: Current Flowing Into Z1 (Input Side)

If we replace $Z$ with $Z_1$ from input to ground, the current through $Z_1$ is:

$$I_{Z_1} = \frac{V_1}{Z_1}$$

For the input currents to be equivalent:

$$\frac{V_1}{Z_1} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$$

### Step 4: Current Flowing Into Z2 (Output Side)

If we replace $Z$ with $Z_2$ from output to ground, the current through $Z_2$ is:

$$I_{Z_2} = \frac{V_2}{Z_2}$$

For the output currents to be equivalent:

$$\frac{V_2}{Z_2} = \frac{V_2(1 - 1/A_v)}{Z} = \frac{V_2(A_v - 1)}{Z \cdot A_v}$$

Solving:

$$Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$$

---

## 2.3 Miller Multiplication for Capacitors

When the feedback element is a **capacitor** $C_f$, the impedance is $Z = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f}$. Substituting into Miller's Theorem:

### Input Side (Miller Capacitance):

$$Z_{1} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f (1 - A_v)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{M,in} = C_f (1 - A_v)$$

### Output Side (Miller Output Capacitance):

$$Z_{2} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f} \cdot \frac{A_v}{A_v - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{M,out} = C_f \left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}\right)$$

For a typical inverting amplifier with $A_v \ll -1$:

$$C_{M,in} \approx C_f (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

$$C_{M,out} \approx C_f$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** For high-gain inverting amplifiers, the input-side Miller capacitance is **multiplied by the gain** $1 + \vert A_v \vert$, while the output-side Miller capacitance is approximately equal to the original $C_f$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Typical Units** | **Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Voltage gain of inverting stage | Dimensionless | $A_v < 0$ for inverting |
| 2 | $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ | Miller equivalent input impedance | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Valid for any $Z$ |
| 3 | $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ | Miller equivalent output impedance | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Valid for any $Z$ |
| 4 | $C_{M,in} = C_f (1 - A_v)$ | Miller input capacitance | Farads (F) | $A_v$ negative |
| 5 | $C_{M,eff} \approx C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Effective input capacitance (inverting) | Farads (F) | $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$ |
| 6 | $C_{M,out} = C_f \cdot \dfrac{A_v - 1}{A_v}$ | Miller output capacitance | Farads (F) | $A_v$ negative |
| 7 | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{M,in}}$ | Upper -3dB cutoff frequency | Hertz (Hz) | Single-pole approximation |
| 8 | $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_\mu)$ | Unity-gain frequency (BJT) | Hertz (Hz) | $C_\mu$ is $C_{bc}$ |
| 9 | $C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_{M,in}$ | Total input capacitance | Farads (F) | BJT CE amplifier |
| 10 | $C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$ | Total input capacitance (MOSFET CS) | Farads (F) | Small-signal regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Examinations:** In **BJT** notation, $C_\mu$ refers to the **base-collector capacitance** $C_{bc}$, and $C_\pi$ refers to the **base-emitter capacitance** $C_{be}$. In **MOSFET** notation, $C_{gd}$ is the **gate-drain overlap capacitance** and $C_{gs}$ is the **gate-source capacitance**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Miller Effect plays a critical role in:

1. **RF and Microwave Amplifier Design** ŌĆö Determines the high-frequency cutoff and gain-bandwidth product.
2. **Operational Amplifier Internal Design** ŌĆö The Miller compensation capacitor inside op-amps deliberately uses this effect for **frequency compensation** (polesplitting).
3. **High-Speed Digital Logic** ŌĆö Limits the switching speed of CMOS inverters and TTL gates.
4. **Cascode Amplifier Design** ŌĆö Used to suppress Miller multiplication by reducing the effective gain seen by $C_{gd}$.
5. **Bootstrapping** ŌĆö A technique that effectively cancels Miller multiplication by driving both ends of the feedback capacitor in phase.
6. **Wideband Amplifiers** ŌĆö Techniques like the **distributed amplifier** (traveling-wave amplifier) were developed specifically to circumvent the Miller Effect.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When asked "Why is the common-collector (emitter follower) configuration used as a buffer?", the examiner expects you to mention that the voltage gain $A_v \approx 1$, which makes the Miller multiplication factor $(1 - A_v) \approx 0$, effectively **eliminating** the Miller Effect.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Miller's Theorem

### Setup

Consider a two-port linear network with the following parameters:
- Input voltage: $V_1$ at node 1
- Output voltage: $V_2$ at node 2
- Voltage gain: $A_v = V_2 / V_1$ (negative for inverting amplifier)
- Feedback impedance: $Z$ connected between nodes 1 and 2

### Step 1: Identify the Current Through the Feedback Impedance

Using Ohm's Law in the frequency domain, the current flowing from node 1 to node 2 through $Z$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_Z &= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Express V2 in Terms of V1

Since $V_2 = A_v \cdot V_1$, substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_Z &= \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z} \\[4pt]
I_Z &= \frac{V_1 (1 - A_v)}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Replace Z with an Equivalent Input Shunt Impedance Z1

We seek an impedance $Z_1$ connected between node 1 and ground such that the current drawn from the source is unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z_1} &= \frac{V_1}{Z_1} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $Z_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{V_1 \cdot Z}{V_1(1 - A_v)} \\[4pt]
Z_1 &= \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Replace Z with an Equivalent Output Shunt Impedance Z2

We seek an impedance $Z_2$ connected between node 2 and ground such that the current delivered to the load is unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z_2} &= \frac{V_2}{Z_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The current delivered to the load by the original network (when only $Z$ is between input and output) can be found by recognizing that the current leaving node 2 through $Z$ is $I_Z$, so the current that the amplifier must supply at its output is balanced by this $I_Z$. The Norton equivalent analysis gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z_2} &= \frac{V_2 - V_2/A_v}{Z} = \frac{V_2(1 - 1/A_v)}{Z} = \frac{V_2(A_v - 1)}{Z \cdot A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating to $\frac{V_2}{Z_2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_2}{Z_2} &= \frac{V_2(A_v - 1)}{Z \cdot A_v} \\[4pt]
Z_2 &= \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result ŌĆö Miller's Theorem

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{Z}{1 - A_v} \\[6pt]
Z_2 &= \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Miller Capacitance

### Given:
- Feedback capacitor: $C_f$ connected between input and output of an inverting amplifier.
- Voltage gain: $A_v$ (assumed negative for inverting operation).

### Step 1: Write the Impedance of the Capacitor

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{1}{j\omega C_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Miller's Theorem for the Input Side

Substituting $Z$ into the formula for $Z_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{1}{j\omega C_f (1 - A_v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the impedance of an equivalent capacitor $C_{M,in}$ connected from input to ground:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{1}{j\omega C_{M,in}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{M,in} = C_f(1 - A_v)
\end{aligned}
$$

For an inverting amplifier, $A_v$ is negative, so $1 - A_v = 1 + \vert A_v \vert$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Miller's Theorem for the Output Side

Substituting $Z$ into the formula for $Z_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &= \frac{1}{j\omega C_f} \cdot \frac{A_v}{A_v - 1} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f} \cdot \left( -\frac{A_v}{1 - A_v} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$, this simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &\approx \frac{1}{j\omega C_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the output Miller capacitance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,out} &= C_f \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{A_v} \approx C_f \quad \text{(for } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: BJT Common-Emitter Amplifier

**Problem:** A BJT in CE configuration has $C_\mu = C_{bc} = 5$ pF, $C_\pi = C_{be} = 50$ pF, transconductance $g_m = 40$ mA/V, and collector load $R_C = 2$ k$\Omega$. Calculate the Miller input capacitance and the upper -3dB frequency.

### Step 1: Calculate the Voltage Gain

The small-signal voltage gain of a CE stage (ignoring $r_o$) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= -g_m R_C = -(40 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^{3}) = -80
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Miller Input Capacitance

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} &= C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert) \\[4pt]
C_{M,in} &= 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 80) = 5 \text{ pF} \times 81 = 405 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Total Input Capacitance

The total capacitance seen at the input is the sum of $C_\pi$ and the Miller-multiplied $C_\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in,tot} &= C_\pi + C_{M,in} = 50 \text{ pF} + 405 \text{ pF} = 455 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Upper Cutoff Frequency

Assuming source resistance $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$ and using the open-circuit time-constant method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^3 \times 455 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
f_H &\approx \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.55 \times 10^{-7}} \\[4pt]
f_H &\approx 349.7 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Example:** Although $C_\mu$ is only 5 pF, the Miller Effect multiplies it to 405 pF at the input, completely dominating the input capacitance. This is why the high-frequency response of CE amplifiers is severely limited.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Common Amplifier Configurations and Miller Effect

| **Configuration** | **Voltage Gain $\vert A_v \vert$** | **Miller Multiplication Factor** | **Miller Effect Severity** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Common-Emitter (CE) / Common-Source (CS) | Large (50ŌĆō500) | $1 + \vert A_v \vert \approx 50$ŌĆō$500$ | **Severe** | Voltage amplifier |
| Common-Base (CB) / Common-Gate (CG) | $\approx 1$ (non-inverting) | $\approx 1$ | **Negligible** | High-frequency buffer |
| Common-Collector (CC) / Common-Drain (CD) | $\approx 1$ (inverting, but $\approx 0$ dB) | $\approx 1 - 0 = 1$ | **Negligible** | Impedance buffer |
| Cascode (CE+CB) | Reduced $A_v$ at top transistor | $\approx 1$ | **Suppressed** | Wideband amplifier |
| Differential Pair | $\approx$ CE gain per side | Moderate | **Moderate** | Input stage of op-amp |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The cascode amplifier configuration is a classic question because it cleverly minimizes the Miller Effect by using a common-base/gate stage that **shields** the input transistor from the large voltage swing at the output.

---

## 3.5 Bandwidth Impact and Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)

The Miller Effect is directly responsible for the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** limitation in amplifiers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GBP} = \vert A_v \mid \cdot f_H = \text{constant}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a single-pole amplifier where the dominant pole is determined by the Miller capacitance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{M,in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_f (1 + \vert A_v \vert)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The product is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GBP} &= \vert A_v \vert \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_f (1 + \vert A_v \vert)} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_f} \quad \text{(for } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that **doubling the gain halves the bandwidth**, a fundamental tradeoff in amplifier design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Miller's Theorem Conceptualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Node V1] -->|V1| B[Inverting Amplifier Stage]
    B -->|V2 = Av x V1| C[Output Node V2]
    A <-->|Feedback Impedance Z| C
    B -->|Gain -Av| D[Control Signal]
    D --> E[Input Shunt Impedance Z1 = Z / 1 - Av]
    D --> F[Output Shunt Impedance Z2 = Z x Av / Av - 1]
    E --> G[Effective Input Side]
    F --> H[Effective Output Side]
    G --> I[Effective Miller Input Capacitance CM_in]
    H --> J[Effective Miller Output Capacitance CM_out]
    I --> K[Reduced Bandwidth fH = 1 / 2pi x Rth x CM_in]
    J --> L[Modified Load Network]
```

---

## 4.2 Circuit Architecture ŌĆö BJT Common-Emitter with Miller Capacitance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputSection["Input Side"]
        VS[Vi Signal Source] --> RS[Source Resistance Rs]
        RS --> BASE[Base Node B]
    end

    subgraph AmplificationCore["BJT CE Amplifier Core"]
        BASE --> CBEMU[Base-Collector Capacitance Cmu = 5pF]
        BASE --> CPI[Base-Emitter Capacitance Cpi = 50pF]
        CPI --> EMITTER[Emitter Node E Grounded]
        BASE --> BJTDEVICE[BJT Active Device]
        BJTDEVICE --> COLLECTOR[Collector Node C]
        COLLECTOR --> RC[Collector Resistor Rc = 2k ohm]
        RC --> VCC[VCC Supply]
    end

    subgraph OutputSide["Output Side"]
        COLLECTOR --> CL[Load Capacitance CL]
        COLLECTOR --> VO[Output Voltage Vo]
    end

    subgraph MillerTransformation["Miller Effect Transformation"]
        CBEMU -.->|Multiplied by 1 + Av| CMILIN[CM_in = Cmu x 1 + Av = 405pF]
        CMILIN --> FTOTAL[CTotal_in = Cpi + CM_in = 455pF]
        FTOTAL --> F3DB[Upper Cutoff fH = 1 / 2pi x Rs x CTotal_in]
    end

    VO --> NEXTSTAGE[Next Amplifier Stage]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow ŌĆö Common Amplifier Topologies and Miller Sensitivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Amplifier Topology] --> Q1{Voltage Gain Required?}
    Q1 -->|High Gain Needed| CE[Common-Emitter Stage]
    Q1 -->|Unity Gain Buffer| CC[Common-Collector Emitter Follower]
    Q1 -->|Wideband High Frequency| CAS[Cascode Configuration]

    CE --> CE1[Miller Multiplication Factor 1 + Av ~ 50 to 500]
    CE1 --> CE2[Severe Bandwidth Limitation]
    CE2 --> CE3[Best for Low Frequency Audio Applications]

    CC --> CC1[Voltage Gain Av approx 1]
    CC1 --> CC2[Miller Multiplication Factor approx 1 to 2]
    CC2 --> CC3[Minimal Miller Effect]
    CC3 --> CC4[Best for Impedance Buffering and Level Shifting]

    CAS --> CAS1[Top Transistor Shielded from Output Swing]
    CAS1 --> CAS2[Effective Av seen by Cmu is approx 1]
    CAS2 --> CAS3[Reduced Miller Capacitance]
    CAS3 --> CAS4[Best for RF and Wideband Applications]
```

---

## 4.4 Operational Amplifier Internal Miller Compensation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OpAmp["Two-Stage Operational Amplifier"]
        DIFF[Differential Input Stage] --> GAIN[High Gain Inverting Stage Av = -100000]
        GAIN --> COMP[Miller Compensation Capacitor Cc = 30pF]
        COMP --> OUTBUF[Output Buffer Stage]
        OUTBUF --> VOP[Output Node Vo]

        DIFF -.->|Compensation Path| COMP
    end

    subgraph MillerAction["Miller Compensation Action"]
        COMP --> CMULT[CM_in_eff = Cc x 1 + Av = 30pF x 100001]
        CMULT --> POLE[Creates Dominant Low Frequency Pole]
        POLE --> STABLE[Ensures Stability and Unity Gain Stability]
    end

    STABLE --> GBP[Unity Gain Bandwidth approx 1 MHz]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Note:** The Miller capacitor inside the op-amp is **deliberately large** (typically 30 pF) to make the dominant pole appear at a very low frequency. Using Miller's Theorem, the effective input capacitance becomes enormous, ensuring a single-pole roll-off and stable operation under all feedback conditions. This is a constructive application of the Miller Effect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**State and explain Miller's Theorem. Derive the expression for the input Miller capacitance.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Miller's Theorem states that an impedance $Z$ connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two shunt impedances: $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ at the input and $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ at the output.

**Derivation (2 Marks):** For a feedback capacitor $C_f$ with impedance $Z = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_f}$, the input Miller capacitance is:

$$C_{M,in} = C_f(1 - A_v)$$

For an inverting amplifier where $A_v$ is negative:

$$C_{M,in} = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

This represents the apparent magnification of capacitance at the input due to the feedback action.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**Explain the significance of the Miller Effect in the high-frequency response of a Common-Emitter amplifier.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Significance (1 Mark):** In a Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier, the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ (or $C_{bc}$) appears as a feedback capacitance between input and output. Due to the Miller Effect, this small capacitance is effectively multiplied at the input.

**Calculation (1 Mark):** The effective input capacitance is:

$$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

where $C_\pi$ is the base-emitter capacitance.

**Impact (1 Mark):** This large input capacitance, combined with the source resistance, creates a low-frequency pole that severely limits the upper -3dB cutoff frequency:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_{in}}$$

The Miller Effect thus reduces the bandwidth of the CE amplifier significantly, making it unsuitable for high-frequency applications.

---

## Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) regulations, students must answer **ONE full question** from each module, with **internal choice** between two questions. Below are both alternatives for Module 2 ŌĆö Miller Effect.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024]

**a)** State and derive Miller's Theorem. Show that an impedance $Z$ connected between input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two equivalent shunt impedances. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** For a MOSFET Common-Source amplifier with transconductance $g_m = 10$ mA/V, drain resistance $R_D = 5$ k$\Omega$, gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, and gate-source capacitance $C_{gs} = 20$ pF, calculate: (i) the voltage gain, (ii) the Miller input capacitance, (iii) the total input capacitance, and (iv) the upper -3dB frequency assuming source resistance $R_S = 600$ $\Omega$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) ŌĆö Miller's Theorem Derivation (7 Marks)

**Statement (2 Marks):** Miller's Theorem is used to simplify the analysis of circuits where an impedance bridges the input and output of an inverting amplifier. It states that such an impedance $Z$ can be replaced by two shunt impedances: $Z_1$ at the input and $Z_2$ at the output.

**Derivation (5 Marks):** Consider an inverting amplifier with input $V_1$, output $V_2 = A_v V_1$, and feedback impedance $Z$ between input and output nodes.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The current flowing through $Z$ from input to output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_Z &= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z} = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** To find $Z_1$, equate the current through $Z_1$ (from input to ground) to the original $I_Z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1}{Z_1} &= \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Similarly, to find $Z_2$, equate the current through $Z_2$ (from output to ground) to the equivalent current leaving the output node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &= \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** For the special case where $Z$ is a capacitor $C_f$ with impedance $\frac{1}{j\omega C_f}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} &= C_f(1 - A_v) = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \quad \text{(for inverting amplifier)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Physical interpretation: The effective input capacitance is multiplied by the factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, dramatically increasing the input time constant and reducing the bandwidth.

### Part (b) ŌĆö MOSFET CS Amplifier Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:** $g_m = 10$ mA/V, $R_D = 5$ k$\Omega$, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, $C_{gs} = 20$ pF, $R_S = 600$ $\Omega$

**(i) Voltage Gain (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= -g_m R_D = -(10 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{3}) = -50
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Miller Input Capacitance (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} &= C_{gd}(1 - A_v) = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \\[4pt]
C_{M,in} &= 2 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 50) = 2 \text{ pF} \times 51 = 102 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Total Input Capacitance (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in,tot} &= C_{gs} + C_{M,in} = 20 \text{ pF} + 102 \text{ pF} = 122 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Upper -3dB Frequency (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 600 \times 122 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 7.32 \times 10^{-8}} \\[4pt]
f_H &\approx 2.17 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the Miller Effect in detail. Derive the expression for the Miller input and output capacitances when a capacitor is connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A BJT Common-Emitter amplifier has the following parameters: $\beta = 100$, $r_\pi = 2.5$ k$\Omega$, $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $R_C = 4$ k$\Omega$, $C_\pi = 30$ pF, $C_\mu = 3$ pF, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the midband voltage gain, (ii) the Miller input capacitance, (iii) the upper cutoff frequency, and (iv) the gain-bandwidth product. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) ŌĆö Miller Effect Explanation (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1.5 Marks):** The Miller Effect is a phenomenon observed in electronic circuits where a feedback impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier appears as a much smaller (or larger) impedance at the input and output terminals. For a capacitor, this effect dramatically multiplies the effective capacitance seen at the input.

**Setup (1 Mark):** Let an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ have a feedback capacitor $C_f$ between input and output. The impedances are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{1}{j\omega C_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Input Capacitance Derivation (2.5 Marks):** Applying Miller's Theorem for the input side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} &= C_f(1 - A_v) = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output Capacitance Derivation (1.5 Marks):** Applying Miller's Theorem for the output side:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,out} &= C_f \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$, $C_{M,out} \approx C_f$.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):** The Miller Effect thus magnifies the input capacitance by a factor of $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, severely impacting the high-frequency response of amplifiers.

### Part (b) ŌĆö BJT CE Amplifier Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\beta = 100$, $r_\pi = 2.5$ k$\Omega$, $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $R_C = 4$ k$\Omega$, $C_\pi = 30$ pF, $C_\mu = 3$ pF, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$

**(i) Midband Voltage Gain (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= -g_m R_C = -(40 \times 10^{-3})(4 \times 10^{3}) = -160
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Miller Input Capacitance (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,in} &= C_\mu(1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \\[4pt]
C_{M,in} &= 3 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 160) = 3 \text{ pF} \times 161 = 483 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Upper Cutoff Frequency (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{in,tot} &= C_\pi + C_{M,in} = 30 \text{ pF} + 483 \text{ pF} = 513 \text{ pF} \\[4pt]
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_{in,tot}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^3 \times 513 \times 10^{-12}} \\[4pt]
f_H &\approx 310 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Gain-Bandwidth Product (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GBP} &= \vert A_v \vert \cdot f_H = 160 \times 310 \times 10^3 \\[4pt]
\text{GBP} &= 4.96 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} \approx 49.6 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Alternatively, using the formula $\text{GBP} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GBP} &= \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{2\pi \times 3 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{1.885 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 2.12 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The discrepancy arises from the simplified single-pole approximation; the more accurate formula accounts for the internal capacitances of the device.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign of $A_v$:** Forgetting that $A_v$ is negative for an inverting amplifier and writing $1 - A_v$ as $1 - A_v$ without substituting $A_v = -\vert A_v \vert$. This is the **#1 most common mistake** in Miller Effect problems.
> 2. **Total Input Capacitance:** Many students forget to add $C_\pi$ (or $C_{gs}$) to the Miller input capacitance. The total input capacitance is $C_\pi + C_{M,in}$, **not** just $C_{M,in}$.
> 3. **Units:** Forgetting to convert pF to F (multiply by $10^{-12}$) and k$\Omega$ to $\Omega$ (multiply by $10^3$) before computation.
> 4. **Output Capacitance:** Students often ignore the Miller output capacitance, which is acceptable for $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$ but is a conceptual error in rigorous analysis.
> 5. **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** The GBP of a CE stage is approximately constant, meaning that as you increase the gain by adjusting $R_C$, the bandwidth decreases proportionally. Students often forget this fundamental relationship.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Miller's Theorem:** An impedance $Z$ between input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ is equivalent to $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ at the input and $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ at the output.

- **Miller Input Capacitance:** $C_{M,in} = C_f(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ for an inverting amplifier. This is the **most important formula** for KTU exams.

- **Miller Output Capacitance:** $C_{M,out} \approx C_f$ for $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$.

- **Voltage Gain Inverting Sign:** Always remember $A_v < 0$ for CE/CS amplifiers.

- **Total Input Capacitance (BJT CE):** $C_{in,tot} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$.

- **Total Input Capacitance (MOSFET CS):** $C_{in,tot} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$.

- **Upper Cutoff Frequency:** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,tot}}$, where $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen at the input.

- **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $\text{GBP} = \vert A_v \mid \cdot f_H \approx \text{constant}$ for a single-pole amplifier. For a BJT, $\text{GBP} = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_\mu}$.

- **Configurations to Suppress Miller Effect:**
  1. **Cascode amplifier** (CE + CB stack) ŌĆö reduces effective $A_v$ seen by $C_\mu$.
  2. **Common-Collector / Common-Drain** ŌĆö voltage gain $\approx 1$, Miller multiplication $\approx 0$.
  3. **Bootstrapping** ŌĆö drives both ends of the feedback capacitor in phase, nullifying the Miller multiplication.
  4. **Differential signaling** ŌĆö uses symmetry to reduce effective Miller capacitance.
  5. **Neutralization** ŌĆö adds an external capacitor to cancel $C_\mu$ (used in RF amplifiers).

- **Deliberate Use of Miller Effect:** Inside operational amplifiers, a Miller compensation capacitor is intentionally placed to create a dominant low-frequency pole, ensuring **stable unity-gain operation** (a constructive application).

- **Frequency Response Impact:** The Miller Effect introduces a dominant pole that limits the upper -3dB frequency, and the GBP becomes a fundamental design constraint.

- **Comparison with Bootstrapping:** Bootstrapping actively cancels the Miller Effect by driving the output in phase with the input, while cascode and CC/CD stages **avoid** it by design.

- **Historical Note:** The Miller Effect was first described by John Milton Miller in 1920 in the context of vacuum tube amplifiers; it remains equally relevant for modern solid-state devices.

- **Numerical Practice:** When solving KTU problems, always convert all units to SI (Farads, Ohms, Hertz) **before** calculation to avoid errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **High-Frequency Response of a Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier** refers to the behavior of the amplifier's voltage gain $A_v$ and phase shift as a function of signal frequency in the upper region of its bandwidth, where the internal **parasitic junction capacitances** of the BJT (specifically the base-emitter diffusion capacitance $C_\pi$ and the base-collector depletion capacitance $C_\mu$) and the **stray wiring capacitances** $C_s$ at the output node become significant. At these frequencies, the previously assumed *short-circuit* behavior of the coupling ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) and emitter-bypass ($C_E$) capacitors is no longer the bandwidth-limiting factor; instead, the **shunt capacitive loading** at the input and output nodes dominates, rolling off the gain at a rate of **ŌłÆ20 dB/decade**.

The transition is quantified by the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$ (also called the upper 3-dB frequency $f_2$), which is the frequency at which the mid-band voltage gain drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., $0.707 \cdot A_{v,mid}$) of its mid-band value, corresponding to a power loss of **3 dB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic belongs to the *High-Frequency Analysis* block of Module 2. The official KTU module outcome mapped is **CO2**: *"Analyze the frequency response of BJT amplifiers using small-signal hybrid-ŽĆ equivalent circuits."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Water-Pipe System

Imagine a household water pipe carrying water (the signal) to a sprinkler (the load). At low flow rates (low frequencies), the pipe is wide open ŌĆö water flows freely and reaches the sprinkler with full force. This is the **mid-band** region.

Now imagine the pipe is fitted with a flexible rubber diaphragm inside (the **junction capacitance**). When you oscillate the water flow quickly back and forth (high frequencies), the rubber diaphragm does not have time to bulge fully in either direction, so the average flow to the sprinkler decreases. The faster you oscillate, the less water gets through. This is the **high-frequency roll-off**.

- The **rubber diaphragm's stiffness** is analogous to the **Miller-multiplied capacitance** at the input.
- The **water hammer** when you suddenly close a tap is analogous to the **Miller effect** ŌĆö a small feedback capacitance (between base and collector) appears much larger at the input because of the voltage amplification across it.
- The **sprinkler head's own restriction** is analogous to the **output node capacitance**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** High-frequency response is *not* a defect of the design ŌĆö it is a fundamental physical consequence of the charge-storage mechanisms inside the transistor's p-n junctions. Designing a wideband amplifier is the art of **minimizing the effective input capacitance** and **maximizing the transconductance $g_m$**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the analysis:

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300\,\text{K}$.
- **BJT Unity-Gain Frequency:** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ (intrinsic device figure of merit, typically **300 MHz ŌĆō 10 GHz** for small-signal BJTs).
- **Typical Junction Capacitance Values:** $C_\pi \approx 10 \text{ pF} ŌĆō 1000 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu \approx 1 \text{ pF} ŌĆō 10 \text{ pF}$.
- **dB Conversions:** $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} A_v$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why High-Frequency Analysis is Needed

In mid-band analysis of the CE amplifier, we assume:
1. Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ are **short-circuited** (their reactance is negligible).
2. Emitter-bypass capacitor $C_E$ is **short-circuited**.
3. **All internal transistor capacitances are open-circuited** (their reactance is very high).

At **high frequencies** (typically above **a few kHz for general-purpose BJTs**), assumption 3 fails because the reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ of $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ becomes small enough to load the signal path. Therefore, a more accurate **small-signal model** that *retains* these capacitances must be used. The standard model for this purpose is the **Hybrid-ŽĆ Model with Capacitances** (also called the Giacoletto model).

## 2.2 The Hybrid-ŽĆ High-Frequency Model

The model replaces the BJT with the following elements between its three terminals (B, C, E):

- **Input side (BŌĆōE):** Resistance $r_\pi$ in **parallel** with diffusion capacitance $C_\pi$.
- **Output side (CŌĆōE):** Resistance $r_o$ in parallel with the controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$.
- **Feedback / Miller element:** Depletion capacitance $C_\mu$ connected between **Base and Collector**.

The mid-band elements $r_\pi$, $g_m$, and $r_o$ retain their usual definitions:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}, \quad r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m}, \quad r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C}$$

For a typical bias current of $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$, $g_m \approx 38.5 \text{ mA/V}$ and $r_\pi \approx 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$.

## 2.3 Miller's Theorem: Taming the Feedback Capacitance

The most analytically challenging element is $C_\mu$, which bridges the input and output nodes. **Miller's Theorem** converts this bridging impedance into two grounded impedances ŌĆö one at the input and one at the output ŌĆö allowing standard nodal analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Miller's Theorem Statement:** If an impedance $Z$ is connected between nodes with voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$, and the gain from node 1 to node 2 is $A = V_2/V_1$, then $Z$ can be replaced by:
> - $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1 - A}$ between node 1 and ground.
> - $Z_2 = Z \cdot \dfrac{1 - A}{A}$ between node 2 and ground.

For a **capacitor** $C_\mu$ with impedance $Z = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_\mu}$, applied to a CE amplifier with mid-band gain $A_v = -g_m R_L'$ (negative sign because of phase inversion), the Miller-multiplied capacitances are:

**At the input:**
$$C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

**At the output:**
$$C_{M2} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_\mu$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $\vert A_v \vert$ is typically large (10ŌĆō1000), the input-side Miller capacitance $C_{M1}$ can be **hundreds of times larger** than the actual $C_\mu$ ŌĆö this is the *Miller effect*, and it is the single most important reason why CE amplifiers have a much lower bandwidth than their emitter-current counterparts (CB or cascade configurations).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

The table below summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for this topic. Boundary conditions and typical units are included.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Range / Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | $1 ŌĆō 100$ mA/V | Depends on bias current $I_C$ |
| 2 | Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | $0.1 ŌĆō 50$ k$\Omega$ | ŌĆö |
| 3 | Mid-band voltage gain | $A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_L'$ where $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$ | $-10$ to $-1000$ | Negative = phase inversion |
| 4 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M1} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | $10$ pF ŌĆō $10$ nF | **Dominant** at high $f$ |
| 5 | Miller output capacitance | $C_{M2} = C_\mu$ | $1$ ŌĆō $10$ pF | Often negligible |
| 6 | Total input capacitance | $C_{in,eq} = C_\pi + C_{M1} + C_{stray,in}$ | ŌĆö | $C_{stray} \approx 5$ pF typical |
| 7 | Total output capacitance | $C_{out,eq} = C_s + C_{M2} + C_{stray,out}$ | ŌĆö | $C_s$ = wiring/load |
| 8 | Th├®venin resistance at input | $R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | $0.1$ ŌĆō $10$ k$\Omega$ | ŌĆö |
| 9 | Th├®venin resistance at output | $R'_{th} = R_C \parallel R_L$ | $0.5$ ŌĆō $10$ k$\Omega$ | ŌĆö |
| 10 | Upper cutoff freq (input pole) | $f_{H1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,eq}}$ | $0.1$ ŌĆō $100$ MHz | Usually **dominant pole** |
| 11 | Upper cutoff freq (output pole) | $f_{H2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R'_{th} C_{out,eq}}$ | $1$ ŌĆō $500$ MHz | Often ignored if $f_{H1} \ll f_{H2}$ |
| 12 | Exact 3-dB frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{f_{H2}^2}}}$ | ŌĆö | Sum of squares |
| 13 | Dominant-pole approximation | $f_H \approx f_{H1}$ if $f_{H1} \ll f_{H2}$ | ŌĆö | Simplifies analysis |
| 14 | Unity-gain bandwidth | $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ | $0.3$ ŌĆō $10$ GHz | Transistor spec |
| 15 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBP} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H \approx \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi C_{M1}} R_L' \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_{th} C_{in,eq}}$ | ŌĆö | Invariant for fixed $g_m$ & $C$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all LaTeX equations, never use the bare vertical bar $\vert$ inside a markdown table ŌĆö use the math command `\vert` or `\mid` instead. The table above has been written using the absolute-value bars from the formulas as they appear in the equation block. **When transcribing into your own notes, always write `$\vert A_v \vert$` in tables, never `|A_v|`.**

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The high-frequency response analysis is **not an academic exercise** ŌĆö it is foundational to:

1. **RF and Microwave Circuit Design:** Designing low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) for cellular base stations, Wi-Fi transceivers, and satellite receivers requires mastery of $f_H$ and GBP.
2. **High-Speed Digital Logic:** Understanding the bandwidth limitation of a single BJT stage explains why **ECL** (Emitter-Coupled Logic) is faster than TTL ŌĆö ECL avoids saturation and minimizes Miller capacitance effect.
3. **Op-Amp Internal Stages:** The first stage of virtually every op-amp (e.g., the classic $\mu$A741, LM358) is a differential pair whose high-frequency limit is governed by precisely the equations derived here.
4. **Cable TV / Video Amplifiers:** Distributed video amplifiers (like those used in CATV head-ends) must maintain flat response from DC to **1 GHz** ŌĆö the design is dominated by the Miller effect.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Upper Cutoff Frequency Using Miller's Theorem (Approximate Single-Pole Method)

We consider a CE amplifier with biasing resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, source resistance $R_S$, collector resistor $R_C$, and load $R_L$. The transistor is replaced by the hybrid-ŽĆ model with $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ included.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Draw the High-Frequency Small-Signal Equivalent

Coupling and bypass capacitors are shorted. The equivalent circuit consists of:
- Source $v_s$ with source resistance $R_S$ driving the base node.
- $R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ at the input, in parallel with $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ (which bridges B to C).
- $R_C \parallel R_L$ at the output, in parallel with $r_o$.
- A controlled current source $g_m v_{be}$ flowing from C to E.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Identify the Mid-Band Gain

At mid-band, the capacitors are open. The voltage gain is:

$$A_{v,\text{mid}} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{be}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) = -g_m R_L'$$

We denote the magnitude as $\vert A_v \vert = g_m R_L'$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_\mu$

The bridging capacitor $C_\mu$ sees a voltage ratio of $v_c / v_b = A_v$ (which is negative). Replacing it:

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$$

This appears between Base and Emitter (grounded). At the output:

$$C_{M,\text{out}} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_\mu \quad \text{(since } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1\text{)}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Sum All Input Capacitances

The total capacitance at the input node becomes:

$$C_{in,T} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L') + C_{s,\text{in}}$$

where $C_{s,\text{in}}$ is the wiring/parasitic capacitance at the input (typically 3ŌĆō10 pF).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Sum All Output Capacitances

$$C_{out,T} = C_{M,\text{out}} + C_s = C_\mu + C_{s,\text{out}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Th├®venin Resistance at the Input Node

Looking from the base node back into the circuit:

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$$

This is the total resistance seen by $C_{in,T}$.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Th├®venin Resistance at the Output Node

$$R'_{th} = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o \approx R_C \parallel R_L \quad \text{(if } r_o \gg R_C, R_L\text{)}$$

### Step 8 ŌĆö Compute the Two Pole Frequencies

Each RC network now behaves as a **single-pole low-pass filter**. The pole frequencies are:

$$f_{H1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T}}$$

$$f_{H2} = \frac{1}{2\pi R'_{th} C_{out,T}}$$

### Step 9 ŌĆö Combine the Two Poles for the Exact 3-dB Frequency

If the two poles are non-interacting (i.e., well-separated), the **exact** 3-dB frequency is:

$$\boxed{\,f_H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{f_{H2}^2}}}\,}$$

### Step 10 ŌĆö Apply the Dominant-Pole Approximation

In nearly all practical CE amplifier designs, $f_{H1} \ll f_{H2}$ because $C_{in,T} \gg C_{out,T}$ (due to the Miller effect). In this case:

$$\boxed{\,f_H \approx f_{H1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} \left[ C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L') \right]}\,}$$

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 2019 / 2023 type):**
A CE amplifier has the following parameters: $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$, $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$, $C_\pi = 100 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 5 \text{ pF}$. Neglect $r_o$ and stray capacitances. Determine the mid-band gain $A_{v,\text{mid}}$ and the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$.

### Solution ŌĆö Step by Step

**Compute small-signal parameters:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{38.46 \text{ mA/V}} = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Compute effective load resistance:**

$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_L' = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Compute mid-band gain:**

$$A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_L' = -(38.46 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.197 \times 10^3) = -122.97 \text{ V/V}$$

In dB: $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(122.97) = 41.79 \text{ dB}$.

**Compute Th├®venin resistance at input:**

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

First, $1 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega = \dfrac{1 \times 3.333}{1 + 3.333} = 0.769 \text{ k}\Omega$.

Then, $0.769 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega = \dfrac{0.769 \times 2.6}{0.769 + 2.6} = 0.594 \text{ k}\Omega = 594 \Omega$.

**Compute Miller-multiplied input capacitance:**

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L') = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 122.97) = 5 \times 123.97 = 619.85 \text{ pF}$$

**Compute total input capacitance:**

$$C_{in,T} = C_\pi + C_{M,\text{in}} = 100 + 619.85 = 719.85 \text{ pF}$$

**Compute upper 3-dB frequency (dominant pole):**

$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 594 \times 719.85 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 4.275 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2.686 \times 10^{-6}} = 372.3 \text{ kHz}$$

**Output pole check:**

$$C_{out,T} \approx C_\mu = 5 \text{ pF}$$

$$R'_{th} = R_L' = 3.197 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$f_{H2} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3197 \times 5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.004 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.95 \text{ MHz}$$

Since $f_{H1} = 372.3 \text{ kHz} \ll f_{H2} = 9.95 \text{ MHz}$, the dominant-pole assumption is fully valid.

**Exact 3-dB frequency using sum-of-squares:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(372.3 \text{ kHz})^2} + \dfrac{1}{(9.95 \text{ MHz})^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.21 \times 10^{-12} + 1.01 \times 10^{-15}}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.21 \times 10^{-12}}} = 372.3 \text{ kHz} \quad \text{(essentially identical to } f_{H1}\text{)}$$

**Compute Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$\text{GBP} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \times f_H = 122.97 \times 372.3 \text{ kHz} = 45.78 \text{ MHz}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Gain-Bandwidth Product Invariance

Starting from the dominant-pole approximation:

$$\text{GBP} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H = (g_m R_L') \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T}}$$

Substituting $C_{in,T} \approx C_\mu \cdot g_m R_L'$ for large Miller multiplication (i.e., $C_\mu g_m R_L' \gg C_\pi$):

$$\text{GBP} = \frac{g_m R_L'}{2\pi R_{th} \cdot C_\mu g_m R_L'} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_\mu}$$

**Conclusion:** The GBP is **independent** of the load resistance $R_L'$ and the mid-band gain. It depends only on the source/biasing network and the feedback capacitance. This is the **fundamental design trade-off** in CE amplifier design ŌĆö *you can trade gain for bandwidth, but the product remains approximately constant*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Hybrid-ŽĆ High-Frequency Model of the CE Amplifier

The following Mermaid block renders a block-level functional architecture flow mapping the signal path and node interactions in the high-frequency small-signal equivalent. (A physical schematic would require a CAD tool; this functional flow captures all the node interactions a KTU student must understand.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef node fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cap fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    VS["Vs Signal Source"]:::node
    RS["Rs Source Resistance"]:::node
    R1R2["R1 parallel R2 Bias Network"]:::node
    RPI["r_pi Base Emitter Resistance"]:::node
    CPI["C_pi Diffusion Capacitance"]:::cap
    CMU["C_mu Feedback Capacitance Bridge"]:::cap
    GMVBE["g_m times v_be Controlled Current Source"]:::node
    RC["Rc Collector Resistor"]:::node
    RO["r_o Output Resistance"]:::node
    RL["Rl Load Resistance"]:::node
    VOUT["Vout Output Node"]:::out
    VBE["v_be Input Node B to E"]:::out

    VS --> RS
    RS --> VBE
    R1R2 --> VBE
    RPI --> VBE
    CPI --> VBE
    VBE --> GMVBE
    CMU --> VBE
    CMU --> VOUT
    GMVBE --> VOUT
    RC --> VOUT
    RO --> VOUT
    RL --> VOUT
    VOUT --> VOUTLABEL["Vout Final Output"]:::out
```

## 4.2 Miller Theorem Transformation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pre fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef post fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef label fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A["Original CE Model with C_mu bridging B to C"]:::pre
    B["Apply Miller Theorem: gain Av = vc per vb is negative"]:::label
    C["Split C_mu into two grounded parts"]:::label
    D["C_M1 = C_mu times 1 plus g_m R_L prime at INPUT node"]:::post
    E["C_M2 = C_mu at OUTPUT node"]:::post
    F["Input RC: R_th in parallel with C_pi plus C_M1"]:::post
    G["Output RC: R_L prime in parallel with C_M2"]:::post
    H["Pole 1: f_H1 = 1 over 2 pi R_th C_in_T"]:::label
    I["Pole 2: f_H2 = 1 over 2 pi R_L prime C_out_T"]:::label
    J["Dominant pole: f_H approx f_H1 since f_H1 much less than f_H2"]:::post

    A --> B --> C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> G
    F --> H
    G --> I
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response of the CE Amplifier (Three-Band Bode Picture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef band fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef rolloff fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef flat fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    L["LOW FREQUENCY BAND: f less than f_L. Gain rises at plus 20 dB per decade. Limited by C_in, C_out, C_E."]:::rolloff
    M["MID BAND: f_L less than f less than f_H. Gain is flat at A_v_mid. Coupling caps short, junction caps open."]:::flat
    H["HIGH FREQUENCY BAND: f greater than f_H. Gain falls at minus 20 dB per decade. Limited by C_pi and Miller multiplied C_mu."]:::rolloff
    F["Lower 3 dB point f_L"]:::band
    FH["Upper 3 dB point f_H"]:::band

    L --> F --> M --> FH --> H
```

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Interpretation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pnode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#3b82f6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef cnode fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#f97316,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef final fill:#14532d,stroke:#22c55e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    P1["Pole 1 at frequency f_H1 = 1 over 2 pi R_th C_in_T"]:::pnode
    P2["Pole 2 at frequency f_H2 = 1 over 2 pi R_L prime C_out_T"]:::pnode
    Z["Optional Zero at high frequency from feedthrough via C_mu"]:::cnode
    COMBINE["Combine in transfer function: Av of s = A_mid times 1 divided by 1 plus s over omega_H1 times 1 plus s over omega_H2"]:::final
    SOLVE["Solve magnitude equals A_mid over sqrt 2 to find f_H"]:::final

    P1 --> COMBINE
    P2 --> COMBINE
    Z --> COMBINE
    COMBINE --> SOLVE
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short-Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term "Miller Effect" as applied to the high-frequency analysis of a CE amplifier. Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Miller Effect refers to the phenomenon in which a capacitance $C_\mu$ connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier appears at the input as a much larger equivalent capacitance $C_{M} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, where $A_v$ is the mid-band voltage gain of the stage.
- **Significance (1.5 marks):** Because $\vert A_v \vert$ in a CE amplifier is typically large (10ŌĆō1000), the Miller-multiplied input capacitance can be **hundreds of times** the actual $C_\mu$. This multiplied capacitance becomes the dominant factor that lowers the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$, severely limiting the bandwidth of the CE amplifier. The Miller effect is the principal reason why CE stages have lower bandwidth than common-base or cascode stages.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: State the formula for the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ of a CE amplifier using the dominant-pole approximation. List the parameters involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Formula (2 marks):** $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')]}$
- **Parameters (1 mark):** $R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ (Th├®venin resistance at input), $C_\pi$ = base-emitter diffusion capacitance, $C_\mu$ = base-collector depletion capacitance, $g_m$ = transconductance, $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$ (effective load).

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long-Answer Questions (Choice Between A and B, 14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**A CE amplifier has the following parameters: $R_S = 600 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_1 = 22 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 5.6 \text{ k}\Omega$, $I_C = 2 \text{ mA}$, $\beta = 120$, $V_T = 25 \text{ mV}$, $C_\pi = 80 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 4 \text{ pF}$. Stray capacitances may be neglected. Assume $r_o \to \infty$.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Compute the mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,\text{mid}}$ and the small-signal parameters $g_m$, $r_\pi$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the transconductance.

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \text{ mA}}{25 \text{ mV}} = 80 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $r_\pi$.

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{80 \text{ mA/V}} = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Compute the effective load $R_L'$.

$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 5.6}{4.7 + 5.6} = \frac{26.32}{10.3} = 2.555 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Compute the mid-band gain.

$$A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_L' = -(80 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.555 \times 10^3) = -204.4 \text{ V/V}$$

In decibels: $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(204.4) = 46.21 \text{ dB}$.

**Final Answers (Part a):** $g_m = 80$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 1.5$ k$\Omega$, $A_{v,\text{mid}} = -204.4$ V/V (or 46.2 dB).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Determine the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ using the dominant-pole approximation. Also compute the gain-bandwidth product. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the bias network resistance.

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{22 \times 10}{22 + 10} = \frac{220}{32} = 6.875 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Th├®venin resistance at the input.

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 0.6 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 6.875 \text{ k}\Omega \parallel 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

First, $0.6 \parallel 6.875 = \dfrac{0.6 \times 6.875}{0.6 + 6.875} = \dfrac{4.125}{7.475} = 0.552 \text{ k}\Omega$.

Then, $0.552 \parallel 1.5 = \dfrac{0.552 \times 1.5}{0.552 + 1.5} = \dfrac{0.828}{2.052} = 0.4035 \text{ k}\Omega = 403.5 \text{ }\Omega$.

**Step 3:** Compute the Miller-multiplied input capacitance.

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L') = 4 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 204.4) = 4 \times 205.4 = 821.6 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the total input capacitance.

$$C_{in,T} = C_\pi + C_{M,\text{in}} = 80 + 821.6 = 901.6 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the upper 3-dB frequency.

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 403.5 \times 901.6 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 3.638 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2.286 \times 10^{-6}} = 437.4 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the Gain-Bandwidth Product.

$$\text{GBP} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \times f_H = 204.4 \times 437.4 \text{ kHz} = 89.4 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answers (Part b):** $f_H = 437.4$ kHz, GBP $= 89.4$ MHz.

---

### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**A CE amplifier uses a BJT with the following small-signal parameters at the bias point: $g_m = 50$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 2$ k$\Omega$, $r_o = 50$ k$\Omega$, $C_\pi = 60$ pF, $C_\mu = 3$ pF. The external components are: $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_1 = 15$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 7.5$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$. The stray capacitance at the output is $C_s = 5$ pF.**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derive the expression for the Miller-multiplied input capacitance and compute its value. Why is the Miller effect the dominant bandwidth-limiting factor? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The effective load seen at the collector.

$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = 5 \parallel 10 \parallel 50 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$5 \parallel 10 = \dfrac{5 \times 10}{15} = 3.333 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$3.333 \parallel 50 = \dfrac{3.333 \times 50}{53.333} = 3.125 \text{ k}\Omega$.

**Step 2:** Mid-band gain magnitude.

$$\vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert = g_m R_L' = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.125 \times 10^3 = 156.25 \text{ V/V}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Miller's theorem to $C_\mu$.

The capacitor $C_\mu$ bridges the input node (base) and the output node (collector), with voltage gain $A_v = -156.25$. Miller's theorem gives:

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert) = 3 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 156.25) = 471.75 \text{ pF}$$

$$C_{M,\text{out}} = C_\mu \left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx 3 \text{ pF} \quad \text{(negligible correction)}$$

**Step 4:** Reason why Miller effect dominates.

- The original $C_\pi = 60$ pF, but the Miller-multiplied $C_{M,\text{in}} = 471.75$ pF ŌĆö an increase of nearly **8├Ś** the base-emitter capacitance.
- After adding both, $C_{in,T} = 60 + 471.75 = 531.75$ pF, of which **~89% is contributed by the Miller effect**.
- This is because the high gain of the CE stage (156.25) directly scales the feedback capacitance seen at the input.
- The output capacitance $C_{out,T} = C_s + C_\mu = 5 + 3 = 8$ pF, paired with $R_L' = 3.125$ k$\Omega$, gives a much higher output pole frequency, so the **input pole becomes dominant**.

**Final Answer (Part a):** $C_{M,\text{in}} = 471.75$ pF; it dominates because it is 8├Ś the original $C_\pi$ and dictates the input pole.

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Calculate the upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ using the exact two-pole formula, and the GBP of the amplifier. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $R_{th}$ at input.

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{15 \times 7.5}{15 + 7.5} = \frac{112.5}{22.5} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1 \parallel 5 \parallel 2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$1 \parallel 5 = 0.833 \text{ k}\Omega$. Then $0.833 \parallel 2 = 0.588 \text{ k}\Omega = 588 \text{ }\Omega$.

**Step 2:** Compute the two pole frequencies.

$$C_{in,T} = C_\pi + C_{M,\text{in}} = 60 + 471.75 = 531.75 \text{ pF}$$

$$f_{H1} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 588 \times 531.75 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.964 \times 10^{-6}} = 509.0 \text{ kHz}$$

For the output pole:

$$C_{out,T} = C_\mu + C_s = 3 + 5 = 8 \text{ pF}$$

$$f_{H2} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L' C_{out,T}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3125 \times 8 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{1.571 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.366 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3:** Combine using the exact two-pole formula.

$$f_H = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{f_{H1}^2} + \dfrac{1}{f_{H2}^2}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{(509 \text{ kHz})^2} + \dfrac{1}{(6.366 \text{ MHz})^2}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{f_{H1}^2} = \frac{1}{2.59 \times 10^{11}} = 3.86 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\frac{1}{f_{H2}^2} = \frac{1}{4.05 \times 10^{13}} = 2.47 \times 10^{-14}$$

$$\text{Sum} = 3.86 \times 10^{-12} + 0.0247 \times 10^{-12} = 3.885 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\sqrt{\text{Sum}} = 1.971 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{1.971 \times 10^{-6}} = 507.3 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Gain-Bandwidth Product.

$$\text{GBP} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \times f_H = 156.25 \times 507.3 \text{ kHz} = 79.27 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answers (Part b):** $f_H = 507.3$ kHz (exact), $\text{GBP} = 79.27$ MHz.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include both $R_1$ and $R_2$** in parallel with $r_\pi$ when computing $R_{th}$. Many students write $R_{th} = R_S \parallel r_\pi$ only and lose **2 marks** per part.
> 2. **Sign error in Miller's theorem** ŌĆö using $C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ with $A_v$ positive instead of $C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. Always use the **magnitude** form for the input-side capacitance.
> 3. **Skipping the dominant-pole justification** ŌĆö KTU evaluators award a 1-mark step for explicitly showing that $f_{H1} \ll f_{H2}$ (i.e., computing both poles and comparing).
> 4. **Unit error in $f_H$** ŌĆö $C$ must be in **farads** (not pF) when plugged into the denominator. Converting $901.6 \text{ pF} = 901.6 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$ is a frequent oversight.
> 5. **Neglecting the $(1 - 1/A_v)$ factor for $C_{M,\text{out}}$** ŌĆö in most practical cases it is negligible, but if the question explicitly asks for the *exact* output capacitance, you must write it down before approximating.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets ŌĆö Print and Pin to Your Wall:**

- Ō£ģ At **high frequencies**, the coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits; only **junction and stray capacitances** matter.
- Ō£ģ Use the **hybrid-ŽĆ model with $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ included** (Giacoletto model).
- Ō£ģ **Miller's Theorem** converts the bridge capacitance $C_\mu$ into a **grounded input capacitance** $C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ and a small output capacitance $C_{M,\text{out}} \approx C_\mu$.
- Ō£ģ The **Miller-multiplied capacitance** is the **single biggest bandwidth-limiting factor** in a CE amplifier.
- Ō£ģ Two poles arise: an **input pole** $f_{H1} = 1/(2\pi R_{th} C_{in,T})$ and an **output pole** $f_{H2} = 1/(2\pi R_L' C_{out,T})$.
- Ō£ģ The **input pole is almost always the dominant pole** in a CE amplifier because $C_{in,T} \gg C_{out,T}$.
- Ō£ģ The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** is approximately **constant** for a fixed bias and $C_\mu$; you can trade gain for bandwidth but cannot exceed the GBP.
- Ō£ģ $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ is the **transistor's intrinsic unity-gain frequency** ŌĆö a hard upper bound on the GBP of any single stage.
- Ō£ģ **Cascode and common-base configurations** are used to *eliminate* or *minimize* the Miller effect when high bandwidth is required.
- Ō£ģ For a CE amplifier, $A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_L'$ (negative sign denotes 180┬░ phase inversion).
- Ō£ģ Always state units clearly: $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in **F** (convert from pF), and $f$ in **Hz**.
- Ō£ģ In KTU 14-mark problems, the expected structure is: (1) compute small-signal parameters, (2) compute $A_{v,\text{mid}}$, (3) compute $R_{th}$, (4) apply Miller's theorem, (5) compute $f_{H1}$ and $f_{H2}$, (6) justify dominant pole, (7) compute GBP.

---

**End of Module 2 Topic: Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier**
*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme OBE framework and Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*
