# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Multistage Amplifier ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Multistage Amplifier** is an electronic amplifier circuit in which two or more single-stage amplifier blocks are cascaded in series so that the output of one stage drives the input of the next. The overall objective is to achieve specifications (voltage gain, current gain, input/output impedance, bandwidth) that a single stage cannot simultaneously deliver.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course PEEET417)**, Module 2 treats multistage amplifiers as a foundational stepping-stone from single-stage small-signal analysis toward integrated-circuit amplifier topologies. The two topologies of interest are:

| Topology | Structure | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **Cascade Amplifier** | Two CE (or CS) stages connected directly | Most common textbook configuration |
| **Cascode Amplifier** | CE stage stacked on top of a CB stage (or CS on CG) | High-frequency, high-gain IC design |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must derive the overall voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance of the cascade and cascode configurations, including the loading effect of the second stage on the first.

## 1.2 Why Do We Need More Than One Stage?

A single BJT/MOSFET amplifier stage is fundamentally limited by the **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product (GBW)**. As you increase the gain of a single stage, its bandwidth collapses. Multistage design allows us to *partition* the gain requirement across multiple stages, each operating at a lower individual gain, thereby *extending* the overall usable bandwidth.

Additionally:
- **Single CE / CS** has a moderate voltage gain (~50ŌĆō500) but suffers from the **Miller Effect**, which drastically reduces its high-frequency response.
- **CB / CG** has excellent high-frequency performance but a very low input resistance.
- Combining them cleverly (Cascode) cancels individual weaknesses.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö "The Water-Pipe Network"

Imagine a water-pump system trying to push water through a long pipe:

- A **single big pump** (single-stage amplifier) generates high pressure (gain) but the pipe vibrates violently at high flow rates ŌĆö it can only work over a narrow flow range (limited bandwidth).
- A **cascade system** uses two medium pumps in a row. Each pump handles part of the pressure. The total pressure is high, but each individual pump runs smoothly over a wider flow range.
- A **cascode system** stacks a *pressure-booster pump* (CE stage) directly on top of a *flow-controller pump* (CB stage). The booster creates the pressure; the controller prevents turbulence at high flow. The result is high pressure **and** smooth high-flow operation ŌĆö i.e., high gain and wide bandwidth simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition Box**
> - **Cascade Amplifier:** Two identical CE (or CS) stages coupled directly. Gain multiplies; bandwidth degrades due to cascading of bandwidths.
> - **Cascode Amplifier:** CE stage feeding a CB stage (cascode = *cascaded triode* ŌĆö historical term). Same low-frequency gain as CE, but bandwidth is dramatically extended because the Miller effect on the CE stage is neutralised by the low input impedance of the CB stage.

---

## 1.4 The Two Topologies at a Glance

**Cascade (CE-CE) Block Diagram:**

```
Vin ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [CE Stage 1] ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [CE Stage 2] ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Vout
```

**Cascode (CE-CB) Block Diagram:**

```
Vin ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [CE Stage] ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Vx ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ [CB Stage] ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Vout
         (common emitter)     (common base)
```

In the cascode, the intermediate node Vx is **low impedance**, which "swamps" the Miller multiplication of the CE stage's collectorŌĆōbase capacitance, $C_\mu$.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cascade Amplifier ŌĆö Two Direct-Coupled CE Stages

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

Two NPN CE stages share the same $V_{CC}$ rail. The collector of Q1 is **directly (DC) coupled** to the base of Q2. Coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors are chosen so that AC signals pass through both stages without attenuation.

```
      Rc1                Rc2
VCC ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆ/\/\ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆ/\/\ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ Vout
         Ōöé          Ōöé            Ōöé
         Ōöé          BŌöĆŌöĆQ2        Ōöé
         CŌöĆŌöĆQ1      Ōöé          RL
         Ōöé          EŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆRE2ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ GND
         B          Ōöé
        Vin        GND
         Ōöé
        RE1
         Ōöé
        GND
```

### 2.1.2 AC Small-Signal Analysis

For each individual CE stage (ignoring Early effect, $r_o \to \infty$), the small-signal parameters are:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}, \qquad r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$$

Without loading, the unloaded voltage gain of a single CE stage is:

$$A_{v,\text{stage}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

### 2.1.3 The Loading Effect ŌĆö Why We Cannot Just Multiply Unloaded Gains

When stage 2 is connected to stage 1, the **input resistance of stage 2** ($r_{\pi 2}$) appears in parallel with $R_{C1}$. So the effective load on stage 1 is:

$$R_{L1,\text{eff}} = R_{C1} \parallel r_{\pi 2}$$

The total voltage gain is therefore:

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$$

where $A_{v1}$ uses the *loaded* collector resistor.

### 2.1.4 Input and Output Resistance

- $R_{in,\text{total}} = R_{in1} = R_B \parallel r_{\pi 1}$ (only the first stage is seen from the source)
- $R_{out,\text{total}} = R_{out2} = R_{C2}$ (only the last stage is seen from the load)

### 2.1.5 Bandwidth of Cascade

If each individual stage has a bandwidth $f_{1}$ (upper $-3$ dB frequency), the *dominant-pole approximation* gives the overall bandwidth:

$$f_H^{\text{cascade}} \approx \frac{f_{1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}} \quad \text{for } n \text{ identical stages}$$

For two identical stages ($n=2$):

$$f_H^{\text{cascade}} = \frac{f_1}{\sqrt{\sqrt{2}-1}} \approx 0.644 \, f_1$$

This is the famous **bandwidth shrinkage** that motivates cascode design.

---

## 2.2 Cascode Amplifier ŌĆö CE Stacked on CB

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

Q1 is in **CE configuration**, Q2 is in **CB configuration**. The emitter of Q2 carries the collector current of Q1. The input is at Q1's base; the output is at Q2's collector.

```
         Rc
VCC ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ/\/\ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ Vout
         Ōöé             Ōöé
         CŌöĆŌöĆQ2         RL
         Ōöé             Ōöé
         E             GND
         Ōöé
         CŌöĆŌöĆQ1
         Ōöé
         B
        Vin
         Ōöé
        RE (bypassed)
         Ōöé
        GND
```

### 2.2.2 Small-Signal Analysis

The CE stage (Q1) sees a load equal to the **input resistance of the CB stage**, which for a BJT is approximately:

$$R_{in,\text{CB}} \approx r_e = \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

Because $r_e$ is very small (typically a few ohms), the voltage gain of Q1 alone is nearly unity. However, the CB stage (Q2) provides the bulk of the voltage gain by acting as a current buffer ŌĆö its current gain Ōēł 1, but it converts the *current* from a low-impedance node to a high-impedance collector load.

The **overall cascode gain** (with both BJTs matched, $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$) is:

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The low-frequency voltage gain of the cascode is *identical* to that of a single CE stage. The cascode's superiority lies **not** in gain but in **bandwidth** and **frequency response**.

### 2.2.3 Why Cascode Has Wide Bandwidth

The Miller effect on Q1 multiplies $C_{\mu 1}$ by $(1 - A_{v1})$. In a CE stage, $A_{v1}$ is large (e.g., $-100$), so the effective input capacitance becomes $\approx 101\, C_{\mu 1}$.

In a cascode, Q1 has voltage gain:

$$A_{v1,\text{cascode}} = -g_{m1} \cdot r_{e2} = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m2}} \approx -1$$

So the Miller multiplication factor is only $(1 - (-1)) = 2$, and the effective input capacitance is merely $2C_{\mu 1}$.

The CB stage Q2 has no Miller multiplication (its collector is the output; its base is grounded for AC, so $C_{\mu 2}$ sees no gain).

Result: **The cascode's $-3$ dB frequency is roughly that of a single CB stage**, which is much higher than that of a single CE stage with the same load.

### 2.2.4 Input and Output Resistance

- $R_{in,\text{cascode}} = R_B \parallel r_{\pi 1}$ (same as a CE stage)
- $R_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx R_C$ (CB output looks into its collector)

If the Early effect (output resistance $r_o$) is included, $R_{out}$ of cascode Ōēł $r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\, r_{o1}\, r_{o2} \cdot R_E$, much higher than a single CE.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce verbatim during the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Memorise the symbols and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | A/V, with $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at 300 K |
| 2 | BaseŌĆōemitter resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Emitter resistance | $r_e = \alpha / g_m \approx 1/g_m$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Single CE voltage gain | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Single CB voltage gain | $A_v = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Single CS voltage gain | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Cascade loaded gain | $A_{v1,\text{loaded}} = -g_{m1}(R_{C1}\parallel r_{\pi 2})$ | First stage only |
| 8 | Cascade total gain | $A_{v,\text{tot}} = A_{v1}\cdot A_{v2}$ | With loading |
| 9 | Cascode CB input resistance | $R_{in,\text{CB}} \approx 1/g_m$ | A few $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Cascode total low-freq gain | $A_v \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Equals CE gain |
| 11 | Miller input capacitance | $C_{M} = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ | Dominates CE HF response |
| 12 | Cascode Miller capacitance | $C_{M,\text{eff}} \approx 2 C_\mu$ | Because $A_v \approx -1$ |
| 13 | CE $-3$ dB frequency | $f_H \approx 1 / [2\pi\, R_{sig}'(C_\pi + C_M)]$ | Hz |
| 14 | Cascade $-3$ dB (2 stages) | $f_H^{\text{casc}} \approx 0.644\, f_{H1}$ | Bandwidth shrinks |
| 15 | Cascode $-3$ dB (approx) | $f_H^{\text{casc-cs}} \approx 1 / [2\pi\, R_{sig}' C_{\pi 1}]$ | Far higher than CE |

> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` when writing $V_T \approx \vert 26\text{ mV}\vert$ to avoid breaking table syntax. *(Editorial note: this is precisely why $V_T$ is shown as an unsigned magnitude.)*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cascade and cascode amplifiers form the **backbone of every analog integrated circuit** manufactured today:

- **Operational amplifiers (e.g., $\mu$A741, LM358, AD8628):** Input differential pair often uses a cascode load to achieve very high DC gain (> 100 dB) while remaining stable at unity-gain bandwidths of several MHz.
- **RF and IF amplifiers in radios:** Cascode stages provide the gain needed at tens to hundreds of MHz without self-oscillation.
- **High-speed ADCs and DACs:** The front-end sample-and-hold uses cascode buffers for wideband, low-distortion operation.
- **Telescopic OTA (Operational Transconductance Amplifier):** A cascoded differential pair widely used in switched-capacitor filters.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly expanded below. Do **not** skim ŌĆö KTU board examiners reward step-by-step working (2ŌĆō3 marks per derived intermediate result).

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Voltage Gain of a Direct-Coupled CE-CE Cascade

**Given:**

- Two identical CE stages with bias resistors $R_{B1}=R_{B2}=R_B$, collector resistors $R_{C1}=R_{C2}=R_C$, emitter resistors fully bypassed.
- BJTs Q1, Q2 are matched: $g_{m1}=g_{m2}=g_m$, $r_{\pi 1}=r_{\pi 2}=r_\pi$, $\beta_1=\beta_2=\beta$.
- Source resistance $R_{sig}$; load $R_L$.

**Objective:** Derive the overall voltage gain $A_{v,\text{tot}} = V_{out}/V_{in}$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Input resistance of stage 2 as seen from the collector of stage 1

The base of Q2 is connected to the collector of Q1. The input resistance looking into Q2's base is $r_{\pi 2}$ (since emitter is AC-grounded by the bypass capacitor).

$$\boxed{R_{in2} = r_\pi}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Effective AC load on Q1's collector

The collector of Q1 sees $R_{C1}$ in parallel with $R_{in2}$:

$$R_{L1}' = R_{C1} \parallel R_{in2} = R_{C1} \parallel r_\pi = \frac{R_{C1}\, r_\pi}{R_{C1} + r_\pi}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage gain of stage 1 (loaded)

$$A_{v1} = \frac{V_{c1}}{V_{in}} = -g_{m1}\, R_{L1}' = -g_m \, \frac{R_{C1}\, r_\pi}{R_{C1} + r_\pi}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Voltage gain of stage 2 (unloaded, since $R_L$ is the only external load)

$$A_{v2} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{c1}} = -g_{m2}\,(R_{C2} \parallel R_L) = -g_m \, \frac{R_{C2}\, R_L}{R_{C2} + R_L}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Overall gain

$$A_{v,\text{tot}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$$

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{tot}} = \left(-g_m \, \frac{R_{C1}\, r_\pi}{R_{C1} + r_\pi}\right) \cdot \left(-g_m \, \frac{R_{C2}\, R_L}{R_{C2} + R_L}\right) = g_m^{\,2}\, \frac{R_{C1}\, r_\pi}{R_{C1}+r_\pi} \cdot \frac{R_{C2}\, R_L}{R_{C2}+R_L}}$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Sign analysis

Two CE stages in cascade invert the signal **twice** ŌåÆ the output is **in phase** with the input (positive overall gain).

### Step 7 ŌĆö Numerical sanity check

If $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $R_{C1}=R_{C2}=4.7$ k$\Omega$, $r_\pi = \beta/g_m = 100/0.04 = 2.5$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$R_{L1}' = \frac{4700 \times 2500}{4700+2500} = \frac{11{,}750{,}000}{7200} \approx 1632\ \Omega$$

$$A_{v1} = -0.040 \times 1632 = -65.3$$

$$R_{L2}' = \frac{4700 \times 10000}{14700} \approx 3197\ \Omega$$

$$A_{v2} = -0.040 \times 3197 = -127.9$$

$$A_{v,\text{tot}} = (-65.3)\times(-127.9) \approx +8350 \quad (\approx 78\ \text{dB})$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Voltage Gain of a CE-CB Cascode

**Given:** Q1 (CE) stacked on Q2 (CB), matched BJTs, single supply $V_{CC}$, $R_E$ at Q1's emitter bypassed by a large capacitor, load $R_L$ at Q2's collector through $R_C$.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the AC load on Q1

Q1's collector drives Q2's emitter. The resistance looking into Q2's emitter (CB input) is:

$$R_{in,E2} = r_{e2} = \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

(For a CB stage, looking into the emitter gives $r_e$ when base is AC-grounded and $r_o$ is neglected.)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Voltage gain from Q1's base to Q2's emitter

$$A_{v,Q1} = -g_{m1} \cdot R_{in,E2} = -g_{m1} \cdot \frac{1}{g_{m2}}$$

Assuming matched transistors $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$:

$$A_{v,Q1} = -1$$

This is the *crux* of the cascode: the CE stage provides almost no voltage gain because its load is very small.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage gain from Q2's emitter to the output

Q2 is a CB stage. Its voltage gain from emitter to collector is:

$$A_{v,Q2} = +g_{m2}\, (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

The CB stage is **non-inverting** (positive gain).

### Step 4 ŌĆö Total cascode voltage gain

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = A_{v,Q1} \cdot A_{v,Q2} = (-1) \cdot \big[+g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)\big] = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{cascode}} \approx -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Compare with single CE

A single CE stage would give $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ ŌĆö *identical magnitude*. The cascode has matched DC gain, but vastly superior HF performance (see Section 3.4).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Cascode Input and Output Resistance

### Step 1 ŌĆö Input resistance

Looking into Q1's base, with emitter bypassed:

$$R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_{\pi 1} = \frac{R_B\, r_{\pi 1}}{R_B + r_{\pi 1}}$$

(For pure BJT without base-bias resistors: $R_{in} = r_{\pi 1}$.)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Output resistance including Early effect

Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output, measure $i_x$. Using the small-signal model with $r_{o1}, r_{o2}$:

$$R_{out} \approx r_{o2}\big[1 + g_{m2}(r_{o1} \parallel R_E)\big] + (r_{o1} \parallel R_E)$$

If $R_E$ is bypassed (AC short), $R_E \parallel \text{anything} \to 0$, and the formula reduces to:

$$\boxed{R_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\, r_{o1}\, r_{o2}}$$

For typical $r_o = 50$ k$\Omega$, $g_m = 40$ mA/V:

$$R_{out} \approx 50k + 50k + 0.040 \cdot 50k \cdot 50k = 100{,}000 + 100{,}000 = 200\ \text{k}\Omega + 100\ \text{M}\Omega \approx 100\ \text{M}\Omega$$

This **enormous** output resistance is what gives cascode stages their high DC gain when used as active loads.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö High-Frequency Response Comparison

### Step 1 ŌĆö Single CE upper $-3$ dB frequency

Using Miller's theorem on $C_\mu$ with voltage gain $A_v = -g_m R_L'$:

$$C_{M} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L')$$

$$f_{H,\text{CE}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\, R_{sig}'\, (C_\pi + C_{M})}$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Cascode upper $-3$ dB frequency

In cascode, Q1's gain to Q2's emitter is $-1$, so:

$$C_{M,\text{eff}} = C_\mu (1 - (-1)) = 2 C_\mu$$

$$f_{H,\text{cascode}} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi\, R_{sig}'\, (C_{\pi 1} + 2C_{\mu 1})}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Numerical comparison

Take $R_{sig}' = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C_\pi = 10$ pF, $C_\mu = 2$ pF, $g_m R_L' = 50$.

For single CE:
$$C_M = 2 \times 51 = 102 \text{ pF} \Rightarrow f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 112\text{pF}} \approx 1.42\ \text{MHz}$$

For cascode:
$$C_M = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ pF} \Rightarrow f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 1000 \cdot 14\text{pF}} \approx 11.4\ \text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cascode bandwidth} \approx 8\times \text{single CE bandwidth, with identical voltage gain.}}$$

---

## 3.5 Design Procedure ŌĆö Cascade Amplifier (KTU Design Question Pattern)

### Step 1 ŌĆö DC Bias design for Q1 and Q2 (assume $V_{CC}=12$ V, target $I_{C1}=I_{C2}=1$ mA, $\beta=100$, $V_{BE}=0.7$ V)

Choose $V_{CE1} = V_{CE2} = 4$ V (mid-rail for maximum swing).

**For Q1:** The collector of Q1 sits at $V_{CE1} = 4$ V, but it must also drive Q2's base at $V_{B2} = V_{BE2} + V_{E2}$. Assuming $V_{E2} = 0$ (emitter grounded), $V_{B2} = 0.7$ V.

DC constraint on Q1's collector:
$$V_{C1} = V_{B2} = 0.7\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{CE1} = 0.7\ \text{V (NOT 4 V!)}$$

This is a key design pitfall ŌĆö direct coupling *forces* $V_{C1} = V_{B2}$. To achieve proper bias, use a level-shifter or add a diode/resistor string between Q1's collector and Q2's base. Alternatively, use emitter degeneration on Q2.

**Choose $V_{CE1}=2$ V, $V_{CE2}=4$ V, $V_{B2}=2$ V, so we insert a 1.3 V drop with a Zener / diode.**

### Step 2 ŌĆö Choose $R_{C1}$ and $R_{C2}$

$$R_{C1} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{C1}}{I_{C1}} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{1\text{ mA}} = 11.3\ \text{k}\Omega \to 11\ \text{k}\Omega$$

But with level-shifted $V_{C1}=2$ V: $R_{C1} = 10$ k$\Omega$.

$$R_{C2} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{C2}}{I_{C2}} = \frac{12 - 7}{1\text{ mA}} = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Verify AC gain

With $g_m = 1\text{ mA}/26\text{ mV} = 38.5$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 100/0.0385 \approx 2.6$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$A_{v1} = -0.0385 \times (11\text{k}\parallel 2.6\text{k}) = -0.0385 \times 2100 = -80.9$$

$$A_{v2} = -0.0385 \times (5\text{k}\parallel 10\text{k}) = -0.0385 \times 3333 = -128.3$$

$$A_{v,\text{tot}} = 80.9 \times 128.3 \approx 10{,}380 \quad (80.3\ \text{dB})$$

---

## 3.6 Design Procedure ŌĆö Cascode Amplifier (KTU Design Question Pattern)

### Step 1 ŌĆö DC Bias

Stack Q1 and Q2 in series between $V_{CC}$ and ground.

Choose $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_C = 1$ mA, $V_{CE1} = V_{CE2} = 3$ V each, so total drop across both = 6 V. With $V_{CC} = 12$ V, the emitter of Q1 (or its degeneration resistor) drops the remaining 6 V.

Place $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$ at Q1's emitter (un-bypassed to set bias and provide DC stability, OR bypassed for max gain).

If $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$ un-bypassed:
- $V_{E1} = I_E \cdot R_E \approx 1\text{ mA} \cdot 1\text{ k}\Omega = 1$ V
- $V_{B1} = V_{E1} + V_{BE1} = 1 + 0.7 = 1.7$ V

Choose $R_B$ divider to set $V_{B1} = 1.7$ V:
- $R_{B1} = 100$ k$\Omega$ (top), $R_{B2} = 16.5$ k$\Omega$ (bottom)

### Step 2 ŌĆö Verify Q2's bias

Q2's base is AC-grounded (large capacitor to ground). DC bias for Q2's base:
- $V_{B2} = V_{C1} = V_{E1} + V_{CE1} = 1 + 3 = 4$ V
- $V_{E2} = V_{C1} = 4$ V, so $V_{BE2} = V_{B2} - V_{E2} = 4 - 4 = 0$ ŌĆö *bias fails!*

This means the cascode has a strict DC constraint: $V_{CE1} \ge V_{BE2} = 0.7$ V to keep Q2 forward-active.

Correct design: choose $V_{CE1} = 0.7$ V + safety margin = 1 V.

Recompute with $V_{CE1} = 1$ V, $V_{CE2} = 4$ V: total drop = 5 V ŌåÆ $V_E = 12 - 5 = 7$ V. Use $R_E = 7$ k$\Omega$.

Better: bypass $R_E$ to maximise AC gain. Then $V_{E1} = 0$ (DC), $V_{B1} = 0.7$ V, $V_{C1} = 1.7$ V, $V_{CE2} = V_{CC} - V_{C1} = 12 - 1.7 = 10.3$ V ŌåÆ large but acceptable.

### Step 3 ŌĆö AC Gain Calculation

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

If $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$A_v = -0.0385 \times 3333 = -128 \quad (\approx 42\ \text{dB})$$

This equals a single CE's gain ŌĆö but the cascode's $f_H$ is ~$\mathbf{8\times}$ higher.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Cascade vs Cascode

| Parameter | CE-CE Cascade | CE-CB Cascode | Winner |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|--------|
| Low-frequency gain | $g_m^2 R_{L1}' R_{L2}'$ | $g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Cascade (higher) |
| Sign | +ve (two inversions) | ŌłÆve (one inversion) | Depends on app. |
| Input resistance | $r_{\pi 1}$ | $r_{\pi 1}$ | Tie |
| Output resistance | $R_{C2}$ | $\approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ | Cascode (much higher) |
| Miller effect on Q1 | Full: $(1+A_v)C_\mu$ | Minimal: $2C_\mu$ | Cascode |
| Upper $-3$ dB freq | $0.644\, f_{H1}$ | $\sim f_{H1}$ (no shrinkage) | Cascode |
| Power consumption | $2 I_C \cdot V_{CC}$ | Same $2 I_C V_{CC}$ | Tie |
| Design complexity | Moderate | Higher (DC bias tricky) | Cascade |
| IC suitability | Lower (less used) | Very high (telescopic OTA) | Cascode |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3-mark short answers) and Part B (14-mark numerical/analytical with internal choice). Each carries a simulated past-year tag and a complete model solution.

---

## PART A ŌĆö 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Define a cascode amplifier. State two advantages over a single CE stage.**

**Model Answer:**

A cascode amplifier consists of a **Common Emitter (CE) stage** stacked on top of a **Common Base (CB) stage**, such that the emitter current of the CB transistor equals the collector current of the CE transistor.

Two advantages over a single CE stage:

1. **Reduced Miller Effect:** The CE stage in cascode has voltage gain Ōēł ŌłÆ1, so the Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$ is only a factor of 2 (instead of ~100 in a standalone CE). This yields a much higher upper cutoff frequency.
2. **Higher output resistance:** When Early effect is considered, $R_{out,\text{cascode}} \approx g_m r_{o1} r_{o2}$, which is far higher than the single CE's $R_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$. This gives higher intrinsic DC gain.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two advantages.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Why does the bandwidth of a multistage cascade amplifier shrink? Give the bandwidth relation for two identical stages.**

**Model Answer:**

When two identical amplifier stages are cascaded, the overall frequency response is the **product** of the individual responses. Each stage contributes a pole at its $-3$ dB frequency $f_1$. The product of two first-order low-pass responses yields a **second-order roll-off ($-40$ dB/decade)** beyond the dominant pole.

For two identical stages, the overall $-3$ dB frequency is:

$$f_H^{\text{cascade}} = \frac{f_1}{\sqrt{2^{1/2}-1}} \approx 0.644 \, f_1$$

This is called **bandwidth shrinkage** ŌĆö the cascade bandwidth is only about 64% of a single stage's bandwidth, while the gain has doubled (in dB). The gainŌĆōbandwidth product remains approximately constant per stage.

*(3 marks: 1 for qualitative reason, 2 for formula and 0.644 numerical.)*

---

## PART B ŌĆö 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** A two-stage CE-CE direct-coupled cascade amplifier uses identical BJTs with $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, and bias currents $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = 1$ mA. Collector resistors are $R_{C1} = 5.6$ k$\Omega$ and $R_{C2} = 4.7$ k$\Omega$. The load is $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$. Compute:
   (i) The small-signal parameters $g_m$, $r_\pi$ for each stage.
   (ii) The loaded voltage gain of stage 1 (accounting for input resistance of stage 2).
   (iii) The voltage gain of stage 2 and the **overall voltage gain** in dB.

**(b)** Compare cascade and cascode amplifiers in terms of (i) voltage gain, (ii) bandwidth, (iii) Miller effect, (iv) output resistance. Which configuration is preferred in integrated-circuit design and why?

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Compute $g_m$ and $r_\pi$:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1\text{ mA}}{26\text{ mV}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{26} \approx 0.03846\ \text{A/V} = 38.46\ \text{mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.03846} \approx 2600\ \Omega = 2.6\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Loaded gain of stage 1:**

The input resistance of stage 2 is $r_{\pi 2} = 2.6$ k$\Omega$. Effective load on Q1's collector:

$$R_{L1}' = R_{C1} \parallel r_{\pi 2} = \frac{5.6 \times 2.6}{5.6 + 2.6} = \frac{14.56}{8.2} = 1.776\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v1} = -g_{m1} R_{L1}' = -0.03846 \times 1776 \approx -68.3$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Gain of stage 2 and total:**

$$R_{L2}' = R_{C2} \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{v2} = -g_{m2} R_{L2}' = -0.03846 \times 3197 \approx -122.96$$

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} = (-68.3) \times (-122.96) \approx +8398$$

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{total,dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(8398) \approx 20 \times 3.924 \approx 78.5\ \text{dB}}$$

*(Award: 1 mark for $g_m$, 1 mark for $r_\pi$, 2 marks for $A_{v1}$ with loading, 2 marks for $A_{v2}$ and $A_{v,\text{tot}}$, 1 mark for dB conversion.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

| Aspect | CE-CE Cascade | CE-CB Cascode | Preferred |
|--------|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| Voltage gain | $g_m^2 R_{L1}' R_{L2}'$ (very high) | $g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ (moderate) | Cascade for raw gain |
| Bandwidth | Shrinks to $0.644 f_1$ | Approx $f_{H,\text{CB}}$, far higher | Cascode |
| Miller effect | Full $(1+A_v) C_\mu$ | Reduced to $2C_\mu$ | Cascode |
| Output resistance | $R_{C2}$ (a few k$\Omega$) | $\sim g_m r_{o1} r_{o2}$ (tens of M$\Omega$) | Cascode |

**Conclusion:** Cascode is preferred in **integrated-circuit design** because:
- ICs cannot use large bypass/ coupling capacitors easily (area constraint).
- High output resistance is essential for high DC gain with **active loads**.
- Cascode's superior bandwidth enables high-frequency IC operation (op-amps, RF, ADC front-ends).
- The telescopic cascode is the workhorse of modern CMOS op-amp input stages.

*(Award: 4 marks for the table, 2 marks for explanation, 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023, Module 2]` *(Alternative choice for Q-A)*

**(a)** Design a CE-CB cascode amplifier using two identical NPN BJTs ($\beta = 150$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V) with $V_{CC} = 15$ V and quiescent collector currents $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = 2$ mA. Take $V_{CE1} = 1.5$ V (to keep Q2 forward-active) and $V_{CE2} = 6$ V. Determine:
   (i) The emitter resistor $R_E$ at Q1's emitter.
   (ii) The collector resistor $R_C$ at Q2's collector.
   (iii) The small-signal voltage gain with a load $R_L = 5$ k$\Omega$.

**(b)** Explain why the cascode's upper $-3$ dB frequency is much higher than that of a single CE stage, with the aid of the Miller-effect equation. If $C_\mu = 2$ pF, $C_\pi = 8$ pF, $R_{sig}' = 1$ k$\Omega$, $g_m R_L' = 40$ (single CE), calculate both bandwidths.

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Determine $R_E$:**

Total voltage drop across the stack from $V_{CC}$ to ground (assuming $R_E$ carries $I_E \approx I_C = 2$ mA):

$$V_{R_E} = V_{CC} - V_{CE1} - V_{CE2} = 15 - 1.5 - 6 = 7.5\ \text{V}$$

$$R_E = \frac{V_{R_E}}{I_E} = \frac{7.5}{2\text{ mA}} = 3.75\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Determine $R_C$:**

$$V_{C2} = V_{CC} - I_{C2} R_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{RC} = I_C R_C$$

With $V_{CE2} = 6$ V, $V_{E2} = V_{C1} = V_{CE1} + V_{RE} - V_{RE}$ ... let's compute directly:

$$V_{E2} = V_{C1} = V_{RE} + V_{CE1} = 7.5 + 1.5 = 9\ \text{V}?$$

Wait ŌĆö recompute. The collector of Q2 is at:

$$V_{C2} = V_{CC} - I_{C2} R_C$$

For Q2: $V_{E2} = V_{C1}$, $V_{CE2} = V_{C2} - V_{E2} = 6$ V, so:

$$V_{C2} = V_{E2} + 6 = V_{C1} + 6$$

We know $V_{C1} = V_{E2} = V_{R_E} = 7.5$ V (since $V_{CE1} = 1.5$ V is across Q1 alone; $V_{C1} = V_{E1} + V_{CE1} = 0 + 1.5$? No ŌĆö $V_{E1}$ is at the top of $R_E$.)

Re-clarify the node voltages (with $R_E$ un-bypassed):
- $V_{E1} = I_E R_E = 2\text{mA} \times 3.75\text{k}\Omega = 7.5$ V
- $V_{B1} = V_{E1} + V_{BE1} = 7.5 + 0.7 = 8.2$ V
- $V_{C1} = V_{E1} + V_{CE1} = 7.5 + 1.5 = 9$ V ŌåÆ this equals $V_{E2}$
- $V_{C2} = V_{E2} + V_{CE2} = 9 + 6 = 15$ V ŌåÆ must equal $V_{CC}$, so $R_C = 0$!

This reveals a design pitfall: the chosen $V_{CE2}=6$ V and $R_E=3.75$ k$\Omega$ **incompatible**. Redesign:

Pick $V_{CE2} = 5$ V instead ŌåÆ $V_{C2} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$:

$$V_{C2} = 9 + 5 = 14\ \text{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_C = \frac{15 - 14}{2\text{mA}} = 0.5\ \text{k}\Omega = 500\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Voltage gain:**

$$g_m = \frac{2\text{ mA}}{26\text{ mV}} = 76.9\ \text{mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{150}{0.0769} \approx 1950\ \Omega$$

$$A_{v,\text{cascode}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -0.0769 \times \frac{500 \times 5000}{5500}$$

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{500 \times 5000}{5500} = 454.5\ \Omega$$

$$A_v = -0.0769 \times 454.5 = -34.96 \quad (\approx 30.9\ \text{dB})$$

$$\boxed{R_E = 3.75\ \text{k}\Omega,\quad R_C = 500\ \Omega,\quad A_v \approx -35}$$

*(Award: 2 marks for $R_E$, 2 marks for $R_C$ with the pitfall, 3 marks for gain.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Miller Effect Explanation:**

In a single CE stage, the collectorŌĆōbase capacitance $C_\mu$ sees a voltage gain $-g_m R_L'$. By Miller's theorem, this capacitance is reflected to the input as:

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + g_m R_L')$$

This capacitance, in parallel with $C_\pi$, dominates the high-frequency response.

In a cascode, Q1's voltage gain to Q2's emitter is only $-1$ (because $R_{in,\text{CB}} = 1/g_m$). So:

$$C_{M,\text{eff}} = C_\mu(1 - (-1)) = 2 C_\mu$$

The CE stage's Miller catastrophe is averted. Additionally, Q2 (CB) has its base AC-grounded, so $C_{\mu 2}$ has no Miller multiplication.

**Bandwidth Calculations:**

**Single CE:**
$$C_{M} = 2\text{ pF} \times (1 + 40) = 82\ \text{pF}$$
$$C_{\text{total}} = C_\pi + C_M = 8 + 82 = 90\ \text{pF}$$
$$f_{H,\text{CE}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{sig}' C_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 90\times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.77\ \text{MHz}$$

**Cascode:**
$$C_{M,\text{eff}} = 2 \times 2\text{ pF} = 4\ \text{pF}$$
$$C_{\text{total}} = 8 + 4 = 12\ \text{pF}$$
$$f_{H,\text{cascode}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 12\times 10^{-12}} \approx 13.26\ \text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{f_{H,\text{cascode}}}{f_{H,\text{CE}}} = \frac{13.26}{1.77} \approx 7.5\ \times \text{ improvement}}$$

*(Award: 3 marks for Miller explanation, 1 mark for CE bandwidth, 2 marks for cascode bandwidth, 1 mark for ratio.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the loading effect in cascade:** Many students write $A_{v,\text{total}} = (-g_m R_{C1})(-g_m R_{C2})$ and lose 3ŌĆō4 marks. Always include $r_{\pi 2}$ in parallel with $R_{C1}$ for stage 1.
> 2. **Skipping the Miller effect in cascode explanation:** KTU board examiners explicitly test this. If you write only "$A_v = -g_m R_C$" without explaining why cascode bandwidth is wider, you will lose 4ŌĆō5 marks.
> 3. **DC bias blunders in cascode:** Stating $V_{CE1} = V_{CE2} = V_{CC}/2$ without checking that $V_{CE1} \ge V_{BE2} = 0.7$ V. The constraint $V_{CE1} \ge V_{BE,\text{on}}$ is mandatory.
> 4. **Sign confusion:** Cascode has *one* inversion (negative gain); cascade of two CE has *two* inversions (positive overall gain). Mixing these up costs 1ŌĆō2 marks.
> 5. **No units in final answer:** Always express gain in dB alongside the linear value. KTU rubrics specifically award 1 mark for unit-aware answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as your **last-minute revision checklist** before entering the exam hall.

- **Cascade** = two (or more) CE (or CS) stages in series. Output of one drives input of next. **Gain multiplies; bandwidth shrinks** ($f_H \to 0.644 f_1$ for two identical stages).
- **Cascode** = CE stage stacked on a CB stage. Same low-frequency gain as a single CE, but **vastly wider bandwidth** because Miller effect is neutralised.
- **Critical formula ŌĆö Cascode gain:** $A_{v,\text{cascode}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$. Same as CE.
- **Critical formula ŌĆö Cascode Miller:** $C_{M,\text{eff}} = 2 C_\mu$ (not $(1+g_m R_L') C_\mu$).
- **Input resistance:** Same as first stage (cascade: $r_{\pi 1}$; cascode: $r_{\pi 1}$).
- **Output resistance:** Last stage's output. For cascode with Early effect: $R_{out} \approx g_m r_{o1} r_{o2}$ (very large ŌĆö key for active loads).
- **DC constraint in cascode:** $V_{CE1} \ge V_{BE,\text{on}} \approx 0.7$ V to keep Q2 in forward-active region.
- **Cascade sign:** Two CE inversions ŌåÆ output in phase with input (positive gain).
- **Cascode sign:** One CE inversion ŌåÆ output 180┬░ out of phase (negative gain).
- **Bandwidth shrinkage formula:** $f_H^{\text{n stages}} = f_1 / \sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$.
- **Why cascode is preferred in ICs:** (a) high $R_{out}$ for active-load high gain, (b) wide bandwidth, (c) no large coupling/bypass capacitors needed in fully-differential IC versions, (d) telescopic structure doubles output swing with proper bias.
- **VT value:** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at 300 K ŌĆö required constant for all small-signal BJT calculations.
- **Typical pitfall to avoid:** Direct-coupled cascade has $V_{C1} = V_{B2}$, which forces low $V_{CE1}$ unless a level-shifter is inserted.
- **Transistor matching:** In cascode design questions, always assume $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$ unless told otherwise ŌĆö this simplifies the gain-of-Q1 step to $-1$.
- **MOSFET cascode (CS-CG):** Same principles, with $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ and $R_{in} \to \infty$ at the gate. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats both BJT and MOSFET versions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect on Gain and Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect on Gain and Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Solid State Devices**, the *Effect on Gain and Bandwidth* describes how the small-signal **voltage gain** ($A_v$) and the operational **bandwidth** ($BW$) of a transistor-based amplifier are intrinsically coupled and jointly governed by the device's internal parasitic capacitances, transit-time effects, and bias conditions. The trade-off is mathematically captured by the **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product (GBW)**, which is a near-constant figure of merit for a given transistor at a given collector/drain current.

For a **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)**, the most important metric is the **Transition Frequency ($f_T$)**, defined as the frequency at which the magnitude of the common-emitter short-circuit current gain $\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert$ drops to **unity (1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **╬▒-cutoff** ($f_\alpha$), **╬▓-cutoff** ($f_\beta$), and **transition frequency** ($f_T$). The relationship $f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_\beta = h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta$ is a high-yield KTU question.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pouring water through a funnel into a bucket:
- The **width of the funnel mouth** is your **Gain** (how much signal gets amplified).
- The **speed at which water flows** (transit time) is your **Bandwidth** (how fast the device can respond to rapid changes).
- The **intrinsic physical size and viscosity of the funnel** are the parasitic capacitances and carrier transit times of the transistor.

If you make the funnel **wider** (more gain by increasing bias current or load), the water flows **slower** (bandwidth drops) because more charge must be moved per cycle. Conversely, a *narrower* funnel (lower gain) lets water pass *faster* (wider bandwidth). The **product** of the funnel-mouth size and the flow speed remains roughly constant ŌĆö this is the **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The **Transit Frequency $f_T$** in **Hz**, often quoted in **GHz** for modern RF transistors, is the single most important "speed rating" of a transistor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
- **Thermal Voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 26$ **mV**
- **Transition Frequency:** $f_T$ (Hz, typically MHz to GHz)
- **Low-frequency current gain:** $h_{fe0}$ or $\beta_0$ (dimensionless)
- **╬▓-cutoff frequency:** $f_\beta$ (Hz)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Frequency Dependence of Current Gain ŌĆö The Single-Pole Model

In a BJT, the common-emitter short-circuit current gain does **not** remain constant with frequency. Above a certain corner frequency, the gain rolls off at **ŌĆō20 dB/decade**. The single-pole approximation is:

$$\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}}$$

Where:
- $h_{fe0}$ is the **low-frequency** common-emitter current gain.
- $f_\beta$ is the **╬▓-cutoff frequency** (the 3-dB corner of the CE current gain).

**Physical Origin of the Pole:**
The dominant pole arises because the **baseŌĆōemitter diffusion capacitance** $C_\pi$ (or $C_{be}$) must be charged and discharged through the baseŌĆōemitter dynamic resistance $r_\pi$. The time constant is:

$$\tau_\beta = r_\pi C_\pi = \frac{1}{2\pi f_\beta}$$

### 2.2 The Transition Frequency $f_T$ ŌĆö Derivation Insight

The transition frequency is found by setting $\vert h_{fe}(f_T) \vert = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_\beta)^2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_T \approx h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta$$

This gives the **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product**:

$$\boxed{f_T = h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}}$$

Where:
- $g_m$ = transconductance $= I_C / V_T$
- $C_\pi$ = baseŌĆōemitter diffusion capacitance
- $C_\mu$ = baseŌĆōcollector (Miller) capacitance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** To maximize $f_T$, designers increase the **collector current** $I_C$ (raises $g_m$ proportionally) and use processes that minimize the **junction capacitances**.

### 2.3 The Miller Effect ŌĆö Why Bandwidth Shrinks

The **baseŌĆōcollector capacitance** $C_\mu$ appears at the input multiplied by the voltage gain $(1 + A_v)$ due to the **Miller Theorem**. This *boosts* the effective input capacitance:

$$C_{in,\text{eff}} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + A_v)$$

The resulting dominant input-pole frequency is:

$$f_{in} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_s \, [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + A_v)]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Gain Trap:** Doubling the voltage gain approximately **doubles** the effective Miller capacitance, **halving** the input bandwidth. This is the heart of the *GainŌĆōBandwidth Trade-off*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h_{fe0}$ | LF common-emitter current gain | $I_C / I_B$ (DC) | dimensionless |
| $f_\beta$ | ╬▓-cutoff frequency | $1 / (2\pi r_\pi C_\pi)$ | Hz |
| $f_T$ | Transition frequency | $h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ | Hz |
| $g_m$ | Transconductance | $I_C / V_T$ | A/V (Siemens) |
| $C_\pi$ | BaseŌĆōemitter capacitance | $C_{je} + \tau_F g_m$ | F |
| $C_\mu$ | BaseŌĆōcollector capacitance | $C_{jc}$ (approx.) | F |
| $C_{M,eff}$ | Miller-multiplied capacitance | $C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | F |
| $A_{v0}$ | Midband voltage gain | $-g_m R_C$ | dimensionless |
| $f_H$ | Upper 3-dB frequency | $1 / [2\pi R_{Th}(C_\pi + C_M)]$ | Hz |
| $GBW$ | GainŌĆōBandwidth Product | $\vert A_{v0} \vert \cdot f_H \approx f_T$ | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** In all rows above, the symbol $\vert x \vert$ denotes *magnitude* and is written using the LaTeX `\vert` command to prevent breaking the markdown pipe `|` syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **RF Amplifier Design:** $f_T$ determines the maximum useful frequency of a BJT in RF front-ends (e.g., mobile phones, Wi-Fi).
- **High-Speed Logic:** Emitter-Coupled Logic (ECL) and current-mode logic rely on high $f_T$ BJTs.
- **Optoelectronics:** The phototransistor's bandwidth is directly tied to $f_T$.
- **FET/MOSFET Analog Design:** The *transition frequency* concept extends to MOSFETs as $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_{gg})$, where $C_{gg}$ is the total gate capacitance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency-Dependent Common-Emitter Current Gain $h_{fe}(f)$

We start from the **hybrid-ŽĆ small-signal model** of the BJT. The input loop sees $r_\pi$ in parallel with $C_\pi$, and the output is short-circuited to find $\beta(f)$.

**Step 1:** Write the input admittance.
The baseŌĆōemitter input admittance is the parallel combination of $r_\pi$ and $C_\pi$:

$$Y_\pi = \frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi$$

**Step 2:** Write the short-circuit output current.
When the output is shorted ($v_{ce} = 0$), the current from the controlled source is $g_m v_\pi$. The base current $i_b$ flows entirely through $Y_\pi$:

$$i_b = v_\pi \cdot Y_\pi \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_\pi = \frac{i_b}{Y_\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Form the current gain.
The short-circuit collector current is $i_c = g_m v_\pi$:

$$h_{fe}(\omega) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{g_m}{Y_\pi} = \frac{g_m}{\frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $g_m r_\pi = h_{fe0}$ (a fundamental BJT identity, since $\beta_0 = g_m r_\pi$).

$$h_{fe}(\omega) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + j\omega r_\pi C_\pi}$$

**Step 5:** Define the time constant $\tau_\beta = r_\pi C_\pi$ and the cutoff $f_\beta = 1/(2\pi \tau_\beta)$.

$$h_{fe}(f) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}$$

**Step 6:** Magnitude.

$$\vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}}$$

> This is the single-pole roll-off central to the *Effect on Gain and Bandwidth*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Transition Frequency $f_T$ in Terms of Device Capacitances

**Step 1:** Start from the $f_\beta$ expression.

$$f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by $h_{fe0} = g_m r_\pi$.

$$f_T = h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta = g_m r_\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\pi}$$

**Step 3:** Account for the baseŌĆōcollector (Miller) capacitance. In a true short-circuit test, $C_\mu$ also appears at the input, so the *total* input capacitance is $C_\pi + C_\mu$:

$$\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}}$$

**Step 4:** Express $C_\pi$ in terms of bias and transit time.

$$C_\pi = C_{je} + C_{d} = C_{je} + \tau_F g_m$$

Where:
- $C_{je}$ = baseŌĆōemitter depletion (junction) capacitance
- $C_d = \tau_F g_m$ = baseŌĆōemitter **diffusion capacitance**
- $\tau_F$ = forward transit time of minority carriers across the base

**Step 5:** Final compact form.

$$f_T = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_F} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{C_{je} + C_\mu}{\tau_F g_m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> At **high collector current**, $g_m$ is large, the denominator $\to 1$, and $f_T \to 1/(2\pi \tau_F)$, saturating at a value set purely by the *base transit time*. This is why $f_T$ vs $I_C$ curves show a peak.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Miller Effect on Input Bandwidth

**Step 1:** In a CE amplifier with midband gain $A_v = -g_m R_C$, the Miller theorem multiplies $C_\mu$ by $(1 - A_v) \approx (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ for an inverting stage.

$$C_{M,eff} = C_\mu (1 + \vert A_v \vert) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 2:** The total effective input capacitance.

$$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

**Step 3:** The Th├®venin source resistance $R_{Th}$ (e.g., $R_s \parallel r_\pi$) forms an RC pole with $C_{in}$.

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

**Step 4:** The GainŌĆōBandwidth Product is therefore:

$$GBW = \vert A_{v0} \vert \cdot f_H = (g_m R_C) \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

**Step 5:** In the limit of large $g_m R_C$ (high-gain stage), the Miller term dominates:

$$GBW \approx \frac{g_m R_C}{2\pi R_{Th} C_\mu g_m R_C} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} C_\mu}$$

> **Conclusion:** The GBW of a *Miller-limited* amplifier is set by the source resistance and the *feedback* capacitance $C_\mu$ ŌĆö **independent of the gain**. This is the rigorous proof of the GainŌĆōBandwidth Trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Frequency Response of a CE BJT Amplifier

The diagram below maps the **causal chain** of how device physics transforms into observable electrical behavior ŌĆö replacing a literal hand-drawn Bode plot with a clean Mermaid-compliant architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal vi at Frequency f] --> B[Source Resistance Rs and Bias Network]
    B --> C[Base Emitter Junction rpi in parallel with Cpi]
    C --> D[Miller Multiplier Block 1 Plus Av]
    D --> E[Effective Input Capacitance Cin eff]
    E --> F[Input Pole Frequency fin equals 1 over 2pi Rs Cin eff]
    F --> G[Controlled Current Source gm vpi]
    G --> H[Output Collector Node with RC Load]
    H --> I[Output Pole at fout]
    I --> J[Voltage Gain Av of Gm times RC]
    J --> K{Bandwidth Decision}
    K -- If f less than fH --> L[Midband Gain Region Constant Av]
    K -- If f greater than fH --> M[Gain Rolls Off at minus 20 dB per Decade]
    L --> N[Gain Bandwidth Product GBW equals Av0 times fH]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Transition Frequency fT limits Maximum GBW]
```

### 4.2 Architecture Narrative (Block-by-Block Explanation)

| Block | Functional Role | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| A ŌåÆ B | Signal injection through source impedance | Determines $R_{Th}$ |
| B ŌåÆ C | BaseŌĆōemitter diffusion + depletion | Defines $r_\pi$, $C_\pi$ |
| D | **Miller Multiplier** $(1+A_v)$ | Core of the *Effect on Gain and Bandwidth* |
| E ŌåÆ F | Formation of input pole | Sets upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$ |
| G ŌåÆ H | Transconductance amplification | Defines DC gain $A_{v0} = g_m R_C$ |
| K | Branching logic: midband vs roll-off | Bode plot interpretation |
| N ŌåÆ O | Link to $f_T$ (device limit) | High-yield KTU relationship |

### 4.3 Schematic Equivalent (Hybrid-ŽĆ Model ŌĆö Text Representation)

```
            B
            |
            |---||---+
            |   C╬╝   |
            rŽĆ  ||   |
            |   CŽĆ   |
            |---||---+---- E
            |        |
            +----||--+-------- C (output node, drives RC load)
            |        |
           vŽĆ     gm┬ĘvŽĆ
            |        |
            +--------+
             (emitter, grounded)

   Input: ib enters base node B
   Output: ic = gm┬ĘvŽĆ leaves collector
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **textual** equivalent of the hybrid-ŽĆ model. It uses ASCII to remain compatible with all markdown renderers. For exam work, draw the proper schematic with $r_\pi$, $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, and the $g_m v_\pi$ current source.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*Define the transition frequency $f_T$ of a BJT. Why is it considered a figure of merit for high-frequency operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** $f_T$ is the frequency at which the magnitude of the common-emitter short-circuit current gain $\vert h_{fe} \vert$ falls to **unity**.
- (1 mark) **Mathematical form:** $f_T = h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$.
- (1 mark) **Figure of merit reasoning:** It is the **upper limit** of useful amplification; a transistor cannot provide current gain above $f_T$. It also equals the **gainŌĆōbandwidth product** for a single-stage CE amplifier, making it a single-number summary of the device's high-speed capability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*What is the Miller effect? How does it affect the bandwidth of a common-emitter amplifier?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** The Miller effect is the multiplication of the feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ (baseŌĆōcollector) by the factor $(1 + A_v)$ when reflected to the input of an inverting amplifier.
- (1 mark) **Effective input capacitance:** $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)$.
- (1 mark) **Bandwidth effect:** The increased $C_{in}$ reduces the input pole frequency $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{Th} C_{in})$, thereby **shrinking the upper 3-dB bandwidth** in proportion to the gain.

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **frequency-dependent common-emitter current gain** $h_{fe}(f)$ of a BJT using the hybrid-ŽĆ model. Clearly identify the ╬▓-cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the hybrid-ŽĆ model, derive the **transition frequency $f_T$** in terms of the device transconductance $g_m$ and the capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$. Explain how $f_T$ varies with collector current $I_C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Sketch and label the hybrid-ŽĆ model with $r_\pi$, $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, and the controlled source $g_m v_\pi$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* The input admittance seen at the baseŌĆōemitter is:

$$Y_\pi = \frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Short-circuit the output ($v_{ce} = 0$). The base current is:

$$i_b = v_\pi \left(\frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi\right)$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* The collector current is $i_c = g_m v_\pi$. Therefore:

$$h_{fe}(\omega) = \frac{i_c}{i_b} = \frac{g_m}{\frac{1}{r_\pi} + j\omega C_\pi}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Use the identity $g_m r_\pi = h_{fe0}$ (low-frequency CE current gain):

$$h_{fe}(\omega) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + j\omega r_\pi C_\pi}$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* The ╬▓-cutoff is where the imaginary part equals the real part:

$$f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi}$$

*Step 7 (1 mark):* Final form:

$$h_{fe}(f) = \frac{h_{fe0}}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \vert h_{fe}(f) \vert = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}}$$

**(b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* $f_T$ is defined by $\vert h_{fe}(f_T) \vert = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{h_{fe0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_\beta)^2}} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_T \approx h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Substitute the expressions:

$$f_T = h_{fe0} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi} = \frac{g_m r_\pi}{2\pi r_\pi C_\pi} = \frac{g_m}{2\pi C_\pi}$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Include the Miller capacitance at the input (true short-circuit test):

$$\boxed{f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}}$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Substitute $g_m = I_C / V_T$:

$$f_T = \frac{I_C}{2\pi V_T (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* As $I_C$ increases, $g_m \propto I_C$ **increases linearly**, but $C_\pi = C_{je} + \tau_F g_m$ also grows linearly. So $f_T$ initially rises with $I_C$.

*Step 6 (1 mark):* At very high $I_C$, the denominator $C_\pi$ grows in step with $g_m$, and $f_T$ **saturates** to $\approx 1/(2\pi \tau_F)$.

*Step 7 (1 mark):* At extremely high currents, high-level injection and base resistance $r_b$ degrade $f_T$, causing it to **fall** ŌĆö producing the characteristic peak in $f_T$ vs $I_C$ curves.

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** With the aid of the Miller theorem, derive an expression for the **upper 3-dB frequency** $f_H$ of a CE amplifier loaded by a resistance $R_C$. Show clearly how the bandwidth is limited by the **Miller-multiplied** capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product (GBW)** of the CE stage. Show that in the high-gain limit, the GBW becomes **independent of the midband voltage gain** and is set only by the source resistance and $C_\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ŌĆö 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* In a CE stage, the baseŌĆōcollector capacitance $C_\mu$ is connected between input and output. By the Miller theorem, its contribution at the input is:

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* The total input capacitance is:

$$C_{in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_C)$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* The Th├®venin resistance driving the base is $R_{Th} = R_s \parallel r_\pi \parallel R_B$.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* The dominant input pole is:

$$f_{H,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* An output pole also exists:

$$f_{H,out} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_C C_L}$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* The dominant (lower) pole is the input pole ŌĆö since $C_\mu$ is **Miller-multiplied**, $f_{H,in}$ is much smaller than $f_{H,out}$ in typical designs.

*Step 7 (1 mark):* Therefore, the bandwidth of the CE amplifier is:

$$f_H \approx f_{H,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

**(b) ŌĆö 7 marks**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Midband voltage gain magnitude: $\vert A_{v0} \vert = g_m R_C$.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* GainŌĆōBandwidth Product:

$$GBW = \vert A_{v0} \vert \cdot f_H = (g_m R_C) \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} [C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + g_m R_C)]}$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Assume high-gain limit where the Miller term dominates: $C_\mu g_m R_C \gg C_\pi + C_\mu$. Then:

$$GBW \approx \frac{g_m R_C}{2\pi R_{Th} \cdot C_\mu g_m R_C}$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* The $g_m R_C$ terms cancel:

$$GBW \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} C_\mu}$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* **Crucial conclusion:** The GBW is **independent of the voltage gain** $A_{v0}$. Doubling $R_C$ doubles the gain but halves the bandwidth, leaving the product constant.

*Step 6 (1 mark):* Engineering insight: To *increase* the GBW, one must **reduce $R_{Th}$** (use a lower source resistance or larger base-bias resistor) and **reduce $C_\mu$** (use a transistor with smaller feedback capacitance, e.g., cascode configuration).

*Step 7 (1 mark):* Note that this GBW is also bounded above by the **transition frequency** $f_T$ of the transistor ŌĆö the device's intrinsic speed limit. For multiple cascaded stages, the overall GBW is further reduced (e.g., the well-known $GBW_{n} \approx GBW_1 / \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ for $n$ identical stages).

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes ŌĆö Mark Losers in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing $f_T$ and $f_{max}$:** $f_T$ is the current-gain cutoff; $f_{max}$ is the power-gain cutoff (which includes $r_b$ and packaging). KTU expects $f_T$.
> 2. **Forgetting $C_\mu$ in the $f_T$ denominator:** Many students write $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_\pi)$ only. The **complete** form is $f_T = g_m / [2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$.
> 3. **Sign error in Miller multiplier:** For an **inverting** CE stage, the Miller multiplier is $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, **not** $(1 - A_v)$. Writing the wrong sign is a **2-mark penalty** in typical KTU papers.
> 4. **Ignoring the diffusion capacitance $C_d = \tau_F g_m$:** When asked for $C_\pi$, both the depletion and diffusion parts must be mentioned.
> 5. **Units:** Always express $f_T$ in Hz, MHz, or GHz. Mixing units (e.g., pF with mA/V inconsistently) is penalized.

---

### ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ **Current gain rolls off** above the ╬▓-cutoff $f_\beta$ at **ŌĆō20 dB/decade**.
- Ō£ģ **Transition frequency** $f_T$ is the unity-gain frequency: $f_T = h_{fe0} \cdot f_\beta = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)}$.
- Ō£ģ **Miller effect** multiplies $C_\mu$ at the input by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, drastically reducing $f_H$.
- Ō£ģ **GainŌĆōBandwidth Product** is approximately **constant** for a single CE stage: $GBW \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{Th} C_\mu}$.
- Ō£ģ **$f_T$ vs $I_C$:** rises linearly, saturates at $\dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_F}$, then falls due to high-level injection.
- Ō£ģ **To widen bandwidth:** reduce $R_{Th}$, reduce $C_\mu$ (cascode), increase $I_C$ modestly.
- Ō£ģ **Cascaded stages** have a *smaller* overall GBW than a single stage (bandwidth shrinks by the $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ rule).
- Ō£ģ **MOSFET analog:** $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$ ŌĆö same physical principle, different symbols.
- Ō£ģ **Key identity:** $g_m r_\pi = h_{fe0} = \beta_0$ (BJT only).
- Ō£ģ **Exam mantra:** *Gain up, Bandwidth down ŌĆö the product stays the same.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Feedback** in a solid-state amplifier or active device circuit is the process of returning a portion of the output signal back to the input terminal, either in-phase (positive feedback / regenerative feedback) or out-of-phase (negative feedback / degenerative feedback) with the original input signal. Mathematically, the **closed-loop gain** $A_f$ of a feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$ is given by the classical **Black's Feedback Equation**: $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The sign convention is governed by the denominator term $(1 \pm A\beta)$. A **negative sign** denotes *negative feedback* (degenerative), while a **positive sign** denotes *positive feedback* (regenerative). This single equation is the **backbone** of nearly all amplifier, oscillator, and control-system analysis in Solid-State Device theory. ### Classification of Feedback | Type | Denominator | Effect on Gain | Stability | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Negative Feedback** | $1 + A\beta$ | Reduces gain | Improves stability | Linear amplifiers, Op-amps | | **Positive Feedback** | $1 - A\beta$ | Increases gain | Causes instability / oscillation | Oscillators, Schmitt triggers | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö The Steering Wheel of a Car** > > Imagine driving a car (the **amplifier**) down a highway. > > - **Negative Feedback** is like *power steering with road feedback* ŌĆö every small deviation from the lane (distortion/noise) is sensed and a counter-correction is applied. The car becomes **smoother, more stable, and easier to control**, but it is slightly **slower to respond** (lower gain). > > - **Positive Feedback** is like *pressing the accelerator when the car drifts* ŌĆö the deviation is *amplified* rather than corrected. The car becomes **unstable** and eventually **oscillates wildly** (a crash in this case, or sustained oscillation in an electronic oscillator). ### Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU) - **Open-Loop Gain ($A$):** The gain of the amplifier **without** any feedback connection. - **Feedback Factor ($\beta$):** The fraction of the output that is fed back to the input (dimensionless, $0 < \beta \leq 1$). - **Loop Gain ($A\beta$):** The product of open-loop gain and feedback factor; represents the signal travelling around the entire feedback loop once. - **Return Difference $(1 + A\beta)$:** Determines how much the feedback modifies the basic amplifier performance. - **Desensitivity Factor $D = 1 + A\beta$:** Measures how insensitive the closed-loop gain is to variations in the open-loop gain $A$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):** > - The **Barkhausen Criterion** requires $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ with a total loop phase shift of **$0┬░$ or $360┬░$** for sustained oscillation. > - **Desensitivity** $D = 1 + A\beta$ is a **dimensionless** quantity. > - For high-fidelity audio amplifiers, the **closed-loop gain** typically uses $\beta$ values in the range **$0.001$ to $0.1$**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Basic Feedback Loop ŌĆö Operational Logic A feedback amplifier consists of **two fundamental blocks**: 1. **Basic Amplifier (Forward Path):** Provides the open-loop gain $A = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}'}$ where $V_{in}'$ is the *net* input after subtraction. 2. **Feedback Network (Reverse Path):** Samples a portion of the output and returns it with factor $\beta$. ### Step-by-Step Logical Flow (Why & How) - **Step 1 ŌĆö Input Summation:** The external input signal $V_{in}$ combines with the feedback signal $V_f = \beta V_{out}$ at the input mixing junction. - **Step 2 ŌĆö Net Input Calculation:** The amplifier only "sees" the error signal: $V_{in}' = V_{in} \pm V_f$. The sign depends on whether feedback is regenerative (+) or degenerative (ŌłÆ). - **Step 3 ŌĆö Amplification:** The amplifier boosts this net input: $V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}'$. - **Step 4 ŌĆö Feedback Sampling:** A fraction $\beta$ of the output is routed back: $V_f = \beta V_{out}$. - **Step 5 ŌĆö Closed-Loop Gain Derivation:** Substituting back yields the **canonical feedback equation** that defines how the gain transforms when the loop is closed. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in Solid-State Devices?** > In transistor and MOSFET amplifier design, **negative feedback** is the primary tool used by analog IC designers to achieve **predictable, stable, and linear** performance despite device-to-device variation, temperature drift, and supply fluctuations. **Positive feedback** is exploited in **oscillator circuits** (Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal, RC-Phase-Shift) to generate sinusoidal carriers used in wireless communication. ## 2.2 Detailed Analysis ŌĆö Negative Feedback (Degenerative) When the feedback signal **opposes** the input (i.e., $V_f$ is $180┬░$ out of phase with $V_{in}$): $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ ### Effects of Negative Feedback 1. **Gain Reduction:** $A_f < A$ because the denominator $(1 + A\beta) > 1$. 2. **Gain Stabilization:** $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ ŌĆö variations in $A$ are reduced by factor $D = 1 + A\beta$. 3. **Bandwidth Increase:** $BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ ŌĆö the gain-bandwidth product remains constant. 4. **Reduced Nonlinear Distortion:** Harmonics are suppressed by factor $D$. 5. **Input/Output Impedance Modification:** Depends on topology (series-shunt, shunt-series, etc.). ## 2.3 Detailed Analysis ŌĆö Positive Feedback (Regenerative) When the feedback signal **aids** the input (i.e., $V_f$ is in phase with $V_{in}$): $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$ ### Effects of Positive Feedback 1. **Gain Enhancement:** $A_f > A$ because the denominator $(1 - A\beta) < 1$. 2. **Reduced Stability:** Sensitivity to variations **increases** ŌĆö the system becomes prone to instability. 3. **Oscillation Condition (Barkhausen):** When $1 - A\beta = 0$, i.e., $A\beta = 1$, the gain becomes mathematically **infinite**, meaning the amplifier oscillates without any external input. 4. **Hysteresis Behavior:** Used in bistable multivibrators and Schmitt triggers. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for KTU board exams on this topic. Memorize this ŌĆö these appear in **almost every previous year paper**. | # | Parameter | Negative Feedback | Positive Feedback | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | Loop gain | $A\beta$ | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Desensitivity factor | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | $D' = 1 - A\beta$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Gain reduction ratio | $\dfrac{A_f}{A} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$ | $\dfrac{A_f}{A} = \dfrac{1}{1-A\beta}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Sensitivity to A | $\dfrac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-A\beta}$ | Smaller is better | | 6 | Bandwidth | $BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | $BW_f = \dfrac{BW}{1 - A\beta}$ | Hz | | 7 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $A_f \cdot BW_f = A \cdot BW = \text{constant}$ | Increases (until oscillation) | Hz | | 8 | Input impedance (series) | $R_{in,f} = R_{in}(1 + A\beta)$ | $R_{in,f} = R_{in}(1 - A\beta)$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 9 | Output impedance (shunt) | $R_{out,f} = \dfrac{R_{out}}{1 + A\beta}$ | $R_{out,f} = \dfrac{R_{out}}{1 - A\beta}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 10 | Oscillation condition | $A\beta = 0$ (no osc.) | $A\beta = 1$, phase = $0┬░$ or $360┬░$ | Barkhausen Criterion | | 11 | Phase margin for stability | $> 45┬░$ (preferred) | $< 45┬░$ (unstable) | Degrees | | 12 | Noise figure reduction | $NF_f = NF + 10\log(1+A\beta)$ | Noise amplified | dB | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Safety Note for Tables:** In the markdown table above, the absolute value / magnitude symbol is rendered as `\vert` (e.g., $\vert A\beta \vert$) to avoid breaking the table's column delimiters. **Do not** use the raw vertical pipe character `|` inside table cells. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps):** Use negative feedback with external $\beta$-networks to set precise, stable gains (inverting, non-inverting configurations). - **Voltage Regulators (LM7805, LM317):** Rely on negative feedback for tight output voltage regulation despite load and line variations. - **Crystal Oscillators & RF Oscillators:** Exploit positive feedback (Barkhausen criterion) for stable frequency generation in communication transmitters. - **Sensors and Instrumentation:** Strain gauges, thermocouples, and biomedical amplifiers use feedback for linearity and noise rejection. - **Audio Power Amplifiers:** Class-AB and Class-D amplifiers use heavy negative feedback ($A\beta \gg 1$) to reduce THD (Total Harmonic Distortion) to below **0.01%**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain Equation ### Derivation 1: Negative Feedback Closed-Loop Gain **Given:** A basic amplifier of gain $A$ with a feedback network that samples output $V_{out}$ and returns a signal $V_f = \beta V_{out}$ to the input summing junction, where the feedback signal **subtracts** from the input. **Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the input variables at the summing junction:** The total voltage appearing at the input of the basic amplifier is the **difference** between the externally applied input and the feedback signal: $$V_{in}' = V_{in} - V_f$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Express the output in terms of the basic amplifier gain:** The basic amplifier multiplies its net input by the open-loop gain $A$ to produce the output: $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}'$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute the feedback network relation:** The feedback signal is the feedback factor $\beta$ times the output voltage: $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:** $$V_{in}' = V_{in} - \beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute Step 4 into Step 2:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot (V_{in} - \beta \cdot V_{out})$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Expand the right-hand side:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} - A \cdot \beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Collect all $V_{out}$ terms on the left-hand side:** $$V_{out} + A\beta \cdot V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$ **Step 8 ŌĆö Factor out $V_{out}$:** $$V_{out} \cdot (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$ **Step 9 ŌĆö Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_f = V_{out}/V_{in}$:** $$A_f = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ This is the **canonical negative feedback gain equation**. $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** When $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep negative feedback), the equation simplifies to $A_f \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$, showing that the closed-loop gain depends **only on the passive feedback network** and is independent of the active device parameters. This is the *secret* behind the precision of op-amp circuits. --- ### Derivation 2: Positive Feedback Closed-Loop Gain **Given:** Same block diagram, but the feedback signal now **adds** to the input (regenerative configuration). **Step 1 ŌĆö Input summing junction (additive):** $$V_{in}' = V_{in} + V_f$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Amplifier output relation:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}'$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Feedback network:** $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute Step 3 into Step 1:** $$V_{in}' = V_{in} + \beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Substitute Step 4 into Step 2:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot (V_{in} + \beta \cdot V_{out})$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Expand:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} + A\beta \cdot V_{out}$$ **Step 7 ŌĆö Collect $V_{out}$ terms:** $$V_{out} - A\beta \cdot V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$ **Step 8 ŌĆö Factor:** $$V_{out} \cdot (1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$ **Step 9 ŌĆö Solve:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$ This is the **positive feedback gain equation**. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Observation:** As $A\beta \to 1^-$, the denominator $(1 - A\beta) \to 0$, and $A_f \to \infty$. Physically, this represents the **onset of oscillation** ŌĆö the Barkhausen criterion. For $A\beta > 1$, the system becomes **mathematically unstable** (saturation/clipping in real devices). --- ### Derivation 3: Sensitivity of Closed-Loop Gain to Open-Loop Gain Variation **Goal:** Quantify how a change in $A$ affects $A_f$ for negative feedback. **Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the closed-loop gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate $A_f$ with respect to $A$ (treat $\beta$ as constant):** Using the quotient rule: $\dfrac{d}{dA}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}\right) = \dfrac{(1+A\beta)(1) - A(\beta)}{(1+A\beta)^2}$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Simplify the numerator:** $$\frac{dA_f}{dA} = \frac{1 + A\beta - A\beta}{(1+A\beta)^2} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Express the fractional change (sensitivity):** $$\frac{dA_f}{A_f} = \frac{1}{(1+A\beta)^2} \cdot \frac{1+A\beta}{A} \cdot dA = \frac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \frac{dA}{A}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Final sensitivity relation:** $$\boxed{\;\frac{dA_f / A_f}{dA / A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}\;}$$ This proves that fractional changes in $A_f$ are **$(1 + A\beta)$ times smaller** than those in $A$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Negative Feedback on Bandwidth **Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the open-loop frequency response of a single-pole amplifier:** $$A(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}$$ where $A_0$ is the midband gain and $\omega_H$ is the upper ŌłÆ3 dB cutoff frequency. **Step 2 ŌĆö The closed-loop transfer function is:** $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A(j\omega)}{1 + A(j\omega)\beta} = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega/\omega_H)$:** $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega/\omega_H + A_0\beta}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Factor out $(1 + A_0\beta)$ from the denominator:** $$A_f(j\omega) = \frac{A_0/(1+A_0\beta)}{1 + \dfrac{j\omega}{\omega_H(1+A_0\beta)}}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Identify the new midband gain and new upper cutoff frequency:** $$A_{f,0} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta}, \qquad \omega_{H,f} = \omega_H(1+A_0\beta)$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Confirm the gain-bandwidth product is invariant:** $$A_{f,0} \cdot \omega_{H,f} = \frac{A_0}{1+A_0\beta} \cdot \omega_H(1+A_0\beta) = A_0 \omega_H = \text{constant}$$ $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is why op-amp datasheets specify the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** as a constant. A designer can trade gain for bandwidth, but cannot exceed the GBW limit. For the popular **╬╝A741 op-amp**, GBW $\approx$ **1 MHz**; for the **LM318**, GBW $\approx$ **15 MHz**. --- ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An amplifier has an open-loop gain $A = 1000$, feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$. If the input is $V_{in} = 10$ mV, find (i) the open-loop output, (ii) the closed-loop gain, (iii) the closed-loop output, and (iv) the feedback voltage. **Solution:** **Step (i) ŌĆö Open-loop output:** $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} = 1000 \times 10 \text{ mV} = 10 \text{ V}$$ **Step (ii) ŌĆö Closed-loop gain (negative feedback):** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{1000}{1 + (1000)(0.01)} = \frac{1000}{11} \approx 90.91$$ **Step (iii) ŌĆö Closed-loop output:** $$V_{out,f} = A_f \cdot V_{in} = 90.91 \times 10 \text{ mV} \approx 909.1 \text{ mV}$$ **Step (iv) ŌĆö Feedback voltage:** $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_{out,f} = 0.01 \times 909.1 \text{ mV} \approx 9.091 \text{ mV}$$ **Step (v) ŌĆö Verify net input equals $V_{in}' = V_{in} - V_f$:** $$V_{in}' = 10 \text{ mV} - 9.091 \text{ mV} = 0.909 \text{ mV}$$ $$V_{out,f} = A \cdot V_{in}' = 1000 \times 0.909 \text{ mV} = 909 \text{ mV} \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The open-loop output (10 V) is much larger than the closed-loop output (909 mV), confirming the **gain-reduction effect** of negative feedback. However, the output is now far more **stable and predictable**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Open-Loop Amplifier | Negative Feedback | Positive Feedback | |---|---|---|---| | Gain | High, variable | Reduced, stable | Increased, unstable | | Bandwidth | Limited | Increased by $(1+A\beta)$ | Decreased | | Distortion | High | Reduced by $D$ | Increased | | Noise | High | Reduced | Amplified | | Input Impedance | $R_{in}$ | $\uparrow$ (series) or $\downarrow$ (shunt) | $\downarrow$ (series) or $\uparrow$ (shunt) | | Output Impedance | $R_{out}$ | $\downarrow$ (shunt) or $\uparrow$ (series) | $\uparrow$ (shunt) or $\downarrow$ (series) | | Linearity | Poor | Excellent | Highly nonlinear | | Sensitivity to $A$ | High | Low by factor $D$ | Very high | | Stability | Marginal | Very stable | Unstable (oscillates) | | KTU Weightage | Low | **Very High** | **Very High** | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Feedback Amplifier The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete feedback architecture used in all solid-state feedback amplifier analyses (both BJT and MOSFET based). ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Vin Source] --> sumJ{Summing Junction +/-} fbNet[Feedback Network Beta] --> sumJ sumJ --> fwdAmp[Forward Amplifier Gain A] fwdAmp --> outNode[Vout] outNode --> load[Load RL] outNode --> fbNet ctrl[Controller Decision] -.-> fwdAmp ctrl -.-> fbNet ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **input source $V_{in}$** enters the **summing junction** (the small circle conceptually). - The **feedback network $\beta$** samples the output and returns a signal to the summing junction. - The **forward amplifier $A$** boosts the net (error) signal to produce $V_{out}$. - The signal flows in a closed loop: $V_{in} \to \Sigma \to A \to V_{out} \to \beta \to \Sigma$. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Effect Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Open Loop Gain A]) --> dec{Apply Feedback Type} dec -->|Negative FB| pathN[Denominator 1 + ABeta] dec -->|Positive FB| pathP[Denominator 1 - ABeta] pathN --> effN[Gain Reduced to Af = A / 1+ABeta] pathP --> effP[Gain Enhanced to Af = A / 1-ABeta] effN --> outN[Stable Linear Amplifier Behavior] effP --> outP{Loop Gain ABeta Reaches 1 ?} outP -->|No| stableP[Enhanced Gain but Reduced Stability] outP -->|Yes| osc[Barkhausen Sustained Oscillation] outN --> advN[Improved Bandwidth, Lower Distortion] osc --> oscUse[Used in Hartley Colpitts Crystal Oscillators] advN --> appUse[Used in OpAmps Regulators Audio Amplifiers] ``` ## 4.3 Effect of Loop Gain on Closed-Loop Behavior ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[ABeta = 0
No Feedback] --> A1[0 less than ABeta less than 1
Negative FB - Stable] A1 --> A2[ABeta = 1
Barkhausen Oscillation] A2 --> A3[ABeta greater than 1
Instability Saturation] A3 --> A4[Closed Loop Gain becomes
Mathematically Infinite] A1 --> note1[Af Approx 1/Beta when ABeta greater than 1] A2 --> note2[Oscillator Start-up Condition] A3 --> note3[Clipping in Real Devices] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram ŌĆö Negative Feedback Amplifier with Explicit Signals ```mermaid flowchart LR vin[V in] --> sumNode((+)) sumNode --> vinPrime[V in prime equals V in minus V f] vinPrime --> ampBlock[Amplifier Block Gain A] ampBlock --> vout[V out] vout --> loadTerm[Load] vout --> fbBlock[Feedback Network Beta] fbBlock --> vf[V f equals Beta V out] vf --> sumNode ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Fallback Note:** For complex physical transistor-level schematics (e.g., CE amplifier with emitter-bypass capacitor and emitter-degeneration resistor as negative feedback), the **block-level functional flow** shown above fully captures the analytical behaviour expected in KTU board examinations. Physical BJT/MOSFET schematics are typically tested in Lab/Design modules, not in this theoretical module. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Set B]** **Define feedback in amplifiers. How does negative feedback affect the gain of an amplifier?** **Model Answer:** > **Feedback** is the process of transferring a portion of the output signal back to the input of an amplifier. It can be **positive (regenerative)** or **negative (degenerative)**. > > In **negative feedback**, the feedback signal opposes the input, reducing the net input to the amplifier. The closed-loop gain becomes: > > $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ > > where $A$ is the open-loop gain, $\beta$ is the feedback factor, and $(1+A\beta) > 1$. Thus, **negative feedback reduces the gain** by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$, but simultaneously improves **stability, bandwidth, linearity, and reduces distortion and noise**. *(Examiner's Note: Students often forget to mention that the reduction in gain is accompanied by a *proportional* increase in bandwidth. Mentioning this earns the extra half-mark.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Set A]** **State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. What is its significance?** **Model Answer:** > The **Barkhausen criterion** states that a feedback circuit will produce sustained sinusoidal oscillations if **two conditions** are simultaneously satisfied: > > **(i) Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity. > $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$ > > **(ii) Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $360┬░$ (or $0┬░$). > $$\angle A\beta = 0┬░ \text{ or } 360┬░, \; 2n\pi \text{ rad}$$ > > **Significance:** This criterion is the **fundamental design rule** for sinusoidal oscillators (Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal, RC-Phase-Shift, Wien Bridge). It determines the **oscillation frequency** and the **minimum gain** required for the active device to sustain oscillations in the presence of positive feedback. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT skip the derivation steps** ŌĆö the Board explicitly awards **5 of 7 marks** in sub-parts (a) for the mathematical derivation alone. > - Always draw the **block diagram** before writing the equations. This is a **2-mark freebie** that most students forget. > - Use **standard notation** ($A$, $\beta$, $A_f$, $A\beta$, $1+A\beta$).
