Mixing symbols like $A_v$, $G$, $K$ costs marks. > - For numerical problems, **always verify the units** in the final answer. > - In comparison-based questions, a **summary table** at the end often fetches 1ŌĆō2 extra marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023, Module 3, Set A]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier. Hence explain its effect on (i) gain, (ii) bandwidth, and (iii) input impedance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An amplifier has an open-loop voltage gain of $2000$, input resistance $1\text{ k}\Omega$, and output resistance $50\text{ }\Omega$. A negative feedback network with $\beta = 0.02$ is introduced. Calculate the new values of closed-loop gain, input resistance, and output resistance (assuming voltage-shunt feedback topology). **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** ### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation and Effects (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the standard feedback block diagram (as shown in Section 4.4). Label: $V_{in}$, summing junction, $A$, $\beta$, $V_{out}$, $V_f$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Derivation (3 Marks):** The feedback signal $V_f = \beta V_{out}$ is subtracted from the input at the summing junction: $$V_{in}' = V_{in} - V_f = V_{in} - \beta V_{out}$$ The amplifier output is: $$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}' = A(V_{in} - \beta V_{out})$$ Solving for the closed-loop gain $A_f = V_{out}/V_{in}$: $$V_{out}(1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$ $$\boxed{\;A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}\;}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Effects (2 Marks):** **(i) Gain:** Gain is reduced from $A$ to $A/(1+A\beta)$. The reduction factor is $(1+A\beta)$. **(ii) Bandwidth:** Bandwidth increases by the same factor: $$BW_f = (1+A\beta) \cdot BW$$ The gain-bandwidth product $A \cdot BW$ remains **constant**. **(iii) Input Impedance:** For **series-mixing** at the input (voltage amplifier): $$R_{in,f} = R_{in}(1 + A\beta)$$ Input impedance is **increased** by factor $(1+A\beta)$, making the amplifier a better voltage amplifier (less loading of the source). --- ### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - Open-loop gain: $A = 2000$ - Open-loop input resistance: $R_{in} = 1\text{ k}\Omega = 1000\text{ }\Omega$ - Open-loop output resistance: $R_{out} = 50\text{ }\Omega$ - Feedback factor: $\beta = 0.02$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate the desensitivity factor:** $$1 + A\beta = 1 + (2000)(0.02) = 1 + 40 = 41$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Closed-loop gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{2000}{41} \approx 48.78$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Input resistance (series-mixing, voltage amplifier):** $$R_{in,f} = R_{in}(1 + A\beta) = 1000 \times 41 = 41{,}000\text{ }\Omega = 41\text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Output resistance (shunt-sampling, voltage amplifier):** $$R_{out,f} = \frac{R_{out}}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{50}{41} \approx 1.22\text{ }\Omega$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Summary Table:** | Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop (Negative FB) | Change Factor | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage gain $A$ | 2000 | 48.78 | Divided by 41 | | Input resistance $R_{in}$ | 1 k╬® | 41 k╬® | Multiplied by 41 | | Output resistance $R_{out}$ | 50 ╬® | 1.22 ╬® | Divided by 41 | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Negative feedback has reduced the gain by a factor of 41, but the input impedance is now 41├Ś higher (better voltage sensing) and the output impedance is 41├Ś lower (better voltage driving capability). This is the classic **voltage amplifier** characteristic ŌĆö high $R_{in}$, low $R_{out}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Module 3, Set B]** **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, derive the closed-loop gain of a positive feedback amplifier. Show that when $A\beta = 1$, the system enters sustained oscillation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the effects of positive and negative feedback on (i) gain, (ii) bandwidth, (iii) stability, and (iv) noise. State the Barkhausen criterion and explain its application in the Colpitts oscillator. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** ### Part (a) ŌĆö Positive Feedback Derivation (7 Marks) **Step 1 ŌĆö Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Same as negative feedback, but the feedback signal is **added** at the summing junction. **Step 2 ŌĆö Derivation (3 Marks):** $$V_{in}' = V_{in} + V_f = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$ $$V_{out} = A(V_{in} + \beta V_{out})$$ $$V_{out} - A\beta V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$ $$V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$ $$\boxed{\;A_f = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}\;}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Oscillation Analysis (2 Marks):** When $A\beta \to 1$, the denominator $(1 - A\beta) \to 0$, and the closed-loop gain $A_f \to \infty$. Physically, this means: with **no external input** ($V_{in} = 0$), the output is **non-zero** and self-sustaining. The amplifier has become an **oscillator**. This condition is the **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained oscillations. --- ### Part (b) ŌĆö Comparison & Colpitts Application (7 Marks) **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Parameter | Positive Feedback | Negative Feedback | |---|---|---| | (i) Gain | Increased: $A_f = A/(1-A\beta)$ | Decreased: $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ | | (ii) Bandwidth | Decreased | Increased by $(1+A\beta)$ | | (iii) Stability | Reduced (oscillates when $A\beta = 1$) | Highly stable | | (iv) Noise | Amplified | Reduced by $D = 1+A\beta$ | **Barkhausen Criterion (1 Mark):** $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 0┬░ \text{ or } 360┬░$$ **Colpitts Oscillator Application (2 Marks):** The **Colpitts oscillator** uses an LC tank circuit with a **capacitive voltage divider** ($C_1$ and $C_2$) as the feedback network. The feedback factor is: $$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$ (positive feedback through the capacitive divider). The oscillation frequency is: $$f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$$ The active device (BJT/FET) provides the gain $A$ such that $A\beta \geq 1$ at the oscillation frequency, satisfying the Barkhausen criterion. The Colpitts oscillator is widely used in **RF signal generation** (e.g., 10 MHz ŌĆō 100 MHz local oscillators in radio receivers). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - [ ] **Master the single most important equation:** $A_f = A/(1 \pm A\beta)$ ŌĆö *negative* uses $+$, *positive* uses $-$. - [ ] **Memorize the desensitivity factor** $D = 1 + A\beta$ ŌĆö appears in *every* sub-question. - [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion** ŌĆö both conditions (magnitude = 1 AND phase = 0┬░/360┬░) are *equally necessary*. - [ ] **Gain-Bandwidth Product is invariant** under negative feedback ŌĆö a high-yield derivation question. - [ ] **Draw the block diagram first** in every derivation ŌĆö earns 2 free marks. - [ ] **Distinguish the four feedback topologies** ŌĆö voltage-series, voltage-shunt, current-series, current-shunt (each has different $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$ effects). - [ ] **Negative feedback** ŌåÆ *reduces* gain, *increases* bandwidth, *improves* stability, *reduces* distortion, *reduces* noise. - [ ] **Positive feedback** ŌåÆ *increases* gain, *reduces* bandwidth, *decreases* stability, *amplifies* noise, *used in oscillators*. - [ ] **Deep feedback** ($A\beta \gg 1$): $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ ŌĆö gain depends only on passive network. - [ ] **Sensitivity formula:** $\dfrac{dA_f/A_f}{dA/A} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}$ ŌĆö desensitization. - [ ] **Numerical problems** usually specify $A$, $\beta$, $R_{in}$, $R_{out}$ ŌĆö compute $D = 1+A\beta$ first, then apply transformations. - [ ] **Standard textbook numerical example** (in KTU 2024 syllabus): $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.01$ ŌåÆ $A_f = 1000/11 \approx 90.91$. - [ ] **Bandwidth multiplication:** $BW_f = BW \times (1+A\beta)$. - [ ] **Colpitts and Hartley** are the most commonly tested oscillators ŌĆö know the $\beta$ expressions and $f_{osc}$ formulas. - [ ] **Op-amp non-inverting amplifier:** $A_f = 1 + R_f/R_1$ ŌĆö derive from $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ with $\beta = R_1/(R_1+R_f)$. - [ ] **Phase margin** must be $> 45┬░$ for stable negative feedback operation. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 Topic Notes ŌĆö "Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain"** > > Master the *single feedback equation* and the *Barkhausen criterion*, and you have secured **at least 60% of the marks** this topic can offer in the KTU ESE. The remaining marks come from numerical practice on the four feedback topologies and oscillator design problems. Good luck!

topics.title: Frequency Response and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Frequency Response ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Response** of an electronic amplifier is the measure of the amplifier's output magnitude (gain) and phase as a function of input signal frequency, while maintaining a constant input amplitude. It defines the operational bandwidth over which the amplifier faithfully reproduces the input signal without attenuation or phase distortion.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET417 ŌĆō Solid State Devices)**, frequency response is mathematically described by the **transfer function** $H(j\omega)$ of the amplifier, where the magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ gives the **gain response** and the angle $\angle H(j\omega)$ gives the **phase response**.

The complete frequency response spectrum is typically divided into three regions:

1. **Low-Frequency Region** (typically < **100 Hz** for audio amplifiers): Dominated by coupling and bypass capacitors which act as high-impedance elements.
2. **Mid-Frequency Region** (typically **100 Hz to 20 kHz** for audio, or **kHzŌĆōMHz** for RF): All reactive components are effectively short circuits (coupling) or open circuits (junction capacitances). Gain is **constant** and equal to mid-band gain $A_{mid}$.
3. **High-Frequency Region** (typically > **20 kHz** for audio): Dominated by internal transistor capacitances (junction, diffusion) and stray wiring capacitances.

## 1.2 Distortion ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Distortion** is any unwanted change in the output waveform of an amplifier relative to the input waveform. It is broadly classified into two categories:
> - **Linear (Frequency) Distortion**: The amplitude of different frequency components is altered unequally. The waveform shape changes, but no new frequencies are created.
> - **Non-Linear (Amplitude) Distortion**: The amplifier's transfer characteristic is non-linear, causing the generation of **new frequency components** (harmonics and intermodulation products) not present in the input signal.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Real-World Analogy for Frequency Response:
Imagine a **musical sound system** with **bass, mid-range, and treble controls**. When you set the bass too high and the treble too low, the same song played through this system sounds **muffled and unbalanced** ŌĆö the high-frequency cymbals vanish, while the low-frequency drums dominate. The system is not equally responsive to all frequencies. This is exactly what happens in an electronic amplifier at low and high frequencies ŌĆö the gain is **frequency-dependent**.

### Real-World Analogy for Distortion:
Take a **perfectly round balloon (the pure input signal)** and press it into an irregular-shaped box. When you inflate it (amplify it), it now has **corners and edges it never had before** ŌĆö these are the **new harmonic frequencies** created by non-linear distortion. The output is shaped differently from the input, even though the input itself was clean.

### Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Values):
- **Cutoff Frequency** ($f_L$, $f_H$): The frequency at which the gain drops to **0.707** (i.e., $-3\,\text{dB}$) of its mid-band value. **Bold symbol: $f_c$**
- **Bandwidth (BW)**: $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_L \ll f_H$).
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)**: A figure of merit, constant for a given amplifier, given by $GBP = A_{mid} \times BW$.
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**: Expressed as a **percentage** of the fundamental; ideally **0%** for a perfect amplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The course outcome mapping for this topic emphasizes **CO2 (Analyze the frequency response characteristics of solid-state devices)** and **CO3 (Evaluate distortion mechanisms in amplifiers)**. Students are expected to derive cutoff frequencies, apply the Miller theorem, and compute THD numerically.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Region Frequency Response Model

The gain of a typical RC-coupled amplifier as a function of frequency can be expressed as:

$$A_v(f) = \frac{A_{mid}}{\left(1 - j\frac{f_L}{f}\right)\left(1 + j\frac{f}{f_H}\right)}$$

This compact transfer function captures the behavior in **all three regions** simultaneously.

### Region-Wise Behavioral Summary:

**1. Low-Frequency Region ($f \ll f_L$):**
- Coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ between source and transistor input presents high impedance ($X_C = 1/2\pi f C$).
- Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ becomes ineffective, introducing degenerative feedback.
- **Gain rolls off at +20 dB/decade** as frequency decreases.
- At $f = f_L$: Gain is at **ŌłÆ3 dB point**, and phase shift is **+45┬░**.

**2. Mid-Frequency Region ($f_L \le f \le f_H$):**
- All coupling/bypass capacitors ŌåÆ act as **short circuits** ($X_C \approx 0$).
- All internal transistor capacitances ŌåÆ act as **open circuits** ($X_C \approx \infty$).
- Gain is **constant** at $A_{mid} = \beta \cdot R'_C / r_\pi$ (for CE configuration).
- Phase shift is **180┬░** (inherent phase inversion in CE amplifier).
- This is the **usable region** of the amplifier.

**3. High-Frequency Region ($f \gg f_H$):**
- Transistor internal capacitances (base-emitter diffusion $C_\pi$, base-collector junction $C_\mu$) become dominant.
- **Miller Effect** multiplies $C_\mu$ by the voltage gain, creating a large effective input capacitance.
- Gain rolls off at **ŌłÆ20 dB/decade** as frequency increases.
- At $f = f_H$: Gain is at **ŌłÆ3 dB point**, and phase shift is **ŌłÆ45┬░** (relative to mid-band).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every key formula tested in KTU 2024 Scheme university examinations for this topic. **Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq,L} C_{eq,L}}$ | Lower cutoff frequency (RC network) | Hz |
| 2 | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq,H} C_{eq,H}}$ | Upper cutoff frequency (RC network) | Hz |
| 3 | $C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$ | Miller input capacitance (CE) | F |
| 4 | $C_{M,out} = C_\mu\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_\mu$ | Miller output capacitance (CE) | F |
| 5 | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Bandwidth | Hz |
| 6 | $GBP = A_{mid} \times BW$ | Gain-Bandwidth Product | Hz |
| 7 | $t_r = \dfrac{0.35}{BW}$ | Rise time (10%ŌĆō90%) | s |
| 8 | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 9 | $D_2 = \dfrac{\vert V_2\vert}{\vert V_1\vert} \times 100\%$ | Second Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 10 | $D_3 = \dfrac{\vert V_3\vert}{\vert V_1\vert} \times 100\%$ | Third Harmonic Distortion | % |
| 11 | $A_v(s) = \dfrac{A_{mid}\,s^2}{(s + \omega_L)(s + \omega_H)}$ | Transfer function in s-domain | ŌĆö |
| 12 | $f_{c,total} = \sqrt{\sum f_{L,i}^2}$ | Combined lower cutoff (independent stages) | Hz |
| 13 | $\dfrac{1}{f_{H,total}} = \sqrt{\sum \dfrac{1}{f_{H,i}^2}}$ | Combined upper cutoff (independent stages) | s |
| 14 | $D_{IM} \approx \dfrac{V_{21}}{V_{11}}$ | Intermodulation Distortion (2nd order approx.) | ratio |

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Audio Power Amplifiers** require flat frequency response from **20 Hz to 20 kHz** to faithfully reproduce the entire audible human hearing range.
- **RF Communication Systems** (mobile phones, Wi-Fi) demand carefully controlled bandwidth at **MHz to GHz** frequencies to select the desired channel and reject adjacent ones.
- **Medical Instrumentation** (ECG, EEG amplifiers) must have ultra-low noise and high-fidelity frequency response in the **0.05 Hz ŌĆō 150 Hz** range to capture bioelectric signals accurately.
- **Oscilloscope Front-End Amplifiers** use the relationship $t_r = 0.35/BW$ to design amplifiers that can display fast transients without ringing.
- **Hi-Fi Audio Systems** specify THD of less than **0.01%** at 1 kHz for premium sound quality.

## 2.4 Distortion Classification ŌĆö Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Linear (Frequency) Distortion
- **Amplitude Distortion**: Unequal amplification of different frequency components.
- **Phase (Delay) Distortion**: Different frequencies suffer different time delays through the amplifier, causing waveform skewing. **Critical for digital signals** ŌĆö different bit edges arrive at different times, causing inter-symbol interference.

### B. Non-Linear (Amplitude) Distortion
- **Harmonic Distortion**: Generation of integer multiples ($2f$, $3f$, $4f$, ...) of the input frequency.
  - **Even-Order Harmonics** ($2f$, $4f$, ...): Sound "musical" and warm (used in guitar tube amplifiers).
  - **Odd-Order Harmonics** ($3f$, $5f$, ...): Sound harsh and dissonant (generally undesirable).
- **Intermodulation Distortion (IMD)**: When two or more frequencies ($f_1$, $f_2$) are applied simultaneously, new frequencies appear at sums and differences ($f_1 + f_2$, $f_1 - f_2$, $2f_1 + f_2$, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Cutoff Frequency from a Coupling RC Network

Consider a source $V_s$ with internal resistance $R_S$ connected through coupling capacitor $C_C$ to a load $R_{in}$ of the next stage. We want to find the frequency at which the output drops to **$1/\sqrt{2}$** of its mid-band value.

### Step 1: Write the Voltage Divider in s-domain
The transfer function of the simple high-pass RC network is:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_s(s)} = \frac{R_{in}}{R_S + R_{in} + \dfrac{1}{sC_C}}$$

### Step 2: Simplify the Expression
Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC_C$:

$$H(s) = \frac{s R_{in} C_C}{s(R_S + R_{in})C_C + 1}$$

### Step 3: Identify the Pole Frequency
This is in standard high-pass form. The pole occurs at $s = -1/[(R_S + R_{in})C_C]$, giving the cutoff frequency:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi(R_S + R_{in})C_C}$$

**Physical interpretation**: Thevenize the source side. The effective resistance seen by the capacitor is $R_{eq} = R_S \parallel R_{in}$ when computing the discharge time constant, but for the high-pass response, the total series resistance is $R_S + R_{in}$. The cutoff falls as the capacitor's reactance $1/(2\pi f C_C)$ becomes comparable to the total series resistance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Miller's Theorem and Upper Cutoff Frequency

The **Miller Effect** is the single most important high-frequency phenomenon in transistor amplifier analysis. It explains why the input capacitance of a common-emitter amplifier is much larger than the physical $C_\mu$ alone.

### Step 1: The Original Miller's Theorem
Consider an impedance $Z$ connected between input node (voltage $V_1$) and output node (voltage $V_2$) of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $A_v = V_2/V_1$.

**Claim**: The current $I_1$ flowing into $Z$ from the input side is:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z} = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z} = \frac{V_1(1 - A_v)}{Z}$$

This is equivalent to an impedance $Z_1 = Z/(1 - A_v)$ connected from input to ground.

### Step 2: Application to CE Amplifier
For a common-emitter amplifier, the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ is connected between input and output. With $A_v$ large and negative, $(1 - A_v) \approx 1 + \vert A_v \vert$.

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$

### Step 3: Compute the Upper Cutoff Frequency
The total input capacitance becomes $C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{M,in} = C_\pi + C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$.

The upper cutoff frequency is determined by the input RC time constant:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq,in} \cdot C_{in,total}}$$

where $R_{eq,in} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$ is the effective Thevenin resistance at the input.

### Step 4: Physical Interpretation
Because $C_{M,in}$ can be **100├Ś to 1000├Ś larger** than $C_\mu$ itself (for typical gains of 50ŌĆō500), it dominates the high-frequency response. This is why CE amplifiers have **much poorer high-frequency response** than common-base or common-collector configurations, where the Miller effect is absent or minimal.

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Harmonic Distortion from Fourier Series

Consider an amplifier with a non-linear transfer characteristic. A pure sinusoidal input $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ produces an output containing harmonics.

### Step 1: Express Output as Fourier Series
The output, being periodic with period $T = 2\pi/\omega$, can be written as:

$$v_o(t) = V_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} V_n \sin(n\omega t + \phi_n)$$

where $V_1$ is the fundamental amplitude, and $V_2, V_3, \ldots$ are the 2nd, 3rd, etc., harmonic amplitudes. The DC offset $V_0$ arises from rectification in the non-linear characteristic.

### Step 2: Define Individual Harmonic Distortions
The second harmonic distortion is:

$$D_2 = \frac{\vert V_2\vert}{\vert V_1\vert} \times 100\%$$

The third harmonic distortion is:

$$D_3 = \frac{\vert V_3\vert}{\vert V_1\vert} \times 100\%$$

### Step 3: Combine into Total Harmonic Distortion
The RMS value of the harmonic content is $\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}$. Normalizing to the fundamental:

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{\vert V_1\vert} \times 100\%$$

### Step 4: Useful Approximation
When $D_2$ and $D_3$ dominate (higher harmonics negligible), the THD reduces to:

$$THD \approx \sqrt{D_2^2 + D_3^2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Rise Time ŌĆö Bandwidth Relationship

The relationship $t_r = 0.35/BW$ is derived by considering a single-pole low-pass RC network and computing its step response.

### Step 1: Step Response of a Single-Pole Low-Pass Filter
For an RC low-pass filter with cutoff $f_H = 1/(2\pi RC)$, the step response is:

$$v_o(t) = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

### Step 2: Compute the 10% and 90% Times
The 10% time $t_1$ satisfies $0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_1/RC}$, giving $t_1 = -RC \ln(0.9) \approx 0.105 \cdot RC$.

The 90% time $t_2$ satisfies $0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_2/RC}$, giving $t_2 = -RC \ln(0.1) \approx 2.303 \cdot RC$.

### Step 3: Compute the Rise Time
$$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = RC(2.303 - 0.105) = 2.197 \cdot RC$$

Since $f_H = 1/(2\pi RC)$, we have $RC = 1/(2\pi f_H)$. Substituting:

$$t_r = \frac{2.197}{2\pi f_H} \approx \frac{0.35}{f_H}$$

This is the **famous KTU-exam-favorite relationship** for a single-pole amplifier.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Three-Region Frequency Response of an Amplifier

The following block diagram illustrates how the input signal flows through different frequency-determining elements in each region of operation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Signal Source] --> B[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    B --> C[Transistor Amplifier Stage]
    C --> D[Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    D --> E[Load Resistor RL]

    subgraph LF[LOW FREQUENCY REGION f less than fL]
        B1[Coupling and Bypass Capacitors Dominate] --> B2[Gain Increases at 20 dB per Decade as f Increases]
    end

    subgraph MF[MID FREQUENCY REGION fL to fH]
        C1[All Capacitors Negligible Reactance] --> C2[Constant Mid Band Gain Amid]
        C2 --> C3[Phase Shift 180 degrees for CE]
    end

    subgraph HF[HIGH FREQUENCY REGION f greater than fH]
        D1[Miller Capacitance and Transistor Internal C Dominate] --> D2[Gain Rolls Off at minus 20 dB per Decade]
    end

    style LF fill:#ffe4e1
    style MF fill:#e0ffe0
    style HF fill:#e0e0ff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Frequency Response Curve (Bode Plot Equivalent)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PLOT[GAIN vs FREQUENCY BODE PLOT]
        X[Frequency Axis Log Scale] --> Y[Gain in dB Linear Scale]
    end

    N1[Region 1: f less than fL, Gain rising at 20 dB per decade] --> N2[Region 2: fL to fH, Flat mid band gain Amid]
    N2 --> N3[Region 3: f greater than fH, Gain falling at minus 20 dB per decade]

    N1 -.- P1[At fL, Gain is Amid minus 3 dB]
    N2 -.- P2[Mid Band Gain Amid is Maximum Flat Gain]
    N3 -.- P3[At fH, Gain is Amid minus 3 dB]

    P1 --> BW[Bandwidth BW equals fH minus fL approximately fH]
    P2 --> GBP[Gain Bandwidth Product GBP equals Amid times BW]
    P3 --> TR[Rise Time tr equals 0.35 divided by BW]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Distortion Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    DIST[Distortion in Amplifiers] --> LD[Linear Frequency Distortion]
    DIST --> ND[Non Linear Amplitude Distortion]

    LD --> AD[Amplitude Distortion: Unequal gain at different frequencies]
    LD --> PD[Phase Delay Distortion: Different time delays per frequency]

    ND --> HD[Harmonic Distortion: New integer multiple frequencies generated]
    ND --> IMD[Intermodulation Distortion: Sum and difference frequencies generated]

    HD --> EH[Even Order Harmonics: 2f, 4f, Warm Musical Sound]
    HD --> OH[Odd Order Harmonics: 3f, 5f, Harsh Dissonant Sound]

    IMD --> F2[Second Order Products: f1 plus f2, f1 minus f2]
    IMD --> F3[Third Order Products: 2f1 plus f2, 2f2 minus f1]

    EH --> THD[Total Harmonic Distortion THD computed as RMS of all harmonics divided by fundamental]
    OH --> THD
    F2 --> IMR[Intermodulation Ratio IMR computed as IMD amplitude divided by fundamental]
    F3 --> IMR
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Miller Effect Physical Picture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ORIGINAL[Original Circuit]
        I1[Input Node Voltage V1] --- CB[Base Collector Capacitance Cmu] --- O1[Output Node Voltage V2 equals Av times V1]
    end

    subgraph MILLER[Equivalent Miller Input Circuit]
        I2[Input Node Voltage V1] --- CM[Effective Miller Capacitance Cmu times 1 plus Av] --- G[Ground]
    end

    subgraph MILLEROUT[Equivalent Miller Output Circuit]
        O2[Output Node Voltage V2] --- CO[Effective Miller Output Capacitance Cmu times 1 minus 1 over Av] --- G2[Ground]
    end

    ORIGINAL --> MILLER
    ORIGINAL --> MILLEROUT

    style ORIGINAL fill:#fff4e1
    style MILLER fill:#e1f4ff
    style MILLEROUT fill:#e1ffe1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define the term Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) and explain its significance in amplifier design.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** THD is the ratio of the RMS sum of the amplitudes of all harmonic components (excluding the fundamental) to the amplitude of the fundamental frequency component in the output, expressed as a percentage.

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

**Significance (3 marks breakdown):**
- **(1 mark)** THD quantifies the degree of non-linear distortion introduced by the amplifier.
- **(1 mark)** Lower THD indicates higher fidelity; high-fidelity audio amplifiers typically require THD < 0.01%.
- **(1 mark)** THD is a key figure-of-merit in specifications of audio amplifiers, power amplifiers, and communication circuits.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Sample Paper]
**State and explain the Miller Effect in a common-emitter amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** The Miller Effect refers to the multiplication of the base-collector capacitance $C_\mu$ by the voltage gain $(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$ when viewed from the input side of a CE amplifier.

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$$

**Explanation (3 marks breakdown):**
- **(1 mark)** $C_\mu$ is connected between the input (base) and output (collector) nodes. Since the output is $A_v$ times the input (and inverted), the voltage across $C_\mu$ is $(1 - A_v)$ times the input.
- **(1 mark)** This causes a much larger current to flow through $C_\mu$ than would flow for the same physical capacitance at the input node.
- **(1 mark)** Equivalently, this current can be modeled as flowing through a much larger effective capacitance $C_{M,in}$ to ground at the input, degrading the high-frequency response.

---

## PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Main Paper Set A]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **lower cutoff frequency** of a single-stage RC-coupled amplifier considering the input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$, the output coupling capacitor $C_{out}$, and the emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$. State clearly the Thevenin resistance seen by each capacitor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Miller Effect** in detail. For a common-emitter amplifier with $C_\mu = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_\pi = 50\,\text{pF}$, mid-band voltage gain $A_v = -100$, and source resistance $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, compute the **upper cutoff frequency** $f_H$. Given $r_\pi = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_B = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1: Identify the three capacitors and their effect.**
The three capacitors that affect the low-frequency response are:
- $C_{in}$: Coupling capacitor between source and transistor input.
- $C_{out}$: Coupling capacitor between transistor output and load.
- $C_E$: Emitter bypass capacitor across the emitter resistor.

**Step 2: Compute Thevenin resistance for $C_{in}$.**
Looking from the terminals of $C_{in}$, the source resistance $R_S$ is in series with the parallel combination of $R_B$ and the input resistance of the transistor at the base. The latter is approximately $R_B \parallel r_\pi$ for low frequencies (without $R_E$ degeneration, since $C_E$ is a short at mid-band but a partial short at low frequencies).

For simplicity at this stage, the effective resistance is:

$$R_{in,th} = R_S + (R_B \parallel r_\pi)$$

Thus the cutoff due to $C_{in}$ alone is:

$$f_{L,in} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in,th} \cdot C_{in}}$$

**Step 3: Compute Thevenin resistance for $C_{out}$.**
Looking from the terminals of $C_{out}$, the output resistance of the transistor $r_o$ (or $R_C$ in a simple model) is in series with the load $R_L$:

$$R_{out,th} = R_C + R_L$$

$$f_{L,out} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{out,th} \cdot C_{out}}$$

**Step 4: Compute Thevenin resistance for $C_E$.**
The bypass capacitor $C_E$ sees the emitter resistance $R_E$ in parallel with the small-signal emitter resistance $r_e \approx 1/g_m$ (referred to the emitter). However, when reflected to the base, the effective resistance becomes $(R_E + r_\pi)/\beta$ approximately. The Thevenin resistance is:

$$R_{E,th} = R_E \parallel \left(\frac{R_S' + r_\pi}{\beta + 1}\right)$$

where $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B$.

$$f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{E,th} \cdot C_E}$$

**Step 5: Combine the three contributions.**
Since the three cutoff frequencies are typically close but not equal, the **dominant pole** (largest $f_L$) sets the overall lower cutoff. As a KTU-accepted approximation:

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L,in},\, f_{L,out},\, f_{L,E})$$

A more accurate expression (1 dB rule):

$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L,in}^2 + f_{L,out}^2 + f_{L,E}^2}$$

### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1: Compute the Miller input capacitance.**
Given $C_\mu = 5\,\text{pF}$ and $A_v = -100$:

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 - A_v) = 5\,\text{pF} \times (1 - (-100)) = 5 \times 101 = 505\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 2: Compute total input capacitance.**

$$C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{M,in} = 50 + 505 = 555\,\text{pF}$$

**Step 3: Compute the effective input resistance.**

$$R_{eq,in} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 10\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Computing step-by-step:
$$R_S \parallel R_B = \frac{1 \times 10}{1 + 10} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.909\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_{eq,in} = 0.909\,\text{k}\Omega \parallel 2\,\text{k}\Omega = \frac{0.909 \times 2}{0.909 + 2} = \frac{1.818}{2.909} \approx 0.625\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 4: Compute the upper cutoff frequency.**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq,in} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 625 \times 555 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.469 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{2.179 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 458.9\,\text{kHz}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_H \approx 459\,\text{kHz}$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Miller Effect:**
> **Common Mistake 1**: Students write $C_{M,in} = C_\mu \times \vert A_v\vert$ instead of $C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$. This loses **1 mark** because the "+1" term is critical and frequently tested.
> **Common Mistake 2**: Students forget to add $C_\pi$ to $C_{M,in}$ before computing $f_H$. The total input capacitance is the **sum** of $C_\pi$ and the Miller-multiplied $C_\mu$.
> **Common Mistake 3**: The Thevenin resistance is **the parallel combination** of $R_S$, $R_B$, and $r_\pi$, NOT the series combination. This is a **2-mark deduction** if wrong.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 ŌĆö Alternative Question) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2024, Main Paper Set B]

**(a)** With a neat Bode plot, explain the **three regions of frequency response** of a typical RC-coupled amplifier. Mark the cut-off frequencies, mid-band gain, and the slopes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An amplifier has the following harmonic content in its output for a 1 kHz fundamental input: $V_1 = 10\,\text{V}$, $V_2 = 0.5\,\text{V}$, $V_3 = 0.3\,\text{V}$, $V_4 = 0.1\,\text{V}$. Calculate (i) the second harmonic distortion $D_2$, (ii) the third harmonic distortion $D_3$, (iii) the Total Harmonic Distortion THD in %, and (iv) the second and third harmonic distortion in **dB**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Bode Plot Description (7 marks):**

The frequency response plot has $\vert A_v\vert$ in dB on the y-axis and frequency $f$ on a logarithmic x-axis. Three distinct regions are identified:

1. **Low-Frequency Region ($f < f_L$):** A straight line rising with a slope of **+20 dB/decade**. Below $f_L$, the gain drops as frequency decreases.

2. **Mid-Frequency Region ($f_L \le f \le f_H$):** A **horizontal line** at the mid-band gain level $20\log_{10}\vert A_{mid}\vert$ in dB.

3. **High-Frequency Region ($f > f_H$):** A straight line falling with a slope of **ŌłÆ20 dB/decade**.

**Key Marking Points on the Plot:**
- $f_L$: Lower cutoff, gain is $A_{mid} - 3\,\text{dB}$.
- $f_H$: Upper cutoff, gain is $A_{mid} - 3\,\text{dB}$.
- BW: $f_H - f_L$, shown on the x-axis.
- $A_{mid}$: Maximum flat gain in the mid-band.
- Phase plot: +45┬░ at $f_L$, 180┬░ in mid-band, ŌłÆ45┬░ at $f_H$ (for CE).

### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**(i) Second Harmonic Distortion $D_2$:**

$$D_2 = \frac{V_2}{V_1} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{10} \times 100\% = 5\%$$

**(ii) Third Harmonic Distortion $D_3$:**

$$D_3 = \frac{V_3}{V_1} \times 100\% = \frac{0.3}{10} \times 100\% = 3\%$$

**(iii) Total Harmonic Distortion THD:**

$$THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{0.5^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.1^2}}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{0.25 + 0.09 + 0.01}}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{\sqrt{0.35}}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{0.5916}{10} \times 100\% \approx 5.92\%$$

**(iv) Harmonic Distortions in dB:**

For $D_2$:

$$D_2(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) = 20\log_{10}(0.05) = 20 \times (-1.301) = -26.02\,\text{dB}$$

For $D_3$:

$$D_3(\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{V_3}{V_1}\right) = 20\log_{10}(0.03) = 20 \times (-1.523) = -30.46\,\text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö THD Calculation:**
> **Common Mistake 1**: Students compute $THD = (V_2 + V_3 + V_4)/V_1$ by simple addition. The correct method is the **root-sum-square** (RSS), since harmonic powers add. This error loses **2 marks**.
> **Common Mistake 2**: Forgetting to express the answer as a **percentage**. THD must be in %, not a decimal ratio. (ŌłÆ1 mark).
> **Common Mistake 3**: Using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$ for voltage ratios. Voltage uses **20 log**, power uses 10 log. (ŌłÆ1 mark).
> **Common Mistake 4**: Not including the fourth harmonic (and beyond) ŌĆö read the question carefully; the cutoff is implicit. In this problem, $V_4 = 0.1$ V was given and must be included.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Frequency Response and Distortion (PEEET417 Module 3)**

### A. Frequency Response Essentials
- Frequency response is the plot of gain $\vert A_v(f)\vert$ (in dB) and phase $\angle A_v(f)$ versus input frequency $f$ (log scale).
- **Three regions**: low-frequency (rising at +20 dB/decade), mid-band (flat), high-frequency (falling at ŌłÆ20 dB/decade).
- **Cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is defined as the ŌłÆ3 dB point where gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of mid-band value.
- **Bandwidth** $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ for most practical amplifiers.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** $GBP = A_{mid} \times BW$ is **constant** for a given amplifier ŌĆö a fundamental trade-off in amplifier design.
- **Rise time** $t_r = 0.35 / BW$ is a direct consequence of the single-pole RC low-pass model.

### B. Coupling & Bypass Capacitors (Low-Frequency Response)
- Lower cutoff is governed by $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_{th} C)$ for each coupling/bypass capacitor.
- **Three main contributors**: input coupling $C_{in}$, output coupling $C_{out}$, emitter bypass $C_E$.
- The **dominant pole** (largest $f_L$) sets the overall $f_L$.
- Increasing capacitor values **lowers** $f_L$ (extends low-frequency response) but at the cost of physical size and cost.

### C. Miller Effect (High-Frequency Response)
- $C_{M,in} = C_\mu(1 + \vert A_v\vert)$ ŌĆö this is the **most-tested formula** in KTU exams on this topic.
- Miller effect **degrades** high-frequency response of CE amplifiers; CB and CC configurations are much better at high frequencies.
- Total input capacitance is $C_\pi + C_{M,in}$.
- Upper cutoff $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{eq,in} C_{in,total})$ where $R_{eq,in} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$.

### D. Distortion Essentials
- **Linear (Frequency) Distortion**: amplitude + phase distortion; no new frequencies.
- **Non-Linear (Amplitude) Distortion**: creates new harmonic frequencies.
- **THD** = RSS of all harmonic amplitudes, normalized to fundamental, expressed as percentage.
- **$D_n$ (in dB)** = $20\log_{10}(V_n/V_1)$ ŌĆö uses 20 log because voltage ratios.
- **Intermodulation Distortion (IMD)** produces sum/difference frequencies when multiple input tones are present.

### E. KTU-Specific Memory Triggers
- A "ŌłÆ3 dB point" is at **$1/\sqrt{2} = 0.707$ of $A_{mid}$**, NOT at $1/2$ or $1/e$. This is a common trap.
- Mid-band gain formula for CE: $A_{mid} = -\beta R'_C / r_\pi$ (negative sign indicates phase inversion).
- Two amplifiers in cascade: combined $f_H = 1/\sqrt{(1/f_{H1}^2 + 1/f_{H2}^2)}$; combined $f_L = \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2}$.
- Always state **units** in numerical answers (Hz, kHz, MHz) ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct for missing units.

### F. Key Constants and Definitions
- $g_m = I_C / V_T$ (transconductance, typically 10ŌĆō50 mS for small-signal BJTs).
- $V_T = 26\,\text{mV}$ at room temperature (300 K).
- $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ (input resistance at the base).
- $r_e = 1/g_m = V_T / I_E$ (intrinsic emitter resistance, typically a few ohms to tens of ohms).
- $-3\,\text{dB} = 20\log_{10}(1/\sqrt{2}) = -3.0103\,\text{dB} \approx -3\,\text{dB}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Topologies and Its Effect on Input and Output Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Feedback Topologies and Its Effect on Input and Output Impedance # SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Feedback in Amplifiers? **Feedback** is the process of coupling a portion of the output signal back to the input of an amplifier. In electronic circuits, this is achieved by means of a **feedback network** (typically a passive resistive/capacitive network) that senses the output and returns a signal to the input. When the returned signal **opposes** the input signal, the feedback is called **Negative Feedback**. Negative feedback is the cornerstone of modern amplifier design because it: - Stabilises the gain against device/parameter variations - Reduces **non-linear distortion** - Increases the **bandwidth** - Modifies (controls) the **input and output impedances** of the amplifier > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Negative Feedback):** > *"Negative feedback is the process of coupling a fraction of the output back to the input such that the fed-back signal is 180┬░ out of phase with the input signal, thereby reducing the effective input signal and producing a net reduction in gain but improving amplifier characteristics."* ## 1.2 The Four Basic Feedback Topologies A feedback topology is identified by **two** properties: 1. **How the output is sampled** ŌĆö either **Voltage** (shunt connection at output) or **Current** (series connection at output). 2. **How the feedback signal is mixed at the input** ŌĆö either **Shunt** (parallel) or **Series** mixing. This gives rise to the **four canonical feedback topologies**, which are central to the KTU ESE Module-3 syllabus: | # | Topology (Name) | Sampling at Output | Mixing at Input | Short Name | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Voltage-Series Feedback** | Voltage | Series | SeriesŌĆōShunt | | 2 | **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** | Voltage | Shunt | ShuntŌĆōShunt | | 3 | **Current-Series Feedback** | Current | Series | SeriesŌĆōSeries | | 4 | **Current-Shunt Feedback** | Current | Shunt | ShuntŌĆōSeries | > [!NOTE] > **Naming convention used by KTU:** The first word denotes the *output sampling* method, and the second word denotes the *input mixing* method. Memorise this ŌĆö it is asked directly in KTU Part-A questions. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Cruise Control of an Amplifier" Imagine driving a car on a highway: - You set a **target speed** (this is your input signal). - The car's actual **speed** is the output. - A **speedomometer** constantly samples the speed and feeds it back to your brain (this is the **feedback network**). - If the car slows down on a hill, you press the accelerator **more**; if it speeds up downhill, you ease off. You are continuously *opposing* the error ŌĆö this is **negative feedback**. In this analogy: - **Voltage sampling** Ōēł reading the actual speed (output voltage). - **Current sampling** Ōēł reading the rate of fuel flow (output current). - **Series mixing** Ōēł the brain **adding** the correction to your command. - **Shunt mixing** Ōēł the cruise-control computer **subtracting** a corrective current from the throttle input. Just as cruise control makes your speed *more stable* and *less sensitive* to disturbances, negative feedback stabilises the amplifier's gain, reduces distortion, and **deliberately reshapes the input and output impedances** to suit the application. ## 1.4 Why Impedance Modification Matters (KTU 2024 Context) - A **voltage amplifier** must have **high input impedance** (so it does not load the source) and **low output impedance** (so it drives the load well). ŌåÆ **Voltage-Series** or **Voltage-Shunt** feedback. - A **current amplifier / transconductance stage** must have **low input impedance** and **high output impedance**. ŌåÆ **Current-Series** or **Current-Shunt** feedback. - A **transimpedance stage** (current-to-voltage converter) must have **low input impedance** and **low output impedance**. ŌåÆ **Voltage-Shunt** feedback. - A **transconductance stage** must have **high input impedance** and **high output impedance**. ŌåÆ **Current-Series** feedback. This impedance-shaping property is precisely **why the four topologies are studied individually** in Module 3 of the KTU 2024 scheme. # SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The General Negative-Feedback Block Diagram Consider the canonical two-port representation: - **Basic amplifier** (A-block) with open-loop gain $A$ ŌĆö it amplifies the difference between the input and the fed-back signal. - **Feedback network** (╬▓-block) with transfer function $\beta$ ŌĆö it samples the output $V_o$ and produces a feedback signal $V_f = \beta V_o$. Mathematically: $$V_o = A \cdot V_{\epsilon}, \qquad V_{\epsilon} = V_i - V_f, \qquad V_f = \beta V_o$$ Combining: $$V_o = A(V_i - \beta V_o) \;\Rightarrow\; V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_i \;\Rightarrow\; A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Term ŌĆö Loop Gain (A╬▓):** The product of the forward gain and the feedback factor. The term $(1 + A\beta)$ is the **amount of feedback** or **desensitivity factor**. It governs *every* beneficial effect of negative feedback. ## 2.2 Effect of Feedback on Input Impedance ŌĆö General Theory The input impedance *with* feedback is related to the open-loop input impedance by: $$R_{if} = R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta) \quad \text{ŌĆö when the feedback signal is in SERIES with the input}$$ $$R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{ŌĆö when the feedback signal is in SHUNT (parallel) with the input}$$ ## 2.3 Effect of Feedback on Output Impedance ŌĆö General Theory Similarly, for the output side: $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} \quad \text{ŌĆö when VOLTAGE is sampled at the output}$$ $$R_{of} = R_o \cdot (1 + A\beta) \quad \text{ŌĆö when CURRENT is sampled at the output}$$ ## 2.4 Combining the Two Rules ŌĆö The Master Formula The input behaviour depends on the **mixing** (input side), and the output behaviour depends on the **sampling** (output side). Hence the complete classification: | Topology | Input Impedance $R_{if}$ | Output Impedance $R_{of}$ | Ideal Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Voltage-Series** | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $\dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | Voltage amplifier | | **Voltage-Shunt** | $\dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | $\dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | Transimpedance (IŌåÆV) amplifier | | **Current-Series** | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ | Transconductance (VŌåÆI) amplifier | | **Current-Shunt** | $\dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ | Current amplifier | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick (the "ŌåæŌåō Rule"):** > - **Series** at a port ŌåÆ Impedance at that port **Ōåæ** (multiplied by $1+A\beta$). > - **Shunt** at a port ŌåÆ Impedance at that port **Ōåō** (divided by $1+A\beta$). > - Apply this rule at the *input* for input impedance, and at the *output* for output impedance. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Audio pre-amplifiers** use **voltage-series** feedback (emitter-follower with collector-to-base feedback resistor) to obtain high $R_{in}$ and low $R_{out}$, ideal for cascading. - **Op-amp inverting configuration** is a **voltage-shunt** topology at the input node ŌĆö the input impedance becomes the external resistor (because $R_{if}$ is reduced to a very small value when $A\beta$ is huge). - **Cascode / long-tailed pair stages** use **current-series** feedback (emitter degeneration resistor) to boost output impedance and stabilise bias. - **Photodiode transimpedance amplifiers (TIA)** use **voltage-shunt** feedback because the photodiode is a current source that demands a near-zero input impedance. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Symbols used in all KTU derivations:** > - $A$ = open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback) > - $\beta$ = feedback fraction (dimensionless for voltage/voltage feedback) > - $A\beta$ = loop gain (a *pure number* for dimensionless feedback) > - $R_i$, $R_o$ = open-loop input and output impedances > - $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$ = closed-loop (with-feedback) input and output impedances > - $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$ = closed-loop gain | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Condition | |---|---|---|---| | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | dimensionless | All four topologies | | Loop gain | $A\beta$ | dimensionless | All four topologies | | Amount of feedback | $1 + A\beta$ | dimensionless | All four topologies | | $R_{if}$ ŌĆö Series mixing | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ | Series input | | $R_{if}$ ŌĆö Shunt mixing | $\dfrac{R_i}{1+A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | Shunt input | | $R_{of}$ ŌĆö Voltage sampling | $\dfrac{R_o}{1+A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | Voltage output | | $R_{of}$ ŌĆö Current sampling | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ | Current output | | Gain stability | $\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta}\,\dfrac{dA}{A}$ | ŌĆö | Sensitivity Ōåō by $(1+A\beta)$ | | Bandwidth product | $(BW)_f = (1+A\beta)(BW)$ | Hz | Gain-BW is conserved | # SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Closed-Loop Gain of a Feedback Amplifier This derivation is the **foundation** for every impedance derivation that follows. KTU frequently asks students to "derive the expression for closed-loop gain". **Starting point** (Basic amplifier with negative feedback at its input): $$V_{\epsilon} = V_i - V_f \qquad \text{(error voltage at the input of the A-block)}$$ **Output of basic amplifier:** $$V_o = A \cdot V_{\epsilon}$$ **Feedback signal (sampled from output):** $$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$ **Substitute $V_f$ into the error equation:** $$V_{\epsilon} = V_i - \beta V_o$$ **Substitute into the output equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_o &= A \cdot (V_i - \beta V_o) \\ V_o &= A V_i - A \beta V_o \\ V_o + A \beta V_o &= A V_i \\ V_o (1 + A \beta) &= A V_i \\ \therefore \quad A_f &= \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{A}{1 + A \beta} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **For $A\beta \gg 1$** (the usual practical case), $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ ŌĆö the gain becomes dependent *only* on the passive feedback network, not on the active device. This is the *principle of gain stabilisation*. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Effect of Series-Mixing on Input Impedance Consider the input port of the basic amplifier with an open-loop input impedance $R_i$. With **series** negative feedback, the feedback voltage $V_f$ appears in *series* with the source voltage $V_i$ ŌĆö the same current $I_i$ flows through both. Applying KVL at the input loop: $$ \begin{aligned} V_i &= V_{\epsilon} + V_f \\ \text{But } V_{\epsilon} &= I_i R_i \quad \text{and} \quad V_f = \beta V_o = \beta A V_{\epsilon} \\ \Rightarrow V_i &= V_{\epsilon} + \beta A V_{\epsilon} = V_{\epsilon} (1 + A\beta) \\ \Rightarrow V_i &= I_i R_i (1 + A\beta) \\ \therefore \quad R_{if} &= \frac{V_i}{I_i} = R_i (1 + A\beta) \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Series mixing **increases** the input impedance by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Effect of Shunt-Mixing on Input Impedance With **shunt** (parallel) feedback, the feedback signal is a current $I_f$ that is *subtracted* from the source current $I_i$ at the input node. The voltage across the input port is $V_i$ for both the source and the basic amplifier. By KCL at the input node: $$ \begin{aligned} I_i &= I_{\epsilon} + I_f \\ \text{But } I_{\epsilon} &= \frac{V_i}{R_i} \quad \text{and} \quad I_f = \beta V_o = \beta A V_i \\ \Rightarrow I_i &= \frac{V_i}{R_i} + \beta A V_i = V_i \!\left( \frac{1}{R_i} + \beta A \right) \\ \Rightarrow I_i &= V_i \!\left( \frac{1 + A\beta R_i}{R_i} \right) \\ \therefore \quad R_{if} &= \frac{V_i}{I_i} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Shunt mixing **decreases** the input impedance by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Effect of Voltage Sampling on Output Impedance To find $R_{of}$, we set the independent input source to zero ($V_i = 0$, hence $V_{\epsilon} = -V_f$) and apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output, then compute the resulting test current $I_t$. For **voltage sampling**, the feedback network is connected in **parallel (shunt)** with the output ŌĆö the output voltage appears directly across the feedback network. Hence the feedback signal $V_f$ depends on $V_o$ only, and the output equation of the basic amplifier (with $R_o$ in series with its internal source $A V_{\epsilon}$) is: $$ V_o = A V_{\epsilon} - I_o R_o $$ With input shorted, $V_{\epsilon} = -\beta V_o$. Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} V_o &= A(-\beta V_o) - I_o R_o \\ V_o (1 + A\beta) &= - I_o R_o \\ \Rightarrow V_o &= -\frac{I_o R_o}{1 + A\beta} \\ \therefore \quad R_{of} &= \left| \frac{V_o}{I_o} \right| = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Voltage sampling **decreases** the output impedance by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 ŌĆö Effect of Current Sampling on Output Impedance For **current sampling**, the feedback network is in **series** with the load ŌĆö it senses the load current $I_o$. The output node equation becomes: $$ V_o = A V_{\epsilon} - I_o (R_o + Z_f) $$ A subtle but important KTU point: when we apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output (with input shorted), the current through the feedback branch is also $I_o$ (since they are in series), so $V_f = \beta I_o$, giving $V_{\epsilon} = -\beta I_o$. Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} V_t &= A(-\beta I_o) - I_o R_o \\ V_t &= -I_o (A\beta + R_o) \\ \therefore \quad R_{of} &= \left| \frac{V_t}{I_o} \right| = R_o + A\beta \;\approx\; R_o (1 + A\beta) \quad \text{for } A\beta \gg R_o \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Current sampling **increases** the output impedance by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. --- ## 3.6 Consolidated Analytical Matrix | Topology | Sampling (Output) | Mixing (Input) | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ | $A_f$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **VoltageŌĆōSeries** | Voltage (Ōłź) | Series | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | | **VoltageŌĆōShunt** | Voltage (Ōłź) | Shunt | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o/(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | | **CurrentŌĆōSeries** | Current (series) | Series | $R_i(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | | **CurrentŌĆōShunt** | Current (series) | Shunt | $R_i/(1+A\beta)$ | $R_o(1+A\beta)$ | $A/(1+A\beta)$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for KTU 2024 ESE:** A common 7-mark sub-question asks to *"tabulate the input and output impedances of all four feedback topologies"*. The table above is the model answer ŌĆö reproduce it verbatim in the answer book. ## 3.7 Numerical Demonstration (KTU-solved style problem) > **Worked Example:** A voltage-series feedback amplifier has an open-loop gain $A = 2000$, feedback factor $\beta = 0.05$, open-loop input impedance $R_i = 2 \,\text{k}\Omega$ and open-loop output impedance $R_o = 75 \,\Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop gain, input impedance and output impedance. **Solution:** **Step 1 ŌĆö Loop gain and amount of feedback:** $$A\beta = 2000 \times 0.05 = 100, \qquad 1 + A\beta = 101$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Closed-loop gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{2000}{101} \approx 19.80$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Closed-loop input impedance (series mixing ŌåÆ Ōåæ):** $$R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta) = 2\,\text{k}\Omega \times 101 = 202\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Closed-loop output impedance (voltage sampling ŌåÆ Ōåō):** $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{75}{101} \approx 0.743\,\Omega$$ **Engineering interpretation:** The amplifier's input impedance jumped from 2 k╬® to 202 k╬® (almost ideal open-circuit), and the output impedance fell from 75 ╬® to less than 1 ╬® (almost ideal voltage source). The price paid is a 100-fold reduction in gain (from 2000 to ~20). This is the classic **gainŌĆōimpedance trade-off** in negative feedback design. # SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Block Diagram ŌĆö Negative Feedback Amplifier (Two-Port) This single block diagram is the foundation for *all four* topologies. The placement of the **mixing junction** (series or shunt) on the input side and the **sampling probe** (voltage or current) on the output side determines the topology. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[("V_i - Source
Input Signal")] -->|"I_i / V_i"| MIX(("╬Ż
Mixing Junction")) MIX -->|"V_epsilon Error Signal"| AMP[/"A - Basic Amplifier
Open-Loop Gain"/] AMP -->|"V_o / I_o"| OUT([Output Port
V_o across R_L]) OUT -.->|"Sampled Quantity"| FB[/"beta - Feedback Network"/] FB -.->|"V_f or I_f
Feedback Signal"| MIX classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef mix fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class A1 src class AMP amp class FB fb class OUT out class MIX mix ``` > [!NOTE] > The **solid arrows** show the forward signal path; the **dotted arrows** show the feedback path. The mixing junction ╬Ż performs either a *series subtraction* (KVL) or a *shunt subtraction* (KCL) depending on topology. ## 4.2 Topology 1 ŌĆö Voltage-Series Feedback (SeriesŌĆōShunt) **Mixing:** Series (KVL at input ŌĆö feedback voltage opposes source voltage) **Sampling:** Voltage (feedback network in parallel with output) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["V_s (Source)"] -->|"I_i"| RN1(["R_s Source Resistance"]) RN1 --> NODE1(("Input Node
V_i")) NODE1 -->|"Series Loop"| SIG["V_epsilon = V_i - V_f"] SIG --> AMP["A - Amplifier Block
Open-Loop Gain A"] AMP -->|"V_o"| NODEOUT(("Output Node")) NODEOUT --> RO(["R_o - Output Resistance"]) NODEOUT -->|"V_o across R_L"| RL(["R_L - Load"]) FBNODE[/"Beta - Voltage Sampling Network
Connected in Parallel"/] -->|"V_f = Beta V_o"| NODE1 NODEOUT -.->|"Senses V_o"| FBNODE classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef lbl fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000 class AMP amp class FBNODE fb class SRC src class RN1,RO,RL,NODE1,NODEOUT,SIG lbl ``` **Effect:** - $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **High input impedance** (good for voltage sensing) - $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **Low output impedance** (good for voltage delivery) - Ideal as a **voltage amplifier**. ## 4.3 Topology 2 ŌĆö Voltage-Shunt Feedback (ShuntŌĆōShunt) **Mixing:** Shunt (KCL at input ŌĆö feedback current opposes source current) **Sampling:** Voltage (feedback network in parallel with output) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["I_s (Current Source)"] -->|"I_i"| NODEIN(("Input Node
V_i")) NODEIN -->|"V_i across R_i"| AMPIN["A_i Input Stage
R_i parallel path"] AMPIN --> AMP["A - Amplifier Block
Transresistance Gain"] AMP -->|"V_o"| NODEOUT(("Output Node")) NODEOUT --> RO(["R_o"]) NODEOUT --> RL(["R_L"]) FBNODE[/"Beta_y Voltage-to-Current Feedback
Shunt-Shunt Network"/] -->|"I_f = Beta_y V_o"| NODEIN NODEOUT -.->|"Samples V_o"| FBNODE classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 class AMP amp class FBNODE fb class SRC src ``` **Effect:** - $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **Low input impedance** (sinks the source current) - $R_{of} = R_o/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **Low output impedance** (voltage source behaviour) - Ideal as a **transimpedance amplifier** (current-to-voltage converter). ## 4.4 Topology 3 ŌĆö Current-Series Feedback (SeriesŌĆōSeries) **Mixing:** Series (KVL at input) **Sampling:** Current (feedback network in series with load ŌĆö senses load current) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["V_s (Source)"] --> RS(["R_s"]) RS --> NODE1(("Input Node")) NODE1 --> SIG["V_epsilon = V_i - V_f"] SIG --> AMP["A - Amplifier Block
Transconductance Gain"] AMP --> NODEOUT(("Output Node")) NODEOUT --> RO(["R_o in series"]) RO --> LOOPNODE(("Series Sampling Point")) LOOPNODE --> RL(["R_L - Load"]) LOOPNODE -->|"I_o flows through"| FBNODE[/"Beta_z Current Sampling Network
Connected in Series with R_L"/] FBNODE -->|"V_f = Beta_z I_o"| NODE1 classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 class AMP amp class FBNODE fb class SRC src ``` **Effect:** - $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **High input impedance** - $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **High output impedance** (good for current delivery) - Ideal as a **transconductance amplifier** (voltage-to-current converter). ## 4.5 Topology 4 ŌĆö Current-Shunt Feedback (ShuntŌĆōSeries) **Mixing:** Shunt (KCL at input) **Sampling:** Current (feedback network in series with load) ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["I_s (Current Source)"] --> NODEIN(("Input Node")) NODEIN --> AMPIN["R_i Input Path"] AMPIN --> AMP["A - Amplifier Block
Current Gain"] AMP --> NODEOUT(("Output Node")) NODEOUT --> RO(["R_o series"]) RO --> LOOPNODE(("Sampling Point")) LOOPNODE --> RL(["R_L"]) LOOPNODE --> FBNODE[/"Beta_i Current Sampling Network"/] FBNODE -->|"I_f = Beta_i I_o"| NODEIN classDef amp fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef fb fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000 classDef src fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 class AMP amp class FBNODE fb class SRC src ``` **Effect:** - $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **Low input impedance** - $R_{of} = R_o(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **High output impedance** (ideal current source) - Ideal as a **current amplifier**. ## 4.6 Decision Flow ŌĆö "Which Topology Do I Need?" ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["Start: Choose Feedback Topology"]) --> Q1{"What is the
source type?"} Q1 -->|"Voltage source
high Z"| Q2{"What is the
load type?"} Q1 -->|"Current source
low Z"| Q3{"What is the
load type?"} Q2 -->|"High Z load
voltage out"| VS["Voltage-Series
R_if up, R_of down"] Q2 -->|"Low Z load
voltage out"| CS["Current-Series
R_if up, R_of up"] Q3 -->|"High Z load
current out"| VSH["Voltage-Shunt
R_if down, R_of down"] Q3 -->|"Low Z load
current out"| CSH["Current-Shunt
R_if down, R_of up"] classDef decision fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef result fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000 class Q1,Q2,Q3 decision class VS,CS,VSH,CSH result ``` # SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024] **List the four basic feedback topologies used in amplifier design and state how the input impedance is modified in each case.** **Model Answer:** The four basic feedback topologies are: 1. **Voltage-Series Feedback** 2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** 3. **Current-Series Feedback** 4. **Current-Shunt Feedback** The input impedance is modified as follows: | Topology | Input Impedance | |---|---| | Voltage-Series | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **increases** | | Voltage-Shunt | $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **decreases** | | Current-Series | $R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **increases** | | Current-Shunt | $R_{if} = R_i/(1+A\beta)$ ŌåÆ **decreases** | The deciding factor is the **mixing at the input** ŌĆö series mixing increases $R_{if}$, shunt mixing decreases $R_{if}$. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023] **Why is negative feedback used in amplifiers? Mention any three benefits.** **Model Answer:** Negative feedback is used in amplifiers to obtain **predictable, stable, and linear** performance despite variations in device parameters, temperature, and supply voltage. Three important benefits are: 1. **Gain Stabilisation:** The closed-loop gain $A_f = A/(1+A\beta) \approx 1/\beta$ depends almost entirely on the *passive* feedback network, not on the active device. Sensitivity is reduced by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. 2. **Reduction of Non-Linear Distortion:** Any harmonic/intermodulation distortion generated in the basic amplifier is reduced by the same factor $(1+A\beta)$. 3. **Impedance Shaping:** The input and output impedances can be **deliberately increased or decreased** by choosing the appropriate topology (series/shunt mixing and voltage/current sampling), making the amplifier compatible with a wide range of source and load impedances. *(Other acceptable benefits: increase in bandwidth, extension of dynamic range.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative-feedback amplifier in terms of the open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the **Voltage-Series** and **Current-Shunt** feedback topologies. Compare their effects on input and output impedances. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **Given:** Basic amplifier of open-loop gain $A$ with feedback network of transfer ratio $\beta$ connected in a negative-feedback configuration. **Step 1:** Define the error signal at the input of the A-block: $$V_{\epsilon} = V_i - V_f$$ **Step 2:** Output of the basic amplifier: $$V_o = A V_{\epsilon}$$ **Step 3:** Feedback signal sampled from output: $$V_f = \beta V_o$$ **Step 4:** Substitute (3) into (1): $$V_{\epsilon} = V_i - \beta V_o$$ **Step 5:** Substitute into (2): $$ \begin{aligned} V_o &= A(V_i - \beta V_o) \\ V_o + A\beta V_o &= A V_i \\ V_o(1 + A\beta) &= A V_i \\ \boxed{A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 (Engineering Insight):** When $A\beta \gg 1$, $A_f \approx 1/\beta$. The closed-loop gain is set by the *passive* network alone ŌĆö a remarkable property exploited in op-amp design. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **Voltage-Series Feedback:** - The output is sampled in **shunt** (voltage across the output port). - The feedback signal is mixed in **series** with the source. - Used as a **voltage amplifier**. Effect on impedances: $$ R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta), \qquad R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} $$ ŌåÆ Input impedance **increases** (ideally infinite), output impedance **decreases** (ideally zero). **Current-Shunt Feedback:** - The output is sampled in **series** (current through the load). - The feedback signal is mixed in **shunt** at the input. - Used as a **current amplifier**. Effect on impedances: $$ R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}, \qquad R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta) $$ ŌåÆ Input impedance **decreases**, output impedance **increases** (ideal current-source behaviour). **Comparison Table:** | Property | Voltage-Series | Current-Shunt | |---|---|---| | Output sampling | Voltage | Current | | Input mixing | Series | Shunt | | $R_{if}$ | High $(R_i(1+A\beta))$ | Low $\left(\frac{R_i}{1+A\beta}\right)$ | | $R_{of}$ | Low $\left(\frac{R_o}{1+A\beta}\right)$ | High $(R_o(1+A\beta))$ | | Application | Voltage amplifier | Current amplifier | --- ### **Question B** [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2023] **(a)** With block diagrams, explain **Voltage-Shunt** and **Current-Series** feedback topologies. State the expressions for input and output impedances in each case. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** A voltage-series feedback amplifier has $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.04$, $R_i = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_o = 50\,\Omega$. Calculate (i) the amount of feedback $(1+A\beta)$, (ii) the closed-loop gain $A_f$, (iii) the input impedance with feedback $R_{if}$, and (iv) the output impedance with feedback $R_{of}$. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **Voltage-Shunt Feedback (ShuntŌĆōShunt):** - Output is sampled in **shunt** (voltage). - Input is mixed in **shunt** (feedback current opposes source current). - Used as a **transimpedance amplifier** (current-to-voltage). $$ R_{if} = \frac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}, \qquad R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta} $$ Both impedances **decrease** by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. This makes the stage behave like a near-ideal current-to-voltage converter ŌĆö exactly what is needed for photodiode amplifiers and current-output sensors. **Current-Series Feedback (SeriesŌĆōSeries):** - Output is sampled in **series** (current). - Input is mixed in **series** (feedback voltage opposes source voltage). - Used as a **transconductance amplifier** (voltage-to-current). $$ R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta), \qquad R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta) $$ Both impedances **increase** by the factor $(1+A\beta)$. This makes the stage behave like a near-ideal voltage-controlled current source. #### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **Given:** $A = 1000$, $\beta = 0.04$, $R_i = 1\,\text{k}\Omega = 1000\,\Omega$, $R_o = 50\,\Omega$. **(i) Amount of feedback:** $$A\beta = 1000 \times 0.04 = 40 \;\Rightarrow\; 1 + A\beta = 41$$ **(ii) Closed-loop gain:** $$A_f = \frac{A}{1+A\beta} = \frac{1000}{41} \approx 24.39$$ **(iii) Closed-loop input impedance (series mixing ŌåÆ Ōåæ):** $$R_{if} = R_i(1+A\beta) = 1000 \times 41 = 41\,000\,\Omega = 41\,\text{k}\Omega$$ **(iv) Closed-loop output impedance (voltage sampling ŌåÆ Ōåō):** $$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1+A\beta} = \frac{50}{41} \approx 1.22\,\Omega$$ **Interpretation:** The amplifier now presents a $41\,\text{k}\Omega$ input (almost an open circuit) and a $1.22\,\Omega$ output (almost a short circuit) ŌĆö i.e. an *ideal* voltage amplifier. The price is a 41-fold reduction in gain ($1000 \rightarrow 24.39$). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing the two words in the topology name.** "Voltage-Series" means *voltage* is sampled (output) and *series* is mixed (input) ŌĆö *not* the other way around. Many students swap these and lose 2ŌĆō3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to apply $(1+A\beta)$ at BOTH the input and output sides.** A common error is to compute $R_{if}$ correctly and then write $R_{of} = R_o$ unchanged. Each side must be modified. > 3. **Mixing up series and shunt rules for impedance.** Remember: **Series ŌćÆ Ōåæ**, **Shunt ŌćÆ Ōåō**. If you are unsure, derive the formula on the spot using KVL/KCL. > 4. **Skipping the "amount of feedback" computation.** KTU almost always expects $(1+A\beta)$ to be calculated and *named* explicitly as the first step. > 5. **Unit errors.** $R_i$ in $\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_o$ in $\Omega$ ŌĆö convert to a common unit before multiplication/division. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):** - Ō£ö **Closed-loop gain:** $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$. The product $A\beta$ is the **loop gain**; $(1+A\beta)$ is the **amount of feedback**. - Ō£ö **Master Rule ŌĆö "Series Ōåæ, Shunt Ōåō":** - Series mixing at a port ŌćÆ Impedance at that port **multiplied** by $(1+A\beta)$. - Shunt mixing at a port ŌćÆ Impedance at that port **divided** by $(1+A\beta)$. - The *sampling* method (voltage/current) at the output behaves the same way for the *output* impedance. - Ō£ö **The Four Topologies & their Impedance Signatures:** | Topology | $R_{if}$ | $R_{of}$ | Function | |---|---|---|---| | Voltage-Series | Ōåæ High | Ōåō Low | Voltage amplifier | | Voltage-Shunt | Ōåō Low | Ōåō Low | Transimpedance amplifier | | Current-Series | Ōåæ High | Ōåæ High | Transconductance amplifier | | Current-Shunt | Ōåō Low | Ōåæ High | Current amplifier | - Ō£ö **Trade-off rule:** Negative feedback reduces gain by $(1+A\beta)$ but improves *every* other parameter (impedance, linearity, bandwidth) by the same factor ŌĆö *GainŌĆōBandwidth Product is conserved*. - Ō£ö **Naming Convention:** First word = output sampling, second word = input mixing. Memorise with the cruise-control analogy in Section 1. - Ō£ö **Derivation sequence to revise:** 1. Closed-loop gain (KVL at mixing junction). 2. $R_{if}$ for series mixing (KVL). 3. $R_{if}$ for shunt mixing (KCL). 4. $R_{of}$ for voltage sampling (test-voltage method). 5. $R_{of}$ for current sampling (test-voltage method with series $Z_f$). - Ō£ö **Two numerical quantities to always compute first in problems:** $A\beta$ and $(1+A\beta)$. - Ō£ö **KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Concepts (high-weightage):** - Identification of topology from a given transistor circuit. - Tabulation of all four topologies with impedance formulae. - Numerical computation of $A_f$, $R_{if}$, $R_{of}$ from given $A$, $\beta$, $R_i$, $R_o$. - Reasoning-type questions: "Why does series mixing increase input impedance?" - Choice of topology for a given application (e.g., "Which topology is suitable for a transimpedance stage?").

topics.title: Feedback Amplifier Circuits Using BJT in Each Feedback Topologies (Analysis of Only Voltage Series Feedback Circuit is Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifier Circuits Using BJT in Each Feedback Topologies (Analysis of Only Voltage Series Feedback Circuit is Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Feedback in an Amplifier?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Feedback* is the process of coupling a portion of the output signal back to the input terminal of an amplifier. When this returned signal **opposes** the original input signal, the system is called **Negative Feedback Amplifier** ŌĆö and it is the single most important technique used to refine amplifier performance.

Mathematically, the closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier is:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- $A$ = Open-loop gain of the basic amplifier
- $\beta$ = Reverse transmission factor (gain) of the feedback network
- $A\beta$ = **Loop gain** (the most critical quantity in feedback design)
- $(1 + A\beta)$ = **Desensitivity factor** or **Return Difference**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For negative feedback, $|1 + A\beta| > 1$, which means the closed-loop gain $|A_f| < |A|$. We **sacrifice gain** to obtain the four golden benefits listed in ┬¦2.5.

---

## 1.2 The Four Feedback Topologies ŌĆö Syllabus Snapshot

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups the four possible feedback combinations based on **two independent classifications**:

| Sampling (Output Port) | Mixing (Input Port) | Topology Name |
|------------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **Voltage** sampling   | **Series** mixing   | Voltage-Series (V-S) |
| **Voltage** sampling   | **Shunt** mixing    | Voltage-Shunt (V-P) |
| **Current** sampling   | **Series** mixing   | Current-Series (C-S) |
| **Current** sampling   | **Shunt** mixing    | Current-Shunt (C-P) |

> *Note:* *V-P and C-P stand for "Voltage-Parallel" and "Current-Parallel" respectively ŌĆö these are alternative names used in KTU reference texts.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Cruise Control of Your Car ­¤ÜŚ

Imagine driving a car at exactly **60 km/h**:

- **Your eyes** measure the *output* (the current speed) ŌåÆ this is **sampling**.
- You compare this with **60 km/h on the dashboard** (the desired *input*).
- If speed is too high, you press the brake *less*; if too low, you press the accelerator *more* ŌĆö you *subtract* the error from the throttle input ŌåÆ this is **series mixing** (your body's force adds/subtracts from the pedal position).

The exact same principle operates inside a **Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier**:

- The amplifier is the **car's engine** (produces raw output).
- A **resistive voltage divider** is your **speedometer** (samples the output voltage).
- The error voltage $V_{in} - V_f$ is the **gap between 60 km/h and the actual speed**.
- The factor $\beta$ is the **calibration constant** of your speedometer.
- The factor $(1 + A\beta)$ is your **driver's alertness** ŌĆö a higher alertness means the speed is *desensitized* to road disturbances (hills, wind).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner loves testing whether you understand the **mixing** vs. **sampling** distinction. Series mixing means voltages add/subtract at the input node; shunt mixing means currents add/subtract.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

- Thermal voltage: $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV at 300 K}}$
- Boltzmann constant: $\mathbf{k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$
- Charge of an electron: $\mathbf{q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$
- Standard h-parameter ranges for a small-signal CE BJT at $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$:
  - $h_{ie} = 1 \text{ to } 5 \text{ k}\Omega$
  - $h_{fe} = 100 \text{ to } 500$
  - $h_{re} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$
  - $h_{oe} = 20 \text{ }\mu\text{S}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Block Diagram of a Feedback Amplifier

Every negative feedback amplifier ŌĆö regardless of topology ŌĆö can be represented by **two distinct two-port networks** placed inside a closed loop:

```
            Basic Amplifier (A)              Feedback Network (╬▓)
   Vs ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║[  A  ]ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ Vo
        Ōöé           Ōöé
        Ōöé           ŌööŌöĆ[ ╬▓ ]ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ŌöĆŌöĆŌö¼ŌöĆŌöĆŌ¢║ŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ (return signal)
        Ōöé                       Ōöé
        ŌööŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆ  ╬Ż  ŌŚäŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöĆŌöś
            (mixing node)
```

The signal $V_f = \beta V_o$ is generated by the feedback network and subtracted from $V_s$ at the summing junction $\Sigma$. The net signal that drives the basic amplifier is the **error signal**:

$$V_{\varepsilon} = V_s - V_f = V_s - \beta V_o$$

and the closed-loop output is:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_{\varepsilon} = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$$

Solving for the gain:

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

---

## 2.2 Sampling at the Output: How Do We Tell Voltage vs. Current Sampling?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Voltage Sampling:** The feedback network is connected in **parallel with the output** load $R_L$. It senses the *output voltage* $V_o$. The output of the feedback network becomes **zero** when $R_L = 0$ (shorted load).
> - **Current Sampling:** The feedback network is connected in **series with the output** load $R_L$. It senses the *output current* $I_L$. The output of the feedback network becomes **zero** when $R_L = \infty$ (open load).

---

## 2.3 Mixing at the Input: Series vs. Shunt

- **Series Mixing (Loop Input):** The signal source $V_s$ and the feedback signal $V_f$ appear in **series** in the input loop. The input is a **voltage** quantity (KVL applies).
- **Shunt Mixing (Node Input):** The signal source $I_s$ and the feedback signal $I_f$ appear in **parallel** at the input node. The input is a **current** quantity (KCL applies).

---

## 2.4 Davidson's Chart ŌĆö Matching BJT Stages to Topologies

This is the **single most important table** the KTU board expects you to reproduce:

| Topology | Sampling at Output | Mixing at Input | Required BJT Stage |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| **V-S** | Voltage (parallel) | Series (loop)   | **CE** or **CC** |
| **V-P (Shunt)** | Voltage (parallel) | Shunt (node) | **CE** |
| **C-S** | Current (series) | Series (loop) | **CE** (with $R_E$ unbypassed) |
| **C-P (Shunt)** | Current (series) | Shunt (node) | **CC (Emitter Follower)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Matching Rule (Davidson's):** A *series* connection at one port of the feedback network must be matched with a *series* connection at the corresponding port of the basic amplifier. A *shunt* connection must be matched with a *shunt* connection. This guarantees that the basic amplifier and feedback network are unilateral and non-loading.

---

## 2.5 The Four Golden Benefits of Negative Feedback

1. **Gain Desensitization:** $dA_f / A_f = \dfrac{1}{1+A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$ ŌĆö gain becomes nearly independent of device parameter variations.
2. **Input/Output Impedance Modification** (see table below).
3. **Bandwidth Extension:** $(BW)_f = (1 + A\beta) \cdot (BW)$
4. **Reduction of Non-linear Distortion:** $D_f = D / (1 + A\beta)$

---

## 2.6 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table ŌĆö every KTU exam paper from 2020ŌĆō2024 has at least one direct question on these formulas.**
