The Colpitts oscillator works exactly like this ŌĆö the *ratio* of $C_1$ to $C_2$ controls the feedback fraction, and their series combination with $L$ sets the natural swinging (oscillation) frequency.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Quality factor (Q) of tank:** typically **50 to 200** for practical LC oscillators
- **Frequency of operation:** **Audio to RF range (1 kHz to several hundred MHz)**
- **Feedback factor:** $\beta = C_1 / C_2$ (this is the inverse of the Hartley's $L_2/L_1$)

---

## 1.2 Crystal Oscillator

### Formal Definition
A **Crystal Oscillator** is a highly stable sinusoidal oscillator in which the frequency-selecting network is a **piezoelectric quartz crystal** that operates at its **mechanical resonance frequency**. The crystal exhibits the **piezoelectric effect** ŌĆö it produces a voltage when mechanically stressed, and conversely vibrates mechanically when a voltage is applied. This electromechanical coupling is modeled as a very high-Q RLC resonant circuit, which is orders of magnitude more stable than discrete LC tanks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The quartz crystal has two resonant frequencies ŌĆö the **series resonance $f_s$** (where crystal impedance is minimum/zero) and the **parallel resonance $f_p$** (where crystal impedance is maximum/infinite). Most crystal oscillators operate *between* $f_s$ and $f_p$ in the **inductive region** of the crystal, where it behaves like a high-Q inductor.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Tuning Fork on Steroids"
Picture an extremely precise **tuning fork** that always vibrates at exactly the same pitch no matter what. If you strike it harder, it doesn't change pitch ŌĆö it just rings louder for longer. A quartz crystal is exactly this: a tiny piece of sliced quartz, cut at a precise angle, that vibrates at one rock-solid frequency determined by its physical dimensions. Electronics use it like a referee in a noisy stadium ŌĆö every time the oscillator's frequency tries to drift, the crystal yanks it back to its natural vibration frequency. The reason quartz is used (rather than steel or aluminum) is that quartz maintains its mechanical properties over temperature changes far better than any metal.

### Standard Metrics
- **Quality factor (Q) of crystal:** typically **10,000 to 1,000,000** (extraordinarily high!)
- **Frequency stability:** **┬▒0.001% to ┬▒0.0001%** (orders better than LC oscillators)
- **Operating frequencies:** typically **1 kHz to 300 MHz** (fundamental mode); up to **GHz** for overtone modes

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Oscillators?

| Feature | Colpitts | Crystal |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency stability | Moderate (LC dependent) | Extremely high |
| Frequency range | Wide (audio to RF) | Narrow (fixed by crystal cut) |
| Tunability | Easily tunable (vary L or C) | Not easily tunable |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (crystal required) |
| Typical use | RF signal generators, local oscillators | Clocks, microcontrollers, watches, RF references |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Colpitts Oscillator ŌĆö Detailed Working Principle

### 2.1.1 Circuit Description
The Colpitts oscillator consists of:
- **An amplifying active device** (BJT in CE configuration or FET)
- **An LC tank circuit** where the inductor $L$ is in parallel with the **series combination of two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$**
- **A capacitive voltage divider** formed by $C_1$ and $C_2$ that taps the tank voltage to feed back to the input
- **A biasing network** to set the Q-point
- **An output coupling** to load

The capacitor $C_1$ is connected between the output (collector/drain) and the tank common node, while $C_2$ is connected between the tank common node and ground (emitter/source). The feedback is taken from the junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (must satisfy both):**
> 1. The loop gain magnitude must be **unity**: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$
> 2. The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero (or an integer multiple of $2\pi$)**: $\angle A \beta = 0, 2\pi, 4\pi, \ldots$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initial Energy Injection**
When the DC supply is switched ON, random noise/transients in the circuit contain a wide spectrum of frequencies. This acts as the **initial excitation energy** to kick-start the oscillations.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tank Circuit Frequency Selection**
The LC tank circuit acts as a **band-pass filter**. The frequency component that matches the **natural resonant frequency** of the tank is reinforced the most, while all other frequencies are heavily attenuated.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Amplification by Active Device**
The active device (BJT/FET) provides the **amplification** ($A > 1$ initially) to compensate for the losses in the tank. It also introduces a **180┬░ phase inversion** between input and output.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Feedback via Capacitive Divider**
The capacitive divider $C_1$ŌĆō$C_2$ samples a portion of the output voltage and feeds it back to the input. The capacitive divider introduces an **additional 180┬░ phase shift** (since the feedback point is at the *opposite* end of the tank from the output). This gives a total loop phase shift of **$180┬░ + 180┬░ = 360┬░ = 0┬░$**, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Steady-State Oscillation**
As the oscillation grows, the gain of the active device (due to non-linearity or AGC) **automatically reduces** until $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, and the oscillator reaches a stable amplitude.

### 2.1.3 Why Use a Capacitive Divider Instead of a Tapped Inductor?
- **Cheaper and more compact** than tapped inductors
- **Better frequency stability** with temperature (capacitors are more stable than inductors)
- **Easier to tune** by trimming capacitors
- **Eliminates magnetic coupling losses** associated with inductors

---

## 2.2 Crystal Oscillator ŌĆö Detailed Working Principle

### 2.2.1 The Piezoelectric Effect
The heart of the crystal oscillator is a **thin slice of quartz** with two metallic electrodes deposited on opposite faces. The quartz is **cut at a specific angle** (AT-cut, BT-cut, etc.) to give the desired frequency-temperature characteristics.

When an alternating voltage is applied across the electrodes, the crystal undergoes **mechanical vibration** at the same frequency (this is the **inverse piezoelectric effect**). Conversely, mechanical vibration generates a voltage across the electrodes (**direct piezoelectric effect**).

### 2.2.2 Equivalent Circuit of a Quartz Crystal
The crystal is modeled by the following equivalent circuit (valid near resonance):
- **Motional arm (series RLC):** $R_s$ (mechanical friction), $L$ (mass/inertia), $C$ (compliance/elasticity)
- **Holder capacitance $C_p$:** static capacitance between the electrodes and lead connections (in parallel with the motional arm)

The series RLC represents the **mechanical resonance**, while $C_p$ represents the **static electrical capacitance** of the crystal package.

### 2.2.3 Two Resonant Frequencies ŌĆö The Key Concept

**Step 1 ŌĆö Series Resonance ($f_s$)**
At series resonance, the motional reactances of $L$ and $C$ cancel each other ($X_L = X_C$). The motional arm becomes purely resistive ($R_s$), which is very small. The total crystal impedance is **minimum (Ōēł $R_s$)**, so the crystal behaves like a **short circuit**.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Parallel Resonance ($f_p$)**
Above $f_s$, the motional arm becomes inductive. At a particular frequency, this inductive reactance resonates with the holder capacitance $C_p$ (parallel resonance). The total crystal impedance is **maximum (very large)**, so the crystal behaves like an **open circuit**.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Operating Region**
For oscillation, the crystal is operated in the **narrow region between $f_s$ and $f_p$** where it exhibits an **inductive reactance**. This inductive behavior is used in place of the inductor in a modified Colpitts or Pierce oscillator circuit.

### 2.2.4 Why Crystals Have Such High Q
- Quartz has **extremely low internal friction** (mechanical losses)
- The crystal vibrates in a **vacuum-sealed metal can** to eliminate air damping
- The ratio of stored energy to energy lost per cycle is enormous
- This translates to **extremely sharp resonance** and **frequency stability**

### 2.2.5 Real-World Utility
- **Digital clocks and watches:** 32.768 kHz crystal (chosen because $2^{15} = 32768$ for 1-second time base)
- **Microcontroller clock sources:** typically 4 MHz, 8 MHz, 16 MHz
- **RF synthesizers and transmitters:** reference frequency for PLLs
- **Computers and communication systems:** USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth all rely on crystal references
- **GPS systems:** atomic-clock-stabilized crystal references

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Colpitts Oscillator | Crystal Oscillator | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frequency of oscillation** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ (series) | Hz |
| 2 | **Equivalent capacitance** | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | ŌĆö | Farads |
| 3 | **Parallel resonance** | ŌĆö | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C \cdot \dfrac{C_p}{C + C_p}}}$ | Hz |
| 4 | **Frequency separation** | ŌĆö | $f_p - f_s \approx \dfrac{f_s}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C}{C_p}$ | Hz |
| 5 | **Feedback factor ($\beta$)** | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | Depends on circuit (Pierce / Miller) | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Minimum gain for startup** | $A \geq \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ | $A \geq 1$ (for Pierce) | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Quality factor (Q)** | 50 ŌĆō 200 (typical) | $10{,}000$ ŌĆō $10^6$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | **Impedance at $f_s$** | ŌĆö | $Z_{min} \approx R_s$ (small) | ohms |
| 9 | **Impedance at $f_p$** | ŌĆö | $Z_{max}$ (very large, ideally Ōł×) | ohms |
| 10 | **Frequency stability** | $\pm 0.1\%$ typical | $\pm 0.001\%$ to $\pm 0.0001\%$ | ŌĆö |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** In the Colpitts formula, students often forget that $C_{eq}$ is the **series** combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$ (not the parallel!). The reason is that the same current flows through both capacitors, and the voltage across each is the *sum*, making them effectively in series for the tank resonance calculation.

---

## 2.4 Comparison Snapshot for Quick Recall

| Property | Colpitts | Crystal |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency-determining element | LC tank (with capacitive divider) | Piezoelectric quartz crystal |
| Feedback network | Capacitive divider ($C_1, C_2$) | Crystal acts as highly selective inductor |
| Q factor | Low to moderate | Extremely high |
| Frequency tunable? | Yes (by varying $C_1, C_2$ or $L$) | No (fixed by crystal cut) |
| Frequency range | 1 kHz ŌĆō 500 MHz | 1 kHz ŌĆō 300 MHz (fundamental) |
| Stability | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | Slightly higher |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Frequency of Oscillation for Colpitts Oscillator

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup
Consider a Colpitts oscillator using a BJT in the common-emitter configuration. The equivalent circuit of the tank can be redrawn by referring all elements to the output side of the transistor.

Let the load resistance at the output of the amplifier be $R_L$, and let the input resistance of the amplifier be $R_i$. The capacitive divider $C_1$ŌĆō$C_2$ taps the output voltage to feed back to the input.

### 3.1.2 Effective Capacitances in the Tank

The total capacitance in the tank that resonates with $L$ is the **series combination** of $C_1$ and $C_2$, because the same current flows through them in the tank loop:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

**Why series?** When two capacitors are connected end-to-end with the tank inductor across their series combination, the alternating current through $L$ must also flow through $C_1$ first, then through $C_2$, and back to $L$. This is the textbook definition of capacitors in series for AC analysis.

### 3.1.3 Condition for Resonance
At resonance, the reactance of the inductor $L$ cancels the reactance of $C_{eq}$:

$$
X_L = X_{C_{eq}}
$$

$$
2\pi f_o L = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C_{eq}}
$$

### 3.1.4 Solving for $f_o$

Rearranging the above equation:

$$
(2\pi f_o)^2 = \frac{1}{L \cdot C_{eq}}
$$

$$
4\pi^2 f_o^2 = \frac{1}{L \cdot C_{eq}}
$$

$$
f_o^2 = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 L \cdot C_{eq}}
$$

Taking the positive square root (since frequency is positive):

$$
\boxed{\,f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}\,}
$$

### 3.1.5 Derivation of Barkhausen Condition (Sustained Oscillation)

For the Colpitts oscillator, the feedback ratio is determined by the **capacitive voltage divider** $C_1$ŌĆō$C_2$. The voltage at the feedback point (junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$) is:

$$
V_f = V_o \cdot \frac{X_{C_2}}{X_{C_1} + X_{C_2}} = V_o \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}{\frac{1}{j\omega C_1} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}}
$$

Simplifying (the $j\omega$ cancels):

$$
V_f = V_o \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

The feedback fraction $\beta$ is defined as $V_f / V_o$:

$$
\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

For the loop gain to be unity ($\vert A \beta \vert = 1$), the amplifier gain must be:

$$
A = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1} = 1 + \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

For practical startup, the gain must **slightly exceed** this value:

$$
\boxed{\,A \geq 1 + \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1}\,}
$$

This is the **Barkhausen condition for sustained oscillation** in a Colpitts oscillator.

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A Colpitts oscillator has $C_1 = 0.01\ \mu F$, $C_2 = 0.1\ \mu F$, and $L = 100\ \mu H$. Find the frequency of oscillation and the minimum amplifier gain required.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate $C_{eq}$:**

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(0.01 \times 10^{-6})(0.1 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.01 + 0.1) \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{0.001 \times 10^{-12}}{0.11 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-15}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.09 \times 10^{-9}\ F = 9.09\ nF
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $f_o$:**

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{100 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.09 \times 10^{-9}}}
$$

$$
L \cdot C_{eq} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.09 \times 10^{-9} = 9.09 \times 10^{-13}
$$

$$
\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}} = \sqrt{9.09 \times 10^{-13}} = 9.534 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}} = 2\pi \times 9.534 \times 10^{-7} = 5.99 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{5.99 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.669 \times 10^{5}\ Hz \approx 166.9\ kHz
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate minimum gain:**

$$
A_{min} = 1 + \frac{C_2}{C_1} = 1 + \frac{0.1}{0.01} = 1 + 10 = 11
$$

**Answer:** $f_o \approx 166.9\ kHz$ and $A_{min} = 11$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Resonant Frequencies for Quartz Crystal

### 3.2.1 Equivalent Circuit Analysis
The crystal equivalent circuit is:
- Series branch: $R_s$, $L$, $C$ (the **motional arm**)
- Parallel branch: $C_p$ (the **holder capacitance**)

The total impedance of the crystal looking into its terminals is:

$$
Z = \frac{(R_s + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C}) \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}{R_s + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C} + \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C_p$:

$$
Z = \frac{R_s + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C}}{j\omega C_p (R_s + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C}) + 1}
$$

### 3.2.2 Series Resonant Frequency $f_s$

At series resonance, the imaginary part of the motional arm becomes zero. The condition is:

$$
\omega_s L - \frac{1}{\omega_s C} = 0
$$

Solving for $\omega_s$:

$$
\omega_s^2 = \frac{1}{LC}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}\,}
$$

At $f_s$, the impedance of the crystal is:

$$
Z(f_s) = R_s \quad \text{(purely resistive, minimum value)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Parallel Resonant Frequency $f_p$

At parallel resonance, the imaginary part of the **total** impedance $Z$ goes to infinity (crystal looks like an open circuit). This happens when the inductive reactance of the motional arm resonates with the parallel combination's capacitive reactance.

The effective capacitance at this stage, considering $C$ in series with $C_p$ when viewed from the inductive side, is:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}
$$

Setting the inductive reactance of $L$ equal to the capacitive reactance of $C_{eff}$:

$$
\omega_p L = \frac{1}{\omega_p C_{eff}} = \frac{C + C_p}{\omega_p C \cdot C_p}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\omega_p^2 = \frac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}}\,}
$$

At $f_p$, the impedance of the crystal is **maximum** (ideally infinite).

### 3.2.4 Frequency Separation $(f_p - f_s)$

Since $C_p \gg C$ (typically $C_p / C \approx 100$ to $1000$), we can use the approximation:

$$
f_p - f_s \approx \frac{f_s}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{C_p}
$$

**Derivation of the approximation:**

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \frac{CC_p}{C+C_p}}} = f_s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{C + C_p}{C_p}} = f_s \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{C}{C_p}}
$$

Using the binomial approximation $\sqrt{1 + x} \approx 1 + \frac{x}{2}$ for small $x$ (since $C/C_p$ is small):

$$
f_p \approx f_s \left(1 + \frac{C}{2C_p}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta f = f_p - f_s \approx \frac{f_s}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{C_p}\,}
$$

This shows that the **frequency range over which the crystal acts as an inductor is very narrow** ŌĆö typically only **0.01% to 0.1%** of $f_s$. This narrow band is precisely what gives the crystal oscillator its incredible frequency stability.

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A quartz crystal has the following parameters: $L = 2.5\ H$, $C = 0.012\ pF$, $C_p = 5\ pF$, $R_s = 50\ \Omega$. Calculate $f_s$, $f_p$, and the Q factor.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate $f_s$:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.5 \times 0.012 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
L \cdot C = 2.5 \times 0.012 \times 10^{-12} = 0.03 \times 10^{-12} = 3 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{3 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.732 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
2\pi \times 1.732 \times 10^{-7} = 1.088 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{1.088 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 919\ kHz
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $f_p$:**

First compute $\dfrac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}$:

$$
\frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p} = \frac{0.012 \times 5}{0.012 + 5} = \frac{0.06}{5.012} = 0.01197\ pF
$$

Now $L \times$ this:

$$
L \times 0.01197 \times 10^{-12} = 2.5 \times 0.01197 \times 10^{-12} = 2.99 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.99 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.729 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.729 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 920.5\ kHz
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate Q factor:**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{50}\sqrt{\frac{2.5}{0.012 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
\frac{L}{C} = \frac{2.5}{0.012 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.083 \times 10^{14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{2.083 \times 10^{14}} = 1.443 \times 10^7
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.443 \times 10^7}{50} = 2.89 \times 10^5 \approx 289{,}000
$$

**Answer:** $f_s \approx 919\ kHz$, $f_p \approx 920.5\ kHz$, $Q \approx 2.89 \times 10^5$. This is an exceptionally high Q ŌĆö orders of magnitude greater than any LC tank!

> [!NOTE]
> **Observations from the numerical example:**
> - $f_s$ and $f_p$ are extremely close (only $\approx 1.5$ kHz apart for a $\sim 920$ kHz crystal)
> - The Q factor is **~289,000** ŌĆö compare this to a typical LC tank Q of 50ŌĆō200. This explains the superb frequency stability of crystal oscillators.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Colpitts Oscillator

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **signal flow architecture** of a Colpitts oscillator. The actual circuit is an LC tank with a capacitive divider; here we present the functional block structure that satisfies the Barkhausen loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCSupply["DC Supply Vcc"] --> BiasNetwork["Biasing Network"]
    BiasNetwork --> ActiveDevice["Active Device BJT or FET Amplifier"]
    ActiveDevice -->|Output Voltage Vo| TankOut["Tank Output Node Collector or Drain"]
    TankOut --> C1["Capacitor C1"]
    C1 --> TankJunction["Tank Junction Node"]
    TankJunction --> C2["Capacitor C2"]
    C2 --> GroundNode["Ground"]
    TankJunction --> InductorL["Inductor L"]
    InductorL --> GroundNode
    TankJunction --> FeedbackLine["Feedback Path to Input"]
    FeedbackLine --> ActiveDevice
    ActiveDevice -->|Sustained Sine Wave| OutputTerminal["Oscilloscope or Load"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The amplifier's output drives the tank. $C_1$ and $C_2$ are in series across the tank inductor $L$ (because both capacitors return to ground through the same AC current path). The junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$ is the **feedback point**, which is routed back to the amplifier input ŌĆö completing the Barkhausen loop.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Crystal Oscillator (Pierce Configuration)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the standard **Pierce crystal oscillator** architecture. The crystal operates in the inductive region between $f_s$ and $f_p$ and forms part of the feedback network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Vcc["Vcc DC Supply"] --> ResistorRF["Resistor RF Biasing"]
    ResistorRF --> ActiveDeviceInverter["Inverting Amplifier BJT or CMOS Inverter"]
    ActiveDeviceInverter -->|Output| CrystalTerm1["Crystal Terminal 1"]
    CrystalTerm1 --> CrystalBlock["Piezoelectric Quartz Crystal Acts as High Q Inductor between fs and fp"]
    CrystalBlock --> CrystalTerm2["Crystal Terminal 2"]
    CrystalTerm2 --> GroundNode2["Ground"]
    CrystalTerm1 --> C1Pierce["Load Capacitor CL1"]
    C1Pierce --> GroundNode2
    CrystalTerm1 --> C2Pierce["Load Capacitor CL2"]
    C2Pierce --> GroundNode2
    ActiveDeviceInverter --> OutputNode["Buffered Sine Output"]
    OutputNode --> Load["Load or Frequency Counter"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How the Pierce oscillator works:** The crystal acts as a **very high-Q inductor** in the frequency range between $f_s$ and $f_p$. The two load capacitors $C_{L1}$ and $C_{L2}$ form the capacitive divider that completes the Colpitts-like feedback. The inverting amplifier provides the 180┬░ phase shift, and the capacitive divider adds another 180┬░ ŌĆö total 360┬░ = 0┬░ loop phase.

---

## 4.3 Reactance vs Frequency Behaviour of Quartz Crystal

This Mermaid diagram conceptually traces the **impedance behaviour** of a quartz crystal as a function of frequency, which is critical for understanding where it operates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LowFreq["Frequency much less than fs"] -->|Crystal Impedance| CapBehavior["Capacitive due to Cp dominates"]
    SeriesRes["At fs Series Resonance"] -->|Crystal Impedance| MinImp["Purely Resistive R_s MINIMUM Z"]
    IndRegion["Between fs and fp"] -->|Crystal Impedance| IndBehavior["INDUCTIVE region Oscillator operates here"]
    ParRes["At fp Parallel Resonance"] -->|Crystal Impedance| MaxImp["Purely Resistive MAXIMUM Z"]
    HighFreq["Frequency much greater than fp"] -->|Crystal Impedance| CapBehavior2["Capacitive behaviour resumes"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The crystal behaves as a **capacitor** at low frequencies, becomes a **pure resistor** at $f_s$, switches to an **inductor** between $f_s$ and $f_p$, becomes a **pure resistor** again at $f_p$, and reverts to **capacitive** above $f_p$. Crystal oscillators are designed to operate **only in the narrow inductive band** between $f_s$ and $f_p$.

---

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartOsc["Need a Sinusoidal Oscillator"] --> Q1{"Need very high frequency stability?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| CrystalPath["Use Crystal Oscillator"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Need variable or tunable frequency?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| ColpittsPath["Use Colpitts Oscillator"]
    Q2 -->|No| ColpittsPath2["Colpitts is also a good choice"]
    CrystalPath --> CrystalApps["Applications Clocks Watches Microcontrollers GPS RF References"]
    ColpittsPath --> ColpittsApps["Applications RF Generators Local Oscillators Signal Sources"]
    ColpittsPath2 --> ColpittsApps
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö June 2023]
**Q: Define a Colpitts oscillator. Write the expression for the frequency of oscillation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Colpitts oscillator** is a sinusoidal LC feedback oscillator that uses a **capacitive voltage divider** (two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$) and an **inductor $L$** in the tank circuit to determine the frequency of oscillation. The capacitive divider $C_1$ŌĆō$C_2$ provides the necessary feedback to sustain oscillations by satisfying the Barkhausen criterion.

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}
$$

where

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

This is because the two capacitors are in **series** as far as the tank inductor is concerned. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022]
**Q: What is a piezoelectric crystal? Mention its two resonant frequencies.**

**Model Answer:**

A **piezoelectric crystal** (typically quartz) is a slice of crystalline material that exhibits the **piezoelectric effect** ŌĆö it converts mechanical stress into electrical voltage (direct effect) and electrical voltage into mechanical vibration (inverse effect). This property allows it to be used as a highly stable frequency-selective element in oscillators.

**The two resonant frequencies are:**

1. **Series resonant frequency $f_s$** ŌĆö at which the motional arm reactances cancel and the crystal impedance is minimum (Ōēł $R_s$).

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}
$$

2. **Parallel resonant frequency $f_p$** ŌĆö at which the inductive reactance of the motional arm resonates with the holder capacitance $C_p$ and the crystal impedance is maximum.

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}}
$$

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Module 4]

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Colpitts oscillator. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (description):**
A Colpitts oscillator consists of a BJT (or FET) in common-emitter configuration with an LC tank circuit. The tank has an inductor $L$ in parallel with two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ connected in series. The output is taken at the collector (or drain) of the active device, and feedback is taken from the junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$ back to the input (base or gate).

**Working Principle:**

1. **When DC supply is switched ON**, transient noise in the circuit excites the tank circuit across a wide frequency range.
2. **The LC tank acts as a band-pass filter**, selecting the natural resonant frequency and reinforcing it.
3. **The BJT amplifies** this resonant signal. The common-emitter configuration introduces a **180┬░ phase shift** between input and output.
4. **The capacitive voltage divider** $C_1$ŌĆō$C_2$ samples a portion of the output voltage and feeds it back to the input. This feedback introduces an **additional 180┬░ phase shift** because the feedback point is at the opposite end of the tank.
5. **Total loop phase shift = 180┬░ + 180┬░ = 360┬░ = 0┬░**, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.
6. **The loop gain condition** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is satisfied when the gain of the amplifier is large enough to compensate for the tank losses, and the amplitude stabilizes at a steady value.

**Derivation of Frequency:**

The tank circuit consists of inductor $L$ in parallel with the **series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$**. The effective capacitance is:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

At resonance, the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance of $C_{eq}$:

$$
2\pi f_o L = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C_{eq}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
f_o^2 = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 L C_{eq}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks] In a Colpitts oscillator, $C_1 = 0.001\ \mu F$ and $C_2 = 0.01\ \mu F$. If the frequency of oscillation is 50 kHz, find the value of the inductor $L$. Also find the minimum gain required to sustain oscillations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $C_1 = 0.001\ \mu F = 10^{-9}\ F$, $C_2 = 0.01\ \mu F = 10^{-8}\ F$, $f_o = 50\ kHz = 5 \times 10^{4}\ Hz$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate $C_{eq}$:**

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(10^{-9})(10^{-8})}{(10^{-9} + 10^{-8})} = \frac{10^{-17}}{1.1 \times 10^{-8}} = 9.09 \times 10^{-10}\ F
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $L$ from the frequency formula:**

Rearranging $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ for $L$:

$$
L = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 f_o^2 C_{eq}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
L = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 (5 \times 10^4)^2 (9.09 \times 10^{-10})}
$$

$$
L = \frac{1}{4 \times 9.87 \times 2.5 \times 10^{9} \times 9.09 \times 10^{-10}}
$$

$$
L = \frac{1}{4 \times 9.87 \times 2.2725}
$$

$$
L = \frac{1}{89.71} \approx 0.01115\ H \approx 11.15\ mH
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate minimum gain:**

The feedback factor for a Colpitts oscillator is:

$$
\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{10^{-9}}{10^{-9} + 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{11}
$$

For sustained oscillations, the minimum gain must satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
A_{min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = 1 + \frac{C_2}{C_1} = 1 + \frac{0.01}{0.001} = 1 + 10 = 11
$$

**Final Answers:** $L \approx 11.15\ mH$ and $A_{min} = 11$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Module 4 ŌĆö Internal Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of a crystal oscillator with a neat equivalent circuit. Derive the expressions for the series and parallel resonant frequencies.**

**Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Circuit of Quartz Crystal:**
The quartz crystal is represented by:
- A **series RLC branch** (motional arm): $R_s$ (mechanical friction), $L$ (inertia/mass), $C$ (compliance/elasticity)
- A **parallel capacitance** $C_p$ (holder capacitance) representing the static capacitance between the electrodes.

**Working Principle:**

The crystal exhibits the **piezoelectric effect** ŌĆö when an AC voltage is applied, the crystal mechanically vibrates at the same frequency. When the applied frequency matches the **natural mechanical resonance** of the crystal, the vibration amplitude is maximum, and so is the induced voltage. This makes the crystal behave as a **very high-Q resonant circuit** at its natural frequency.

**Two Resonant Frequencies:**

**1. Series Resonant Frequency $f_s$:**
At series resonance, the reactances of $L$ and $C$ in the motional arm cancel each other:

$$
\omega_s L = \frac{1}{\omega_s C}
$$

Solving:

$$
\omega_s^2 = \frac{1}{LC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}
$$

At $f_s$, the crystal impedance is **minimum (Ōēł $R_s$)**, and the crystal behaves as a short circuit.

**2. Parallel Resonant Frequency $f_p$:**
At parallel resonance, the inductive reactance of the motional arm resonates with the parallel holder capacitance $C_p$. The effective capacitance becomes the series combination of $C$ and $C_p$:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p}
$$

Setting inductive and capacitive reactances equal:

$$
\omega_p L = \frac{1}{\omega_p C_{eff}} = \frac{C + C_p}{\omega_p \cdot C \cdot C_p}
$$

$$
\omega_p^2 = \frac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}}}
$$

At $f_p$, the crystal impedance is **maximum (ideally infinite)**, and the crystal behaves as an open circuit.

**Operating Region:**
Between $f_s$ and $f_p$, the crystal exhibits an **inductive reactance**, which is exploited in Pierce and Miller crystal oscillator circuits to replace the bulky inductor in a Colpitts-type configuration. The very high Q of the crystal ($10^4$ to $10^6$) gives **exceptional frequency stability**.

**(b) [7 Marks] A quartz crystal has the following parameters: $L = 3\ H$, $C = 0.02\ pF$, $C_p = 6\ pF$, $R_s = 60\ \Omega$. Calculate (i) series resonant frequency, (ii) parallel resonant frequency, (iii) quality factor, and (iv) the frequency separation $f_p - f_s$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 3\ H$, $C = 0.02\ pF = 0.02 \times 10^{-12}\ F = 2 \times 10^{-14}\ F$, $C_p = 6\ pF = 6 \times 10^{-12}\ F$, $R_s = 60\ \Omega$.

**(i) Series Resonant Frequency:**

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{3 \times 2 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
LC = 6 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{6 \times 10^{-14}} = 2.449 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
f_s = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.449 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.498 \times 10^5\ Hz
$$

$$
\boxed{f_s \approx 649.8\ kHz}
$$

**(ii) Parallel Resonant Frequency:**

First compute $C_{eff}$:

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{C \cdot C_p}{C + C_p} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-14})(6 \times 10^{-12})}{(2 \times 10^{-14} + 6 \times 10^{-12})}
$$

$$
C_{eff} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-25}}{6.02 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 1.993 \times 10^{-14}\ F \approx 0.01993\ pF
$$

Then:

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{3 \times 1.993 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
L C_{eff} = 5.979 \times 10^{-14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{L C_{eff}} = 2.445 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.445 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 6.51 \times 10^5\ Hz
$$

$$
\boxed{f_p \approx 651.1\ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Quality Factor:**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{60}\sqrt{\frac{3}{2 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
\frac{L}{C} = \frac{3}{2 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.5 \times 10^{14}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{14}} = 1.225 \times 10^7
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.225 \times 10^7}{60} \approx 2.04 \times 10^5
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 2.04 \times 10^5 \text{ (i.e., 204,000)}}
$$

**(iv) Frequency Separation:**

Using the approximation (valid since $C_p \gg C$):

$$
\Delta f = f_p - f_s \approx \frac{f_s}{2} \cdot \frac{C}{C_p}
$$

$$
\frac{C}{C_p} = \frac{0.02}{6} = 0.00333
$$

$$
\Delta f \approx \frac{649.8 \times 10^3}{2} \times 0.00333 = 324.9 \times 10^3 \times 0.00333 \approx 1082\ Hz
$$

**Verification by direct subtraction:** $651.1\ kHz - 649.8\ kHz = 1.3\ kHz$ Ō£ō (matches our approximation).

$$
\boxed{\Delta f \approx 1.08\ kHz \text{ (very narrow band of operation)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong equivalent capacitance:** Many students write $C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2$ (parallel) instead of $C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (series). **Always remember**: in a Colpitts tank, the capacitors are in **series** because the same AC current flows through them. Lose 2 marks if you make this error.
> 2. **Forgetting the minimum gain condition:** Marks are awarded for explicitly stating the Barkhausen condition $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$ for the Colpitts amplifier. A derivation of $A \geq (C_1+C_2)/C_1$ is essential.
> 3. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_p$ formulas:** In crystal problems, students often interchange the two formulas. **Mnemonic**: $f_s$ is the *simpler* formula (only $L$ and $C$); $f_p$ involves the *extra* $C_p$ (parallel).
> 4. **Forgetting to square the frequency when isolating L or C:** When solving for $L$ from $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, remember that $\sqrt{LC} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o}$, so $LC = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 f_o^2}$.
> 5. **Not showing the operating region:** For crystal oscillator theory questions, always mention that the crystal operates in the **inductive region between $f_s$ and $f_p$**. Examiners award marks for this conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Summary Checklist

- [ ] **Colpitts Oscillator** uses a **capacitive voltage divider** ($C_1, C_2$) in the tank circuit, in place of the tapped inductor in a Hartely oscillator.
- [ ] **Frequency formula:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (**series combination** ŌĆö most common mistake!).
- [ ] **Feedback factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_1 + C_2}$; **minimum gain:** $A_{min} = \dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{C_1} = 1 + \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$.
- [ ] **Loop phase shift:** 180┬░ from CE amplifier + 180┬░ from capacitive divider feedback = **360┬░ = 0┬░** (Barkhausen phase satisfied).
- [ ] **Quartz crystal** operates on the **piezoelectric effect** ŌĆö mechanical vibration Ōåö electrical voltage.
- [ ] **Equivalent circuit of crystal:** series $R_s$ŌĆō$L$ŌĆō$C$ (motional arm) in parallel with $C_p$ (holder capacitance).
- [ ] **Series resonance $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$** ŌĆö impedance is **minimum** (Ōēł $R_s$), crystal is a short circuit.
- [ ] **Parallel resonance $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C + C_p}{L \cdot C \cdot C_p}}$** ŌĆö impedance is **maximum**, crystal is an open circuit.
- [ ] **Operating region:** between $f_s$ and $f_p$, the crystal behaves as a **high-Q inductor** ŌĆö this is where it is used in oscillators.
- [ ] **Frequency separation:** $\Delta f = f_p - f_s \approx \dfrac{f_s}{2} \cdot \dfrac{C}{C_p}$ ŌĆö typically a very small fraction of $f_s$.
- [ ] **Q-factor of crystal:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$, typically $10^4$ to $10^6$ ŌĆö orders of magnitude higher than LC tanks.
- [ ] **Q-factor of LC Colpitts:** typically 50 to 200 ŌĆö hence **crystal is more stable** but Colpitts is **tunable**, while crystal is not.
- [ ] **Real-world uses of crystal oscillators:** digital watches (32.768 kHz), microcontrollers, GPS, RF references, communication systems.
- [ ] **Barkhausen Criterion (always state for any oscillator problem):** $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ **AND** total loop phase = $0┬░$ (or $2n\pi$).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy:** Memorize the three golden formulas: **(1)** Colpitts $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$, **(2)** Crystal $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, and **(3)** Crystal $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C + C_p}{L C C_p}}$. All KTU problems in this module reduce to one of these three formulas combined with the Barkhausen condition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RC Phase Shift Oscillator (RCPSO)**: A sinusoidal oscillator that uses a Common-Emitter (CE) transistor amplifier (or op-amp inverting stage) in conjunction with a **lattice of three identical RC sections** arranged in a feedback path. Each RC section contributes a phase shift of **60┬░**, the amplifier inverts (**180┬░**), making the total loop phase shift exactly **360┬░ (0┬░)**, satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillations.

### Barkhausen Criterion Recap
A linear oscillator requires two simultaneous conditions around the closed feedback loop:
1. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
2. **Loop Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

For the **3-stage RCPSO**, the amplifier must supply a gain of at least **29**, and the passive RC ladder must produce a phase shift of exactly **180┬░** to compensate for the **180┬░** phase reversal of the CE stage.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy ŌĆö Pushing a Swing:**
> Imagine a child on a swing. To keep the swing oscillating forever, you must **push at exactly the right moment** (phase) and with the **right force** (gain). If you push too early or too late (wrong phase), the swing will jerk awkwardly. If you push too gently (gain < 1), the swing will slow and stop. If you push too hard (gain > 1), the swing will go wild.
>
> The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** works the same way: the transistor amplifier is the "pusher," the RC ladder delays the feedback signal just enough to push the transistor output at the right instant, and the gain (Ōēł 29) is precisely tuned to compensate for the energy lost in the resistors ŌĆö keeping oscillations alive forever.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
| :--- | :--- |
| Phase shift per RC section | **60┬░** |
| Total passive phase shift | **180┬░** |
| Amplifier phase shift (CE) | **180┬░** |
| Minimum voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | **29** |
| Frequency factor $k$ | $\sqrt{6} \approx 2.449$ |
| Useful frequency range | **1 Hz to 10 MHz** (audio dominant) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The RC phase shift oscillator is *universally* classified as a **low-frequency audio oscillator**, used extensively in function generators, alarm tone generators, and musical synthesizers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Concept

A sinusoidal oscillator is essentially a **positive feedback amplifier with zero net input**. When the loop is broken and the closed-loop gain is set to unity, the circuit self-oscillates at a single preferred frequency determined by the reactive (RC) network.

### 2.1.1 Functional Block Architecture

1. **Active Block (CE Amplifier):**
   * Provides voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ and inherent **180┬░** inversion between input and output.
   * The DC bias network sets the Q-point in the active region of the BJT.
   * The AC load line determines the small-signal gain: $A_v = -g_m R_C \parallel R_L$.

2. **Feedback Block (3-Stage RC Ladder):**
   * Three cascaded RC high-pass sections.
   * Each section is a simple **voltage divider** with complex impedance ratio.
   * Output of ladder is fed back to the input of the CE stage.

3. **Frequency-Determining Block:**
   * The RC time constants $(\tau = RC)$ collectively set the **natural frequency of oscillation** $f_0$.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the 60┬░ Per Section Claim

> **Why 60┬░ per RC section?** A single RC high-pass section has a transfer function of the form $\dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$. As $\omega \to \infty$, the phase approaches **+90┬░**; as $\omega \to 0$, the phase approaches **0┬░**. When three such sections are cascaded, their individual phase shifts *add*, and at one particular frequency $\omega_0$, the total phase shift is exactly **180┬░** (because of loading interactions, this divides as **60┬░ + 60┬░ + 60┬░**).

---

## 2.3 Why 3 Sections and Not 2 or 4?

| Number of RC Sections | Total Phase Shift | Total Loop Phase | Verdict |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 section | At best 90┬░ | 90┬░ + 180┬░ = 270┬░ | **Not feasible** |
| 2 sections | At best 180┬░ (theoretical) | 180┬░ + 180┬░ = 360┬░ Ō£ō | Loading prevents clean 90┬░ each; **unstable** |
| 3 sections | **Exactly 180┬░** at $f_0$ | 180┬░ + 180┬░ = 360┬░ Ō£ō | **Used in practice** |
| 4 sections | 240┬░ (no 180┬░ crossover) | 420┬░ | Excess attenuation; not preferred |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** More RC sections mean more resistive attenuation, requiring a higher gain amplifier. The 3-section design is the *optimum engineering compromise* between phase precision, component count, and gain demand.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Frequency of oscillation (3-stage RCPSO) |
| 2 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | Angular frequency of oscillation |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{29}$ (magnitude) | Attenuation factor of RC ladder at $f_0$ |
| 4 | $\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$ | Minimum amplifier gain for sustained oscillation |
| 5 | $R_C \parallel R_L = 29 \cdot r_e$ | Design relation for bias resistors |
| 6 | $V_{CC} \approx 2 \cdot I_C \cdot (R_C + R_E)$ | DC bias design guideline |
| 7 | $\angle\beta = 180┬░$ | RC ladder phase at $f_0$ |
| 8 | $\angle A = 180┬░$ | CE stage inversion |
| 9 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Transconductance (with $V_T \approx 26$ mV) |
| 10 | $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E \text{(mA)}}$ | Small-signal emitter resistance |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Favourite:** Derive $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$ and show $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$. These two results are worth **8 out of 14 marks** in a typical KTU ESE Part-B question.

---

- **Function Generators & Audio Test Equipment**
- **Boat-Horn and Burglar-Alarm Tone Synthesizers**
- **Electronic Musical Instruments** (organ tone generators)
- **Servo-System Excitation Sources**
- **Inverters** for low-power DC-to-AC conversion

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation & Loop Gain

### Step 0 ŌĆö Circuit Topology

Consider the 3-stage RC ladder shown in the diagram (SECTION 4). The ladder consists of:

- $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$
- $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$
- The collector of the BJT is the **input** to the ladder (node A).
- The output of the ladder is taken at the junction of $R_3$ and $C_3$ (node D), which is connected back to the **base** of the BJT.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Node Voltage Definitions

Let us label the nodes and define:

- $V_C$ = Output voltage of CE stage = Voltage applied at the *input* of the RC ladder.
- $V_1$ = Voltage at the junction of $R_1$ and $C_1$.
- $V_2$ = Voltage at the junction of $R_2$ and $C_2$.
- $V_3$ = Voltage at the junction of $R_3$ and $C_3$ = feedback voltage $V_f$.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Apply KCL at Each Node

> **At Node $V_1$ (junction of $R_1$, $C_1$, and $R_2$):**

The current entering the node from the amplifier output equals the current leaving through $C_1$ to ground plus the current leaving through $R_2$ toward $V_2$.

$$
\dfrac{V_C - V_1}{R} = \dfrac{V_1}{1/(j\omega C)} + \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{V_C - V_1}{R} = j\omega C \, V_1 + \dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R}
$$

Multiply both sides by $R$:

$$
V_C - V_1 = j\omega RC \, V_1 + V_1 - V_2
$$

Simplify:

$$
V_C = (2 + j\omega RC) V_1 - V_2 \quad \text{...... (Eq. 1)}
$$

> **At Node $V_2$ (junction of $R_2$, $C_2$, and $R_3$):**

$$
\dfrac{V_1 - V_2}{R} = j\omega C \, V_2 + \dfrac{V_2 - V_3}{R}
$$

Multiply by $R$:

$$
V_1 - V_2 = j\omega RC \, V_2 + V_2 - V_3
$$

Simplify:

$$
V_1 = (2 + j\omega RC) V_2 - V_3 \quad \text{...... (Eq. 2)}
$$

> **At Node $V_3$ (junction of $R_3$, $C_3$, and the BJT base):**

The base of the BJT draws negligible current (assuming $\beta_{DC}$ is high and the base is fed by a high-impedance path). Hence, $R_3$ carries only the displacement current into $C_3$.

$$
\dfrac{V_2 - V_3}{R} = j\omega C \, V_3
$$

Simplify:

$$
V_2 - V_3 = j\omega RC \, V_3
$$

$$
V_2 = (1 + j\omega RC) V_3 \quad \text{...... (Eq. 3)}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Solve the System of Three Equations

From **Eq. 3**, express $V_2$ in terms of $V_3$:

$$
V_2 = (1 + j\omega RC) V_3
$$

Substitute into **Eq. 2**:

$$
V_1 = (2 + j\omega RC)(1 + j\omega RC) V_3 - V_3
$$

Let $\alpha = j\omega RC$ for compactness. Then:

$$
V_1 = (2 + \alpha)(1 + \alpha) V_3 - V_3
$$

Expand $(2 + \alpha)(1 + \alpha)$:

$$
(2 + \alpha)(1 + \alpha) = 2 + 2\alpha + \alpha + \alpha^2 = 2 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2
$$

Hence:

$$
V_1 = (2 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2) V_3 - V_3 = (1 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2) V_3
$$

Substitute this $V_1$ and $V_2$ into **Eq. 1**:

$$
V_C = (2 + \alpha)(1 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2) V_3 - (1 + \alpha) V_3
$$

Expand $(2 + \alpha)(1 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2)$:

$$
(2)(1 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2) + \alpha(1 + 3\alpha + \alpha^2)
$$

$$
= 2 + 6\alpha + 2\alpha^2 + \alpha + 3\alpha^2 + \alpha^3
$$

$$
= 2 + 7\alpha + 5\alpha^2 + \alpha^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_C = (2 + 7\alpha + 5\alpha^2 + \alpha^3) V_3 - (1 + \alpha) V_3
$$

$$
V_C = (2 + 7\alpha + 5\alpha^2 + \alpha^3 - 1 - \alpha) V_3
$$

$$
V_C = (1 + 6\alpha + 5\alpha^2 + \alpha^3) V_3
$$

The feedback factor is $\beta = V_3 / V_C$:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{V_3}{V_C} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6\alpha + 5\alpha^2 + \alpha^3}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute Back $\alpha = j\omega RC$

$$
\beta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 6(j\omega RC) + 5(j\omega RC)^2 + (j\omega RC)^3}
$$

$$
\beta = \dfrac{1}{1 + j6\omega RC - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2 - j\omega^3 R^3 C^3}
$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Group Real and Imaginary Parts

The denominator is:

$$
D = \underbrace{(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2)}_{\text{Real Part}} + j\underbrace{(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3)}_{\text{Imaginary Part}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3)}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Apply the Oscillation Phase Condition

For the loop phase shift to be **0┬░ (or 360┬░)**, the imaginary part of the denominator must vanish:

$$
6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0
$$

Factor:

$$
\omega RC (6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) = 0
$$

Since $\omega \neq 0$ (we need non-zero oscillation frequency):

$$
6 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6
$$

$$
\omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}}
$$

And the corresponding **frequency of oscillation**:

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}
$$

### Step 7 ŌĆö Apply the Oscillation Magnitude Condition

Substitute $\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 6$ into the real part of the denominator:

$$
\text{Re}(D) = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29
$$

So at the oscillation frequency, the feedback factor becomes a *real* (purely resistive) value:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{1}{-29} = -\dfrac{1}{29}
$$

The negative sign confirms the **180┬░ phase shift** across the RC ladder (a real negative number).

The magnitude:

$$
\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}
$$

From the Barkhausen criterion, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$
\vert A \vert \cdot \dfrac{1}{29} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29}
$$

This is the **minimum voltage gain** that the CE stage must provide to sustain oscillations.

---

## 3.2 Design Equations for Transistor Stage

For the CE amplifier to deliver a gain of 29 using a BJT:

$$
A_v = -\dfrac{R_C \parallel R_L}{r_e} = -29
$$

Where the small-signal emitter resistance:

$$
r_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E (\text{mA})}
$$

A standard design choice: set $I_E = 1$ mA, giving $r_e = 26\,\Omega$, and then:

$$
R_C \parallel R_L = 29 \times 26 = 754 \, \Omega
$$

Choosing $R_C = 1.5$ k$\Omega$ and $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$ is a typical practical combination.

---

## 3.3 Summary of Results

| Quantity | Result | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Oscillation frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}\,RC}$ | Resonant frequency set by RC |
| RC ladder attenuation | $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ | Energy lost in ladder |
| Required amplifier gain | $\vert A_v \vert = 29$ | Compensates for attenuation |
| Phase of $\beta$ | 180┬░ | Ladder phase at $f_0$ |
| Phase of $A$ | 180┬░ | CE inversion |
| Total loop phase | 360┬░ | Barkhausen phase condition Ō£ō |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]
**State the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Why is the RC phase shift oscillator preferred at audio frequencies?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion** has two conditions:
> 1. The magnitude of the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
> 2. The total phase shift around the loop $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ (i.e., 0┬░ or multiples of 360┬░)
>
> The RC phase shift oscillator is preferred at **audio frequencies (20 Hz ŌĆō 20 kHz)** because the inductors required for LC oscillators at such low frequencies would be impractically large, bulky, and expensive. RC components are compact, precise, and inexpensive. Ō£ō

### Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]
**What is the minimum gain required for a 3-stage RC phase shift oscillator using a CE BJT amplifier? Why?**

**Model Answer:**

> The minimum gain required is $\vert A_v \vert = 29$. This is because the 3-stage RC ladder has a magnitude attenuation of $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ at the oscillation frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{6}RC)$. To satisfy $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, the amplifier must exactly compensate this attenuation: $\vert A \vert = 1/\vert \beta \vert = 29$. Ō£ō

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ŌŁÉ Question A ŌĆö Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT in CE configuration. Show all intermediate steps.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT to generate a sine wave of **2 kHz**. Assume $V_{CC} = 12$ V and the transistor has $\beta = 100$ and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V.

---

### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Derivation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** State Barkhausen criterion and circuit configuration.
A 3-stage RC ladder with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$ is connected between the collector and base of a CE BJT amplifier.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Write KCL at the three nodes of the ladder (already derived in SECTION 3, Step 2):

$$
V_C = (2 + j\omega RC) V_1 - V_2 \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

$$
V_1 = (2 + j\omega RC) V_2 - V_3 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

$$
V_2 = (1 + j\omega RC) V_3 \quad \text{...(3)}
$$

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Solve the simultaneous equations to obtain the transfer function:

$$
\beta = \dfrac{V_3}{V_C} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2 + j(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3)}
$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Apply the phase condition (set imaginary part to zero):

$$
6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3 = 0 \implies \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}
$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Write the final frequency and gain:

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}\,RC}, \quad \vert A_v \vert \geq 29
$$

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Design [7 Marks]

**Given:** $f_0 = 2$ kHz, $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $\beta_{DC} = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V.

**Step 1 [1 mark]:** Choose $C$ and find $R$ from the frequency formula. A standard choice: $C = 0.01\,\mu F$.

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_0 C \sqrt{6}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{1}{3.077 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3.25 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

So $R \approx 3.3$ k$\Omega$ (use standard value).

**Step 2 [1 mark]:** Compute the small-signal emitter resistance. Choose $I_E = 1$ mA:

$$
r_e = \dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 26\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]:** Determine the required AC load:

$$
R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = 29 \times r_e = 29 \times 26 = 754\,\Omega
$$

For practical implementation, choose $R_C = 1.5$ k$\Omega$ and a load $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$:

$$
R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{1500 \times 10000}{1500 + 10000} = \dfrac{15{,}000{,}000}{11{,}500} \approx 1304\,\Omega
$$

Since $1304 > 754$, the gain condition $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$ is satisfied. Ō£ō

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Bias design ŌĆö Choose $V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2 = 6$ V for maximum symmetrical swing.

**Step 5 [1 mark]:** Set $I_C \approx I_E = 1$ mA. Then:

$$
R_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{I_C} = \dfrac{12 - 6}{1 \text{ mA}} = 6 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 6 [1 mark]:** For voltage-divider bias with $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower), the Thevenin voltage at the base should be $V_B = V_E + V_{BE} = V_E + 0.7$. Choose $V_E = 2$ V, so $V_B = 2.7$ V:

$$
R_2 = \dfrac{V_B}{V_{CC}} \times R_1, \quad R_1 + R_2 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{I_2}
$$

Choosing $I_2 = 0.1 I_B = 0.1 \times (I_C/\beta) = 0.1 \times 1/100 = 1\,\mu A$. But a safer choice: $I_2 = 10 I_B = 10 \times 10\,\mu A = 100\,\mu A$.

$$
R_1 + R_2 = \dfrac{12 \text{ V}}{100\,\mu A} = 120 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

And $R_2 = (2.7/12) \times 120 = 27$ k$\Omega$, $R_1 = 93$ k$\Omega$.

**Step 7 [1 mark]:** Choose $R_E$:

$$
R_E = \dfrac{V_E}{I_E} = \dfrac{2 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

Bypass $R_E$ with a large capacitor (e.g., $C_E = 100\,\mu F$) to maintain AC gain.

**Final Answer:**

$$
R = 3.3 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad C = 0.01\,\mu F, \quad R_C = 6 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_E = 2 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_1 = 93 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_2 = 27 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

---

### ŌŁÉ Question B ŌĆö Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working principle of an RC phase shift oscillator with a neat block diagram. Discuss why three RC sections are used.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A 3-stage RC phase shift oscillator uses $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu F$. Calculate the **frequency of oscillation** and the **minimum gain** required.

---

### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Working Principle [7 Marks]

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A **CE BJT amplifier** (or op-amp inverting stage) that provides voltage gain and a **180┬░** phase inversion.
- A **3-stage RC ladder network** as the feedback element.

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Working:
- The amplifier amplifies any small noise voltage at its base.
- The output at the collector drives the RC ladder.
- The ladder produces a phase shift and attenuates the signal.
- The attenuated, phase-shifted signal is fed back to the base.
- If the loop phase = 360┬░ and $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, **sustained oscillations** occur at $f_0$.

**Step 3 [2 marks]:** Why 3 sections?
- A single RC section can produce at most **90┬░** phase shift.
- Two sections would require each to provide **90┬░** exactly, which only happens at $\omega = 0$ or $\omega = \infty$ ŌĆö not usable.
- Three sections divide the required **180┬░** into a workable **60┬░ per section** at a finite $\omega_0$.
- Four or more sections cause excessive attenuation and unnecessary complexity.

**Step 4 [1 mark]:** Note practical considerations:
- In real circuits, gain is kept slightly > 29 (e.g., 30ŌĆō32) so that oscillations build up from noise.
- Amplitude stabilization is achieved using nonlinear elements (thermistors, diodes, or automatic gain control).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical [7 Marks]

**Given:** $R = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu F = 10^{-8}$ F.

**Step 1 [2 marks]:** Compute the frequency:

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 2.449} = \dfrac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 649.7 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]:** Compute the angular frequency:

$$
\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi \times 649.7 \approx 4082 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 [3 marks]:** Minimum gain:

$$
\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29
$$

This is the *universal* value for any 3-stage RC phase shift oscillator, independent of the component values of $R$ and $C$. Ō£ō

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to use $\sqrt{6}$ in the frequency formula. Writing $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ is a **1-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **minimum gain condition** $\vert A_v \vert = 29$ in the derivation. Examiners allocate **2 marks** for this. Without it, your derivation is incomplete.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the *attenuation* ($\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$) with the *gain* ($\vert A \vert = 29$). They are reciprocals. **1-mark deduction** if inverted.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing the amplifier phase shift as **0┬░** instead of **180┬░**. The CE stage is an *inverting* amplifier. **1-mark deduction**.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **­¤ÜĆ Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ŌŁÉ RC Phase Shift Oscillator uses **3 identical RC sections** + 1 CE amplifier.
- ŌŁÉ Each RC section gives **60┬░** phase shift; amplifier gives **180┬░** ŌåÆ total **360┬░** loop.
- ŌŁÉ Barkhausen Criterion: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, $\angle A\beta = 0┬░$ (or $2n\pi$).
- ŌŁÉ **Frequency of oscillation:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{6}\,RC}$ (memorize this verbatim).
- ŌŁÉ **Feedback factor magnitude:** $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ (purely real, negative at $f_0$).
- ŌŁÉ **Required CE gain:** $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$.
- ŌŁÉ Derivation uses KCL at 3 nodes of the RC ladder.
- ŌŁÉ The frequency-determining equation emerges from setting the **imaginary part of $1/\beta$ to zero**.
- ŌŁÉ Gain emerges from the **real part** at the resonant frequency.
- ŌŁÉ Practical designs set gain slightly higher (30ŌĆō32) to ensure oscillation build-up.
- ŌŁÉ Best suited for **audio frequency** range (20 Hz to 20 kHz).
- ŌŁÉ Compared to LC oscillators: cheaper, smaller, more stable at low frequencies; but output is more distorted and amplitude regulation is more difficult.
- ŌŁÉ The amplifier's **small-signal emitter resistance** $r_e = 26/I_E$ (mA) sets the AC gain.
- ŌŁÉ A **voltage-divider bias** with bypassed $R_E$ is the standard bias arrangement.
- ŌŁÉ Amplitude stabilization uses thermistors or diode limiters in practical designs.
- ŌŁÉ All resistors and capacitors in the ladder are assumed **identical** in the standard analysis.
- ŌŁÉ The negative sign of $\beta$ confirms the 180┬░ phase shift of the ladder.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal electronic amplifier designed to deliver substantial amounts of power to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, motor, or transducer) with acceptable efficiency and minimum distortion. Unlike small-signal voltage amplifiers that operate on the linear, active region of the transistor and handle signals in the millivolt-to-volt range, a power amplifier is biased and operated to handle large voltage and current swings simultaneously.

Power amplifiers are classified based on the **conduction angle** (also called the **angle of flow**) of the output transistor ŌĆö that is, the portion of the input signal cycle (out of the full 360┬░) during which the transistor remains in conduction (i.e., the collector current flows). This conduction angle is the primary figure-of-merit upon which the entire classification system (A, B, AB, C, D, etc.) is built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, classification of power amplifiers is the foundational concept. The examiner expects students to know **(i)** the conduction angle, **(ii)** the position of the Q-point, **(iii)** the maximum theoretical efficiency, and **(iv)** the typical application of each class (A, B, AB, C). Memorize the symbol $\theta_c$ for conduction angle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition ŌĆō Conduction Angle ($\theta_c$):**
> The conduction angle is the angular measure (in degrees or radians) of the input signal cycle over which the output device conducts. Mathematically, if $i_C(\omega t) > 0$ for $\omega t \in [\alpha, \beta]$, then:
> $$\theta_c = \beta - \alpha \quad \text{(in radians, or converted to degrees)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Water Tap" Model

Think of a power amplifier as a **high-power water tap** that controls a heavy industrial hose:

- A **small-signal amplifier** is like adjusting a fine laboratory burette ŌĆö the flow is delicate, precise, and linear, but the volume delivered is tiny.
- A **power amplifier** is like controlling a fire-hose valve ŌĆö you need a much bigger mechanical structure, and you can either let water flow *the entire time* (Class A), *only half the time* (Class B), *just a quick burst* (Class C), or use a *rapid on/off pulse-width modulation* (Class D).

The **classification** answers a single practical question: *How much of the time is the valve open?*

| Real-World Analogy | Amplifier Class | Conduction Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Tap always half-open | Class A | Conducts 360┬░ |
| Tap on for one half-cycle | Class B | Conducts 180┬░ |
| Tap on for slightly more than half | Class AB | 180┬░ < ╬Ė < 360┬░ |
| Tap on for a small burst | Class C | ╬Ė < 180┬░ |
| Tap pulsed digitally (PWM) | Class D | Switched mode |

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters ŌĆö The Engineering Trade-off

Every power amplifier designer faces a fundamental triangle of trade-offs:

1. **Efficiency** (how much DC power is converted to AC signal power).
2. **Linearity / Fidelity** (how faithfully the output reproduces the input waveform).
3. **Power delivered to load** (the actual useful output).

The class of operation determines *where* in this trade-off triangle the amplifier sits:

- More conduction ŌåÆ better linearity, lower efficiency.
- Less conduction ŌåÆ higher efficiency, more distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics (Bold-formatted for emphasis):**
> - **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$):** ratio of AC output power to DC supply power, unitless (or %).
> - **Maximum Efficiency ($\eta_{max}$):** theoretical upper bound for that class.
> - **Power Dissipation ($P_D$):** $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC}$, in **watts (W)**.
> - **Conduction Angle ($\theta_c$):** in **degrees** or **radians**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Classification Table (As Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The classification is built on the position of the **Q-point (quiescent operating point)** on the transistor's transfer / load-line characteristics, which directly dictates the conduction angle $\theta_c$.

| Class | Q-Point Location | Conduction Angle $\theta_c$ | Theoretical Max Efficiency $\eta_{max}$ | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Center of load line | 360┬░ | **50%** (transformer-coupled) / 25% (series-fed) | Hi-Fi audio pre-final stages |
| **B** | At cutoff | 180┬░ | **78.5%** ($\pi/4$) | Push-pull audio output |
| **AB** | Slightly above cutoff | 180┬░ < ╬Ė < 360┬░ | Between 50% and 78.5% (typically 60ŌĆō70%) | High-fidelity push-pull audio |
| **C** | Below cutoff | < 180┬░ | **100%** (theoretical) | RF tuned transmitters, IF stages |
| **D** | Switched (sat/cutoff) | Switched (PWM) | **90ŌĆō100%** | Switching audio, DCŌĆōDC converters |

## 2.2 Operating Principle ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Each Class

### 2.2.1 Class A Power Amplifier
- The Q-point is biased at the **midpoint** of the load line so that the output transistor conducts for the **entire input cycle** ($\theta_c = 360┬░$).
- **Why full conduction?** Because the transistor never enters cutoff or saturation during normal signal excursion, the output is a faithful, linear reproduction of the input.
- **The cost:** Even with zero input signal, the transistor draws a large quiescent current $I_{CQ}$, which means continuous DC power is drawn from the supply and dissipated as heat.
- **Why so inefficient?** Since the quiescent current flows 100% of the time, a large portion of the supply power is wasted as heat even when no signal is being amplified. Theoretical maximum efficiency is only **50%** for transformer-coupled (and **25%** for series-fed) configurations.

### 2.2.2 Class B Power Amplifier
- The Q-point is biased exactly at the **cutoff** boundary, so the transistor conducts for **only half** the input cycle ($\theta_c = 180┬░$).
- **Why only half?** By eliminating the quiescent current, the no-signal DC power consumption drops to zero ŌĆö a huge efficiency win.
- **The cost:** A single-ended Class B amplifier produces severe **half-wave** distortion. Hence, Class B is *always* used in a **push-pull** configuration (two complementary transistors, each handling one half-cycle), and the two halves are recombined at the load.
- **Why efficiency 78.5%?** Because each transistor conducts for only half the cycle, the *average* DC current drawn from the supply is the *average* of a half-sine ŌĆö which is $I_m/\pi$. The AC power delivered to the load is $I_m^2 R_L / 2$, and the ratio yields exactly $\pi/4 \approx 0.785$ or **78.5%**.

### 2.2.3 Class AB Power Amplifier
- The Q-point is biased **slightly above cutoff** so the conduction angle lies between 180┬░ and 360┬░ (typically 200┬░ŌĆō220┬░).
- **Why this compromise?** It eliminates the **crossover distortion** (the dead zone near zero-crossing) inherent in Class B push-pull, while still retaining most of the efficiency benefit.
- **KTU Focus:** Most real-world audio power amplifiers (e.g., op-amp output stages, audio amplifier ICs like LM3886, TDA2050) operate in Class AB.

### 2.2.4 Class C Power Amplifier
- The Q-point is biased **well below cutoff** so the transistor conducts for **less than 180┬░** of the cycle (typically 80┬░ŌĆō120┬░).
- **Why such short conduction?** It maximizes efficiency ŌĆö the transistor is "off" most of the time, so very little DC power is wasted.
- **The catch:** The output is a series of narrow current pulses, severely distorted. A **tuned (LC) load** is used to reconstruct (ring) a sinusoidal waveform at the resonant frequency.
- **Application:** RF power amplifiers in transmitters, where the signal is naturally sinusoidal at a single carrier frequency, and distortion is filtered by the tuned circuit.

### 2.2.5 Class D Power Amplifier (Switching Mode)
- The transistor operates as a **switch** ŌĆö either fully ON (saturation) or fully OFF (cutoff) ŌĆö never in the active region.
- The output is a **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM)** square wave, which is then low-pass filtered to recover the audio.
- **Why this works:** When the transistor is in saturation, $V_{CE}$ is nearly zero (so $P = V \cdot I \approx 0$); when cutoff, $I_C = 0$ (so again $P \approx 0$). Power is only dissipated during the brief switching transitions.
- **Efficiency:** Practically **90ŌĆō95%**, theoretically approaching 100%.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you need for KTU 2024 Module 4 numerical and derivation problems. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of literal `|` symbols to keep the markdown table parser happy.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}}$ | Dimensionless (or %) |
| 2 | Power Dissipated by transistor | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | **Watts (W)** |
| 3 | Class A max $\eta$ (transformer coupled) | $\eta_{max} = 50\% = 0.5$ | At $V_{CE,\max} = 2 V_{CC}$ |
| 4 | Class A max $\eta$ (series fed) | $\eta_{max} = 25\% = 0.25$ | $P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2}$ |
| 5 | Class B max $\eta$ (push-pull) | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$ | Theoretical limit |
| 6 | Class B ŌĆō DC supply power | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot V_{CC} \cdot I_m$ | $I_m$ = peak load current |
| 7 | Class B ŌĆō AC output power | $P_{AC} = \dfrac{I_m^2 \cdot R_L}{2}$ | RMS-based |
| 8 | Class C theoretical $\eta_{max}$ | $\eta_{max} = 100\%$ (unreachable in practice) | Practical 80ŌĆō90% |
| 9 | Conduction angle relationship | $\theta_c = 2 \cdot \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{\vert V_{BB} \vert}{V_{im}}\right)$ | For Class AB/C with bias $V_{BB}$ |
| 10 | Power across load (general) | $P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$ | $V_m$ = peak AC voltage |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power amplifier classification is not just an academic exercise ŌĆö it directly drives **device selection** in industry:

- **Class A** is reserved for **high-fidelity, low-power** applications (e.g., headphone amplifiers, audiophile pre-amps) where linearity trumps efficiency.
- **Class B / AB** dominate **commercial audio** (car stereos, home theaters, PA systems) ŌĆö the sweet spot of acceptable distortion and high efficiency.
- **Class C** is the workhorse of **RF transmitters** (AM/FM broadcast, radar, two-way radios) where the load is a tuned circuit.
- **Class D** is the modern choice for **battery-powered devices** (smartphones, Bluetooth speakers, EVs) where every milliwatt of efficiency translates to longer battery life.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Real-World Linkage:** The 2024 syllabus emphasizes that students should be able to *justify* the choice of class for a given application. In ESE answers, always pair your class selection with a *quantitative efficiency number* and a *qualitative application example*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is written out explicitly. No step is skipped. This is exactly the level of detail expected in a KTU 14-mark derivation question.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Maximum Efficiency of Class A Series-Fed Amplifier

**Given circuit:** A single transistor with collector resistor $R_C$ connected to $V_{CC}$ (no output transformer). The Q-point is biased at the midpoint of the load line.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify quiescent current.**
With the Q-point at the midpoint of the DC load line, the quiescent collector current is:
$$I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö DC power drawn from the supply.**
The DC power consumed from the supply is the supply voltage times the *average* (DC) collector current. Since the quiescent current is constant:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_C}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Maximum AC power delivered to the load.**
The maximum AC voltage swing on the collector is $V_m = V_{CC} / 2$ (limited by the Q-point at half the supply). The AC power into the equivalent load is:
$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{(V_{CC}/2)^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Conversion efficiency (general).**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2 / 8 R_L}{V_{CC}^2 / 2 R_C} = \dfrac{R_C}{4 R_L}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Maximum efficiency condition.**
Maximum efficiency is reached when $R_L = R_C$ (matched load for maximum power transfer). Substituting:
$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{R_C}{4 \cdot R_C} = \dfrac{1}{4} = 0.25 = \mathbf{25\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The series-fed Class A amplifier can never exceed **25%** efficiency, because half the supply voltage is wasted as a *DC drop* across $R_C$ even at zero signal. This is why transformer-coupled Class A is preferred ŌĆö it recovers this DC drop.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Maximum Efficiency of Transformer-Coupled Class A Amplifier

**Step 1 ŌĆö DC power from supply.**
In transformer-coupled operation, the DC resistance of the transformer primary is negligible. The DC current drawn is the quiescent current $I_{CQ}$, and the DC power is:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum AC power into the reflected load.**
The transformer reflects the load $R_L$ as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ at the collector. The AC voltage swing on the collector can reach from $0$ to $2 V_{CC}$ (since the DC drop on the transformer primary is essentially zero). So the peak AC voltage is:
$$V_m = V_{CC}$$

The maximum AC power is:
$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L'} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / R_L'$** (since $R_L' = V_{CC} / I_{CQ}$ at the matched Q-point).
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the efficiency.**
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L')}{V_{CC}^2 / R_L'} = \dfrac{1}{2} = \mathbf{50\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Transformer coupling doubles the maximum theoretical efficiency of Class A from 25% to **50%** by removing the DC voltage drop across the collector resistor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Maximum Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier (THE KTU FAVOURITE)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Current waveform analysis.**
In a Class B push-pull amplifier, each transistor conducts for exactly 180┬░. The output current is therefore a **full sine wave** with peak amplitude $I_m$ across the load $R_L$. The *DC component* of a full-wave-rectified sine (i.e., a sine wave's average absolute value) is:
$$I_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \dfrac{2 I_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö DC power drawn from each supply.**
Each half of the push-pull pair draws from a $+V_{CC}$ or $-V_{CC}$ rail. The total DC power from both supplies combined is:
$$P_{DC} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{2 I_m}{\pi} = \dfrac{4 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

But by convention, in the standard form, since each rail supplies half the average:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC,\text{total}} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö AC power delivered to the load.**
The output is a full sine wave of peak $I_m$, so:
$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Maximum efficiency condition.**
For maximum power transfer, the peak current is $I_m = V_{CC} / R_L$. Substituting into $P_{AC}$:
$$P_{AC, max} = \dfrac{(V_{CC} / R_L)^2 \cdot R_L}{2} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

The corresponding DC power is:
$$P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Compute maximum efficiency.**
$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{P_{AC, max}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi R_L)} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = \mathbf{78.5\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Class B push-pull achieves **78.5%** (i.e., $\pi/4$) maximum theoretical efficiency, almost 30 percentage points higher than transformer-coupled Class A. This is the key reason Class B (and its variant AB) dominates audio power amplifier design.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö Power Dissipation in Class B Amplifier

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total power dissipation.**
$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} - \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute $V_{CC} = I_m R_L$ to express in terms of $I_m$ alone.**
$$P_D = \dfrac{2 I_m^2 R_L}{\pi} - \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2} = I_m^2 R_L \left(\dfrac{2}{\pi} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Find $I_m$ at which $P_D$ is maximum.**
Differentiate $P_D$ with respect to $I_m$ and set to zero:
$$\dfrac{dP_D}{dI_m} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - I_m R_L = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow I_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute back to find $P_{D,max}$.**
$$P_{D,max} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}\right)^2 R_L$$

$$= \dfrac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \dfrac{2}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L}$$

$$\boxed{P_{D,max} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx 0.2026 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This result tells the designer the *worst-case heat sink requirement*. Power dissipation is **maximum not at full output**, but at $I_m = 2 V_{CC} / (\pi R_L)$ ŌĆö roughly 63.7% of the peak output current. A common exam trap!

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Last-Minute Revision)

| Parameter | Class A (Transformer) | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class AB | Class C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction angle $\theta_c$ | 360┬░ | 180┬░ | 180┬░ŌĆō360┬░ | < 180┬░ |
| Q-point | Center of load line | At cutoff | Slightly above cutoff | Below cutoff |
| $\eta_{max}$ (theoretical) | 50% | 78.5% | 50ŌĆō78.5% | ~100% (theoretical) |
| Linearity | Excellent | Good (push-pull) | Very Good | Poor (tuned only) |
| Crossover distortion | None | Yes (severe) | Minimized | N/A |
| Typical application | Hi-Fi audio | Audio output | Commercial audio | RF transmitters |
| Heat sink needed? | Large | Medium | Medium | Small |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric Mermaid node IDs** (e.g., `classA`, `thetaC`) and **plain uppercase text** inside node labels ŌĆö no bold, italics, or markdown inside the labels. This is to ensure clean compilation.

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    PA[POWER AMPLIFIER CLASSIFICATION] --> CONDUCTION[BY CONDUCTION ANGLE]
    PA --> BIAS[BY Q-POINT LOCATION]
    PA --> MODE[BY OPERATING MODE]

    CONDUCTION --> CLASS_A[CLASS A: THETA C = 360 DEG]
    CONDUCTION --> CLASS_B[CLASS B: THETA C = 180 DEG]
    CONDUCTION --> CLASS_AB[CLASS AB: 180 LT THETA C LT 360 DEG]
```
