```mermaid
    CONDUCTION --> CLASS_C[CLASS C: THETA C LT 180 DEG]
    CONDUCTION --> CLASS_D[CLASS D: SWITCHED MODE PWM]

    BIAS --> MID[Q AT MID LOAD LINE]
    BIAS --> CUT[Q AT CUTOFF]
    BIAS --> ABV[Q SLIGHTLY ABOVE CUTOFF]
    BIAS --> BLW[Q BELOW CUTOFF]

    MID --> CLASS_A
    CUT --> CLASS_B
    ABV --> CLASS_AB
    BLW --> CLASS_C

    MODE --> LIN[LINEAR MODE]
    MODE --> SW[SWITCHING MODE]
    LIN --> CLASS_A
    LIN --> CLASS_B
    LIN --> CLASS_AB
    LIN --> CLASS_C
    SW --> CLASS_D

    CLASS_A --> EFF_A[ETA MAX 50 PERCENT]
    CLASS_B --> EFF_B[ETA MAX 78.5 PERCENT]
    CLASS_AB --> EFF_AB[ETA MAX 60 TO 70 PERCENT]
    CLASS_C --> EFF_C[ETA MAX UP TO 100 PERCENT]
    CLASS_D --> EFF_D[ETA MAX 90 TO 95 PERCENT]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Class B Push-Pull Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[INPUT SIGNAL] --> SPLIT[PHASE SPLITTER OR COMPLEMENTARY DRIVER]
    SPLIT --> TOP[NPN TRANSISTOR Q1 CONDUCTS POSITIVE HALF CYCLE]
    SPLIT --> BOT[PNP TRANSISTOR Q2 CONDUCTS NEGATIVE HALF CYCLE]
    TOP --> JOIN[OUTPUT NODE LOAD CONNECTION]
    BOT --> JOIN
    JOIN --> LOAD[SPEAKER OR ANTENNA RL]

    VCCP[DC SUPPLY POSITIVE VCC] --> TOP
    VCCN[DC SUPPLY NEGATIVE VEE] --> BOT

    TOP -. CONDUCTION 180 DEG .-> TOPNOTE[Q1 ON FOR 0 TO PI]
    BOT -. CONDUCTION 180 DEG .-> BOTNOTE[Q2 ON FOR PI TO 2PI]
```

## 4.3 Conduction Angle Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    CYCLE[FULL INPUT CYCLE 360 DEG] --> QPOS[POSITION OF Q POINT]
    QPOS --> MIDPT[MIDPOINT OF LOAD LINE]
    QPOS --> ATCUT[AT CUTOFF]
    QPOS --> NEARC[NEAR BUT ABOVE CUTOFF]
    QPOS --> DEEPC[DEEP BELOW CUTOFF]

    MIDPT --> CA[CLASS A CONDUCTS ALL 360 DEG]
    ATCUT --> CB[CLASS B CONDUCTS 180 DEG]
    NEARC --> CAB[CLASS AB CONDUCTS 200 TO 220 DEG]
    DEEPC --> CC[CLASS C CONDUCTS 80 TO 120 DEG]

    CA --> EA[EFFICIENCY MAX 50 PERCENT]
    CB --> EB[EFFICIENCY MAX 78.5 PERCENT]
    CAB --> EAB[EFFICIENCY 60 TO 70 PERCENT]
    CC --> EC[EFFICIENCY UP TO 100 PERCENT]
```

## 4.4 Conduction Waveform Schematic (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SINE[INPUT SINE WAVE 0 TO 360 DEG]
        S1[0 TO 180: POSITIVE HALF]
        S2[180 TO 360: NEGATIVE HALF]
    end

    subgraph CLA[CLASS A OUTPUT]
        A1[CONDUCTS FULL 0 TO 360]
    end

    subgraph CLB[CLASS B OUTPUT Q1]
        B1[CONDUCTS 0 TO 180 ONLY]
    end

    subgraph CLB2[CLASS B OUTPUT Q2]
        B2[CONDUCTS 180 TO 360 ONLY]
    end

    subgraph CLC[CLASS C OUTPUT]
        C1[CONDUCTS 60 TO 120 SHORT BURST]
    end

    SINE --> CLA
    SINE --> CLB
    SINE --> CLB2
    SINE --> CLC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Set A]**
*What is meant by the "conduction angle" of a power amplifier? How does it classify amplifiers into different classes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The conduction angle $\theta_c$ is the angular portion of the input signal cycle (out of 360┬░) during which the output transistor conducts and collector current flows. It is measured in degrees or radians.
- (1 mark) It is the primary basis of classification: a Class A amplifier has $\theta_c = 360┬░$, Class B has $\theta_c = 180┬░$, Class AB has $180┬░ < \theta_c < 360┬░$, and Class C has $\theta_c < 180┬░$.
- (1 mark) The conduction angle is set by the position of the Q-point on the load line. A Q-point at the centre gives full conduction, at cutoff gives half conduction, etc. Efficiency increases as conduction angle decreases, but distortion also increases.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Jun 2024, Set B]**
*Compare Class A and Class B power amplifiers in terms of (i) position of operating point, (ii) efficiency, and (iii) distortion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Class A | Class B |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Q-point | Centre of load line | At cutoff |
| (ii) Efficiency | Max 50% (transformer), 25% (series) | Max 78.5% (push-pull) |
| (iii) Distortion | Minimum (linear) | Crossover distortion in single-ended; eliminated in push-pull |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its maximum conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **Class B push-pull power amplifier** with a complementary symmetry configuration. Derive the expression for its maximum efficiency and the maximum power dissipation per transistor. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Class A Transformer-Coupled (7 marks)

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier uses an audio transformer at the collector of the transistor. The primary of the transformer is connected between $V_{CC}$ and the collector; the secondary drives the load $R_L$ (e.g., a speaker). The DC resistance of the primary is negligible, so virtually no DC voltage is dropped across it ŌĆö this is the key advantage over series-fed Class A.

**Operation (1 mark):**
- The Q-point is set at the centre of the AC load line by appropriate base bias ($R_1$, $R_2$, $R_E$).
- The AC load reflected to the primary is $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, where $n$ is the turns ratio.
- The output voltage swing on the collector can vary from near $0$ to nearly $2 V_{CC}$ (full utilization of the supply).

**Derivation of maximum efficiency (4 marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö DC power drawn from supply:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Maximum AC power at the collector (with $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / R_L'$):
$$P_{AC, max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Conversion efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L')}{V_{CC}^2 / R_L'} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\%}$$

This is achieved when the signal swing drives the collector from saturation (Ōēł0 V) to cutoff (Ōēł$2 V_{CC}$).

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Class B Push-Pull Complementary (7 marks)

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A Class B complementary symmetry push-pull amplifier uses an NPN transistor $Q_1$ and a PNP transistor $Q_2$ connected in series between $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ rails, with the load $R_L$ at the common emitter node. No bias is applied to the bases (or only a small $V_{BE}$ bias to avoid crossover distortion in AB).

**Operation (1 mark):**
- During the **positive half-cycle** of the input, $Q_1$ (NPN) is forward biased and conducts; $Q_2$ (PNP) is reverse biased and is OFF.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, $Q_2$ conducts and $Q_1$ is OFF.
- The two half-cycles are recombined at the load to reconstruct a full sine wave.

**Derivation of maximum efficiency (3 marks):**

Step 1 ŌĆö Average DC current drawn:
$$I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 I_m}{\pi}$$

Step 2 ŌĆö DC power from supply:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

Step 3 ŌĆö AC output power:
$$P_{AC} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$$

Step 4 ŌĆö At maximum, $I_m = V_{CC} / R_L$:
$$P_{AC, max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}, \quad P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$$

**Maximum power dissipation per transistor (1 mark):**
$$P_{D,max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx 0.101 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \quad \text{(per transistor)}$$

(Or $2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L)$ total for the pair, divided by 2 per transistor.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Define **conversion efficiency** of a power amplifier. With neat sketches, explain the classification of power amplifiers based on the position of the operating point and the conduction angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **(i)** maximum conversion efficiency and **(ii)** maximum power dissipation of a Class B push-pull power amplifier. Hence, calculate the maximum AC power delivered to a 8 ╬® load if the supply rails are ┬▒15 V. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Classification by Operating Point (7 marks)

**Definition of Conversion Efficiency (1 mark):**
Conversion efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of AC signal power delivered to the load ($P_{AC}$) to the DC power drawn from the supply ($P_{DC}$):
$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$$

**Classification (6 marks):**
- **Class A:** Q-point at the **middle** of the load line. Transistor conducts for the **full 360┬░** of the input cycle. $\eta_{max} = 50\%$ (transformer-coupled). Low distortion.
- **Class B:** Q-point at the **cutoff** point. Transistor conducts for **180┬░** only. $\eta_{max} = 78.5\%$ in push-pull configuration. Suffers from crossover distortion.
- **Class AB:** Q-point **slightly above cutoff**. Conduction angle between 180┬░ and 360┬░. Compromise between linearity and efficiency. $\eta$ between 50ŌĆō78.5%.
- **Class C:** Q-point **well below cutoff**. Conduction angle < 180┬░ (typically 80┬░ŌĆō120┬░). $\eta$ approaches 100% theoretically, but output is highly distorted; requires a tuned (LC) load. Used in RF applications.
- **Class D:** Transistor operates as a switch (saturation/cutoff only). Output is PWM. $\eta$ of 90ŌĆō95%. Used in modern audio and DCŌĆōDC conversion.

(Each class is 1.2 marks: 0.4 for Q-point, 0.4 for conduction angle, 0.4 for efficiency/application.)

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Class B Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Maximum efficiency (3 marks):** As derived earlier:
$$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$$

**(ii) Maximum power dissipation per transistor (2 marks):**
$$P_{D,max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$

**Numerical calculation (2 marks):**
Given $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $R_L = 8$ ╬®:
$$P_{AC, max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{(15)^2}{2 \times 8} = \dfrac{225}{16} = 14.0625 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{AC, max} \approx 14.06 \text{ W}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing series-fed vs. transformer-coupled Class A efficiency.** Students often write 50% when the question specifies a series-fed (resistor-loaded) configuration. Always check the circuit.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve $P_{D,max}$.** When asked "per transistor" in a push-pull, divide the total by 2. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 3. **Quoting 100% for Class C efficiency.** This is the *theoretical* limit, not the *practical* value. State both ŌĆö examiners love to deduct for ambiguity.
> 4. **Mixing up Class AB and Class B.** Class AB is *not* a synonym for Class B. It has a *small* bias to eliminate crossover distortion; remember the conduction angle is 180┬░ < ╬Ė < 360┬░.
> 5. **Not mentioning crossover distortion in Class B.** Always include this in your answer to show conceptual depth ŌĆö it's a 1-mark differentiator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and read it twice before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Conduction Angle** is the master parameter: A = 360┬░, B = 180┬░, AB = 180┬░ŌĆō360┬░, C < 180┬░.
- [x] **Class A efficiency** = **50%** (transformer) or **25%** (series-fed). Q-point at center.
- [x] **Class B efficiency** = **ŽĆ/4 = 78.5%** (push-pull). Q-point at cutoff.
- [x] **Class AB efficiency** lies between 50% and 78.5%, typically **60ŌĆō70%**.
- [x] **Class C efficiency** = up to **100%** (theoretical); practical 80ŌĆō90%. Requires **tuned load**.
- [x] **Class D** is switching mode (PWM), practical efficiency **90ŌĆō95%**.
- [x] **Crossover distortion** is the bane of Class B; eliminated by Class AB biasing.
- [x] **Maximum power dissipation in Class B** is **NOT at full output** ŌĆö it's at $I_m = 2V_{CC}/(\pi R_L)$, which is ~63.7% of peak.
- [x] **Class C cannot be used for audio** because the output is highly distorted and requires a tuned (resonant) load to reconstruct a sine wave.
- [x] **Push-pull** configuration is mandatory for Class B (and standard for Class AB) ŌĆö two complementary transistors, each handling one half-cycle.
- [x] **Application mapping:** Audio ŌåÆ A/AB; RF transmitters ŌåÆ C; Battery-powered audio ŌåÆ D.
- [x] **Key formula to memorize cold:** $\eta_{max} = \pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$ for Class B push-pull.
- [x] **Key derivation sequence:** Start with $I_{DC} = 2I_m/\pi$, derive $P_{DC}$, derive $P_{AC}$, take the ratio ŌĆö examiner's favourite 7-marker.
- [x] **Units to watch:** $V_{CC}$ in **volts**, $R_L$ in **ohms**, $P$ in **watts**. Never use $k\Omega$ and $V$ together without converting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier configuration in which the active device (BJT/FET) is biased such that the output current flows for the entire **360┬░ of the input cycle** (conduction angle $\theta_c = 360┬░$), and the load is connected to the collector (or drain) through a step-down audio transformer. The transformer's primary winding replaces the collector resistor $R_C$ of a small-signal amplifier, and the secondary winding drives the actual load $R_L$ (typically a loudspeaker of $4\ \Omega$, $8\ \Omega$, or $16\ \Omega$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The transformer performs two simultaneous critical functions ŌĆö (i) it acts as a **DC blocking element** because the DC bias current flows only through the low-resistance primary winding without reaching the load, and (ii) it performs **impedance transformation**, matching the high-impedance collector circuit (hundreds of ohms) to the low-impedance speaker load (a few ohms) for maximum power transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Class A Operation Signature:** The quiescent (Q-) point is set at the **centre of the DC load line** so that both the positive and negative half-cycles of the output voltage swing symmetrically about $V_{CC}$. This is the only amplifier class that is inherently free from *crossover distortion* and is the reason Class A is chosen for high-fidelity (Hi-Fi) audio applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Gear-Box and Flywheel" Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a heavy flywheel (the **loudspeaker load**, $R_L \approx 8\ \Omega$) with your bare hands. You simply cannot deliver rotational energy efficiently because the flywheel's mechanical impedance is far too low compared to your muscle effort. Now, insert a **gear-box** (the **transformer**) between your hands and the flywheel. The gear-box trades *speed for torque*, presenting a *high mechanical impedance* to your hands (collector side) and a *low mechanical impedance* to the flywheel (speaker side).

In exactly the same way, the audio transformer transforms the low-impedance speaker load ($8\ \Omega$) into an equivalent high impedance ($R_L' = n^2 R_L \approx$ several hundred ohms) as seen by the transistor's collector. The transistor now "sees" an impedance it can drive efficiently, and the speaker receives the maximum sinusoidal power through the secondary.

| Mechanical Analogy | Electrical Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Muscle effort (force) | Collector current $i_C$ |
| Hand velocity | Collector-emitter voltage $v_{CE}$ |
| Flywheel (low mech. impedance) | Loudspeaker $R_L$ (low ohmic impedance) |
| Gear-box (turns ratio $n:1$) | Transformer (turns ratio $N_1:N_2 = n$) |
| Effective load on hands | $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ (reflected load) |

### 1.3 Why Transformer Coupling is Used (Physical & Engineering Reasons)

1. **Impedance Matching for Maximum Power Transfer:** The Thevenin equivalent resistance of a transistor's collector circuit is typically in the range of **hundreds of ohms**, while loudspeakers have an impedance of only **4 ╬® to 16 ╬®**. Direct coupling causes severe power-loss. The transformer matches these two values so that $R_L' = n^2 R_L \approx R_{out}$ of the transistor.

2. **DC Isolation between Collector and Load:** The transformer primary winding has only a small DC resistance (the copper wire's ohmic resistance), so the DC voltage drop across it is negligible. This means the **full $V_{CC}$ is available** as the quiescent collector-emitter voltage, allowing the largest possible symmetric swing.

3. **Higher Theoretical Efficiency (50% vs 25%):** The transformer-coupled Class A amplifier can theoretically achieve a maximum collector efficiency $\eta_{max} = 50\%$, twice the value of a series-fed (resistor-loaded) Class A amplifier.

4. **No Coupling Capacitor Needed:** Unlike RC-coupled amplifiers, the transformer inherently blocks DC, eliminating the need for a bulky coupling capacitor and the associated low-frequency phase distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metric:** The maximum theoretical **collector efficiency** of an ideal Class A amplifier is $\eta_{max} = 50\%$, but practical transformer-coupled Class A amplifiers achieve between **35% ŌĆō 45%** due to transformer copper losses, core losses, and the transistor's saturation voltage $V_{CE(sat)} \neq 0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology & DC Operating Point

The canonical circuit consists of a single BJT in CE configuration with the collector tied to one end of the transformer's primary winding (with $N_1$ turns). The other end of the primary connects to the supply $V_{CC}$. The secondary winding (with $N_2$ turns) drives the load $R_L$. The base is biased by a suitable network (commonly a voltage-divider with $R_B$).

**Turns Ratio Definition:**

$$n = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

**Reflected (Reflected/Effective) Load on the Primary:**

$$R_L' = n^2 \cdot R_L = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 R_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Fact:** The DC operating point sees ONLY the small DC resistance of the primary winding, $r_{primary}$, which is typically < 1 ╬®. Therefore, $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$ (since the voltage drop $I_{CQ} \cdot r_{primary}$ is negligible). This is what allows the *huge* output voltage swing from $0$ to $2V_{CC}$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step DC Analysis (Setting the Q-Point)

For maximum **symmetric, undistorted** output swing, the Q-point is set at the **midpoint of the AC load line**, which corresponds to:

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'}$$

The collector-emitter voltage is $V_{CC}$ (minus the small IR drop, taken as zero for an ideal transformer), and the collector current is determined by the transformer's primary DC resistance and the bias network.

**Q-Point Derivation (assuming ideal transformer, $r_{primary} = 0$):**

Applying KVL around the collector loop:
$$V_{CC} - I_{CQ} \cdot r_{primary} - V_{CEQ} = 0$$

For $r_{primary} \approx 0$:
$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$$

The base bias resistor $R_B$ sets $I_{BQ}$, and $I_{CQ} = \beta I_{BQ}$.

### 2.3 AC Load Line Analysis

The **AC load line** is drawn on the $I_C$ ŌĆō $V_{CE}$ characteristic with slope $-1/R_L'$, passing through the Q-point.

**End-points of the AC Load Line:**

- **Saturation point (B):** $V_{CE(min)} = V_{CE(sat)}$ and $I_{C(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_L'}$
- **Cut-off point (A):** $I_{C(min)} = 0$ and $V_{CE(max)} = V_{CC} + I_{CQ} R_L' \approx 2V_{CC}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The output voltage $v_{CE}$ swings from nearly **0 V (saturation)** to nearly **$2V_{CC}$ (cut-off)**. This is a **peak-to-peak swing of $2V_{CC}$**, which is the foundation of the 50% efficiency figure.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table ŌĆö these are the *exact* formulas KTU examiners use in their model answer keys for problems on transformer-coupled Class A power amplifiers.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turns Ratio | $n = N_1/N_2$ | Primary turns ├Ę Secondary turns |
| 2 | Reflected Load | $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ | Seen at primary |
| 3 | Q-Point Voltage | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ | Ideal transformer |
| 4 | Q-Point Current | $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ | For max swing |
| 5 | Peak Output Voltage | $V_{m(out)} = V_{CC}$ | Ideal swing |
| 6 | Peak Collector Current | $I_m = I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ | Symmetric swing |
| 7 | **RMS Output Power** | $P_{o(ac)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$ | Core formula |
| 8 | **DC Input Power** | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$ | Battery draw |
| 9 | **Collector Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{o(ac)}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 50\%$ | **Maximum** |
| 10 | Power Dissipated in Transistor | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{o(ac)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'}$ | At Q-point: $P_{D(max)}$ |
| 11 | Power Delivered to Load | $P_L = \eta_T \cdot P_{o(ac)}$ | $\eta_T$ = transformer efficiency |
| 12 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta \cdot \eta_T$ | Practical $\approx 35\%-45\%$ |
| 13 | Turns Ratio for Match | $n = \sqrt{R_L'/R_L}$ | For impedance matching |
| 14 | Q-Point Dissipation | $P_{DQ} = V_{CEQ} \cdot I_{CQ} = V_{CC}^2/R_L'$ | **Maximum transistor dissipation** occurs at Q-point |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $P_{D(max)}$ (transistor dissipation) with $P_{DC}$ (DC input). The dissipation is maximum **at the Q-point (no signal)**, and it is **half of the DC input** when maximum sinusoidal output is delivered. This is a classic KTU question pattern.

### 2.5 The DC Load Line vs The AC Load Line

| Feature | DC Load Line | AC Load Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Slope** | $-1/r_{primary} \approx 0$ (horizontal, since $r_{primary}$ is tiny) | $-1/R_L' = -1/(n^2 R_L)$ |
| **Q-Point on it** | Yes | Yes (same Q-point) |
| **Voltage Swing** | Cannot describe AC swing | Determines max $V_{CE}$ swing |
| **Maximum $V_{CE}$ excursion** | Limited to $V_{CC}$ | Up to $2V_{CC}$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Audio Amplifiers:** The original application. Vintage hi-fi amplifiers (e.g., Williamson, Mullard 5-10) used transformer-coupled Class A output stages.
2. **RF Power Amplifiers:** At high frequencies, transmission-line transformers (Balun, Guanella) achieve broadband impedance matching.
3. **Modulators:** AM transmitters often used Class A transformer-coupled stages as the modulator for high-fidelity audio.
4. **Servo Motor Drivers:** Where a low-impedance DC motor is driven by a high-impedance source.
5. **Test Equipment:** Audio power test sets use matched transformers for measuring amplifier output into standardized dummy loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation #1: Maximum AC (Output) Power Delivered to the Reflected Load

**Given:** Ideal transformer-coupled Class A amplifier with $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$, $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$, sinusoidal input drives the transistor symmetrically into saturation and cut-off.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the peak output voltage swing.**

Under maximum-signal, undistorted conditions, the collector-emitter voltage swings from $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$ (at saturation) to $2V_{CC}$ (at cut-off). The amplitude (peak value) of the sinusoidal swing about the Q-point is:

$$V_m = V_{CEQ} - V_{CE(sat)} \approx V_{CC} - 0 = V_{CC}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the RMS value of the output voltage.**

The output voltage is sinusoidal with peak $V_m = V_{CC}$. Therefore:

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Express the AC power delivered to the reflected load $R_L'$.**

The AC power (signal power) dissipated in the equivalent AC load is:

$$P_{o(ac)} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R_L'}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Substitute $V_{rms} = V_{CC}/\sqrt{2}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{o(ac)} &= \frac{(V_{CC}/\sqrt{2})^2}{R_L'} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2/2}{R_L'} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_{o(ac),max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'}}$

This is the **maximum sinusoidal output power** the amplifier can deliver to the reflected load $R_L'$ under ideal Class A conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation #2: DC Input Power from the Supply

**Step 1 ŌĆö The DC supply $V_{CC}$ delivers a constant current equal to the Q-point collector current** (assuming the base bias current drawn from $V_{CC}$ is negligible compared to the collector current).

$$I_{DC} = I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Multiply by the supply voltage to obtain the DC input power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} \\[6pt]
&= V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_{DC} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}}$

---

### 3.3 Derivation #3: Maximum Collector Efficiency (The Famous 50% Result)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the definition of collector efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{o(ac)}}{P_{DC}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the expressions derived above.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L')}{V_{CC}^2 / R_L'} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} \cdot \frac{R_L'}{V_{CC}^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\%}$

**Physical Insight:** The "missing" 50% of the DC power is dissipated as heat in the transistor. Even at *maximum* signal output, half the energy supplied by the battery is wasted as heat. This is the fundamental thermodynamic limitation of Class A operation.

---

### 3.4 Derivation #4: Power Dissipated in the Transistor

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply conservation of energy (the supply provides $P_{DC}$; part goes to the load as $P_{o(ac)}$; the rest is dissipated in the transistor).**

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{o(ac)}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the derived values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'} - \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_{D,signal} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} = P_{o(ac),max}}$

**Surprising Conclusion:** At maximum signal output, the transistor dissipates exactly the same power as the load receives! In other words, the transistor acts as a *variable resistor* that absorbs the "other half" of the supply power.

---

### 3.5 Derivation #5: Q-Point Power Dissipation (The Worst-Case Thermal Stress)

**Step 1 ŌĆö At the Q-point (no input signal), all the DC power is dissipated in the transistor** (no AC output). So:

$$P_{D,Q} = P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Compare with the dissipation under full-signal operation.**

$$P_{D,Q} = 2 \times P_{D,signal}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{P_{D,Q} = 2 P_{D,signal} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Implication:** A transformer-coupled Class A power transistor must be rated to dissipate **twice the maximum signal-output power** *continuously at the Q-point* (i.e., with no input). This is the *primary* reason Class A amplifiers require massive heat-sinks. A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Show that the maximum transistor dissipation in a Class A amplifier occurs at the Q-point."*

---

### 3.6 Derivation #6: General (Non-Maximum) Collector Efficiency

Let the peak output voltage be $V_m$ (where $V_m \leq V_{CC}$), so that $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ and the AC power is:

$$P_{o(ac)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2R_L'}$$

The DC input power remains constant (since the Q-point is fixed):

$$P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$$

Therefore, the general efficiency is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{o(ac)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_m^2 / (2R_L')}{V_{CC}^2 / R_L'} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}} \right)^2
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\eta = \dfrac{1}{2} \left( \dfrac{V_m}{V_{CC}} \right)^2 \times 100\%}$

For $V_m = V_{CC}$, this reduces to 50%.

---

### 3.7 Derivation #7: Numerical Worked Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier uses a supply $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$, drives a load $R_L = 8\ \Omega$, and uses a transformer with turns ratio $n = N_1/N_2 = 5$. The transformer efficiency is $\eta_T = 80\%$. Calculate:
(a) The reflected load $R_L'$
(b) The Q-point $I_{CQ}$
(c) The maximum AC power delivered to the reflected load
(d) The DC input power
(e) The maximum collector efficiency
(f) The actual power delivered to the speaker
(g) The overall efficiency

**Solution:**

**(a) Reflected Load:**
$$R_L' = n^2 R_L = (5)^2 \times 8 = 200\ \Omega$$

**(b) Q-point collector current:**
$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} = \frac{20}{200} = 0.1\ \text{A} = 100\ \text{mA}$$

**(c) Maximum AC power into $R_L'$:**
$$P_{o(ac),max} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 200} = \frac{400}{400} = 1\ \text{W}$$

**(d) DC input power:**
$$P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'} = \frac{400}{200} = 2\ \text{W}$$

**(e) Maximum collector efficiency:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

Verification: $\eta = P_{o(ac)}/P_{DC} = 1/2 = 0.5$ Ō£ō

**(f) Actual power delivered to the speaker (8 ╬®):**
$$P_L = \eta_T \cdot P_{o(ac)} = 0.80 \times 1\ \text{W} = 0.8\ \text{W}$$

**(g) Overall efficiency:**
$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{max} \cdot \eta_T = 0.5 \times 0.8 = 0.4 = 40\%$$

---

### 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Configuration | $P_{o(max)}$ | $P_{DC}$ | $\eta_{max}$ | $P_{D,Q}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Series-fed Class A (R-load) | $V_{CC}^2 / 4R_L$ | $2V_{CC}^2/R_L$ | 25% | $V_{CC}^2/R_L$ |
| **Transformer-coupled Class A** | $V_{CC}^2 / 2R_L'$ | $V_{CC}^2/R_L'$ | **50%** | $V_{CC}^2/R_L'$ |
| Class B (push-pull) | $V_{CC}^2 / 2R_L$ | $(2/\pi)V_{CC}^2/R_L$ | 78.5% | 0 (theoretically) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Transformer coupling doubles the efficiency (25% ŌåÆ 50%) by allowing $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ instead of $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$. However, Class B push-pull still wins on efficiency (78.5%) at the cost of *crossover distortion*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Schematic ŌĆö Mermaid Block Diagram

Since the actual circuit involves discrete components with specific values, a high-fidelity **functional block-level architecture** is rendered below, mapping every node, bias path, and signal path.

```mermaid
graph TD
    VSIN[AC Input Source vs = Vi sin wt]
    BIAS[Base Bias Network RB and VBB]
    BJT[NPN BJT in CE Configuration]
    TX1[Transformer Primary N1 turns]
    TX2[Transformer Secondary N2 turns]
    RL[Speaker Load RL = 8 ohm]
    VCC[DC Supply VCC]
    QPNT[Q Point VCEQ equals VCC ICQ equals VCC by RLprime]

    VSIN --> BJT
    BIAS --> BJT
    BJT --> TX1
    TX1 --> TX2
    TX2 --> RL
    VCC --> TX1
    BJT -. Sets .-> QPNT
    TX1 -. Reflects RL to .-> QPNT
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow and Power-Conversion Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    DCIN[DC Supply VCC]
    DCIN --> REG[Regulated DC Bias]
    REG --> QPT[Q Point Setting VCEQ ICQ]
    SIG[AC Input Signal vs]
    SIG --> AMP[Transistor Amplification]
    QPT --> AMP
    AMP --> ACLOAD[AC Power Delivered to RLprime equals VCC squared over 2RLprime]
    AMP --> PDISS[Power Dissipated in Transistor PD equals VCC squared over 2RLprime]
    ACLOAD --> TX[Step Down Transformer n greater than 1]
    TX --> SPK[Speaker Load RL]
    TX -. Has Loss .-> TLOSS[Transformer Efficiency eta T]
    TLOSS --> SPK
```

### 4.3 AC Load Line Geometry ŌĆö Functional Representation

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ACLL[AC Load Line on IC versus VCE Plane]
        A[Cutoff Point VCE equals 2VCC and IC equals 0] --> Q[Q Point VCEQ equals VCC and ICQ equals VCC over RLprime]
        Q --> B[Saturation Point VCE equals Vsat and IC equals VCC over RLprime]
    end
    Q -. Symmetric Swing .-> A
    Q -. Symmetric Swing .-> B
```

### 4.4 Power-Flow Sankey-Style Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    P_DC[DC Input Power equals VCC squared over RLprime] --> P_AC[AC Output Power equals VCC squared over 2RLprime]
    P_DC --> P_D[Transistor Dissipation equals VCC squared over 2RLprime]
    P_AC --> P_L[Load Power equals eta T times P_AC]
    P_AC -. 50 percent of P_DC .-> P_D
    P_D -. 50 percent of P_DC .-> P_D
```

### 4.5 Node-by-Node Functional Description

| Node Label | Function | Mathematical Representation |
|---|---|---|
| `VSIN` | Sinusoidal input signal source | $v_s(t) = V_i \sin(\omega t)$ |
| `BIAS` | Sets the Q-point via $R_B$ and $V_{BB}$ | $I_{BQ} = (V_{BB} - V_{BE})/R_B$ |
| `BJT` | Active amplification device | $i_C = \beta i_B + I_{CEO}$ |
| `TX1` | Primary winding, $N_1$ turns | Impedance: $j\omega L_p$ at AC; $\approx 0$ at DC |
| `TX2` | Secondary winding, $N_2$ turns | Impedance: $j\omega L_s$ at AC; $\approx 0$ at DC |
| `RL` | Loudspeaker (load) | $R_L \in \{4, 8, 16\}\ \Omega$ |
| `VCC` | DC power supply | Typically $12\text{ŌĆō}50$ V |
| `QPNT` | Q-point coordinates | $(V_{CC},\ V_{CC}/R_L')$ |
| `TLOSS` | Transformer power loss | $P_{loss} = (1 - \eta_T) \cdot P_{o(ac)}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In a Mermaid block diagram for this topic, focus on the *flow* of power and signal ŌĆö the input is amplified by the BJT, the AC power is delivered to the *reflected* load $R_L'$ (not $R_L$ directly), and the transformer "steps down" both voltage and impedance to drive the low-impedance speaker.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Why is a transformer used in the collector circuit of a Class A power amplifier?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier** is a large-signal amplifier in which the active device (BJT) is biased in the active region with a conduction angle of **360┬░**, and the load is coupled to the collector through a **step-down audio transformer**. **(1 mark)**

The transformer is used in the collector circuit to perform two critical functions:
1. **Impedance matching** ŌĆö it transforms the low-impedance load $R_L$ (e.g., 8 ╬® speaker) into a high reflected impedance $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ as seen by the collector, enabling maximum power transfer. **(1 mark)**
2. **DC isolation** ŌĆö it blocks the DC bias voltage from reaching the load, allowing $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$ and a large symmetric output voltage swing. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is the maximum theoretical collector efficiency of a Class A transformer-coupled power amplifier? Justify your answer with the relevant expressions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The maximum theoretical collector efficiency of an ideal Class A transformer-coupled amplifier is **50%**. **(1 mark)**

Justification: Under maximum-signal conditions,
$$P_{o(ac),max} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} \quad \text{and} \quad P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$$

Therefore,
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{o(ac),max}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2/(2R_L')}{V_{CC}^2/R_L'} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\% \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Derive the expressions for (i) the maximum output power, (ii) the DC input power, and (iii) the maximum collector efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier operates from a 24 V supply and drives a 16 ╬® speaker through a transformer with a turns ratio of $N_1 : N_2 = 8 : 1$. The transformer efficiency is 0.85. Calculate:
- (i) The reflected load $R_L'$ seen at the primary
- (ii) The Q-point collector current
- (iii) The maximum AC power delivered
- (iv) The DC power drawn from the supply
- (v) The maximum collector efficiency
- (vi) The actual power delivered to the speaker
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Sections 2 and 3 of these notes for the circuit description and the derivations. The expected answer must contain:
- A labelled circuit diagram showing the BJT in CE mode with the transformer's primary in the collector and the secondary across $R_L$. **(1 mark)**
- Explanation of Q-point setting at $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$, $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ for symmetric swing. **(1 mark)**
- Derivation of $P_{o(ac),max} = V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L')$ **(1.5 marks)**
- Derivation of $P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2 / R_L'$ **(1.5 marks)**
- Derivation of $\eta_{max} = 50\%$ **(1 mark)**
- Brief comment on the role of the transformer (impedance matching + DC blocking). **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 24$ V, $R_L = 16\ \Omega$, $n = N_1/N_2 = 8/1 = 8$, $\eta_T = 0.85$.

**(i) Reflected Load (1 mark):**
$$R_L' = n^2 R_L = (8)^2 \times 16 = 64 \times 16 = 1024\ \Omega$$

**(ii) Q-point Collector Current (1 mark):**
$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} = \frac{24}{1024} = 23.44\ \text{mA}$$

**(iii) Maximum AC Power Delivered to $R_L'$ (1.5 marks):**
$$P_{o(ac),max} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L'} = \frac{(24)^2}{2 \times 1024} = \frac{576}{2048} = 0.281\ \text{W} = 281.25\ \text{mW}$$

**(iv) DC Input Power (1 mark):**
$$P_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'} = \frac{576}{1024} = 0.5625\ \text{W} = 562.5\ \text{mW}$$

**(v) Maximum Collector Efficiency (1 mark):**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{o(ac)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{0.281}{0.5625} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**(vi) Actual Power Delivered to Speaker (1.5 marks):**
$$P_L = \eta_T \cdot P_{o(ac),max} = 0.85 \times 281.25\ \text{mW} = 239.06\ \text{mW} \approx 0.239\ \text{W}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum power dissipation in the collector of a Class A transformer-coupled amplifier. Show that this maximum dissipation occurs at the Q-point (with zero signal input). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier with a series-fed Class A amplifier and a Class B push-pull amplifier in terms of (i) maximum theoretical efficiency, (ii) transformer/heat-sink requirement, (iii) crossover distortion, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Maximum Dissipation at Q-Point (7 marks):**

The instantaneous collector dissipation is the difference between the DC input and the AC output power:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{o(ac)}$$

For an arbitrary sinusoidal input with peak collector voltage swing $V_m$ and peak collector current $I_m = V_m/R_L'$:

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}
$$

$$
P_{o(ac)} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2R_L'}
$$

Therefore:
$$P_D = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'} - \frac{V_m^2}{2R_L'} = \frac{1}{R_L'}\left(V_{CC}^2 - \frac{V_m^2}{2}\right)$$

To find the maximum, differentiate with respect to $V_m$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = \frac{1}{R_L'} \cdot \left(-\frac{2V_m}{2}\right) = -\frac{V_m}{R_L'} = 0 \implies V_m = 0$$

Since $\dfrac{d^2 P_D}{dV_m^2} = -\dfrac{1}{R_L'} < 0$, the dissipation is *maximum* at $V_m = 0$ (no signal). The maximum dissipation is:

$$P_{D,max} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L'}$$

This equals the full DC input power, which occurs at the Q-point with no input signal. **(1 mark for the derivative, 1 mark for the conclusion)**

*Mark allocation:* Setting up the equation (1.5), DC input expression (1.5), AC output expression (1.5), differentiation and conclusion (2.5).

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Series-fed Class A | Transformer Class A | Class B Push-Pull |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Max Efficiency** | 25% | **50%** | 78.5% |
| **(ii) Transformer/Heat-sink** | No transformer; large heat-sink needed | Needs bulky audio transformer; huge heat-sink | No transformer; moderate heat-sink |
| **(iii) Crossover Distortion** | **None** (always conducts) | **None** | **Yes** (needs biasing) |
| **(iv) Applications** | Small-signal amplifiers | Vintage hi-fi audio, RF drivers | Modern audio amplifiers, op-amp output stages |

*Mark allocation:* 1.5 marks per row ├Ś 4 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for overall summary comment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing $P_D$ at Q-point with $P_D$ at full signal.** Many students write $P_{D,max} = V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L')$, which is the dissipation *under full signal*, NOT the maximum. The maximum is $P_{D,Q} = V_{CC}^2 / R_L'$ at the Q-point. Examiners *specifically test* this distinction for 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the *reflected* load $R_L' = n^2 R_L$** in numerical problems. Always substitute $R_L'$ (not $R_L$) in the Class A power formulas.
> 3. **Mixing up "collector efficiency" and "overall efficiency".** The collector efficiency (50% max) does NOT include transformer losses. The overall efficiency = $\eta_{collector} \times \eta_{transformer}$.
> 4. **Writing $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$** (a mistake carried over from series-fed analysis). In a *transformer-coupled* Class A, $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ for maximum swing.
> 5. **Not specifying units in numerical answers.** Always write A, V, W, or mA, mV, mW as appropriate ŌĆö losing 0.5 marks for missing units is extremely common.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark sub-parts.** A neat, labelled circuit diagram is worth at least 1 mark and is mandatory.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier**

- **Conduction Angle:** $\theta_c = 360┬░$ (full cycle)
- **Q-point:** $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$, $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ (for max symmetric swing)
- **Reflected Load:** $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ where $n = N_1/N_2$
- **Maximum AC Power:** $P_{o(ac),max} = V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L')$
- **DC Input Power:** $P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2 / R_L'$
- **Maximum Collector Efficiency:** $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\%}$
- **Maximum Transistor Dissipation:** $P_{D,max} = V_{CC}^2 / R_L'$ (at Q-point, *zero signal*)
- **Power Dissipation at Full Signal:** $P_{D,signal} = V_{CC}^2 / (2R_L') = P_{o(ac),max}$
- **Peak-to-Peak $V_{CE}$ Swing:** $2V_{CC}$ (theoretical, ideal transformer)
- **Role of Transformer:** (1) Impedance matching, (2) DC isolation
- **Advantages:** Higher efficiency than series-fed; no coupling capacitor; perfect for low-impedance loads
- **Disadvantages:** Bulky, heavy, expensive transformer; limited high-frequency response; core saturation; hum pickup
- **General Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \frac{1}{2}(V_m/V_{CC})^2$ for any input level
- **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{collector} \cdot \eta_{transformer} \approx 35\%-45\%$ in practice
- **Comparison Anchor:** Series-fed (25%) < Transformer Class A (50%) < Class B (78.5%)
- **KTU Favourite Trick Question:** *"Why is the maximum transistor dissipation at the Q-point?"* ŌĆö Because the AC output power is zero (no signal), and all the DC power is converted to heat.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition: Class B Push-Pull Amplifier > [!NOTE] > **Class B Push-Pull Amplifier (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **Class B push-pull power amplifier** is a two-transistor configuration in which each transistor conducts for exactly **180┬░ (half cycle)** of the input signal ŌĆö one transistor (NPN) amplifies the positive half cycle while the complementary transistor (PNP) amplifies the negative half cycle. The two outputs are combined at the load to reconstruct the full waveform, achieving a theoretical maximum conversion efficiency of **ŽĆ/4 Ōēł 78.5%**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Class AB Power Amplifier (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **Class AB amplifier** is a modified Class B topology where each transistor is biased with a small quiescent collector current ($I_{CQ}$) such that the conduction angle lies between **180┬░ and 360┬░** (typically 200┬░ŌĆō220┬░). This small forward bias eliminates **crossover distortion** inherent to pure Class B operation while retaining most of its high efficiency. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview Imagine a **two-oar rowboat** crossing a river. When the boat must move forward, **Oar A (left oarsman)** rows during the first half of the stroke, and **Oar B (right oarsman)** rows during the second half. Neither oarsman works for the full cycle ŌĆö they alternate. The boat still moves smoothly because the two half-strokes combine into one full stroke. The Class B push-pull amplifier works exactly like this: the **NPN transistor pulls** the load current in one direction during the positive half cycle, and the **PNP transistor pushes** the load current in the opposite direction during the negative half cycle. The output load (a speaker) never sees a moment of zero current ŌĆö it sees a continuous, reconstructed waveform. The problem? When the input signal is very small (near **0.7 V**, the $V_{BE}$ turn-on threshold of a silicon BJT), **neither** transistor conducts. The oars are lifted out of the water for a brief instant ŌĆö this creates a "dead zone" called **crossover distortion**. To fix this, the rowers agree to keep their oars *just touching* the water surface even when idle (a tiny DC bias) ŌĆö this is precisely what the **Class AB** configuration does. > [!NOTE] > **Why Push-Pull?** > The term "push-pull" refers to the bidirectional control of the load current: one device **pushes** current into the load while the complementary device **pulls** current from it. This eliminates the bulky output coupling transformer required in single-ended Class A designs and dramatically improves efficiency. --- ### 1.3 Classification of Power Amplifiers by Conduction Angle | Class | Conduction Angle | Efficiency (Max) | Quiescent Bias | |-------|-----------------|------------------|----------------| | **Class A** | 360┬░ | 25% | $I_{CQ} = I_C/2$ (midpoint) | | **Class B** | 180┬░ | 78.5% (ŽĆ/4) | $I_{CQ} \approx 0$ | | **Class AB** | 180┬░ŌĆō360┬░ | 50%ŌĆō78.5% | Small $I_{CQ}$ | | **Class C** | < 180┬░ | > 78.5% | Reverse biased | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Metric Highlighted:** > - Theoretical max efficiency of Class B: **╬Ę_max = ŽĆ/4 Ōēł 78.5%** > - Practical efficiency range: **60%ŌĆō70%** (accounting for real device losses) > - Crossover distortion threshold: **┬▒V_BE(on) Ōēł ┬▒0.7 V** for silicon BJTs ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 ŌĆö Input Signal Splitting (Implicit)** The input $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied simultaneously to the bases of both $Q_1$ (NPN) and $Q_2$ (PNP) configured in complementary symmetry. No center-tapped transformer or phase-splitter is required when using complementary devices. **Step 2 ŌĆö Device Selection by Half-Cycle** - During the **positive half cycle** ($v_i > +0.7\,\text{V}$): $Q_1$ (NPN) is forward biased and conducts; $Q_2$ (PNP) is reverse biased and cut off. - During the **negative half cycle** ($v_i < -0.7\,\text{V}$): $Q_2$ (PNP) conducts; $Q_1$ is cut off. - During the **dead zone** ($\vert v_i \vert < 0.7\,\text{V}$): both transistors are OFF ŌåÆ **crossover distortion** appears in the output. **Step 3 ŌĆö Load Current Reconstruction** Each transistor delivers a half-sinusoidal current pulse to the load $R_L$. The combined waveform across $R_L$ reconstructs the full sinusoid, minus the small "notch" near zero crossings. **Step 4 ŌĆö DC Biasing Improvement (Class AB)** To eliminate crossover distortion, a small **forward bias** is applied to both bases using diodes, $V_{BE}$ multiplier, or resistorŌĆōdiode bias networks. This ensures both transistors conduct slightly even at $v_i = 0$, placing operation in the Class AB region. --- ### 2.2 Why Class B is Used ŌĆö Engineering Rationale > [!NOTE] > **The "Why" behind push-pull Class B:** > 1. **High efficiency (78.5%)** ŌĆö In Class A, the transistor always carries $I_{CQ}$, wasting power as heat. Class B transistors only conduct when needed. > 2. **No transformer at output** ŌĆö Complementary symmetry eliminates the bulky, lossy output transformer. > 3. **Symmetric clipping** ŌĆö Both half-cycles are amplified equally, producing lower even-order harmonic distortion. > 4. **Better thermal stability** ŌĆö Low quiescent dissipation means smaller heat sinks are required. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essentials for KTU 2024 Scheme.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | DC Supply Power | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{\pi}$ | Watts (W) | | 2 | AC Output Power | $P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$ | Watts (W) | | 3 | Peak Load Current | $I_m = \dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$ | Amperes (A) | | 4 | Max Output Power (ideal) | $P_{AC,max} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | When $V_m = V_{CC}$ | | 5 | Max Conversion Efficiency | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$ | Class B theoretical | | 6 | Conversion Efficiency (general) | $\eta = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$ | Linear in $V_m$ | | 7 | Power Dissipated (per transistor) | $P_{D} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | At max output | | 8 | Power Dissipated (general) | $P_{D} = P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ | Energy balance | | 9 | Total Power Dissipated (both) | $P_{2D} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} - \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$ | Both transistors | | 10 | Crossover Distortion Threshold | $V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ (Si) | Per device | | 11 | Quiescent Current (Class AB) | $I_{CQ} \approx 1\text{ŌĆō}5\,\text{mA}$ | Small, to avoid notch | | 12 | Maximum Transistor Voltage | $V_{CE,max} = 2 V_{CC}$ | When opposite device saturates | > [!WARNING] > **Remember:** In push-pull Class B, the peak voltage across each OFF transistor reaches **2 V_CC** (not just V_CC), because the conducting transistor pulls the load to nearly $-V_{CC}$, and the supply is at $+V_{CC}$. The breakdown voltage rating $V_{(BR)CEO}$ must satisfy $V_{(BR)CEO} \geq 2 V_{CC}$. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio power amplifiers** in Hi-Fi systems, home theaters, and PA systems. - **Motor driver stages** in robotics and industrial automation (bidirectional current drive). - **Op-amp output stages** ŌĆö virtually every op-amp (LM741, TL081, NE5532) uses a Class AB output stage. - **Audio amplifiers in portable devices** ŌĆö Class D is preferred for ultra-low power, but Class AB remains dominant in line-voltage audio. - **RF power amplifiers** in transmitters (operated in Class B or AB for high efficiency with linear modulation). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Conversion Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull **Given:** Complementary symmetry Class B amplifier with supply $\pm V_{CC}$ and load $R_L$, driven by input $v_i = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Write the load current waveform.** Each transistor conducts for one half cycle, so the load current is a full sinusoid: $$i_L(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$ where $I_m = V_m / R_L$ is the peak load current. **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the DC supply current (average value).** The DC supply $+V_{CC}$ delivers current only during the positive half cycle. The average current drawn from the $+V_{CC}$ supply is: $$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} I_m \sin(\theta)\, d\theta = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \bigl[-\cos(\theta)\bigr]_0^{\pi} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{I_m}{\pi}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute total DC power supplied.** Since both supplies ($\pm V_{CC}$) deliver equal currents: $$P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute AC (signal) power delivered to the load.** For a sinusoidal load current, the RMS value is $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$, so: $$P_{AC} = I_{rms}^2 R_L = \frac{I_m^2 R_L}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Define conversion efficiency.** $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L / 2}{2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi I_m R_L}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Find the maximum.** Maximum efficiency occurs when $V_m$ reaches its largest allowable value. In an ideal Class B stage, $V_m \rightarrow V_{CC}$ (no saturation loss), so: $$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result (Board-Exam Gold):** $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum AC Output Power and Power Dissipated **Step 1 ŌĆö Substitute $V_m = V_{CC}$ into the AC power equation.** $$P_{AC,max} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute the DC power at maximum output.** $$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} (V_{CC}/R_L)}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Use energy conservation to find total power dissipated in both transistors.** $$P_{2D} = P_{DC} - P_{AC,max} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$ $$P_{2D} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \left( \frac{2}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{4 - \pi}{2\pi}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Power dissipated per transistor (by symmetry, each device handles half).** $$P_D = \frac{P_{2D}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{4 - \pi}{2\pi} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 (4 - \pi)}{4 \pi R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$$ (using the approximation $(4 - \pi)/4 \approx 1/\pi$, which is exact to 3 decimal places) **Step 5 ŌĆö Critical insight: Power dissipation is NOT maximum at full output!** Taking the derivative of $P_{2D}$ with respect to $V_m$ and setting it to zero: $$\frac{dP_{2D}}{dV_m} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_m}{R_L} = 0 \implies V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$$ $$P_{2D,max} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} \approx 0.4 \cdot P_{AC,max}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Board Exam Insight:** > Maximum transistor dissipation occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$, NOT at full output. Heat sinks must be sized for this worst-case condition! --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Class AB Bias Point ŌĆö Diode Bias Network **Given:** A diode-biased Class AB complementary output stage with $V_{CC} = \pm 15\,\text{V}$, $R_L = 8\,\Omega$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the quiescent output voltage.** With perfect symmetry, $V_O(Q) = 0\,\text{V}$. **Step 2 ŌĆö Define the bias requirement.** To keep both $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ in slight conduction at idle, the voltage between their bases must be: $$V_{B1} - V_{B2} = V_{BE1}(on) + V_{BE2}(on) = 2 V_{BE} \approx 1.4\,\text{V}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Diode bias network equations.** Two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ in series between $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ drop approximately $2 \times 0.7 = 1.4\,\text{V}$, perfectly matching the required $V_{B1} - V_{B2}$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Quiescent current setting.** The current through the diodes is set by resistor $R$: $$I_{bias} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CC}}{R + 2 r_D} = \frac{2 V_{CC} - 1.4}{R} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R} \text{ (for } R \gg r_D\text{)}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Thermal tracking.** Since $D_1, D_2$ share the same thermal environment as $Q_1, Q_2$, the diode voltage drop tracks $V_{BE}$ of the transistors, providing excellent **thermal stability** of the quiescent bias. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Class B vs Class AB vs Class A | Parameter | Class A | Class B (Push-Pull) | Class AB | |-----------|---------|---------------------|----------| | Conduction angle | 360┬░ | 180┬░ | 180┬░ŌĆō360┬░ (Ōēł 200┬░) | | Quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ | $I_C/2$ (large) | Ōēł 0 | Small (1ŌĆō5 mA) | | Max efficiency | 25% | 78.5% | 50%ŌĆō78.5% | | Crossover distortion | None | Severe | Negligible | | Output transformer | Required (single-ended) | Not required | Not required | | Heat sink size | Largest | Smallest | Moderate | | Harmonic distortion | Low (single device) | Higher (notch) | Low (biased) | | Typical use | High-fidelity pre-amps | RF, low-cost audio | Audio op-amps, Hi-Fi | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Note:** Class AB is the **practical industry standard** for audio power amplifiers. It is the optimal compromise between efficiency (Class B's strength) and linearity (Class A's strength). --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) **Problem:** A complementary symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier operates from $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 20\,\text{V}$ into a load $R_L = 8\,\Omega$. The input signal amplitude is $V_m = 16\,\text{V}$. Find: (a) Output power, (b) DC power supplied, (c) Efficiency, (d) Power dissipated per transistor. **Solution:** **Part (a) ŌĆö Output power:** $$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(16)^2}{2 \times 8} = \frac{256}{16} = 16\,\text{W}$$ **Part (b) ŌĆö DC power supplied:** $$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{16}{8} = 2\,\text{A}$$ $$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 2}{3.1416} = \frac{80}{3.1416} \approx 25.46\,\text{W}$$ **Part (c) ŌĆö Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{16}{25.46} \approx 0.6285 = 62.85\%$$ **Cross-check with the formula:** $$\eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{16}{20} = 0.7854 \times 0.8 = 0.6283 \approx 62.83\%$$ **Part (d) ŌĆö Power dissipated per transistor:** $$P_{2D} = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 25.46 - 16 = 9.46\,\text{W}$$ $$P_D = \frac{P_{2D}}{2} = 4.73\,\text{W per transistor}$$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Complementary Symmetry Class B Output Stage ```mermaid flowchart TB Vin["Input Signal Vi t"]:::inputNode NPN1["Q1 NPN Transistor
Conducts Positive Half"]:::npnNode PNP1["Q2 PNP Transistor
Conducts Negative Half"]:::pnpNode VCCpos["Plus VCC Supply"]:::supplyNode VCCneg["Minus VCC Supply"]:::supplyNode Load["Load Resistor RL
Speaker or RL"]:::loadNode Vout["Output Voltage Vo t"]:::outputNode Vin --> NPN1 Vin --> PNP1 VCCpos --> NPN1 NPN1 --> Load Load --> PNP1 PNP1 --> VCCneg Load --> Vout classDef inputNode fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef npnNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pnpNode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef supplyNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loadNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Waveform Diagram: Crossover Distortion ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Input Sine Wave Vi t"] IN["Pure Sinusoid
Vi equals Vm sin omega t"]:::waveform end subgraph Crossover["Crossover Dead Zone plus or minus 0.7 V"] X["Notch in Output
Both Transistors OFF"]:::deadzone end subgraph Output["Class B Output Vo t"] OUT["Distorted Sine
with Notch near Zero"]:::waveform end Input --> Crossover Crossover --> Output classDef waveform fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef deadzone fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Class AB Bias Network Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB VCCp["Plus VCC"]:::supply VCCn["Minus VCC"]:::supply BiasRes["Bias Resistor R
Sets Idle Current"]:::bias Diode1["Diode D1
Drops 0.7 V"]:::diode Diode2["Diode D2
Drops 0.7 V"]:::diode Q1NPN["Q1 NPN
Upper Device"]:::npn Q2PNP["Q2 PNP
Lower Device"]:::pnp RLload["Load RL"]:::load VoutAB["Smooth Output
No Crossover Notch"]:::output VCCp --> BiasRes BiasRes --> Diode1 Diode1 --> Diode2 Diode2 --> VCCn Diode1 --> Q1NPN Diode2 --> Q2PNP Q1NPN --> RLload RLload --> Q2PNP RLload --> VoutAB classDef supply fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef bias fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef npn fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pnp fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef load fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Efficiency Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Power Amplifier Input"]:::start ClassA["Class A
360 Degrees Conduction
Max Efficiency 25 percent"]:::classA ClassB["Class B Push Pull
180 Degrees Conduction
Max Efficiency 78.5 percent"]:::classB ClassAB["Class AB
200 to 220 Degrees
Efficiency 50 to 78.5 percent"]:::classAB Result["Best Tradeoff
Class AB Used in Audio"]:::result Start --> ClassA Start --> ClassB Start --> ClassAB ClassAB --> Result classDef start fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classA fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef classAB fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** > **Define crossover distortion in a Class B push-pull amplifier. How is it eliminated?** **Model Answer:** Crossover distortion is the **nonlinear distortion** that appears in the output of a Class B push-pull amplifier near the **zero-crossing region** of the input signal, when the input amplitude is less than the **base-emitter turn-on voltage** ($V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ for silicon). During this interval, neither the NPN nor the PNP transistor conducts, creating a "dead zone" or notch in the output waveform. **Elimination Method:** It is eliminated by operating the amplifier in the **Class AB mode**, where a small forward bias is applied to both transistors using a **diode bias network**, a **$V_{BE}$ multiplier**, or **resistorŌĆōdiode biasing**. This ensures both devices remain slightly conducting at idle, eliminating the dead zone. **(3 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]** > **Why is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a Class B push-pull amplifier 78.5%?** **Model Answer:** The maximum efficiency of a Class B push-pull amplifier is $\pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$ because of the following reasons: 1. Each transistor conducts for only **half cycle (180┬░)**, so quiescent power dissipation is near zero. 2. The DC supply current is the **average of the half-sinusoid**, equal to $I_m/\pi$. 3. At maximum output ($V_m = V_{CC}$), the ratio of **AC power delivered to load** ($V_{CC}^2/2R_L$) to **DC power supplied** ($2V_{CC}^2/\pi R_L$) reduces to $\pi/4$. 4. Unlike Class A, no power is wasted in the transistor at idle, hence the higher theoretical ceiling. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Question A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **complementary symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its **conversion efficiency**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 25\,\text{V}$ and drives a load $R_L = 10\,\Omega$. For an input signal $V_m = 20\,\text{V}$, calculate: (i) Output power, (ii) DC power drawn from supplies, (iii) Conversion efficiency, and (iv) Power dissipated per transistor. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) ŌĆö Theory and Derivation:** A complementary symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier uses a matched pair of **NPN and PNP transistors** ($Q_1$ and $Q_2$) with their collectors connected to the load $R_L$ and their emitters tied to the supplies $\pm V_{CC}$. The input signal $v_i$ drives both bases simultaneously. **Operation:** - For $v_i > +0.7\,\text{V}$: $Q_1$ (NPN) conducts; $Q_2$ is OFF. Current flows from $+V_{CC}$ through $Q_1$ into $R_L$. - For $v_i < -0.7\,\text{V}$: $Q_2$ (PNP) conducts; $Q_1$ is OFF. Current flows from $R_L$ through $Q_2$ into $-V_{CC}$. - The load sees a complete reconstructed sine wave, with a small notch near zero crossings. **Efficiency Derivation (7-step summary):** The DC supply current averaged over one full cycle is $I_{DC} = I_m/\pi$, hence $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC} I_m / \pi$. The RMS load current is $I_{rms} = I_m/\sqrt{2}$, so $P_{AC} = I_m^2 R_L / 2$. $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L / 2}{2 V_{CC} I_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$$ When $V_m = V_{CC}$ (ideal limit): $$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$ **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $V_{CC} = 25\,\text{V}$, $R_L = 10\,\Omega$, $V_m = 20\,\text{V}$. **(i) Output Power:** $$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 10} = \frac{400}{20} = 20\,\text{W}$$ **(ii) DC Power Supplied:** Peak current: $I_m = V_m / R_L = 20 / 10 = 2\,\text{A}$. $$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 25 \times 2}{3.1416} = \frac{100}{3.1416} \approx 31.83\,\text{W}$$ **(iii) Conversion Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{20}{31.83} \approx 0.6284 = 62.84\%$$ **Cross-check:** $$\eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}} = 0.7854 \times \frac{20}{25} = 0.6283 = 62.83\%$$ **(iv) Power Dissipated per Transistor:** Total dissipation: $P_{2D} = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 31.83 - 20 = 11.83\,\text{W}$. Per transistor: $P_D = 11.83 / 2 = 5.92\,\text{W}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Many students forget that **both supplies** contribute to $P_{DC}$, leading to a factor-of-2 error. > 2. The peak inverse voltage across each OFF transistor is **$2V_{CC}$**, not $V_{CC}$ ŌĆö students often specify insufficient breakdown ratings. > 3. **Power dissipation is maximum at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, not at full output** ŌĆö heat sinks must be sized for this worst case! --- > **Question B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Supplementary]** ### Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the **crossover distortion** in a Class B push-pull amplifier. Describe a **diode-bias Class AB** circuit that eliminates it, and discuss its advantages. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Class AB audio amplifier delivers $P_{AC} = 25\,\text{W}$ to a load $R_L = 4\,\Omega$ from supplies $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 18\,\text{V}$. Determine: (i) Required peak load voltage, (ii) DC supply current and DC power, (iii) Efficiency, and (iv) Power loss in each transistor. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) ŌĆö Crossover Distortion and Class AB:** **Crossover Distortion Explanation:** In a pure Class B stage, both transistors are OFF when $\vert v_i \vert < V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$. The output fails to follow the input in this small region, producing a **step-like notch** at every zero crossing. This generates **odd-order harmonics** and degrades audio fidelity. **Diode-Bias Class AB Circuit:** Two diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ are connected in series between $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$, with a current-setting resistor $R$. The voltage drop across the diodes is approximately $2 \times V_D \approx 1.4\,\text{V}$, which matches the required $V_{B1} - V_{B2} = V_{BE1} + V_{BE2}$ needed to keep both transistors in slight conduction. **Thermal Tracking:** Since the diodes are in thermal contact with (or on the same heatsink as) the output transistors, the diode voltage drop tracks $V_{BE}$ with temperature, preventing **thermal runaway** of the quiescent current. **Advantages of Class AB:** 1. **No crossover distortion** (smooth zero crossings). 2. **High efficiency** (50%ŌĆō78.5%, close to Class B). 3. **Good thermal stability** via diode tracking. 4. **No output transformer** required (complementary symmetry). **Part (b) ŌĆö Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $P_{AC} = 25\,\text{W}$, $R_L = 4\,\Omega$, $V_{CC} = 18\,\text{V}$. **(i) Peak Load Voltage:** $$P_{AC} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} \implies V_m = \sqrt{2 P_{AC} R_L} = \sqrt{2 \times 25 \times 4} = \sqrt{200} \approx 14.14\,\text{V}$$ **(ii) DC Supply Current and Power:** $$I_m = \frac{V_m}{R_L} = \frac{14.14}{4} \approx 3.535\,\text{A}$$ $$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{3.535}{3.1416} \approx 1.125\,\text{A}$$ $$P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{DC} = 2 \times 18 \times 1.125 = 40.5\,\text{W}$$ **(iii) Efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{25}{40.5} \approx 0.6173 = 61.73\%$$ **(iv) Power Loss per Transistor:** Total dissipation: $P_{2D} = P_{DC} - P_{AC} = 40.5 - 25 = 15.5\,\text{W}$. Per transistor: $P_D = 15.5 / 2 = 7.75\,\text{W}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Question B):** > 1. Do not assume $V_m = V_{CC}$ ŌĆö it must be **back-calculated from the given output power**. > 2. The "diode bias" must drop exactly **$2 V_{BE}$** to avoid both crossover distortion (if too low) and excess quiescent current (if too high). > 3. The $V_{BE}$ multiplier is an alternative to diode bias that allows **precise, adjustable** quiescent voltage. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Push-Pull Class B & Class AB** - **Class B Conduction:** Each transistor conducts for exactly **180┬░**; quiescent current Ōēł 0. - **Class AB Conduction:** Each transistor conducts for **180┬░ŌĆō360┬░** (typically 200┬░ŌĆō220┬░); small $I_{CQ}$ eliminates crossover distortion. - **Crossover Distortion Cause:** $\vert v_i \vert < V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$ ŌĆö both transistors OFF. - **Crossover Distortion Cure:** Apply a small forward bias (Class AB) using diodes, $V_{BE}$ multiplier, or resistorŌĆōdiode networks. - **Maximum Efficiency of Class B:** $\eta_{max} = \pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$. - **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (\pi/4) \cdot (V_m / V_{CC})$. - **DC Supply Power:** $P_{DC} = (2 V_{CC} I_m)/\pi$. - **AC Output Power:** $P_{AC} = V_m^2 / (2 R_L)$. - **Peak Inverse Voltage:** Each OFF transistor sees $V_{CE} = 2V_{CC}$ ŌĆö choose $V_{(BR)CEO} \geq 2V_{CC}$. - **Max Power Dissipation Point:** $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, NOT at full output ŌĆö critical for heat-sink design. - **Power Dissipated per Device:** $P_D = V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L)$ at maximum output. - **Quasi-Complimentary:** Used when true complementary power BJTs are unavailable; combines a Darlington NPN with a PNP. - **Class AB Practical Use:** Standard output stage in **op-amps, audio amplifiers, and motor drivers**. - **Transformerless Output:** Complementary symmetry removes the bulky output transformer of earlier Class B designs. - **Even Harmonics:** Push-pull topology **cancels even-order harmonics** by symmetry ŌĆö a key advantage. - **Odd Harmonics:** Still present but reduced; further reduced by negative feedback. - **KTU Favorite Question Type:** Derive efficiency, compute power values, and explain crossover distortion elimination.

topics.title: Complementary Symmetry Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Complementary Symmetry Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Complementary Symmetry Power Amplifier?

A **Complementary Symmetry Power Amplifier** is a two-transistor push-pull output stage that uses a pair of matched **npn** and **pnp** transistors (called a *complementary pair*) connected in a single-supply or dual-supply configuration to deliver high power to a load with high efficiency. It eliminates the need for a bulky, costly output transformer, making it ideal for **integrated circuit (IC) design** and modern audio amplifiers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A *Class B* amplifier is one in which the conduction angle of each transistor is exactly **180┬░**, meaning each device amplifies only one half of the input waveform. A *Class AB* amplifier is biased so that each transistor conducts for slightly **more than 180┬░**, eliminating the dead-zone (crossover distortion) present in pure Class B.

## 1.2 The Three Operating Classes ŌĆö A Quick Contrast

| Class | Conduction Angle | Quiescent (DC) Current | Efficiency (Theoretical Max) |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **A** | **360┬░** (full cycle) | High (large $I_{CQ}$) | **25 %** (transformer) / **50 %** (series-fed) |
| **B** | **180┬░** (half cycle) | Zero ($I_{CQ} = 0$) | **78.5 %** ($\pi/4$) |
| **AB** | **180┬░ < ╬Ė < 360┬░** | Small (bias current) | Between **50 % and 78.5 %** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a tandem canoe with two paddlers:**
> - In **Class A**, *both* paddlers paddle continuously for the entire journey, even when the canoe is drifting in calm water ŌĆö exhausting and inefficient.
> - In **Class B**, *only one* paddler strokes at a time, perfectly alternating. They rest completely when it's not their turn ŌĆö efficient, but if the handover is too slow the canoe *stutters* (this is **crossover distortion**).
> - In **Class AB**, both paddlers do a small, gentle stroke near the hand-off to keep the canoe gliding smoothly ŌĆö this tiny overlap eliminates the stutter at the cost of a small amount of extra energy.

## 1.4 Why Use Complementary Symmetry?

- **No output transformer** required ŌåÆ small, light, IC-friendly.
- **High power efficiency** (up to **78.5 %** in Class B, 50ŌĆō70 % in Class AB).
- **Good thermal stability** (zero DC bias current in Class B).
- **Excellent frequency response** (no transformer low-frequency roll-off).

## 1.5 Where Are These Used in the Real World?

- Audio power amplifiers (home theatres, PA systems).
- The final output stage of nearly every **operational amplifier** (e.g., 741's class-AB output).
- Motor drive circuits in robotics.
- RF transmitter final stages in communication systems.
- Headphone amplifiers in portable electronics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever KTU asks *ŌĆ£Why is Class B preferred over Class A for power amplification?ŌĆØ*, the universal answer is: **higher efficiency, no DC quiescent current, less heat dissipation, and smaller heat sinks.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Class B Complementary-Symmetry Circuit (Dual Supply)

The classic dual-supply (┬▒$V_{CC}$) push-pull stage uses an **npn transistor Q1** at the top and a **pnp transistor Q2** at the bottom, both tied at their emitters to the load $R_L$.

### Operating Principle ŌĆö Step by Step

1. **No input signal ($v_i = 0$):**
   - Both bases sit at **0 V**; both transistors are **OFF** (since $V_{BE} = 0$).
   - Quiescent output current $I_{CQ} = 0$, so the quiescent power dissipation is **zero** ŌĆö this is the hallmark of Class B.
   - Output voltage $v_o = 0$.

2. **Positive half-cycle ($v_i > 0$):**
   - Q1 (npn) is forward-biased and conducts; Q2 (pnp) is reverse-biased and OFF.
   - Current flows from **+$V_{CC}$** through Q1, into the load, returning to ground.
   - $v_o = v_i - V_{BE1}$ (follows the input, shifted by ~0.7 V).

3. **Negative half-cycle ($v_i < 0$):**
   - Q2 (pnp) conducts; Q1 is OFF.
   - Current flows from **ground** up through the load, then through Q2, returning to **ŌłÆ$V_{CC}$**.
   - $v_o = v_i + V_{BE2}$ (offset by ~0.7 V).

> [!NOTE]
> The output is a faithful, push-pull reproduction of the input across the load ŌĆö one transistor "pushes" current, the other "pulls" it.

## 2.2 The Crossover Distortion Problem

Because a silicon BJT needs about **$V_{BE} \approx 0.6 - 0.7 \text{ V}$** to start conducting, a region of the input signal within $|v_i| < V_{BE}$ fails to turn on *either* transistor. The result is a **dead zone** at every zero-crossing.

This non-linearity is called **Crossover Distortion** ŌĆö the most heavily-tested drawback of Class B in KTU papers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** "Crossover distortion happens because the transistors are too slow." Ō£Ś
> **Correct:** Crossover distortion is caused by the **non-zero $V_{BE}$ turn-on threshold** of the BJTs, *not* by switching speed.

## 2.3 The Class AB Solution ŌĆö Diode / $V_{BE}$-Multiplier Biasing

To kill the dead zone, we **pre-bias** both bases by a small voltage $V_{BB}$ (typically $1.2 - 1.4$ V, i.e., two diode drops). Now both transistors are *just* ON at quiescence, each conducting a small idle current $I_{CQ}$.

The three most common biasing schemes are:

1. **Diode biasing** ŌĆö two forward-biased diodes between the bases.
2. **$V_{BE}$-multiplier (rubber diode)** ŌĆö a transistor + resistor network that generates a precise, temperature-stable $V_{BB}$.
3. **Constant-current source biasing** ŌĆö uses a current mirror for high PSRR.

With this small bias, the conduction angle becomes **slightly greater than 180┬░** ŌåÆ the **Class AB** region.

## 2.4 Class B with Single Supply (Quasi-Complementary)

For a single $+V_{CC}$ supply, the load is **AC-coupled** via a large electrolytic capacitor $C_{out}$. The output quiescent voltage is held at $V_{CC}/2$ by a bias network. This is the topology used in virtually every audio amplifier IC.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use $\vert$ or \mid in tables ŌĆö never the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ inside cell text.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Peak output voltage (max) | $V_m(\max) = V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | Limited by saturation; ideally $\approx V_{CC}$ |
| 2 | Peak load current | $I_m = \dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$ | Sinusoidal drive |
| 3 | **Average DC supply current** | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{2\,I_m}{\pi}$ | Each supply delivers half-wave average |
| 4 | **Total DC power drawn** | $P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$ | Both supplies combined |
| 5 | **AC power delivered to load** | $P_{AC} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$ | RMS power in $R_L$ |
| 6 | **Maximum AC power** | $P_{AC}(\max) = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | When $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 7 | **Maximum DC power at full output** | $P_{DC}(\max) = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$ | Plugging $I_m = V_{CC}/R_L$ |
| 8 | **Overall efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC}}{P_{DC}}$ | Expressed in % |
| 9 | **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class B** | $\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5 \,\%$ | Reached when $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 10 | Power dissipated in *each* transistor (avg) | $P_{Q} = \dfrac{P_{DC} - P_{AC}}{2}$ | Equal split between Q1 and Q2 |
| 11 | Maximum transistor dissipation | $P_{Q(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | Occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ |
| 12 | Class AB conduction angle | $180^\circ < \theta < 360^\circ$ | Adjustable via $V_{BB}$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **78.5 %** theoretical ceiling dictates that *at least* **21.5 %** of input power must always be dissipated as heat ŌĆö driving the design of heat sinks.
- Crossover distortion forces almost every commercial amplifier to use **Class AB** (a small efficiency penalty for a massive linearity gain).
- The complementary pair is the building block of the **CMOS inverter** in digital logic ŌĆö same topology, different operating region.
