> **Negative Feedback Principle:** This is a classic closed-loop negative feedback system. The op-amp has *very high open-loop gain* $A_{OL}$ (typically $10^5$), which forces the closed-loop gain to be set entirely by the feedback network ŌĆö guaranteeing $V_{out}$ is independent of $A_{OL}$, temperature, and transistor $\beta$ variations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The table below uses `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid breaking KTU markdown rendering. **Memorize these equations verbatim.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampled feedback voltage | $V_{fb} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Op-amp non-inverting input; $V_{fb} = V_Z$ at equilibrium |
| 2 | Regulated output voltage | $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | Assumes ideal op-amp with $A_{OL} \to \infty$ |
| 3 | Output (simple BJT version) | $V_{out} = V_Z + V_{BE}$ | Used when no op-amp; $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V for Si BJT |
| 4 | Line regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \approx \dfrac{1}{A_{OL} \cdot \beta_{fb}}$ | $\beta_{fb} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ feedback factor |
| 5 | Load regulation | $LDR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} \approx \dfrac{r_{o,pass}}{A_{OL} \cdot \beta_{fb}}$ | $r_{o,pass}$ = output resistance of pass transistor |
| 6 | Closed-loop output impedance | $Z_{out,cl} = \dfrac{Z_{out,ol}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$ | Standard feedback theory result |
| 7 | Power dissipation (pass element) | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | Critical for thermal design / heat sink sizing |
| 8 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \cdot 100\%$ | Maximum when $V_{in} \approx V_{out} + V_{DO}$ (LDO mode) |
| 9 | Dropout voltage | $V_{DO} = V_{CE,sat} \approx 0.2$ŌĆō$2$ V | For LDO, $V_{DO} \approx V_{DS,sat} \approx 0.1$ŌĆō$0.5$ V |
| 10 | Zener current | $I_Z = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$ | Must satisfy $I_{Z,min} < I_Z < I_{Z,max}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Series regulators are the **workhorse** of:
- **On-board local regulation** on PC motherboards (LM7805, LM7812 ŌĆö classic series IC regulators).
- **Low-Dropout (LDO)** regulators in battery-powered mobile devices (e.g., Qualcomm PMIC LDOs).
- **Reference voltage rails** in ADCs, DACs, and op-amp power supply pins.
- **Post-regulation** stage after a switching pre-regulator, combining the *efficiency* of SMPS with the *low noise* of linear regulation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Linear regulators are inherently **inefficient** when $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ is large. For example, regulating **12 V ŌåÆ 3.3 V at 1 A** wastes $(12-3.3) \times 1 = 8.7$ W as heat. This is why LDO design (minimizing $V_{DO}$) and hybrid SMPS+LDO architectures dominate modern power design.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage Expression for Op-Amp Controlled Series Regulator

### Circuit Setup
- A non-inverting op-amp error amplifier has its non-inverting input tied to $V_Z$.
- The inverting input is connected to the **node between $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom)**, where $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a divider across $V_{out}$.
- The op-amp output drives the **base of the NPN pass transistor** $Q_1$ whose emitter is $V_{out}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We apply the **virtual short principle** at the op-amp inputs. Because the op-amp has negative feedback and infinite open-loop gain, the voltage at both inputs must be equal in the steady state.

$$V_{-} = V_{+} = V_Z$$

The inverting input voltage $V_{-}$ is set by the resistive divider formed by $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$V_{-} = V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Equating the two expressions for $V_{-}$ (using virtual short):

$$V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z$$

Multiplying both sides by $(R_1 + R_2)$:

$$V_{out} \cdot R_2 = V_Z \cdot (R_1 + R_2)$$

Dividing by $R_2$:

$$V_{out} = V_Z \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$. Note that the output is **independent of $\beta$ of the pass transistor** and **independent of the op-amp open-loop gain** ŌĆö a key advantage of feedback regulation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Line Regulation (Closed-Loop)

### Setup
Define the **feedback factor** $\beta_{fb} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ and the **open-loop gain** $A_{OL}$ of the error amplifier (which includes the transconductance of the pass transistor).

### Derivation

In an open-loop configuration, any change $\Delta V_{in}$ appears across the pass transistor's $V_{CE}$, and a fraction $1$ is transmitted to $V_{out}$. With the feedback loop closed, the closed-loop gain from $V_{in}$ to $V_{out}$ becomes:

$$A_{cl} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$$

The change in output for a small change in input is:

$$\Delta V_{out} = A_{cl} \cdot \Delta V_{in} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}} \cdot \Delta V_{in}$$

The line regulation is defined as:

$$LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \dfrac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$$

For $A_{OL} \beta_{fb} \gg 1$ (the standard design condition):

$$LR \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta_{fb}} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Paradox resolution:** Although the closed-loop gain from $V_{in}$ to $V_{out}$ is approximately $\dfrac{1}{\beta_{fb}}$, this number is **typically much less than 1** because the op-amp's *output* drives the pass transistor, which itself has an *internal* gain. In the standard treatment, the line regulation from the **input pin** (raw $V_{in}$) to the output is suppressed by the **loop gain** $A_{OL} \beta_{fb}$. The "gain from $V_{in}$ to $V_{out}$" being $1/\beta_{fb}$ refers to the **control-to-output** transfer function, not the **input-to-output** suppression. The true line regulation is:

$$LR_{true} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \approx \dfrac{1}{A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$$

This is the form used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Regulation

Load regulation is defined as the change in $V_{out}$ per unit change in load current $I_L$:

$$LDR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L}$$

The **open-loop output impedance** of the regulator (looking back from $V_{out}$ into the collector of the pass transistor) is $Z_{out,ol} \approx r_{o,pass}$ (the small-signal output resistance of the pass BJT/MOSFET).

With the feedback loop closed, the output impedance is reduced by the loop gain:

$$Z_{out,cl} = \dfrac{Z_{out,ol}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}} = \dfrac{r_{o,pass}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$$

Therefore:

$$LDR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} \approx Z_{out,cl} = \dfrac{r_{o,pass}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta_{fb}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Because the loop gain appears in the denominator, a **high-gain op-amp** (or Darlington pass transistor) gives **excellent load regulation** (small $LDR$). This is why monolithic regulators like the **LM7805** use Darlington pass elements internally.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Voltage of the Simple BJT (No Op-Amp) Series Regulator

### Circuit
- Zener $D_Z$ sets $V_Z$ at the **base** of the NPN pass transistor $Q_1$.
- The emitter is the output: $V_{out} = V_E$.
- The collector receives unregulated $V_{in}$.

### Derivation

For an NPN BJT in the active region (emitter follower):

$$V_{BE} = V_B - V_E = 0.7 \text{ V (typical for silicon)}$$

Since the base is held at the Zener voltage (the Zener clamps the base):

$$V_B = V_Z$$

Therefore:

$$V_E = V_B - V_{BE} = V_Z - V_{BE}$$

$$V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Correction:** For an NPN emitter follower, $V_E = V_B - V_{BE}$. So $V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE} \approx V_Z - 0.7$ V. (Some textbooks write $+V_{BE}$ for PNP configurations; KTU expects the NPN convention.)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Series vs. Shunt Regulator

| Parameter | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| Pass element position | In **series** with load | In **parallel** with load |
| Load current | $I_L = I_{pass}$ (full current) | $I_L = I_S - I_{shunt}$ |
| Efficiency | **High** (especially LDO) | **Low** (idle current always flows) |
| Short-circuit protection | Easy to add (current limit) | Difficult |
| $V_{in}$ range | Wide (only needs $V_{in} > V_{out} + V_{DO}$) | Limited (Zener must always conduct) |
| Best for | High-current applications | Low-noise, low-current references |
| Heat dissipation | In pass transistor | In series resistor $R_S$ |
| KTU example IC | **LM7805, LM317** | TL431 shunt reference |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A series regulator uses a Zener diode with $V_Z = 5.6$ V, sampling resistors $R_1 = 2$ k╬®, $R_2 = 1$ k╬®, and an ideal op-amp. Find (a) the regulated output voltage, (b) the power dissipated in the pass transistor if $V_{in} = 15$ V and $I_L = 100$ mA.

### Solution

**(a)** Using the closed-form expression derived in Section 3.1:

$$V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right) = 5.6 \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2000}{1000}\right)$$

$$V_{out} = 5.6 \cdot 3 = 16.8 \text{ V}$$

**(b)** Power dissipated in the pass transistor:

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (15 - 16.8) \cdot 0.1 = -0.18 \text{ W}$$

A **negative** result is impossible ŌĆö this signals that $V_{in} = 15$ V is **insufficient** to maintain regulation. The regulator has **dropped out**. The minimum input voltage required is:

$$V_{in,min} = V_{out} + V_{CE,sat} = 16.8 + 0.2 = 17.0 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting to check the **dropout condition** $V_{in} > V_{out} + V_{CE,sat}$. The regulator cannot step *up*; it can only step *down*. Always verify $V_{in} > V_{out}$ before computing $P_D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

The diagram below maps the **signal flow** and **feedback topology** of an op-amp controlled series voltage regulator. All node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain no formatting characters that could break Mermaid's parser.

```mermaid
    D --> E["FEEDBACK VOLTAGE VFB TO INVERTING INPUT"]

    G --> H
    H --> I["ERROR SIGNAL EPSILON DRIVES BASE OF Q1"]

    C --> L["LOAD RL"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a series voltage regulator. State its two main advantages over a shunt regulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **series voltage regulator** is a linear regulator in which the **pass transistor is connected in series** between the unregulated input and the regulated output, such that the entire load current flows through the pass element. The transistor operates in the active region, dropping the excess voltage $(V_{in} - V_{out})$ as heat.

**Two advantages over shunt regulator:**
1. **Higher efficiency**, especially at high load currents, because no excess current is diverted to ground.
2. **Easier short-circuit protection** ŌĆö a current-sense resistor and comparator can be added to fold back the base drive when $I_L$ exceeds a safe limit.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage = 3 marks)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Zener diode in a series voltage regulator? Why is it placed at the non-inverting input of the error op-amp?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Zener diode** provides a **stable, temperature-compensated DC reference voltage** $V_Z$ against which the sampled output voltage is compared. It is biased in **reverse breakdown** through a series resistor $R_S$ that limits its current.

It is placed at the **non-inverting input** of the op-amp so that, by the virtual-short principle, the inverting input (which receives the feedback voltage $V_{fb}$) is forced to equal $V_Z$. This makes $V_{out}$ a precise multiple of $V_Z$, set by the feedback divider.

*(1.5 marks for role, 1.5 marks for placement logic = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **series voltage regulator using an op-amp error amplifier**. Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage in terms of the Zener voltage and the sampling resistors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the regulator in part (a), a load current change of $\Delta I_L = 50$ mA produces an output voltage change of $\Delta V_{out} = 5$ mV. If the open-loop output resistance of the pass transistor is $r_o = 50$ ╬®, calculate the **loop gain** $A_{OL}\beta_{fb}$ of the feedback loop. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**

Draw the circuit with:
- Unregulated $V_{in}$ at collector of NPN pass transistor $Q_1$.
- Emitter of $Q_1$ at $V_{out}$.
- Zener $D_Z$ (with series resistor $R_S$ to $V_{in}$) at non-inverting ($+$) input of op-amp.
- Resistor divider $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom) from $V_{out}$ to ground; the midpoint connects to the inverting ($-$) input of op-amp.
- Op-amp output drives the base of $Q_1$.

**Operation (2 marks):**
The op-amp continuously compares the scaled feedback voltage $V_{fb} = V_{out} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ with the reference $V_Z$. The difference (error) is amplified and applied to the base of $Q_1$, modulating its $V_{CE}$ to absorb line and load disturbances.

**Derivation (2 marks):** Applying virtual short, $V_{fb} = V_Z$:

$$V_{out} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_Z \implies \boxed{V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}$$

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **[7 marks]**

The load regulation is given by:

$$LDR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = \dfrac{5 \text{ mV}}{50 \text{ mA}} = 0.1 \text{ V/A}$$

From the closed-loop output impedance formula (derived in Section 3.3):

$$Z_{out,cl} = \dfrac{r_{o,pass}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta_{fb}} = LDR$$

Substituting values:

$$0.1 = \dfrac{50}{1 + A_{OL}\beta_{fb}}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$1 + A_{OL}\beta_{fb} = \dfrac{50}{0.1} = 500$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{A_{OL}\beta_{fb} = 499 \approx 500}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks split:** 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for solving, 1 for final answer.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` ŌĆö 14 Marks

**(a)** Compare **series and shunt voltage regulators** in terms of efficiency, short-circuit protection, and load current handling. State two situations where a series regulator is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series regulator supplies $V_{out} = 9$ V at $I_L = 500$ mA from an unregulated input of $V_{in} = 18$ V. Calculate (i) the power dissipated in the pass transistor, (ii) the efficiency, and (iii) the heat-sink thermal resistance required if the maximum junction temperature is $T_J = 150$ ┬░C and ambient is $T_A = 50$ ┬░C. Assume junction-to-case thermal resistance $\theta_{JC} = 2$ ┬░C/W. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) **[7 marks]**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Series | Shunt |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency at high $I_L$ | **High** | Low (current wasted in $R_S$) |
| Short-circuit protection | Easy (sense + limit) | Difficult |
| Load current range | Wide | Limited by $R_S$ |
| Component stress | $Q_1$ must handle full $I_L$ | $R_S$ must handle full $V_{in}$ |

**Two situations where series is preferred (3 marks):**
1. **High-current, high-efficiency applications** (e.g., LM7805 in micro-controller boards).
2. **Battery-powered portable devices** using LDO (low-dropout) topology where $V_{in} - V_{out}$ is small.

---

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) **[7 marks]**

**(i) Power dissipated in pass transistor (2 marks):**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (18 - 9) \cdot 0.5 = \boxed{4.5 \text{ W}}$$

**(ii) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_L} = \dfrac{9}{18} = \boxed{50\%}$$

**(iii) Heat-sink thermal resistance (3 marks):**

Total thermal resistance from junction to ambient:

$$\theta_{JA} = \dfrac{T_J - T_A}{P_D} = \dfrac{150 - 50}{4.5} = \dfrac{100}{4.5} \approx 22.22 \text{ ┬░C/W}$$

Heat-sink thermal resistance:

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

Assuming $\theta_{CS} \approx 0.5$ ┬░C/W (typical insulator):

$$\theta_{SA} = \theta_{JA} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS} = 22.22 - 2 - 0.5 = \boxed{19.72 \text{ ┬░C/W}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing NPN and PNP** emitter-follower sign: $V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ (NPN) vs. $V_{out} = V_Z + V_{BE}$ (PNP). KTU almost exclusively uses **NPN**.
> 2. **Forgetting the virtual short** and writing the op-amp equation as $V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V_+ - V_-)$ instead of using feedback. This loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> 3. **Not specifying units** in numerical answers ŌĆö KTU deducts $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Drawing the Zener backwards** (cathode toward ground) ŌĆö KTU examiners specifically check the **Zener direction** in circuit diagrams.
> 5. **Ignoring dropout** when $V_{in}$ is too low ŌĆö always verify $V_{in} > V_{out} + V_{CE,sat}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series regulator** = pass element **in series** with load; full load current flows through it.
- **Output voltage** (op-amp version): $V_{out} = V_Z \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$.
- **Output voltage** (simple BJT version): $V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE} \approx V_Z - 0.7$ V.
- **Line regulation** is suppressed by the **loop gain** $A_{OL}\beta_{fb}$ ŌĆö use high-gain op-amp for better regulation.
- **Load regulation** = closed-loop output impedance $= \dfrac{r_{o,pass}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta_{fb}}$.
- **Dropout condition**: $V_{in} > V_{out} + V_{CE,sat}$ ŌĆö regulator cannot step-up.
- **Pass transistor power**: $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ ŌĆö must be heat-sunk.
- **Efficiency** $\eta = V_{out}/V_{in}$ ŌĆö fundamentally limited; use SMPS for high step-down ratios.
- **Zener direction**: cathode toward higher potential, biased in reverse breakdown.
- **Feedback network**: $R_1$ (top) connects to $V_{out}$, $R_2$ (bottom) to ground; midpoint to inverting input.
- **Current limiting**: Add a sense resistor $R_S$ in series with the load and a second comparator to fold back base drive.
- **Darlington pass transistor**: Used in monolithic ICs (e.g., LM7805) to multiply $\beta$ and reduce base current draw from the op-amp.
- **LM317 (KTU favorite)**: Adjustable three-terminal series regulator with $V_{out} = V_{REF} \left(1 + R_2/R_1\right)$, where $V_{REF} = 1.25$ V.
- **LDO** = series regulator with $V_{DO} < 0.5$ V; uses a **P-channel MOSFET** as the pass element.
- **Always draw the op-amp with $+$, $-$, and output clearly labeled** ŌĆö KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabeled pins.
- **Verification trick**: If the calculated $V_{out}$ is greater than $V_{in}$, the regulator is in **dropout** ŌĆö recheck your assumption.
- **Sampling divider current**: Choose $R_1, R_2$ such that the divider current $\approx 1$ mA ŌĆö high enough to ignore op-amp bias current, low enough to avoid wasting power.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shunt Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shunt Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shunt Voltage Regulator** is a type of linear voltage regulator in which the **controlling/regulating element is connected in parallel (shunt)** with the load, while a **series dropping resistor (R_S)** is placed between the unregulated input supply and the load. The shunt element dynamically varies its current draw to maintain a constant output voltage across the load despite variations in input voltage (line regulation) or load current (load regulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Shunt regulators are studied as the foundational topology of linear regulators, often using a Zener diode as the reference and control element, or enhanced with a BJT/MOSFET and op-amp feedback for higher current capability and tighter regulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Overflow Drain" of a Water Tank

Imagine a **water tank** with an inlet pipe (unregulated input) supplying water to a tap (load). The water level inside the tank is the **output voltage**, and it must remain constant. To keep the level constant, you install a **side drain pipe at a fixed height** that opens automatically whenever the water rises above the mark.

- If **input flow increases** ŌåÆ the excess water spills out through the drain ŌåÆ tank level stays constant.
- If the **tap is closed** (load decreases) ŌåÆ drain lets more water out to compensate.
- If the **tap is opened more** (load increases) ŌåÆ drain restricts its flow to allow more water to the tap.

The **side drain = Shunt element**, the **pipe connecting the source to the tank = Series resistor (R_S)**, and the **tank water level = Regulated output voltage**. This is exactly how a shunt regulator works in the electrical domain!

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unregulated Input Voltage | $V_{in}$ | **Volts (V)** | Raw DC supply from rectifier/transformer |
| Regulated Output Voltage | $V_{out}$ | **Volts (V)** | Constant voltage delivered to load |
| Series (Dropping) Resistor | $R_S$ | **Ohms (╬®)** | Limits current and develops variable drop |
| Shunt Current | $I_{sh}$ | **Amperes (A)** | Current diverted through the control element |
| Load Current | $I_L$ | **Amperes (A)** | Current drawn by the load |
| Quiescent Current | $I_Q$ | **Amperes (A)** | Minimum current needed to keep regulator in regulation |
| Zener Voltage | $V_Z$ | **Volts (V)** | Reference/clamping voltage in zener-based regulators |
| Line Regulation | $LR$ | **mV/V** (or %) | $V_{out}$ change per unit $V_{in}$ change |
| Load Regulation | $LDR$ | **mV/mA** (or %) | $V_{out}$ change per unit $I_L$ change |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, you are expected to identify the **three operating states** of a shunt regulator: (i) **Cut-off** ($I_{sh} = 0$), (ii) **Normal regulation** ($I_{sh} > 0$ with control active), and (iii) **Saturation/Overload** ($I_{sh}$ at maximum, regulation lost).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö The Why and The How

The shunt regulator operates on a single elegant principle: **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node**.

$$\boxed{I_S = I_{sh} + I_L}$$

where $I_S$ is the current through the series resistor $R_S$.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Regulation

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the input current through R_S:**
  The current entering the node equals $(V_{in} - V_{out})/R_S$. This current is **forced** by the supply and resistor; it cannot change instantly.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Load draws $I_L$:**
  Whatever current the load needs is subtracted at the output node.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Shunt absorbs the remainder:**
  The shunt element (Zener/BJT) absorbs the **difference**. If $I_L$ decreases ŌåÆ $I_{sh}$ increases. If $I_L$ increases ŌåÆ $I_{sh}$ decreases.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Why $V_{out}$ stays constant:**
  In a Zener shunt regulator, $V_{out}$ is **clamped** to $V_Z$ by the Zener diode's breakdown characteristic. In an active shunt regulator, an op-amp feedback loop forces a transistor to sink exactly the right amount of current to hold $V_{out}$ at a precise reference.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Why this works for input variations:**
  If $V_{in}$ rises, $I_S$ tends to rise, but $I_L$ is fixed by the load, so the **extra current flows into the shunt element**, keeping $V_{out}$ unchanged. The increased voltage drop occurs across $R_S$, not across the load.

## 2.2 Three Implementations of Shunt Regulators

### (a) Zener Diode Shunt Regulator (Simplest)
- Zener diode placed in parallel with load, anode to ground, cathode to $V_{out}$.
- Operates in **reverse breakdown region**.
- $V_{out} = V_Z$.

### (b) BJT Shunt Regulator
- An NPN transistor with collector tied to $V_{out}$, emitter to ground, and base driven by a reference network.
- $I_C$ is dynamically controlled to act as the variable shunt current.
- Better current handling than a Zener alone.

### (c) Op-Amp Controlled Shunt Regulator (Precision)
- An op-amp compares a sample of $V_{out}$ to a precise reference voltage.
- The op-amp output drives the base of a BJT (or gate of a MOSFET) acting as the shunt element.
- Provides **tight regulation** with high loop gain.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_Z$ | Output equals Zener voltage (basic Zener regulator) |
| 2 | $I_S = (V_{in} - V_{out})/R_S$ | Series resistor current (KVL) |
| 3 | $I_S = I_{sh} + I_L$ | KCL at output node |
| 4 | $R_S = (V_{in} - V_Z)/(I_L + I_Z)$ | Design equation for series resistor |
| 5 | $P_{Z,max} = V_Z \cdot I_{Z,max}$ | Maximum Zener power dissipation (must not exceed rated $P_{ZM}$) |
| 6 | $P_{R_S} = (V_{in} - V_Z)^2/R_S$ | Power dissipated in series resistor |
| 7 | $\text{Line Regulation} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \cdot 100\%$ | Expressed in %/V or mV/V |
| 8 | $\text{Load Regulation} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \cdot 100\%$ | $V_{NL}$ = no-load, $V_{FL}$ = full-load voltage |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_{in}}$ | Efficiency of the regulator |
| 10 | $\eta_{max}$ when $I_{sh} = 0$ and $V_{in}$ is minimum | Best-case efficiency occurs at full load & minimum input |
| 11 | $I_{in,min} = (V_{in,min} - V_Z)/R_S$ | Minimum series current to maintain regulation |
| 12 | $I_{sh,max} = I_{in,max} - I_{L,min}$ | Maximum shunt current (worst-case heat) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Shunt regulators are widely used in **precision voltage references** for ADCs and DACs, **overvoltage protection (crowbar) circuits**, **bias supplies** for op-amps and sensors, and as **low-noise references** in instrumentation. Their main drawback is **low efficiency** at light loads, which is why series regulators dominate in battery-powered systems.

## 2.4 Why the Shunt Element Must Be a Variable "Current Sink"

The genius of the shunt topology is that the shunt element behaves like a **programmable current sink**. By sinking more or less current, it keeps the **sum** of $I_{sh} + I_L$ constant, equal to the forced current $I_S$ through $R_S$. This makes the regulator robust against **both** load and line disturbances simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Series Resistor $R_S$ Design Equation

### Given:
- Unregulated input: $V_{in}$
- Desired output: $V_{out} = V_Z$
- Load current range: $I_{L,min}$ to $I_{L,max}$
- Zener current range: $I_{Z,min}$ to $I_{Z,max}$

### Goal:
Find the value of $R_S$ such that regulation is maintained across **all** operating conditions.

### Derivation:

By KVL around the input loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= V_{R_S} + V_{out} \\
V_{in} &= I_S \cdot R_S + V_Z
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $R_S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S \cdot R_S &= V_{in} - V_Z \\
R_S &= \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

By KCL at the output node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= I_{sh} + I_L
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{R_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_{sh} + I_L}}
$$

### Two Boundary Cases for Robust Design:

**Case A ŌĆö Worst case for minimum Zener current (light load, max input):**

The Zener must still carry at least $I_{Z,min}$ to stay in breakdown. Use $I_L = I_{L,min}$ and $V_{in} = V_{in,max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S,max} &= \frac{V_{in,max} - V_Z}{I_{Z,min} + I_{L,min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case B ŌĆö Worst case for maximum Zener current (no load, max input):**

The Zener must not exceed $I_{Z,max}$ (else it burns). Use $I_L = 0$ and $V_{in} = V_{in,max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S,min} &= \frac{V_{in,max} - V_Z}{I_{Z,max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Design Rule:
Choose $R_S$ such that:

$$
R_{S,min} \le R_S \le R_{S,max}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Line Regulation of a Zener Shunt Regulator

### Definition:
Line regulation quantifies how much $V_{out}$ changes when $V_{in}$ changes, with $I_L$ held constant.

### Starting Point:
For a Zener regulator, ideally $V_{out} = V_Z$ is constant. However, the Zener has a **finite dynamic resistance** $r_Z$ in the breakdown region, modeled as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_Z + I_{sh} \cdot r_Z
\end{aligned}
$$

### Change in $V_{out}$ for a change in $V_{in}$:

The change in shunt current $\Delta I_{sh}$ for a change $\Delta V_{in}$ (with $I_L$ constant) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta I_{sh} &= \frac{\Delta V_{in} - \Delta V_{out}}{R_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, $\Delta V_{out} = \Delta I_{sh} \cdot r_Z$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_{out} &= \frac{\Delta V_{in} - \Delta V_{out}}{R_S} \cdot r_Z \\
\Delta V_{out} \cdot R_S &= (\Delta V_{in} - \Delta V_{out}) \cdot r_Z \\
\Delta V_{out} \cdot R_S + \Delta V_{out} \cdot r_Z &= \Delta V_{in} \cdot r_Z \\
\Delta V_{out} \cdot (R_S + r_Z) &= \Delta V_{in} \cdot r_Z
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{Line Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}}
$$

### Engineering Insight:
Since typically $R_S \gg r_Z$, the ratio approaches zero, indicating **excellent line regulation**. To improve line regulation further, **increase $R_S$** (but this worsens efficiency and minimum dropout).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Regulation of a Zener Shunt Regulator

### Definition:
Load regulation quantifies how much $V_{out}$ changes when $I_L$ changes, with $V_{in}$ held constant.

### Setup:
For a fixed $V_{in}$, the series current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_S} \approx \text{constant}
\end{aligned}
$$

But $V_{out}$ varies slightly. Differentiating $I_S = I_{sh} + I_L$ with respect to $I_L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{dI_{sh}}{dI_L} + 1 \\
\frac{dI_{sh}}{dI_L} &= -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, $\Delta V_{out} = r_Z \cdot \Delta I_{sh} = -r_Z \cdot \Delta I_L$. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\Delta V_{out} = -r_Z \cdot \Delta I_L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Load Regulation} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = -r_Z \;\; \Omega}
$$

The **negative sign** indicates $V_{out}$ decreases as $I_L$ increases. The **magnitude** equals the Zener dynamic resistance. A **low $r_Z$ Zener** gives better load regulation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Efficiency Analysis

### Efficiency:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_{in}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a shunt regulator, $I_{in} = I_S = I_{sh} + I_L$. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot (I_{sh} + I_L)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Maximization Insight:
- At **no load** ($I_L = 0$): $\eta = 0$ (all power is wasted in the shunt).
- At **full load** ($I_{sh} \to 0$): $\eta \to V_{out}/V_{in}$.
- Therefore, $\eta$ is **maximum at full load and minimum input voltage**.

This is the **fundamental efficiency weakness** of shunt regulators: they waste power continuously to maintain regulation.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Comparison of Shunt Regulator Variants

| Parameter | Zener Shunt | BJT Shunt | Op-Amp Shunt |
|---|---|---|---|
| Complexity | Very Low | Medium | High |
| Output Current Capability | Low (mA) | Medium (hundreds of mA) | High (with power BJT) |
| Regulation Accuracy | PoorŌĆōModerate | Good | Excellent |
| Line Regulation | $\dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$ | Better | Best (loop gain limited) |
| Load Regulation | $-r_Z$ | Better | Best |
| Efficiency at Light Load | **Very Poor** | Poor | Poor |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical Use | Reference, protection | Bias supplies | Precision regulators |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of a Shunt Regulator

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA[Unregulated DC Input V_in] --> resB[Series Dropping Resistor R_S]
    resB --> nodeC[Output Node V_out]
    nodeC --> loadD[Load R_L]
    loadD --> gndE[Ground]
    nodeC --> zenerF[Zener Diode V_Z to Ground]
    nodeC --> fbG[Sense / Feedback Divider]
    fbG --> opampH[Op-Amp Comparator]
    refI[Precision Reference V_REF] --> opampH
    opampH --> bjtJ[Shunt BJT Driver]
    bjtJ --> gndE
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The unregulated input flows through $R_S$ to the output node. The Zener provides a coarse clamp, while the feedback branch (used in precision variants) senses $V_{out}$, compares it with $V_{REF}$, and drives a BJT that dynamically sinks excess current to ground. This is the **dual-path regulation strategy**.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Operating State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Cutoff
    Cutoff --> Regulating : V_in exceeds V_Z, Zener enters breakdown
    Regulating --> Saturation : Load current drops to zero, I_sh reaches I_Zmax
    Saturation --> Regulating : Load demand resumes
    Regulating --> Cutoff : V_in falls below V_Z + V_RS minimum
    Cutoff --> [*]
```

> **State Explanation:** The regulator cycles between three discrete operating states. **Cutoff** = shunt element inactive. **Regulating** = shunt current dynamically varies to absorb disturbances. **Saturation** = shunt element at maximum dissipation, regulation lost (fault condition).

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Current Flow Partitioning

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Input Source V_in] -->|I_S| rsB[Series Resistor R_S]
    rsB -->|I_S| outC[Output Node V_out]
    outC -->|I_L| loadD[Load]
    outC -->|I_sh| shuntE[Shunt Element to Ground]
```

> **Current Accounting (KCL):** $I_S = I_L + I_{sh}$. The series resistor enforces $I_S$; the load takes $I_L$; the shunt absorbs the remainder $I_{sh}$. This is the **single equation** that governs all shunt regulator behavior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define a shunt voltage regulator and explain its basic operating principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A shunt voltage regulator is a linear voltage regulator in which the **controlling element is connected in parallel (shunt) with the load**, while a **series resistor (R_S)** is connected between the unregulated input and the load. **(1 mark)**
The shunt element dynamically varies its current ($I_{sh}$) such that the total current through $R_S$ is split between the load and the shunt element as $I_S = I_{sh} + I_L$ (KCL). **(1 mark)**
When $V_{in}$ increases or $I_L$ decreases, the excess current is absorbed by the shunt element, keeping $V_{out}$ across the load constant. The simplest implementation uses a **Zener diode** operating in reverse breakdown, giving $V_{out} = V_Z$. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Compare a shunt voltage regulator with a series voltage regulator in terms of efficiency and regulation performance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Shunt Regulator | Series Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | **Low**, especially at light load (wasteful $I_{sh}$) | Higher, since pass element carries only $I_L$ |
| Line Regulation | Good ($\frac{r_Z}{R_S+r_Z}$) | Excellent (feedback controlled) |
| Load Regulation | Moderate ($-r_Z$) | Excellent (feedback controlled) |
| Short-circuit Protection | **Inherent** (shunt limits $I_S$) | Needs external protection |
| Complexity | Simple | Moderate to complex |
**Conclusion:** Shunt regulators are preferred for **simple, low-current, short-circuit-proof** applications, while series regulators are chosen where **efficiency and tight regulation** matter. **(1 mark synthesis)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Zener diode shunt regulator**. Derive the expression for the series resistance $R_S$ and the range of $I_Z$ for safe operation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Zener shunt regulator has $V_{in} = 15\text{ V} \pm 20\%$, $V_Z = 9.1\text{ V}$, $I_{Z,min} = 5\text{ mA}$, $I_{Z,max} = 50\text{ mA}$, and load current $I_L$ varies from $5\text{ mA}$ to $40\text{ mA}$. Design a suitable value of $R_S$ and find the power dissipation in the Zener under worst-case conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Circuit Diagram Description (textual, 2 marks):**
- Unregulated DC input $V_{in}$ ŌåÆ Series resistor $R_S$ ŌåÆ Output node $V_{out}$
- Zener diode connected from $V_{out}$ to ground, with **cathode at $V_{out}$** (reverse-biased).
- Load $R_L$ connected across $V_{out}$.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- When $V_{in} > V_Z$, the Zener enters reverse breakdown and clamps $V_{out} = V_Z$.
- KCL: $I_S = I_Z + I_L$, where $I_S = (V_{in} - V_Z)/R_S$.
- If $V_{in}$ rises, $I_S$ rises, and the **extra current flows through the Zener** (since $I_L$ is unchanged by $V_{in}$).
- If $I_L$ falls, $I_S$ stays fixed, so $I_Z$ rises to absorb the leftover current.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
KVL: $V_{in} = I_S R_S + V_Z$ ŌåÆ $R_S = (V_{in} - V_Z)/I_S$
KCL: $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ ŌåÆ $R_S = (V_{in} - V_Z)/(I_Z + I_L)$

For safe Zener operation: $I_{Z,min} \le I_Z \le I_{Z,max}$

Worst-case limits:
- $I_{Z,min}$ condition: $V_{in,min}$, $I_{L,max}$ ŌåÆ gives **$R_{S,max}$**
- $I_{Z,max}$ condition: $V_{in,max}$, $I_{L,min}$ ŌåÆ gives **$R_{S,min}$**

Choose $R_{S,min} \le R_S \le R_{S,max}$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $V_{in} = 15\text{ V} \pm 20\%$ ŌåÆ $V_{in,min} = 12\text{ V}$, $V_{in,max} = 18\text{ V}$
- $V_Z = 9.1\text{ V}$
- $I_{Z,min} = 5\text{ mA}$, $I_{Z,max} = 50\text{ mA}$
- $I_{L,min} = 5\text{ mA}$, $I_{L,max} = 40\text{ mA}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate $R_{S,max}$ (use $V_{in,min}$ and $I_{L,max}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S,max} &= \frac{V_{in,min} - V_Z}{I_{Z,min} + I_{L,max}} \\
&= \frac{12 - 9.1}{(5 + 40) \text{ mA}} \\
&= \frac{2.9 \text{ V}}{45 \text{ mA}} \\
&\approx 64.4 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate $R_{S,min}$ (use $V_{in,max}$ and $I_{L,min}$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S,min} &= \frac{V_{in,max} - V_Z}{I_{Z,max}} \\
&= \frac{18 - 9.1}{50 \text{ mA}} \\
&= \frac{8.9 \text{ V}}{50 \text{ mA}} \\
&= 178 \; \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Choose a standard value within the range:**
We need $R_S$ such that $64.4 \; \Omega \le R_S \le 178 \; \Omega$.
Choose **$R_S = 100 \; \Omega$** (standard E12 value, mid-range for safety).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify worst-case Zener current:**
Worst case for Zener = maximum $I_Z$, occurring at $V_{in,max} = 18\text{ V}$ and $I_{L,min} = 5\text{ mA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z,max,actual} &= \frac{V_{in,max} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_{L,min} \\
&= \frac{18 - 9.1}{100} - 0.005 \\
&= 0.089 - 0.005 \\
&= 0.084 \text{ A} \\
&= 84 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Issue:** $84 \text{ mA} > I_{Z,max} = 50 \text{ mA}$. The Zener will **overheat and fail**.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Re-select $R_S$:**
Use $R_S = 178 \; \Omega$ to satisfy the upper limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z,max,actual} &= \frac{18 - 9.1}{178} - 0.005 = 0.050 - 0.005 = 0.045 \text{ A} = 45 \text{ mA} \le 50 \text{ mA} \;\; \text{OK}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verify lower limit at $V_{in,min} = 12\text{ V}$ and $I_{L,max} = 40\text{ mA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z,min,actual} &= \frac{12 - 9.1}{178} - 0.040 = 0.0163 - 0.040 = -0.0237 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Issue:** Negative $I_Z$ means the Zener drops out of breakdown. The chosen $R_S$ is **too large**.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Re-evaluate design constraints:**
The problem has **inconsistent specifications** ŌĆö to satisfy both bounds, $R_{S,max} \le R_{S,min}$, which is impossible. Hence, either $I_{L,max}$ must be reduced or $I_{Z,max}$ must be increased. As a compromise for KTU, **select $R_S = 150 \; \Omega$** (closest standard value, acceptable trade-off).

**Step 7 ŌĆö Worst-case power dissipation in Zener:**
At $V_{in,max} = 18\text{ V}$, $I_{L,min} = 5\text{ mA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z,wc} &= \frac{18 - 9.1}{150} - 0.005 = 0.0593 - 0.005 = 0.0543 \text{ A} \\
P_{Z,wc} &= V_Z \cdot I_{Z,wc} = 9.1 \times 0.0543 \approx 0.494 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** **$R_S = 150 \; \Omega$**, **$P_{Z,wc} \approx 0.5 \text{ W}$**. Select a Zener with $P_{ZM} \ge 1\text{ W}$ for safety.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`
**(a)** Draw the block diagram of an **op-amp controlled shunt regulator** using a BJT pass element. Explain its operation and derive the expression for the regulated output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An op-amp based shunt regulator uses a 5.1 V Zener reference and a feedback resistor network with $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$. If the unregulated input varies from 12 V to 18 V and load current varies from 10 mA to 100 mA, calculate: (i) the regulated output voltage, and (ii) the power dissipated in the shunt BJT under worst-case conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- $V_{in}$ ŌåÆ $R_S$ (series resistor) ŌåÆ $V_{out}$ node
- A **resistor divider** ($R_1$ from $V_{out}$ to inverting input, $R_2$ from inverting input to ground) senses $V_{out}$.
- **Non-inverting input** of op-amp is connected to **Zener reference** $V_{REF} = V_Z$.
- **Op-amp output** drives the **base of an NPN BJT** whose collector is at $V_{out}$ and emitter at ground.

**Operation (2 marks):**
- The op-amp operates in linear region with very high open-loop gain $A$.
- It forces $V_- = V_+$ (virtual short), so the sensed voltage at the inverting input equals $V_{REF}$.
- Hence, the op-amp adjusts the BJT base voltage such that the collector current $I_C$ sinks exactly the right amount to maintain $V_{out}$ at the regulated value.
- If $V_{out}$ tries to rise ŌåÆ $V_-$ rises ŌåÆ $V_{out,op-amp}$ falls ŌåÆ $V_{BE}$ falls ŌåÆ $I_C$ falls ŌåÆ less current sunk ŌåÆ $V_{out}$ pulled back down (negative feedback).

**Derivation (3 marks):**
At the inverting input of the op-amp, by voltage divider:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_- &= V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

By virtual short, $V_- = V_+ = V_{REF} = V_Z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_Z &= V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V_{out}$:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = V_Z \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)}
$$

> **Insight:** The op-amp feedback loop gives a regulation accuracy of $V_{out}/(A \cdot \beta)$ where $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$, which is much tighter than a passive Zener regulator.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- $V_Z = 5.1\text{ V}$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$
- $V_{in}$: 12 V to 18 V, $I_L$: 10 mA to 100 mA

**(i) Regulated Output Voltage (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_Z \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right) \\
&= 5.1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{10}{5}\right) \\
&= 5.1 \cdot 3 \\
&= 15.3 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Issue:** $V_{out} = 15.3\text{ V}$ exceeds $V_{in,min} = 12\text{ V}$! The regulator **cannot operate** because there is no headroom across $R_S$.

**Resolution:** This means the design as given is infeasible. To fix, we need either $V_{in,min} > V_{out} + V_{R_S,min}$ or a lower $V_{out}$.

**For KTU exam purposes, assume $V_{in,min} = 18\text{ V}$ (re-read the range as 18 V to 18 V is too restrictive, so let's re-interpret):**
- Take $V_{in} = 18\text{ V}$ (nominal), so $V_{out} = 15.3\text{ V}$ and $V_{R_S} = 2.7\text{ V}$.

**(ii) Worst-case BJT Power Dissipation (4 marks):**

Worst case for the BJT = **maximum $V_{CE}$ (= $V_{out}$) AND maximum $I_C$**.
This occurs at **minimum load current** ($I_L = 10\text{ mA}$) where the BJT must sink almost all of $I_S$.

Assume $R_S$ is designed to allow enough current at $V_{in,max} = 18\text{ V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

KCL: $I_S = I_C + I_L$, so at $I_{L,min} = 10\text{ mA}$, $I_C \approx I_S - 0.010 \text{ A}$.

If $R_S = 100 \; \Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_S &= \frac{18 - 15.3}{100} = 0.027 \text{ A} = 27 \text{ mA} \\
I_C &= 27 - 10 = 17 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Power dissipated in BJT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{BJT} &= V_{CE} \cdot I_C = V_{out} \cdot I_C \\
&= 15.3 \times 0.017 \\
&\approx 0.260 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer:** **(i) $V_{out} = 15.3\text{ V}$**, **(ii) $P_{BJT} \approx 0.26\text{ W}$** under worst-case conditions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls ŌĆö Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing shunt and series regulators:** Students often describe a series regulator's operation when asked about shunt. Always emphasize that the **control element is in PARALLEL with the load**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $I_{Z,min}$ constraint:** Many students design $R_S$ using only the $I_{Z,max}$ constraint, forgetting that the Zener needs a **minimum holding current** to stay in breakdown. If $I_Z$ falls below $I_{Z,min}$, the Zener exits breakdown and $V_{out}$ collapses.
>
> 3. **Sign error in load regulation:** The load regulation of a Zener shunt regulator is **negative** ($\Delta V_{out} = -r_Z \cdot \Delta I_L$). Writing it as a positive number loses a mark.
>
> 4. **Efficiency at no load:** A common error is claiming $\eta$ can be high at no load. In reality, $\eta = 0$ at no load for a shunt regulator. This is its **fundamental weakness**.
>
> 5. **Unit errors:** Always express $R_S$ in $\Omega$ when $V$ and mA are used: $R_S (\Omega) = V (\text{V}) / I (\text{mA}) \times 1000$. Forgetting the factor of 1000 is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Worst-case identification:** Students often check only one extreme. KTU expects you to check **both** extremes ŌĆö $V_{in,max}/I_{L,min}$ (Zener overheating) AND $V_{in,min}/I_{L,max}$ (Zener dropout).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Shunt Voltage Regulator**
>
> Ō£ģ **Topology:** Series resistor $R_S$ in series, control element in **parallel (shunt)** with load.
>
> Ō£ģ **Core Equation (KCL):** $I_S = I_{sh} + I_L$ ŌĆö governs all behavior.
>
> Ō£ģ **Output Voltage:** $V_{out} = V_Z$ (Zener) or $V_{out} = V_{REF}(1 + R_1/R_2)$ (op-amp version).
>
> Ō£ģ **Design Rule for $R_S$:** $R_{S,min} \le R_S \le R_{S,max}$ ŌĆö two boundary conditions must be satisfied.
>
> Ō£ģ **Line Regulation:** $\dfrac{r_Z}{R_S + r_Z}$ ŌĆö improves with larger $R_S$.
>
> Ō£ģ **Load Regulation:** $-r_Z$ ŌĆö magnitude equals Zener dynamic resistance; **negative sign is mandatory**.
>
> Ō£ģ **Efficiency:** Best at full load, worst at no load ($\eta = 0$).
>
> Ō£ģ **Three States:** Cutoff, Regulating, Saturation ŌĆö recognize each from KCL conditions.
>
> Ō£ģ **Key Advantage:** **Built-in short-circuit protection** ŌĆö current through $R_S$ is naturally limited.
>
> Ō£ģ **Key Disadvantage:** **Poor efficiency at light load** ŌĆö shunt element continuously wastes power.
>
> Ō£ģ **Variants:** Zener (simple) ŌåÆ BJT (medium) ŌåÆ Op-amp (precision) ŌĆö increasing complexity and performance.
>
> Ō£ģ **KTU Favored Topics:** Design problems involving $R_S$ calculation, line/load regulation derivation, and BJT shunt regulator analysis appear frequently in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET417 - SOLID STATE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Short Circuit Protection (SCP)

**Short Circuit Protection** is an essential current-limiting safeguard embedded within power supplies, voltage regulators, and power electronic converters. Its primary function is to prevent catastrophic damage to the semiconductor devices (BJTs, MOSFETs, IGBTs) and the load when the output terminals are accidentally shorted, driving the load impedance toward zero.

When a short circuit occurs at the output, the load current would theoretically rise to infinity (limited only by parasitic resistances). Without protection, this **inrush current** can thermally destroy the pass transistor within milliseconds. Short Circuit Protection actively senses this abnormal current surge and intervenes by either:

1. **Shutting down** the regulator (latch-off / hiccup mode).
2. **Limiting** the current to a safe maximum ceiling (constant current limit).
3. **Reducing** the current to a very low holding value (foldback current limit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Short circuit protection is classified as a non-functional protection scheme. It is **mandatory** in all linear regulators (78xx, 79xx, LM317) and switch-mode power supplies (SMPS) to satisfy the reliability standards outlined in the NEP 2020 outcome-based curriculum.

---

## 1.2 Fold Back Protection (Foldback Current Limiting)

**Foldback Protection** is a refined, more sophisticated version of short circuit protection. Instead of holding the output current at a constant maximum value (Isc_max) during an overload or short circuit, the foldback scheme progressively *reduces* the current as the output voltage collapses.

The current-voltage (I-V) operating locus traces a path like this:

- **Normal Operation:** Load current $I_L$ flows; output voltage $V_O$ is at its regulated value.
- **Overload:** As the load impedance drops, the regulator enters the current-limit region at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$.
- **Foldback:** As $V_O$ continues to drop toward zero (toward a dead short), the current limit itself folds back to a much lower value $I_{SC(\text{min})}$.

The result is a characteristic I-V curve that *folds back* on itself ŌĆö hence the name.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy: The Smart Tap Water Valve

Imagine a municipal water pipeline feeding your house:

- **Constant Current Limit** is like a pressure valve that caps the water flow at, say, 100 liters/minute. Even if you open every tap in the house, the flow stays at 100 L/min ŌĆö the pump motor runs *hot* at full load continuously, wasting energy.
- **Foldback Protection** is like a smart pressure valve that *reduces* the maximum flow to just 10 L/min when it detects that the main pipe is ruptured (i.e., $V_O \to 0$). The pump motor cools down, drawing only a trickle of current to maintain the fault condition safely.

This way, the system survives a sustained short circuit without overheating, and the user (or fuse) has ample time to identify and clear the fault.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Isc(max)** = Maximum short-circuit (or current-limit) current at the knee of the I-V curve.
> - **Isc(min)** = Foldback holding current at $V_O = 0$.
> - **Foldback Ratio** = $I_{SC(\text{max})} / I_{SC(\text{min})}$
> - **V_K (Knee Voltage)** = Output voltage at which foldback initiates.
> - **I_L(max)** = Maximum rated load current in normal operation.

---

## 1.4 Why Foldback Over Simple Current Limit?

| Criterion | Simple Current Limit | Foldback Protection |
|---|---|---|
| Stress on pass transistor during short | High (full Isc flows) | Low (current folds back to small value) |
| Power dissipation in fault | $P = I_{SC} \times V_{IN}$ (maximum) | $P = I_{SC(\text{min})} \times V_{IN}$ (much lower) |
| SOA utilization | Poor | Excellent |
| Recovery behavior | Abrupt | Smooth (with auto-retry or latch) |
| Design complexity | Low | Moderate (extra sense + feedback network) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **"why foldback"** concept. Always justify using the **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** argument: foldback keeps the pass transistor inside its Second Breakdown region during sustained faults.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of Foldback Protection

A classic foldback current limiter is built around a **current-sense resistor** ($R_S$) placed in series with the load, and a **sensing transistor** ($Q_1$) coupled with a **control transistor** ($Q_2$) that progressively shunts the base drive of the pass element.

The logical sequence of operation is:

1. **Current Sensing:** The voltage drop across $R_S$ is $V_{RS} = I_L \cdot R_S$. This voltage is proportional to the instantaneous load current.
2. **Comparison:** $V_{RS}$ is compared to an internal reference (often derived from the bandgap reference or the output voltage itself, depending on topology).
3. **Threshold Crossing:** When $V_{RS} \geq V_{BE(\text{on})}$ of $Q_1$ (approximately **0.6 V to 0.7 V** for silicon BJTs), $Q_1$ begins to conduct.
4. **Base Drive Diversion:** Conduction of $Q_1$ diverts a portion of the base drive current away from the pass transistor, thereby limiting the load current.
5. **Foldback Action:** A feedback network (typically a resistor divider $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ from the output) is arranged such that as $V_O$ decreases, the threshold for $Q_1$ conduction is *lowered*. Hence, the current-limit point itself shifts downward ŌĆö producing the foldback characteristic.

---

## 2.2 Conditions of Operation

The foldback protection circuit transitions through three distinct regimes:

- **Regulated Region ($0 \leq I_L \leq I_{L(\text{max})}$):** Normal voltage regulation. The current-sense voltage $V_{RS}$ is below the conduction threshold of $Q_1$. The pass transistor behaves as a linear emitter follower (in series-pass regulators).
- **Current-Limit Region ($I_{L(\text{max})} < I_L \leq I_{SC(\text{max})}$):** $V_{RS}$ has reached the threshold. $Q_1$ conducts and clamps the base drive. The current is held approximately constant at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$.
- **Foldback Region ($V_O \to 0$):** As $V_O$ falls, the feedback divider reduces the available headroom, forcing the current-limit threshold down. The operating point slides along the foldback locus toward $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ at $V_O = 0$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the `$\vert$` LaTeX command for absolute value bars inside any markdown table to avoid breaking table syntax.

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Definition** | **Typical Range / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum short-circuit current | $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ | $I_{SC(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}$ | $1.1$ to $1.5 \times I_{L(\text{max})}$ (A) |
| Foldback (holding) current | $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ | $I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1}$ | $0.1$ to $0.3 \times I_{L(\text{max})}$ (A) |
| Sense resistor | $R_S$ | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{SC(\text{max})}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Knee voltage (start of foldback) | $V_K$ | $V_K = V_O \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$ | V |
| Foldback Ratio | $FR$ | $FR = \dfrac{I_{SC(\text{max})}}{I_{SC(\text{min})}}$ | Dimensionless, typically $3$ to $6$ |
| Power dissipation in pass transistor | $P_{Q}$ | $P_Q = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$ | W (worst case at $V_O = V_{IN}/2$) |
| Power dissipation during short | $P_{Q(\text{SC})}$ | $P_{Q(\text{SC})} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})}$ | W (heavily reduced by foldback) |
| Base-emitter threshold voltage | $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ | Material property (silicon BJT) | $\approx \mathbf{0.65}$ V at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Sense-resistor voltage drop | $V_{RS}$ | $V_{RS} = I_L \cdot R_S$ | V |

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Linear Power Supplies (78xx series):** Foldback is built into the LM78xx regulators. The IC survives a sustained output short to ground without burning out ŌĆö a major reliability advantage over discrete designs.
- **SMPS (Switch-Mode Power Supplies):** Foldback is implemented using current-mode control (peak current sensing via a shunt or current transformer). When the current spike exceeds a programmed threshold, the PWM duty cycle is terminated for that cycle, and the average current folds back.
- **Battery Chargers:** Foldback is critical to prevent the charger from sourcing destructive currents into a shorted lithium-ion cell, which would otherwise cause thermal runaway.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Foldback current limits are mandated by ISO 7637 and ISO 16750 to handle load-dump and short-to-battery conditions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Short-Circuit Current $I_{SC(\text{max})}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Define the sense resistor drop.**
The sense resistor $R_S$ is in series with the load, so the load current $I_L$ produces a voltage drop:

$$V_{RS} = I_L \cdot R_S$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö State the conduction condition of the sensing transistor.**
The sensing BJT ($Q_1$) begins to conduct when the voltage across $R_S$ reaches the base-emitter turn-on voltage. This is the **current-limit threshold**.

$$V_{RS} = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Equate and solve for the limiting current.**
Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$I_L \cdot R_S = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$

Therefore, the maximum current the regulator can deliver is:

$$\boxed{I_{SC(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}}$$

This is the *knee current* ŌĆö the current at which foldback *initiates*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Foldback (Holding) Current $I_{SC(\text{min})}$

**Step 1 ŌĆö Introduce the feedback divider.**
A voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (between $V_O$ and the base of $Q_1$) and the inherent Thevenin resistance of the sense network feeds back a fraction of the output voltage into the sensing transistor's base.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Thevenin equivalent at the base of $Q_1$.**
The Thevenin voltage seen at the base of $Q_1$ is:

$$V_{TH} = V_O \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(for the divider arrangement)}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply the conduction condition at $V_O = 0$ (dead short).**
At the extreme foldback point, $V_O = 0$, so the divider contributes nothing. The base of $Q_1$ is now held only by the voltage across $R_S$. The conduction condition reduces to:

$$I_{SC(\text{min})} \cdot (R_S + R_1) = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$

(The $R_1$ term enters because the divider resistor $R_1$ is in series with the sense resistor from the perspective of the current path when $V_O = 0$.)

**Step 4 ŌĆö Solve for $I_{SC(\text{min})}$.**

$$\boxed{I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Foldback Ratio

The **foldback ratio** quantifies how aggressively the protection reduces current at a dead short.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Write the ratio of the two derived currents.**

$$FR = \dfrac{I_{SC(\text{max})}}{I_{SC(\text{min})}}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute the expressions from 3.1 and 3.2.**

$$FR = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}}{\dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1}}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Cancel the common $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ term.**

$$FR = \dfrac{R_S + R_1}{R_S} = 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_S}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final form.**

$$\boxed{FR = 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_S}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The foldback ratio is purely a **resistor ratio**. It does not depend on $V_{BE(\text{on})}$, which makes the design robust against temperature and process variations ŌĆö a beautifully elegant result.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Design a foldback protection circuit for a 5 V, 1 A linear regulator. Choose $I_{SC(\text{max})} = 1.2$ A and a foldback ratio of 4. Assume $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.65$ V. Find $R_S$ and $R_1$.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Find $R_S$ from $I_{SC(\text{max})}$.**

$$R_S = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{I_{SC(\text{max})}} = \dfrac{0.65 \text{ V}}{1.2 \text{ A}} = 0.5417 \; \Omega$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Use the foldback ratio to find $R_1$.**

$$R_1 = (FR - 1) \cdot R_S = (4 - 1) \cdot 0.5417 = 1.625 \; \Omega$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute $I_{SC(\text{min})}$.**

$$I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{0.65}{0.5417 + 1.625} = \dfrac{0.65}{2.1667} = 0.30 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verify the maximum pass-transistor power dissipation in fault.**

If $V_{IN} = 12$ V (typical LDO input), then:

$$P_{Q(\text{SC})} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})} = (12 - 0) \cdot 0.30 = 3.6 \; \text{W}$$

Compare this to a *constant* current limit of 1.2 A:

$$P_{Q(\text{const})} = 12 \cdot 1.2 = 14.4 \; \text{W}$$

> The foldback scheme reduces fault power dissipation by a factor of **4** ŌĆö a dramatic improvement that keeps the pass transistor well within its SOA.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Protection Schemes

| **Protection Scheme** | **Current at Short** | **Power in Pass Device** | **Complexity** | **Recovery** | **SOA Margin** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No protection | Unbounded (catastrophic) | Destructive | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | None |
| Fuse (one-shot) | Sustained until fuse blows | High until cleared | Very Low | Manual replacement | Poor |
| Constant Current Limit | $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{max})}$ | Low | Auto | Moderate |
| **Foldback** | $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})}$ | Moderate | Auto | **Excellent** |
| Hiccup Mode | Pulsed (averaged low) | Very Low (averaged) | High (timed latch) | Auto-retry | Excellent |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Foldback-Protected Regulator

The schematic of a classical foldback-protected series regulator is presented below in block-diagram form. The blocks map directly onto physical circuit elements ŌĆö $Q_{\text{pass}}$ is the series-pass transistor, $Q_1$ is the sense transistor, and so on.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVIn["DC INPUT VOLTAGE V_IN"] --> nodePass["Q_PASS SERIES PASS TRANSISTOR"]
    nodePass --> nodeRS["R_S CURRENT SENSE RESISTOR"]
    nodeRS --> nodeVOut["DC OUTPUT V_O TO LOAD"]
    nodeVOut --> nodeLoad["R_LOAD"]
    nodeLoad --> nodeGND["GROUND"]

    nodeVOut --> nodeR1["R_1 FEEDBACK DIVIDER UPPER"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2["R_2 FEEDBACK DIVIDER LOWER"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeGND

    nodeRS --> nodeQ1["Q_1 SENSE BJT BASE"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ2["Q_2 CONTROL BJT BASE DRIVE DIVERTER"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodePass
    nodeQ2 --> nodeGND

    nodeVOut --> nodeVref["BANDGAP V_REF FOR REGULATION LOOP"]
    nodeVref --> nodeAmp["ERROR AMPLIFIER"]
    nodeAmp --> nodePass
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When the load current rises, the voltage across $R_S$ increases. Once it exceeds $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ of $Q_1$, $Q_1$ turns on, which in turn activates $Q_2$. $Q_2$ shunts base drive away from $Q_{\text{pass}}$, throttling the load current. As $V_O$ collapses during a short, the divider $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ reduces the headroom at the base of $Q_1$, lowering the current threshold ŌĆö the *foldback* action.

---

## 4.2 I-V Characteristic: Output Curve With and Without Foldback

The diagram below shows the **load line** traced by the regulator under (a) constant current limit and (b) foldback current limit. This is the classic KTU-board-style question figure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WITHOUT_FOLDBACK["A OUTPUT CURVE WITHOUT FOLDBACK"]
        dirA[" "] --- ptA1["REGULATION REGION V_O NOMINAL I_L NORMAL"]
        ptA1 --- ptA2["CURRENT LIMIT FLAT TOP I_SC_MAX CONSTANT"]
        ptA2 --- ptA3["DEAD SHORT V_O ZERO I_L EQUALS I_SC_MAX"]
    end

    subgraph WITH_FOLDBACK["B OUTPUT CURVE WITH FOLDBACK"]
        dirB[" "] --- ptB1["REGULATION REGION V_O NOMINAL I_L NORMAL"]
        ptB1 --- ptB2["KNEE I_L PEAKS AT I_SC_MAX WHEN V_O EQUALS V_K"]
        ptB2 --- ptB3["FOLDBACK SLOPE I_L DECREASES AS V_O FALLS"]
        ptB3 --- ptB4["DEAD SHORT V_O ZERO I_L EQUALS I_SC_MIN VERY LOW"]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key visual cue for the KTU exam:** The foldback curve **slopes backward** (current decreases as voltage decreases), tracing a "hook" or "knee" shape. The constant-current-limit curve is a **horizontal step** at the top.

---

## 4.3 Functional State Machine of the Protection Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S_NORMAL
    S_NORMAL: STATE S_NORMAL REGULATED OUTPUT
    S_NORMAL --> S_LIMIT: I_L EXCEEDS I_SC_MAX
    S_LIMIT: STATE S_LIMIT CONSTANT CURRENT LIMIT ACTIVE
    S_LIMIT --> S_FOLDBACK: V_O DROPS BELOW V_K
    S_FOLDBACK: STATE S_FOLDBACK CURRENT FOLDS BACK TO I_SC_MIN
    S_FOLDBACK --> S_NORMAL: FAULT CLEARED V_O RECOVERS
    S_LIMIT --> S_NORMAL: FAULT CLEARED
    S_FOLDBACK --> S_SHUTDOWN: SUSTAINED FAULT TIMER EXPIRES
    S_SHUTDOWN: STATE S_SHUTDOWN LATCH OFF OR HICCUP
    S_SHUTDOWN --> S_NORMAL: MANUAL RESET OR AUTO RETRY
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Many commercial regulators (e.g., LM317, LT1083) implement a **variant** of this state machine in silicon. The foldback region in such ICs is internally trimmed using on-chip resistor ratios matched to the bandgap reference.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]** Define **Short Circuit Protection** in a voltage regulator. Why is it considered a non-functional but essential block?

**Model Answer:**

Short Circuit Protection (SCP) is a current-limiting safeguard incorporated within voltage regulators and power supplies. Its function is to detect an abnormal surge in load current ŌĆö caused by an output short circuit ŌĆö and intervene to limit or shut down the current path, thereby protecting the pass transistor from thermal destruction.

Although SCP does **not** contribute to the regulated output voltage under normal conditions (hence *non-functional*), it is **essential** because:

- The pass transistor's Safe Operating Area (SOA) is finite.
- A short circuit forces $V_O \to 0$, meaning the **full** $V_{IN}$ is dropped across the pass device.
- Without SCP, $P_Q = V_{IN} \cdot I_L$ can exceed the transistor's $P_{D(\text{max})}$ rating within microseconds, causing **secondary breakdown** and permanent failure.

> **Examiner's Note:** 3-mark questions expect crisp definitions + 2ŌĆō3 lines of justification. Avoid lengthy derivations.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **constant current limiting** and **foldback current limiting**. State one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Constant Current Limit | Foldback Current Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Output current during short | Held constant at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ | Reduced to a small $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ |
| Power dissipation in pass transistor | High ($V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{max})}$) | Much lower ($V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})}$) |
| I-V curve shape | Flat-top (rectangular) | Hook / knee shape |
| SOA margin | Moderate | Excellent |
| Design complexity | Lower | Slightly higher |

**Advantage of constant current limit:** Simplicity ŌĆö requires only one sense resistor and one transistor.

**Advantage of foldback:** The pass transistor dissipates far less power during sustained faults, dramatically improving long-term reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module-level questions carry 14 marks. Students must answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B** (internal choice). Each question has two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Module 4, Set A]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **foldback current-limited series regulator**. Show the complete I-V characteristic and mark the regulation, current-limit, and foldback regions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive expressions for $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ and $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ in terms of the sense resistor $R_S$, divider resistor $R_1$, and $V_{BE(\text{on})}$. A regulator is designed for $V_O = 5$ V, $I_{L(\text{max})} = 1$ A, and $V_{IN} = 12$ V. If $I_{SC(\text{max})} = 1.2$ A and the foldback ratio is 5, determine $R_S$, $R_1$, and the power dissipated in the pass transistor during a sustained short circuit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

The foldback current-limited series regulator consists of:

- A **series pass transistor** $Q_{\text{pass}}$ (typically a power BJT or Darlington).
- A **sense resistor** $R_S$ in series with the load.
- A **sensing transistor** $Q_1$ whose base-emitter junction senses $V_{RS} + (\text{divider feedback})$.
- A **control transistor** $Q_2$ that shunts base drive from $Q_{\text{pass}}$ when activated.
- A **feedback divider** $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ sampling the output voltage.
- A **reference and error amplifier** (bandgap + op-amp) providing the regulation feedback.

**Operating regions (refer to Section 4.2 diagram):**

1. **Regulation Region:** $V_{RS} < V_{BE(\text{on})}$, so $Q_1$ is off. The error amplifier drives $Q_{\text{pass}}$ to maintain $V_O$ constant. The I-V curve is a horizontal line at $V_O = V_{\text{nominal}}$.

2. **Current-Limit Region:** When $I_L$ rises to $I_{SC(\text{max})} = V_{BE(\text{on})} / R_S$, $Q_1$ turns on. $Q_2$ begins to divert base drive, clamping $I_L$ at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$. The curve is flat-topped ŌĆö current is constant as $V_O$ begins to droop.

3. **Foldback Region:** As $V_O$ decreases, the divider $R_1$ŌĆō$R_2$ reduces the available voltage at the base of $Q_1$, so the threshold for conduction is reached at a *lower* load current. The operating point slides down the foldback slope, reaching $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ at $V_O = 0$.

**I-V Characteristic:** The plot is a "hook" ŌĆö regulation (vertical segment), knee at $(V_K, I_{SC(\text{max})})$, foldback slope, terminating at $(0, I_{SC(\text{min})})$.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Derivation of $I_{SC(\text{max})}$:**

The sense transistor $Q_1$ turns on when the voltage across $R_S$ equals its base-emitter turn-on voltage:

$$V_{RS} = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$
$$I_{SC(\text{max})} \cdot R_S = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$
$$I_{SC(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}$$

**Derivation of $I_{SC(\text{min})}$:**

At a dead short ($V_O = 0$), the divider contributes no headroom. The conduction path now sees $R_S$ and $R_1$ in series:

$$I_{SC(\text{min})} \cdot (R_S + R_1) = V_{BE(\text{on})}$$
$$I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1}$$

**Numerical computation:**

Given: $I_{SC(\text{max})} = 1.2$ A, $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.65$ V, $FR = 5$.

$$R_S = \dfrac{0.65}{1.2} = 0.5417 \; \Omega$$

$$R_1 = (FR - 1) \cdot R_S = 4 \times 0.5417 = 2.167 \; \Omega$$

$$I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{0.65}{0.5417 + 2.167} = \dfrac{0.65}{2.7083} = 0.240 \; \text{A}$$

**Pass-transistor power dissipation during sustained short:**

$$P_{Q(\text{SC})} = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})} = (12 - 0) \cdot 0.240 = 2.88 \; \text{W}$$

Without foldback, the dissipation would be $12 \times 1.2 = 14.4$ W. The foldback scheme reduces the fault dissipation by a factor of **5** ŌĆö matching the foldback ratio.

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 4, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain why **foldback protection** is preferred over **simple constant current limiting** in high-current linear regulators. Discuss the role of the **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear regulator has $V_{IN} = 15$ V, $V_O = 5$ V, $I_{L(\text{max})} = 2$ A, and a sense resistor $R_S = 0.25 \; \Omega$. The foldback divider resistor is $R_1 = 0.75 \; \Omega$. Compute the maximum short-circuit current, the foldback holding current, the foldback ratio, and the worst-case pass-transistor power dissipation. Assume $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a)

In a linear regulator, when the output is shorted, $V_O$ collapses to zero. The pass transistor must therefore drop the **entire** $V_{IN}$ across itself, while simultaneously conducting the full load current. The instantaneous power dissipation is:

$$P_Q = V_{IN} \cdot I_L$$

If $I_L$ is held constant at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ by a simple current limit, the regulator operates at a single high-stress point on the transistor's SOA curve. For a typical 5 A power BJT, this point often lies **outside** the DC SOA boundary ŌĆö placing the device in the **secondary breakdown** region, where localized thermal runaway destroys the chip.

**Foldback protection addresses this in two ways:**

1. **Reduced fault current:** At $V_O = 0$, the current is reduced to $I_{SC(\text{min})}$, which can be 3ŌĆō6 times smaller than $I_{SC(\text{max})}$. Power dissipation drops by the same factor.

2. **SOA trajectory safety:** The foldback locus traces a path that remains *within* the SOA boundary of the pass transistor across the entire range of $V_O$ from $V_K$ down to 0. This is because the locus is approximately a hyperbola of constant power ($P_Q = V_{CE} \cdot I_C \approx \text{constant}$), which the SOA curve closely resembles.

In contrast, the constant-current-limit locus is a **vertical line** at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ extending from $V_O = V_{\text{nominal}}$ down to $V_O = 0$. This vertical line cuts through the SOA boundary at low $V_O$ (high $V_{CE}$), causing destructive operation.

**Conclusion:** Foldback keeps the pass transistor within its SOA during sustained faults, making it the preferred protection method for high-current linear regulators.

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b)

**Given:** $V_{IN} = 15$ V, $V_O = 5$ V, $I_{L(\text{max})} = 2$ A, $R_S = 0.25 \; \Omega$, $R_1 = 0.75 \; \Omega$, $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Maximum short-circuit current:**

$$I_{SC(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.25} = 2.8 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Foldback (holding) current:**

$$I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.25 + 0.75} = \dfrac{0.7}{1.00} = 0.70 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Foldback ratio:**

$$FR = \dfrac{I_{SC(\text{max})}}{I_{SC(\text{min})}} = \dfrac{2.8}{0.70} = 4.0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Worst-case pass-transistor power dissipation:**

The worst case in normal operation occurs when $V_O = V_{IN}/2$ (maximum differential voltage across pass element):

$$V_{CE} = V_{IN} - V_O = 15 - 7.5 = 7.5 \; \text{V (at mid-load)}$$

$$P_{Q(\text{max, normal})} = 7.5 \cdot 2 = 15 \; \text{W}$$

During a sustained short circuit:

$$P_{Q(\text{SC})} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})} = 15 \cdot 0.70 = 10.5 \; \text{W}$$

If constant current limit were used instead:

$$P_{Q(\text{const})} = 15 \cdot 2.8 = 42 \; \text{W}$$

> The foldback scheme reduces the fault stress by a factor of 4 (matching the foldback ratio). The designer must verify that $P_{Q(\text{SC})} = 10.5$ W is below the transistor's $P_{D(\text{max})}$ rating with adequate heatsinking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+R_1$ term** in the denominator of $I_{SC(\text{min})}$. Many students incorrectly write $I_{SC(\text{min})} = V_{BE} / R_S$, which is the *same* as $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ ŌĆö a sure sign of conceptual confusion. **Penalty: 2ŌĆō3 marks.**
> 2. **Conflating $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ with $I_{L(\text{max})}$.** These are *different* quantities. $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ is always $1.1$ŌĆō$1.5 \times I_{L(\text{max})}$, not equal to it.
> 3. **Not drawing the I-V characteristic.** KTU examiners allocate **2ŌĆō3 marks** for the qualitative shape of the I-V curve. A textual explanation alone is insufficient ŌĆö sketch the curve with labeled regions.
> 4. **Ignoring units.** Always carry units through every step. A numerical answer without units loses the final mark.
> 5. **Omitting the SOA justification.** When asked "why foldback?", students often give surface answers like "less power dissipation." The **SOA argument** is the deeper, marks-rich justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save this for last-minute review):**

- **Short Circuit Protection (SCP):** Current-limiting safeguard that prevents pass-transistor damage during output faults.

- **Two main flavors of SCP:**
  - **Constant Current Limit:** Holds $I_L$ at $I_{SC(\text{max})}$; simple but stress-heavy.
  - **Foldback Limit:** Reduces $I_L$ to $I_{SC(\text{min})}$ as $V_O \to 0$; complex but SOA-safe.

- **Core formulas (memorize these verbatim):**
  - $I_{SC(\text{max})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S}$
  - $I_{SC(\text{min})} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S + R_1}$
  - $FR = 1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_S}$
  - $P_{Q(\text{SC})} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC(\text{min})}$

- **Key insight:** The foldback ratio is **purely a resistor ratio** ŌĆö independent of $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ ŌĆö making the design robust against temperature.

- **Why foldback over constant limit?** Keeps the pass transistor inside its **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** during sustained faults, avoiding secondary breakdown.

- **I-V curve signature:** Foldback traces a **hook / knee** shape; constant limit traces a **rectangular step**. This is the single most-tested visual concept.

- **Typical design values:** $I_{SC(\text{max})} = 1.2 \cdot I_{L(\text{max})}$, foldback ratio $= 3$ to $6$, $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.65$ V at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$.

- **Worst-case dissipation location:** In normal operation, $P_Q$ peaks at $V_O = V_{IN}/2$ (mid-range load). In fault, it peaks at $V_O = 0$ but is reduced by foldback.

- **Commercial examples:** LM78xx, LM317, LT1083, LT1084 ŌĆö all implement internal foldback in silicon.

- **SOA curve shape:** Hyperbolic ($V_{CE} \cdot I_C = \text{constant at high } V_{CE}$). The foldback locus is *designed* to mimic this hyperbola, ensuring the operating point never escapes the safe region.

- **Hiccup mode:** A further refinement where the regulator auto-restarts periodically during a fault, dramatically reducing average dissipation. Used in modern SMPS controllers.

- **KTU exam weightage:** Expect a 7-mark sub-question on the **derivation** of $I_{SC(\text{max})}$ and $I_{SC(\text{min})}$, and a 3-mark conceptual question on **why foldback**.

- **Common mistake to avoid:** Never write $I_{SC(\text{min})} = V_{BE}/R_S$ ŌĆö the presence of $R_1$ in the denominator is the *defining feature* of foldback.
